review_text:
I took my family to the Holocaust Museum because none of us should ever forget what happened to the European Jews under a totalitarian regime. We only visited the children's wing this time -- because I didn't want to overwhelm our kids too much (9, 11 & 12 years old).

The exhibit was excellent. It taught our children from an experiential point of view about one child, named Daniel, who survived the Holocaust. It made a big impact on them, especially my son, who's almost 10. He's still thinking about Daniel, and mentions him from time to time.

The visit has led us, as a family, to discuss the reality of evil in general and antisemitism in particular.

This is a great museum. The films are moving and strangely touching. The flow is perfect and easily moves guests through the build up and completion of the holocaust. The images are powerful and text is easily understandable. The division of topics makes this place super understandable for anyone above 12. 

However, this place is typically too crowded to navigate around crowds and enjoy any of that. The exhibits are usually blocked by too many bodies to read the wall or see the images. The films are full before they start and standing room is not even available. The halls which promote the excellent flow are choked to a halt which prevents patrons from moving forwardin the exhibits. Also not inline similarity themed museums, this museum allows small children who scream over the recordings and detract from the mood which the museum tries so hard to promote.

The Holocaust was the ultimate horror of the twentieth century.  If you're going to have a museum of the Holocaust, with a profound message to subsequent generations then it needs to hard hitting, powerful, shocking.  This isn't, it's sanitised, it's Disney does Holocaust.  What an opportunity wasted.

Like it or not, it's the sight of the gas chambers, the emaciated skeletons, the dead bodies by the hundred that does this.  I don't expect to see kids laughing and smiling here, but that's what you get.  It's so far off the mark.  See Berlin Holocaust Museum or the Museum of the Warsaw Uprising in Warsaw for how it should be done.  What an opportunity missed!

First this is a free museum but you must plan ahead.  It is more of a gallery of photos that take you through the timeline of the Holocaust.  I don't know what I was expecting but was not wowed by the material overall.  If the Holocaust is new to you or you haven't studied it much this will provide a lot of new things to you.
What an important museum. Milder than I expected, I was worried for my young daughter to be traumatized but I prepped her before hand and she was ready. Make sure you book in advance.

I was highly disappointed in this museum. I am not sure why I reserved advanced tickets when there were so many people there at the same time as my family. It was extremely hot and crowded from the time we stepped off the elevator. There is no way possible that this organization is abiding by fire regulations. A significant amount of the museum was based on reading information, but with the excessive number of people, that was not possible. My husband had to take my son out because of the closeness of people and lack of space and air. The worst part about the experience was that no one of authority seemed to care. I will never suggest this museum to anyone. As a matter of fact, I will do just the opposite and suggest they find something else to spend their time with while in this great city.
It's a must visit this museum! Its fantastic, very realistic!

I have never seen so many people walking through a museum so quietly. Still so hard to imagine all the atrocities. There was a lot of reading required so while I did not think my 11 year old was too young, she did lose interest in reading all the exhibits.

This is truly a memorial that everyone should experience. Being Jewish and having friends who were in the Holocaust, it is especially moving. They did an excellent job portraying how it could happen and how it should never happen again. This is a must for every one.

When your visiting this museum, please give yourself a minimum of 2 hours. The exhibits were well displayed and it really brings you to sad time in history that every generation need to pass to another.

This was my second time going.  I don't have the vocabulary to describe this museum.  From the time you walk in the customer service is great.  The free admission (like most of the DC museums) makes this a MUST for any DC visitors.  The exhibits are extremely touching, mesmerizing, heart-tugging, and overwhelming - in a good way.  They have 'calming' areas between floors - which you will need - that really help.  To see what the Jewish race went through is horrifying and disturbing, but seeing all of this make you realize that nothing you've experienced has made you feel this way (most likely).  Save 3-4 hours of your day for this, as you will want to take you time.  You'll probably spend some time in the reflecting room near the end,, very pacifying.  Oh, and don't forget to follow the person who's card you select...it is a concentration camp member and you don't know their fate until the end.  Take the time to sit thru the videos in each section.

Went here one morning and was my least favourite of all the museums. If you want a very factual trip or have a special interest in the Holocaust then go but for the general tourist there are better things to do.

This museum is something that all the children of the world especially need to see and understand what WWII was all about.  It had been 22 years since I had seen the museum...and this place had changed in location and in the displays.....Daniel's Story is portrayed and very spell bounding.  You feel like you really understand what he must have felt.  Very humbling......Wish they had something more of Anne Frank and her story as well....Wasn't able to see the movie we got there to late tickets were already gone for the day...

The Holocaust Museum is very informative and educational for one to learn about the awful things done.  It is a powerful, somber experience.  It does take some time to go through this museum properly.

I booked tickets for this well in advance. I doubt you would get in just by turning up. The exhibition is very informative but depressing nonetheless. It certainly paints the horrors of the holocaust, but having been on a trip to Auschwitz last year could not hope to portray the evil of the place.  A must see for those who know little.
Second time visiting for me, first for my daughter. We covered every inch of this wonderful museum. This is a must see, it is so well done and informative. We learned so much beyond what we expected.

This is one of the finest although saddest museums in the world.

Designed brilliantly with suggestions of the spotlights securing incarceration, heating elements for cremation, stark walls, bare metal flooring, and heavy gates and bars, a visit here whether in the ticketed museum or other sections is unforgettable.

With a tremendous amount of people who practice antihistory and deny that these black, tragic chapters of Judaic and world history as manufactured , lest we forget, this life changing museum is a mandatory visit.
it might be that it wasn't what we were looking for - fun and entertainment; but we went thru really fast. We've seen a lot of kids inside; it's probably better be visited with the guide

This to be fair is not the best museum but it is about the holocaust. There are plenty of interesting and horrifying information to read about. Certainly it should never be forgotten. The building is very nice.

This is an exhaustive look into the Holocaust...I'm a history teacher, and found the descriptions overwhelming and have lost the effect it used to have on teens and young adults.  I respect they want to provide a comprehensive story and timeline of events, but my teen practically begged me to scuttle on through after the 1.5 hour mark, because we still had two floors to cover.   

I don't remember it being so overwhelming ten years ago when I brought 30 students and they left in tears (it got to the point back then)...now there's too much reading of minuscule progressions of what happened back then.  I understand they want to provide as much detail as possible, but Overwhelming is the best way to describe it...

Order tix online before the trip, or order the morning of (I think at 6am, pronto...$1/person), or go to the museum when it opens (10am, free),or you'll br standing outside wishing you could get in.

Must see for everyone just to see how it all developed over more than a decade before the "final solution" was actually put into practice. How a society and the world in general really set the stage for all this to occur causes one to think about how we go about things in the modern world.

The only thing that was a pain about the place was the parking. The outside staff was clueless about where it was or how to get there. They continuously gave wrong directions resulting in erroneous trips over the bridge into Virginia and back. Bush league staff for sure.
Excellent learning experience, laid out in chronological order along six floors.  The BAD:  There is only one public restroom....in the basement!!

Emotionally this was the most somber experience of my D.C. trip but as the saying inscribed in the museum we must never forget so that such tragedy never repeats itself.  Be prepared for extremely long lines and crowds, give it about three hours and although free to visit I highly suggest booking a timed ticket for entry months in advance for $1 per ticket.

I had high expectations for this museum. We first went in Daniel's story which tells the life story of a child who survived the holocaust. You almost get to walk in his shoes as you live out his diary. You start in a mockup of his home, transition to the ghetto and then to the camps. It was a great exhibit and I was expecting more of the same from the "permanent" exhibit. Unfortunately that was as good as it got. 

Like others have said, get there early and get your ticket. They are free but they go fast. We got there at 10 and our time to enter was 1:45. Going in I really was hoping to get a true "feel" for what it was like to have been there. As we got off the elevator we were greeted with a gridlock of people. At first I thought this was part of the "feel", kind of like Daniels story. Alas it was simply a result of a vastly over crowded exhibit and poor planning. The upside is it was self guided so when a hole opened up we could dart through and move on though we missed parts when doing so. Instead of a neat exhibit all you got was a lot of pictures accompanied by voluminous amounts of reading. I am not sure I read that much in grad school. The biggest problem for my family was due to the crowds, unless you were tall, you couldn't see or read most of the information. My youngest was very disappointed. For those with young kids you must know that there are some very gruesome videos however they do a good job, with screens and walls, of making them hard to view for young ones. Still my youngest (9yrs old) was able to view them with some effort. I don't personally mind as I not a fan of sugar coating something as serious as the holocaust, especially when we have people that believe it never happened.

Some parts like the diorama of the intake and gassing process were amazingly well done. Others like the rail car seemed like an after thought. You get to walk through it. Why not close us in it and play the sounds of the tracks, crying babies and barking dogs at the camp? I am not asking for Disney Land but what a wasted opportunity! 

If I were Jewish and had a family connection to certain camps or ghettos I am sure I might have a different evaluation. As a non-Jew however I felt I wasn't part of their target audience. The volume of information seemed like over kill and the crowds ruined the experience. Just my two cents worth.

We enjoyed the Holocaust Museum overall.  We did have to get tickets and then wait about one hour so be prepared for that.  The museum space is beautiful and the exhibits are well done. Since we have visited Dachau and the Anne Frank House previously, we were prepared for the feelings of sadness this evokes.  The museum takes you from the rise of Hitler to the rescue at the concentration camps.   I thought the clothing, shoes and personal items touched me the most. The train that was similar to the ones that were used  give you a good idea of the horrible transit conditions.  They also have the bunk beds I believe from Auschwitz.  There is a model showing the process the Nazis used when sending people to the gas chamber.  Another exhibit showed how medical tests were performed on prisoners.  The video at the end was very interesting and highly recommended for stories of survivors.

The Holocaust museum serves an important purpose about reminding us about Nazi Grrmany. However I felt the museum did a poor job of explaining why were Jews actually outlawed in Germany by Hitler. It needed a bit more of that background before showing all the things that happened to them to give a complete picture of history.

A very unique experience that certainly causes reflection.  Requires planning if going in a time period when tickets are required.  The top floor was way to dark for me and my contacts so I couldn't read a thing so that was disappointing but overall a great experience.

No matter who you are, you can't help but feel the terror and fear that Hitler did to the Jews in the 20th century. The genocide, death camps, and murders can only be compared to the Nanjing Massacre by the Japanese in China in the same period.

Unfortunately we didn't realize you need an advance ticket. Since we did not have one there was not much to see. Daniel's story was a quick walk through mostly aimed at younger folks, the children's tile wall and a gift shop. That was it - very dissappointing.

My 12 year old son and I went to the memorial last week.  It was super crowded and hard to see anything.  There were crying babies and toddlers everywhere (it was mid-afternoon).  I would not recommend this unless you go during off-season.
Plan to spend quite a few hours here and - know emotionally it will be difficult.  A true tribute and one not to be missed.

I initially came early in the week and was told I needed to get tickets ahead of time. I ordered one online, $1, for later in the week. The museum is informative. Very crowded but the images are powerful. I appreciated the narratives throughout. Well worth visiting.

Visit after August and before April and there are no reservations needed. You should know what you are getting into - this is a remembrance of some of man's most inhumane treatment of his fellow man/woman/children. At least the Germans have admitted their sins, so this is about prevent further incidents (and a place that some of our present leaders need to visit repeatedly!!) of hate and prejudice. A must for anyone who cares or needs to care.

Although sad to see the hatred and evil things that man can do to another man it was enlightening to see.  There is hope that by being aware of such atrocities people will not repeat the same inhumane behavior - yet we see it repeated over and over again even today.  Genocide in Africa ...
Perhaps we need a higher source of guidance?

Another free museum:)
Please note this place is not appropriate for children under 12. We saw one girl crying hysterically.
What is there to say really about a place like this? As I was walking around I thought that all of those strange people who deny the Holocaust ever happened should come and take a look.
If this kind of thing really interests you, you will think its great. I just became more and more depressed as I went round and actually didn't read about 30% of the displays.
I appreciate that Jews were one of his targets but other groups such as homosexuals, Romany's and the disabled were only fleetingly mentioned.
Some of the most emotive items on display were the railway carriage that took people to one of the camps, actual bunks from Auschwitz and........the shoes.

This is a great memorial museum.  It's strange how so many people can be inside the exhibit but yet there is silence.  It is a very humbling experience.  I saw people of all ages.  As I walk through, and this has been my second time, and I have read a lot about the Holocaust, I simply felt sick to my stomach..not that the museum isn't very well done, just the simple reality of this actually happening just sickens me.  And yet we are asked to "remember" so it won't happen again. But look throughout the world folks, it still is in other countries.

The exhibit is extremely informative and interesting.  Negatives are: you must get there early to obtain (free) tickets then wait for your start time (or they may be out for the day); it take a while to actually go through - we missed the last floor because they were closing; and it can get very congested in places. 
Overall a must see.

There is a lot of information in this memorial, and I would recommend setting a time limit (1-2hrs) for yourself.  It becomes overwhelming, and of course the nature of the material itself can bog you down.  However, the memorial does do a very tasteful job and definitely aims to inform in a way that never lets something like this happen again.

Informative and pulls at your heart-strings. The last time I went it was super crowded (holiday weekend) so would definitely avoid that. There is an area for kids but I would caution about the explaining you will want to do.
Be prepared to fight the crowds.  I wish I would have gotten their earlier.

My husband and I were visiting DC for the first time and a visit to the Holocaust Museum was a must for me.  I am of Jewish decent and I have studied WWII and Holocaust History most of my adult life.  I have also been to Dachau on a visit to Germany.   I was disappointed because I was expecting more "real life" stories and experiences from survivors. As another reviewer stated they do a good job at "hiding many of the atrocities" However I believe that is a dis-service to hide those horrible acts.  This museum while set up in a very educational manner focuses on Hilter's rise to Power which although very important I did not find spent enough time on the victims.  Seemed very educational; geared towards children ( which is a good thing) and the uninformed.     For me and my husband it was lacking a personal feeling and connection.   I know I will be in the minoritiy in my review but felt it was necessary to share.

We went in a rainy day and that made the visit seem so surreal.  The elevator, the room and smell of the shoes, the US Army arriving at the camp and radioing to the commander stating " omg , we 'be got some bad situation here..."  I could barely stand it.  Man's inhumanity to man!

Something was missing though.  As some posters previously said the mood was thrown off by the other guests - mainly school trips, laughing and playing.  I guess they should say take it seriously and respect the history and magnitude before groups enter.

We were excited to experience this museum until we got out of the elevator at the top and it was literally shoulder to shoulder with people. We didnt move for at least 10 minutes and you couldnt even read anything. Plus there was at least 2-3 school groups who really had no interest in seeing anything at all but just taking up space. We were expecting at least more artifacts, but it is all reading. Overall it was hot, crowded and I could have learned more watching documentaries. Not sure why all the good reviews.

After waiting in line for almost an hour, and researching this place before visiting, we were hoping for a great experience that pays homage to those who died and were held captive. Unfortunately, it just did not live up to our expectations. It was very anticlimactic and definitely not worth the wait. We have visited other holocaust memorials throughout Europe and this just did not compare. I would not recommend this place.

It is a sad museum, but very informational. There are some graphic pictures (after all it was the holocaust). It has some artifacts and lots of descriptions, including first-hand accounts. I went during the anniversary and it was packed.

I was looking forward to showing my 15 year old son the museum. As we wound through the exhibits it dawned on me that it has been toned down a lot since the last time I visited in the early 2000s. the whole first part is about how Hitler came into power, the second part is about how the Jews were put in the ghettos. there is only a relatively small part on the actual camps. and the last part is how the allies found the camps. there where exhibits missing that I felt lend gravity to the situation, for example the room full of mens, womens and childrens shoes that they were made to take off before they entered the gas chambers, was missing. the bails of human hair that was shorn from the victims was missing too. when I went the first time there was a lot more information on the many people the Nazis discriminated against, for example the disfigured, the mentally ill, religions that did not support them etc. I am one of Jehovah's Witness and the first time I went there was a whole exhibit on them, the second time I went they had removed the exhibit and spread it throughout the museum this last time I went, there was 2 paragraphs that mention the Witnesses that I could find in the whole museum, there was more info in the gift shop about them. But other than that, it is a somber look at what people are willing to do to each other especially when you hold power over them. and how people are willing to look the other way if they feel it will benefit them. and also how history repeats itself.

This "museum" was bizarre. One good exhibit based around the story of a Jewish boy, David and his journey through the war years. Once you have seen that, the rest was just video screens, lots of writing on big boards and computer screens so you could look things up. If you wanted to do that, you could do that at home. Lots of empty space. Very poor signage with exits signs leading to dead ends and toilet blocks. We were left bemused and disappointed.

Though this is not one of the best museums in the area, it certainly is moving.  It is difficult to write that a museum that has a focus on such a horrific period of history is not an A+ museum, but it really isn't.  Many of the exhibits have elements that are repeated throughout the entire museum, and in many cases the same photos are used numerous times.  I point this out because as I went through this museum I believed that there was more that could have been done, and in a way that was better than offered.  For instance, you will move from room to room - imagining with the help of photos - that you are a Jew in Germany and what it would be like to move from home, to restricted area, to camp... Throughout this there are photos that are repeated and the overall effect seems a bit corny after a while.  

It is certainly history that needs to be reviewed by everyone.  And this museum does  a very good job of providing information in a way that is appealing to most.  It just falls a bit flat for me.

This is a tribute to the Jewish people and others who perished under the evil hand of hitler and his followers. It is so sad to see the shoe exhibit, so sad to see what the gas chambers did, yet a reminder that the world is filled with evil to this day and we cannot allow it to spread.

I visited this place because my daughter wanted to receive extra credit for a school project.  Otherwise, I would not have gone there.  While the genocide that we refer to as the Holocaust perpetrated during World War II was horrible, there have been many other genocides throughout human history that have been equally as bad, if not worse.  Indeed, entire peoples have been wiped from the face of the earth and their plight has been ignored in this museum.  A gross oversight.
Good information on the history of the Holocost. Took along time to go through.

I was so excited to go to this museum. We had to get up at 6am to secure tickets online and i was so excited! Unfortunately, there were so many kids at the museum you couldnt even stop to read or look at anything. The kids didnt want to be there, they were so rude and obviously not interested in the museum like other people. Seems like it would be an awesome idea not to shove hundreds of kids in a historical and popular museum that people are wanting to actually visit and learn something. I appreciated the museum though from what i saw.

My husband & I (both Roman Catholics , FYI) really found the HOLOCAUST MUSEUM informative, interactive, historical  and very well presented.

The guides lead you to an elevator in the lobby which takes you to the 4th floor. You then walk down the stairs to see each exhibit. Very clever to have the presentation from top to bottom.
The exhibits are displayed in chronological order from 1933-1945.

There are many exhibits, including one exhibit of major American newspapers front pages during war time. Another exhibit is a passage through a box car & through a concentration camp. Most exhibits have authentic photos. All exhibits have written historical information beside the exhibit. 

One of my favorite exhibits was the walls giving praise of those who helped Jews during wartime: Priests, normal citizens, citizens with influence like Schindler , etc

People attending were polite & quiet .

You may think you don't want to see this, but I say don't miss it.  My wife has relatives that were in concentration camps and did not want to see this but went and was glad she did.  It paints the history of the rise and fall of Germany including Hitlers childhood. Anything that you might not want to view is hidden behind 3' walls to hide from children or anyone else that does not care to see the graphic scenes of the era.  We ended up spending a good 3-4 hours here.

If you graze through the recent reviews, you gain a certain impression.  And sadly, this general thought is accurate.  The information that is presented is accurate, thoughtful, and powerful.  There are aspects of the Holocaust which are shown that few books, movies, or the media deal with.  However, the lay out of the museum does not work.  This type of layout may work in some cities; however, D.C. is known for school trips that do basically the same itinerary.  Therefore, this kind of cattle drive style was exhausting.  For the first twenty minutes, we simply stood in line and read a single panel on a wall.  I mentioned to my family if this was meant to humble us; how dare we think that our inconvenience is bad when in front of us is true pain!   

I'm really not sure why the displays are in such a confining way.  Even going to the bathroom becomes an agonizing process.

Great information, but not organized in a a practical, usable way.
overall the museum is educative. somewhat repetitive after a while.

My husband and I visited with our 17yr old granddaughter and for all of us this was the most important thing we experienced in DC. This museum presents extremely difficult and painful information in a powerful, personal way. My husband and Granddaughter prefer to power walk most museums, but we stayed 2.5 hours and could have used more time. I will never forget "the shoes". So well done, but some of it is very hard to witness. We must never forget.

This memorial museum is excellent. The artifacts and the personal stories bring home the horror that was the holocaust. When you're having a bad day at work and think you've got it bad, picture yourself in that situation and you'll realize the true meaning of hardship. There is no low that humans won't stoop to inflict harm on other humans given the right circumstances. We must never forget what happened and try to make sure similar circumstances don't arise again.
The museum is definitely something to see while in the capital.

A very in-depth museum dedicated to the Holocaust. It definitely achieves its goal of inspiring citizens and leaders worldwide to confront hatred and prevent genocide. Yet as you walk through the museum, you realise that genocide unfortunately still continues in certain parts of the world now. 

I hope more people visit the museum and is made aware of how important it is to maintain democratic values and to take action to keep hatred and anti-semitism in check.

I have been numerous times to Yad Vashem in Jerusalem, Israel and although having had high expectations when visiting the Holocaust Museum in Washington DC, I found it to be of a lesser standard than Yad Vashem. Considering that it has a couple of "casts" and not the originals, a lot of oversize posters and videos, I still think that it is worth visiting for the purpose of making people aware of the history, the more so if you are unable to go to Israel to see their Holocaust Museum (Yad Vashem).
For me, the most disgusting experience at the Museum in Washington DC was the HUGE amount of school groups, small children as well as young teens, visiting the museum with no respect for the fellow visitors..but even worse, NO respect for the memory, the purpose of why the museum is there. They were loud, noisy, on their cellphones, giggling...while in a quiet corner a woman was crying. Outrages, totally disrespectful, disappointing behavior which in turn prevented us from appreciating the efforts of the displays.
But to give credit....yes, worth seeing. If you are more informed about the Holocaust than the "usual public", you might be disappointed, especially if you have been to Yad Vashem with the extended parks, walkways etc. 
I was really disappointed, mostly in the behavior of the school groups that clearly did not respect or comprehend what they witnessed. 
I would advised people to NOT go during school breaks.

The permanent part of the museum requires a timed ticket (I thought this meant they would limit people to control the crowds). The amount of people they allowed through the main exhibit at one time did not allow for being able to see much (5-6 people deep to see in the exhibits enclosures). If the museum wanted to recreate the sensation of herding humans as experienced at the concentration camps then they were successful but if they want to educate people the amount of people did not allow for one to take in the exhibits.

I was a little disappointed in this particular museum as I have visited one in Israel.  My husband was not with me in Israel so I wanted him to have the experience as I think it's a great reminder to all of humanity the evil that humans are capable of to a fellow human being.  There was not a dry eye that came out of the museum in Israel.  The images of what happened to the Jewish people were up and out in the open.  The videos that showed the atrocities were not hidden behind waist high wall partitions that I remember.  There was no denying the horrors that these individuals experienced.  I remember vividly a sequence of photos showing men digging their own graves and then watching one by one as their fellow Jews were executed and put in the grave they just dug for themselves.  They had to watch and wait for their own bullet.  The women were made to strip down naked and then watch as each woman was shot and killed knowing the next bullet would be theirs.  It wasn't just killing, it was humiliating each person and taking all hope.  In those moments caught on camera and video I connected with those human beings and felt the humiliation, defeat, fear and knowing life was about to end.  It was a moment where you really realize these were human beings executed solely because they were Jewish.  It was so impactful.
This particular museum seemed to focus more on Hitler and his rise to power.  Personally I felt that the atrocities that the Jews experienced were somewhat lost as the most horrific parts were hidden on televisions that played on videos behind waist high walls.  I guess this is to prevent children from seeing these images however these events are every bit a part of what made the Holocaust so horrible.  They happened and hiding them changes the impact of the event.  There were A LOT of school groups going through the exhibit while we were there and they were laughing and snickering as they quickly moved through because they weren't stopping to see what was on the video screens.  I'm pretty sure a lot of them didn't even know the televisions were there because there were so many people waiting to view the area that people just passed the exhibit out of impatience.  In all honesty we skipped some of the televisions because the wait time and number of people were too much.  I felt like many of the kids just saw a man who rose to power, took people from their homes and put them in camps.  Some of them were gassed.  The clay display of the gas chamber is impactful but it's still not real.  When you see the actual videos of the people, then it becomes real.  These were real people and you connect with them.  I feel like that connection with reality is somewhat lost in the exhibit.  The video of the survivors at the end is wonderful.  I believe it would be even more impactful if individuals going through the exhibit were allowed to connect with the real life individuals in the moments that were captured on video and photographs.
The facilities are really nicely done.  Museum was very clean and the staff were great.  My lower rating is only reflective of hiding some of what I feel are super important parts that I believe ought to be out in the open for everyone to see.  Truth is truth and hiding parts of an event I believe changes the story and the impact.

The check in process is complicated on line. The exhibits are thorough, sobering, informative, and depressive. You will be worn out after a visit here. The temporary exhibits were nice at the beginning but we did not get to see the end of either because special events were planned in these areas. However, nothing was posted and forced many people to miss out on these exhibits. The permanent exhibit needs to have a max amount of people at one time. There were so many people corralled into the start of the exhibit and then shoved into the line of people waiting for the movie that it was cramped, claustrophobic, sweaty, and unenjoyable. The entire starting floor was so packed with people that I could not see, enjoy, or navigate civilly. This was one of the worst experiences of any museum or attraction in 38 years of life.

One of the staff members was very nice and helpful when trying to navigate through the temporary exhibits initially and during the potential bomb scare/lock down. The other staff and security were not very helpful, calm, or friendly. Most of the personnel were only worried there special event on the lower floor. One security guard even ran at and screamed at patrons in the lobby. 

The museum is free but they charge a user fee to get the ticket. While it is only a dollar, just say that admission is $1. 

I did not have a pleasant day here!
Donald wood,is writing alyered history about germany,at the sterling club community center,in white plains unless that computer room 202 is a hologram!

This exhibit is very moving, and tells an important story. There were parts that I loved so much. However, the exhibit relies too much on reading a lot of lengthy signs. In several places the crowd just stops in mass. It makes it difficult to appreciate the exhibition. I also think that the video footage was the most compelling aspect which rendered a lot of the reading unnecessary. This is not a place for small children. If you still need a stroller your child does not belong here.

You have to get a timed (free) ticket to this museum and I got one of the last times. This definitely took away from my experience a little as it was very crowded making it difficult to read everything. Be prepared, there is a lot of reading to do in this museum but it is definitely worthwhile. You are given a little book with a profile of a real Jewish person from this time period and as you go from floor to floor, you read about this person in your book and discover their story. This made the museum much more personal and even more moving. Worth a visit if you have the time.

Do not go!  An elavator ride takes you to the 4th floor from which you work your way down.  The elevator deposits you along with dozens of other people into a very, very crowded space. You are expected to view pictures and read the text ualdescriptions. I could not get close enough to see or read a thing.  People where pushing, shoving and stepping on my feet. Young children where crying and fussing because of the congestion. I exited the museum very quickly without seeing anything. 
It is a shame because I am sure there is a great deal to study and from which to learn.  The museum needs to work out a much better space.  I will not be back and will not recommend this to visitors.

Got up at 6:00 Am, started to try to get tickets online. They had one ticket at noon and one at 1. That was it! So I was going to get them. Started to buy but by the time I tried to pay the other was gone. 5 popped up at 1:00 so I got them.

It was nice that you dont have to be there right at your time slot. They give you an hour leeway to start your tour. The only thing I didnt  like is that at the beginning of your tour, after you exit the elevator, you are bottlenecked. Not a good experience. If you are claustrophobic forget it. I almost feel like they do that on purpose to give you a feeling what it might have been like for the Jewish people back then to be crammed in a small space with a lot of people and no way out. So glad we did this tour. It was a very solid experience.
Be sure to go online a couple of days before you visit. I was not able to get in and i was only needing 2 tickets.

A very different take on the holocaust to that of Yad Vashem in Jerusalem, and a very different educational agenda - much more universalistic. This was the only DC museum we went to where the staff were surly and unpleasant.

Entry is FREE! Do not buy "a ticket" online through a "tour" website. We went in the "off season" so we could enter at any time. Durning the in season the website states that its good to go ahead and get tickets for a time slot. This is to prevent over crowding, so everyone can move at there own pace. Honestly, i thought the experience would be far more horrific than it was. Meaning that - this exhibit is appropriate for (most) young children's eyes. I went with my Jewish friends who had family that lived through the camps. They said this exhibit is as good as the one in Israel. Totally worth a trip. Great family conversation starter on many topics including segregation and racism.

This is an amazing museum.  Done very well and a very moving experience.  However, even with limited tickets at specific times, this museum is WAY too crowded to appreciate the message.  Teens in groups were not very respectful - my own teenagers commented on this as well.  I think there could be signage to suggest to stay quiet or be respectful.  Some people have no clue.  The museum does not seem like it was build to handle the massive crowds.  The entrance to the permanent exhibit was about a 25 minute wait with poor queueing capability.  Staff just yelled at you where to go and line up without any signage, ropes, etc.  So there is chaos as a result.

This museum is housed in a beautiful structure. The exhibits are elaborate & informative, the movies are well done. All visitors are expected to begin on the top level which has narrow passageways & as a result, gets very crowded. We eventually gave up in trying to read the placards (which could be larger) and moved to a lower floor where we were able to experience the exhibits more easily. There are some very knowledgeable docents present, but they spoke in very loud voices & were somewhat intrusive given the somber tone of the museum.

I enjoyed this wonderful museum, however I did not enjoy being crammed into an elevators and coming out into an area with many more people.  It was very cramped and slow moving to get started.  Once you got beyond this area it was much easier to move around and view everything.  The beginning definitely needs to be changed.

We got to the museum too late to get the full access ticket to see everythimg. If you want to see more, get there very early as tickets are limited. What we did get to see was a childs diary story Daniel, and an exibit about their friends and neighbors turning them in to yhe nazis

An excellent temporary exhibition on collaborators of the Nazi regime and those individuals who had the courage to resist. For today's Europeans it is essential to remember how it all started in an elected democratic system. Lessons to keep in mind in today's France, Flanders, Netherlands, Sweden, Greece, Hungary and many other countries. It can happen again and quite respectable fellow citizens support racism and intolerance.
This museum is so informative.  The main museum is so interesting.  The exhibit that they have in the basement has not been changed for two years.

As you would expect, the Holocaust Museum is relentlessly intense.  For the most part, sadly, it's not as moving as I expected. Rather, like the nearby African-American Museum it piles fact upon fact, proving that humans can be profoundly inhumane to each other.  Only two exhibits were truly moving and emotionally engaging:  1)the videos of concentration camp survivors speaking about their experiences, almost the last stop in the tour; 2) the room filled with shoes taken from the victims, which seemed much more real than citing another large number or percentage mortality.  In practical terms, for a summer visit be sure to get your timed entry pass online in advance.  It's a busy museum, which made seeing some of the displays quite challenging.

I think the beginning was a bit crowded, how they shove a lot of people in, but I think that may have been the design intention.  The most impacting exhibit, in my opinion is going down that hallway with the collection of shoes.  It brought to my mind of all the communities and people that was erased.

A very somber Museum & a return visit with our son. The permanent museum provides a chronological journey through the rise of Hitler & the Nazi Party, the systematic destruction of millions of Jews to the liberation of Europe including the many death camps. The visuals can be confronting and some of the video footage (behind raised walls) is NOT suitable for younger persons. Very challenging, thought provoking & shocking so not for the faint hearted. There are other exhibits one can also visit & ponder. Very much focussed on the plight of the Jews. I would recommend visiting even if you have a couple of hours. Definitely makes you think!

This museum is a learning experience regardless of how much you think you know.  I could have done with a little less news footage and a little more personal stories to hear about what various individuals went through.  Learning how daily life changed for somebody as time passed and the ghettos got smaller would have made this inhumane experience more personal.  
I do not recommend this museum for anyone under 16 or who isn't ready to handle some of the pictures.

Having studied the Holocaust, Nazi medicine and been to Auschwitz twice and other concentration camps I have some knowledge on the history of the holocaust and the Nazis.

The museum for the most part encapsulated the keys themes of the holocaust. It portrayed all the information of how this came to be very well. The museum is well laid out and very interactive which is very important. I like walking though the actual rail cars used, gave a sense of being there. If you are never able to visit the key sites in Europe then this is a good start. Also it will never let us forget.

The difficulty for me, was it was very swayed to the Jews, who admittedly had the greatest loss but could have included more history about the other groups. Also as there could have been more about Nazi Medicine and the experimentation. There is more of this in Auschwitz.

Worth a visit, and remind us never to forget!
Somber reminder of the past but not impressed with museum as a whole. Maybe I've watched the History Channel with my husband too much.
Very sobering but something that every adult should see.  I don't recommend it for children under 15.  Plan at least 4 hours, otherwise you'll miss too much.

After hearing so much talk about this museum for years I was very excited to see this museum while in DC. We waited very long to get in the door. We thought that would be like a normal Smithsonian and you could walk around at your leisure. This was not the case. You are directed to get a ticket, which is free, the time that's on the ticket is the time you can view the exhibit. We arrived at 12:30 and our tickets said 2:30. There was absolutely nothing to do until then. Sadly, we ended up leaving the museum. Word of advice. Go when it first opens and you may have a better experience then we did

This was my first time visiting the Holocaust Memorial Museum. Although the visit started off a bit stressful (there was no timed entry, so the first part of the permanent exhibit was a madhouse!), the museum is beautifully organized, with amazing and impactful exhibits. It isn't a stretch to say that this museum is life changing.
You need advance tickets to see this memorial, without it the memorial is hard to see. Wished we had been able to visit the entire museum.

The museum itself is outstanding and a must for anyone who wants to learn about this dark period in world history. BUT, security could be from Berlin circa 1934. Standing in line, with my hands by my side, an aggressive security agent demanded "Do you have any carry bags?" My hands were empty, so I replied "Do you see any bags?"  He grew incensed and got in my face and demanded: "What did you say?" I repeated my statement that he could clearly see I wasn't carrying anything. He barked, "You could have a bag hidden under your coat!"  Of course, I could have a lot of things hidden, but before you enter you have to go through x-ray security that's tougher than an airport--and ruder as well.  After this exchange, he loudly demanded that I show him my iphone.  He demanded it! I complied and he said OK.  I imagine he thought I was recording his tirade.  I've read many reviews like this.  Management is well aware of the problem and chooses to do nothing.  I've been to many such museums and will not return to this one. No donations either.

Never got to visit. The Museum website says same day tickets are released at 7 AM each day. My fiancée and I tried 3 days in a row, diligently getting up early to be on the website at 7, and each time we ran into problems: The website giving CAPTCHAs that didnt work, or delaying us enough that each time slot was gone by the time the CAPTCHA refreshed; saying a slot was available and then when I clicked to reserve the slot, saying no tickets were available for the chosen time slot. Tickets were released at 7 and gone by 7:05- even on a weekday!

 Didnt realize wed need to reserve months and months and months in advance just to come see this, and looking at other reviews Im not sure even that wouldve helped. Very disappointed.

The museum is a must see while visiting D.C. The Daniel Story exhibit and all of the exhibits will bring tears to your eyes as you witness the crimes that were committed. The museum is very well laid out and very educational. However this was the only stop on our nine day vacation where the staff was extremely rude. We approached three employees from the back of the building as we had walked from the Jefferson Memorial that were all on their cell phones and as I asked for directions for the entrance one of the young ladies said go down the alley. She did not point in which direction just go down the alley. When we arrived at the front of the museum complete chaos as tourists from around the world see two lines but no one explaining which line is for what. We approached one line and three security guards stared at everyone with no instructions as we had received at The Ford Theater. We chose the line to the left because that is what we do at Disney World and we were herded like cattle through security into the museum. I approached the front desk not because I was told to but because I did not have a clue so I just decided that looked like a great place to start. I was given two tickets that said 11:15 am. I asked should we return or what would she suggest. She stared at me and said I don't care what you do but you can start with Daniel's Story. Both lines do the same they are security points so you can enter the building. The desk is where you get your tickets with the time you will be allowed to tour the museum. The Daniel Story is a must especially if you have children with you so they can see how he lived.

We got in line at a perfect time to get an early ticket. no complaint there. But I found the exhibit lacking in many ways.  We know the story, and we have seen some atrocious pictures. I guess I was expecting this exhibit to have more depth.  I would have liked to see coverage of the stolen art. Or more artifacts.  And there was a lot of reading, too much really.

Four of us visited this museum recently.  We learned a lot.  One important point for all veterans - if you show proof of your status as a veteran, you can get free "untimed" tickets which means you have flexibility as to when to enter the museum.  My husband is a vet - so we took advantage of this opportunity.

A solid line 3-6 feet thick through most of the main exhibit on July 23rd.  I  kept expecting it to get better around each corner but it never did! Exhibits are mostly reading and the line creeps making exhibits inaccessible.  I feel remorse because my teens went to the one place in the USA that was supposed to really educate them on the holocaust and they would have gotten more out of a PBS show or YouTube video!  
We witnessed an employee allow someone without a ticket in; please review your crowd control policy and ticketing system. Otherwise its probably a great place!
The museum is huge and It takes a long time to go through and look at everything.  It was a very emotional experience.  There is a lot to see and a lot to learn here.
I was a little disappointed in this museum. I expected a lot more historical artifacts and information. Daniels story exhibit was really good though.
great setting , very interesting displays , a museum not to be missed in Washington . great also for students and young people in general

We had this museum on our "must have" list due to my son's studies in school.  As you would expect, it was a heavy topic and no real way to sugar coat it.  But it was a very nice museum and protected you from the extreme representations if you were not up for that.  The approach was how to avoid this happening again.  We are glad we went.

Make sure you prebook one of the exhibits. You have to go on there website to obtain a timed entry.
Well worth the visit. Lots to see and learn about. 
Very sad, but many different genocides have occurred around the world and the museum touches on them as well.

You will need timed tickets to enter the permanent exhibit which allows for better crowd control. There is a LOT of reading involved so I would not recommend this for young children. There are also some graphic videos which are shielded from view of young (short) people. The permanent exhibit tells the history of why it happened (how it could be allowed you happen) and what happened.   The permanent exhibit can take you anywhere from 2-4 hours. 

But there are two other exhibits that don't require a timed entry ticket:

#1. To see the actual inside muesem you need tickets.  No cost but only so many are admitted during the day.  They have a website to print  tickets 
#2.  Plan to spend at least 2 to 3 hours ... we were there from 1 until it closed and could have used more time. 
#3 some areas are very crowded but worth to take the time to see and read as much as you can. There are many floors to cover.
#3.  They usually have actual survivors at a table to answer questions and share stories.  Wish we had gone earlier to get in on that.  We arrived just before our ticketed time .. 
#4. Even if you don't get tickets there is still so much for you to see .. including films 
#5. They have a great gift shop with a great selection of books and gifts... they were closing as we got in there. But enough time to pick up a few books.
#6.  Sobering to see and hard to take in .. but you won't leave the same as you went in. My grandson is 14 and he was so interested and will never forget his visit.

I was moved by this museum, but I was expecting more. There is so much reading, but less artifacts. I really enjoyed Daniel's story.  The layout was confusing and the museum was very crowded and noisy. I was looking for a more sober atmosphere to give honor to this tragic event.

This museum was so big as you walk you go through so much history. It is sad what these people went through. The museum is a must see it is clean and well maintain. It was definitely worth the walk. The staff is very helpful and friendly this is a must see if you are in DC.

Having just visited Auschwitz-Birkenau, I was looking forward to see the connection between there and this museum as our tour guide in Auschwitz indicated that some of the items were went to the DC museum. However, when I got there there were no tickets left to the permanent exhibit. The only way to get in was to buy a membership and get two tickets, but since I was traveling alone, there was not point of getting two tickets. I only saw the Americans and the Holocaust display and Daniel's house which was informative and powerful.

Exploring the United States Holocaust Memorial Museum in Washington, DC was one of the most emotionally-draining tours I've been on. You hear of the Holocaust as a young kid, but the experience is elevated to a whole new different level when you get to see artifacts of one of the most horrific times in the history of the world with your own eyes.

It was off-season when I visited (no entrance fee), so the line to get in was not long. Security was tight; they'll check your bags before you get in. Then you'll head to the elevators, where you will be given a booklet detailing the story of one Holocaust victim. You are encouraged to think of this victim during the tour. You can easily spend four hours in the museum. It is huge. And masterfully designed. I think the crowds could detract from your experience. Avoid peak seasons. Come early in the morning if possible.

The Holocaust Museum is filled with a mix of actual artifacts and replicas from a grim period. Shoes of innocent victims. Hats. Wedding rings. It shows tons of pictures of thin workers, cramped in tiny bunk beds. Dioramas of the gas chambers. Movie clips of the killings and burials. It is thought-provoking. It makes you think about the twisted ideology of racial supremacy. It makes you think about the innocent victims fighting for their lives, struggling to thrive in an environment where death is in the foreseeable future.

One thing that should be pointed out is that the Nazis targeted hundreds of thousands of other groups, aside from the Jews. Jews became their main target, but they also singled out various groups for persecution because they were either "enemies of the state" or they were sources of social, racial, or biological "degeneration." Communists, trade unionists, liberals, and pacifists were arrested for their political views. Dissenting clergy faced imprisonment if they spoke out against the regime. Jehovah's Witnesses were persecuted for their refusal to swear allegiance to the state. Homosexuals were targeted because of their sexual orientation. Gypsies, Czechs, Poles, and Slavic people from the Soviet Union were stigmatized as racially "inferior." Mentally and physically disabled people were regarded as "lives unworthy of life." What kind of twisted thinking is that?!

And the sad part is that persecution still exists today. People are discriminated based on race, religion, sexual orientation, mental health, etc. The Holocaust Museum made me emotional. Furious. But it also reminded me that although we have moved forward and have gone a long way, there is still a lot of work that needs to be done. I need to be more vigilant. To speak out when needed. And to be the voice for people who haven't found their voice.

Powerful. Haunting. Gut-wrenching.

The U.S. Holocaust Museum needs to be on your bucket list. Take a break from your fast-paced life and visit this museum. You will come out with a renewed vigor for life.

You have heard about it, but when you see the videos, photos, and listen to actual survivors speaking about it, you humbly wish that would never happen again. Not an experience for younger kids, it shows horrible stuff, and puts everything in perspective. Not a joyful experience. It is dark and somber. But the best way to avoid this to be repeated is to learn about it and pass the info to the next generations.
Liked the kids perspective and disliked the crowds. The films they showed were from the history channel.
Very sad. If you are someone prone to tears bring a box of tissues.

I found this museum to be dark and depressing. It certainly was not for children. There was tons on writing and pictures- but few actual displays. I had my daughter with me (we have done other holicaust museums) but this one wasn't appropriate for her- we left

We were lucky enough to see a survivor talking about his experience, and it was a wonderful and unique opportunity! After that, we entered the exhibition. At the beginning, all the history and how things happened was very interesting, but it slowly became overwhelming to me. It shows you the suffering of the people in every way possible. When I walked out of it I was feeling so bad! I know this museum is the result of hard work, and the people that got together to make it possible did a very detailed research job, but it was a little too much for me. The word horrible feels short when you want to describe the things that happened there! If this visit is one that you don't want to miss, please be prepared and maybe plan something fun to do after to change your mood. Oh, and don't go with kids please! ;)

We got there a little after 11 and there was a long line to get in. We were told to go around the block to the other entrance and got in through security pretty fast.  When we went to get tickets we were asked if we were part of a union, or teacher or anything like that. My sister is a nurse and she said that, but we were confused as to why they were asking when the tickets are supposed to be free. We were then told to go over to the elevators and get a identification card. I feel that when you are given this person there should be references throughout the museum that you can than relate to your person and their story. The elevator takes you up to the 4th floor. The first thing we did was go to the restroom. There was a huge line for 2 stalls.  Most museum bathrooms are much larger. As soon as we started walking we came to s complete stop. There was way too many people. Strollers, families, that personally do not belong in this museum. There was a theatre, but it was too crowded. As soon as we could we just decided to walk and not look at anything to get away from the crowd. The path takes you to the 3rd floor, which was a little less crowded. Most of the interactive displays were filled, however it was easier to breathe and really look at the displays. We finished off the rest of the museum, however will have to go back to do the 4th floor and I believe that is where Anne Frank is. We did see the shoes and the drawings. I would think by now the museum would have better control on how many people to let up to the 4th floor. I did not see anyone who worked there or security up there, which if they were should have radioed down and informed them to hold up sending anymore people up until crowds thinned out.

I believe you have to sign up ahead of time for a tour and you pick a time to go. The museum is fairly big, there are several floors with different experiences that really immerse you in the history of the holocaust. I believe parking was hard to come by and you can only take clear liquid inside. Your bags will be checked.

There is nothing "good" about this. The holocaust is horrible, therefore, it makes the info hard to take sometimes. I do think that there are ways things need to be worked around. Especially the beginning was so bottlenecked and crowded. I almost couldn't stay. It's obvious why scheduled times are needed. I do wish they had things recorded that we could listen to instead of having to read everything because it was extremely difficult due to crowds AND content to read every single thing they had throughout. Narration would make a huge impact for sure. But it was gut-wrenching as expected and I am glad we went. Be prepared to read A LOT!

This is not a "fun" thing to do, but it should be done by everyone that has a soul.  Humanity can be ugly and we ought to guard against that path ever happening again by seeing it as close to firsthand as possible.  This is an excellent presentation that gives you that firsthand feeling.

Visiting DC with my 16 year old son who had one request, to visit the USHMM. I tried reservations in advance with no luck. Got up at 6:30 to try for the same day tickets was able to place 2 tickets in the cart several times, only to be told they were not available. The system is most definitely hacked by bots. There is no other explanation that would cause all time slots to be gone in a matter of 6mins. I am so very disappointed that the museum has not found a better way. Its not easy to get teens motivated by history and to drive here and leave without they experience is very sad.

This is an excellent museum, very well put together and it's free. However, I gave it 4 stars because in a city with so many wonderful attractions if you have limited time it wouldn't be among among my top choices.

This museum was a very powerful and informative museum that really brought to light something that we today can't even imagine ever happened. The museum has some pretty PG-13 stuff in it and I would not recommend little kids go through the regular museum. There is however a kids museum edition that is very much suitable for young kids not old enough to embrace the regular museum. There is a lot of reading and a lot of people so sometimes there will be lines to read a certain board or look at an exhibit. Another must see
So many rememberances of the holocaust. A specific story of of boy and his experiences. A big pile of shoes of the victims of the holocaust...just haunting.

The permanent exhibition hasn't changed (read updated to reflect decades of innovative scholarship) in twenty years or at least when I last went - soon after it opened. The Holocaust Museum - particularly it's  library and archival holdings - is a fantastically rich resource. The building's architecture is innovative; go to the bookstore and pick up the book detailing the Museum's origins. However, the vast crowds of bored schoolchildren (and their equally bored parents) make going through the exhibit space dispiriting. Rarely did I see people actually bother to read the captions on exhibits. After all they'd actually have to read something. If people have to be forced to go to a museum, perhaps they should just skip it.
Finally, the Holocaust was more than Auschwitz and the death camps; millions of Jews were shot to death in German-occupied territories of the Soviet Union; the Museum needs to change it's exhibits to reflect what we now know about the Holocaust.

The exhibiton starts on the top floor and tells the story of Hitler's rise as well as the mood and stories being told in post WW I Germany.  There are many video clips of old news reels as well as survivor accounts.  

I did not find it as depressing or shocking as I expected, but that I think is because I have been exposed to the story before.

Though there was one exhibit showing a sculpture of the whole "gassing" practice the Germans used to murder the Jews that did leave me feeling a bit ill.

Overall, a highly valuable tour of something that should not be allowed to fade from our consciousness because it continues to happen in places like Cambodia and Rwanda.

Very odd layout that was difficult to navigate. Most things appeared to be more special exhibits and films vs standard museum. We did learn more about why America was so slow/hesitant to get into the war so that was a good takeaway. Disappointed in the Daniel childrens exhibit as everything was written in cursive which many kids cannot read so I read a lot of the content to my kids vs them being able to really grasp it hands on. Kids are 13, 9, 5 - just didnt really grab any of us. Ok as a fill in activity if you need something to do but this wouldnt be on my must do list.

Very well done and organised, quite a few videos to watch.  Had a good and clear route to follow.

Had to access a different building for food through two sets of security which was unexpected and ate into the time.

This place is so amazingly well done that it will captivate you from the very first step. A shocking realization of what a man can do to another man. All history lovers and all people seeking for the good should visit this place.

The museum takes a few hours to see it all, but it is worth it if you have time. The exhibits seek to inform viewers of the political and social atmosphere in which Hitler rose to power and examines how he maintained it. There is an excellent children's section to introduce your kids to the subject without scaring them.

Is this the most informative or  impressive Holocaust museums I've visited? No. But it has a lot of information and you can learn and experience a lot, and it's worth a visit if it's your first visit to DC or if you haven't been to other Holocaust museums. On this visit last week, I particularly enjoyed their temporary exhibit: Americans and the Holocaust (an exhibition that examines the motives, pressures, and fears that shaped Americans' responses to Nazism, war, and genocide). However, I found the permanent exhibition much, much, much too crowded - even with timed entry (we had tickets to enter between noon and 1pm). There were things I wanted to read that I skipped just because I didn't have time to wait ten minutes to get close enough to read something. The crowds certainly affected my experience. It was a good museum, but certainly too crowded to experience everything you want want to.

This museum was highly recommended and called a "must-see" but I really wish that we would have spent the time here at just about any other DC point of interest.  To start with, the security check was a lot more inconvenient than at all the other museums we went to on this trip.  To see the permanent exhibit you have to get passes from the information desk.  While it wasn't a problem on the day that we were there it is apparently common for them to surpass the amount of tickets they will issue in a day, so not every visitor is guaranteed the chance to get in.  

Besides those fairly minor concerns, we had a few issues with the museum exhibit itself.  To start with, I expected there to be a lot of actual artifacts from the holocaust/WWII but there were surprisingly few.  Most of the exhibit is pictures on the wall with written panels describing what the pictures are and events of the time period.  There is a lot of repetition of ideas and occurrences throughout the panels, so you spend a lot of time reading things similar to something you just finished reading.  Lastly, if you paid attention in history class or read any books on the period outside of school there is very little information presented that you haven't already heard.

I think if you are interested in learning more about the holocaust that a book will suit you better.

I found the Holocaust Museum very moving and a very sombre experience to what happened and found the museum had shown great respectfulness.I think the Holocaust Museum in Berlin had shown more of the background to the plight of the Jewish people leading up to the Second World War than what I saw here in  Washington DC

This was someplace I had wanted to go to since it opened.  It was very moving and so sad.  Most of what you saw required a lot of reading and it was quite crowded.  The museum covers four floors and it would have been nice to have an audio recording or something that would to accompany the exibits.

The museum is such an important piece of history. But the amount of people they let in is extremely heavy. If you have any interest in reading the displays, forget about it.
It was to the point that we almost left. And I have to think it's a fire hazard and not within the legal limits. Very dissapointed that a manager allows it to be this way.

I visited this museum and although I think it would be very interesting to see everything  it was way too crowded and I left within 1 hour. You do have to go through a security check. They made me throw away my bottle of water, however there is no trash can at security so I had to go outside and throw it away and then fight the crowd to meet up with my family again. I understand security is cut and dry but why not put up signs letting us no No Water bottles so we can throw it away before and not assume that tourists would know you don't allow it. Plus put a trash can there so we can stay with our family. It was not easy to see exhibits if you were not up against the wall due to the crowd, I felt like a was in a herd of sheep. Sorry, maybe I went at the wrong time.

My daughter wanted to see this, I just came for the ride. But Im glad I did. Dont expect fun, though.
But what you will see is the tragic outcome of what happens when good people do nothing and let evil thrive.
The story of the Holocaust is tragic, horrifying, and compelling. You look at what happened, and the worst thing is that it shows that every human has the capacity to do unthinkably evil things, yet the best thing is that, collectively, good people cannot be crushed.
Everyone needs to see this museum, so hopefully we will never let it happen again. A good lesson to renew, given the current new crop of world leaders.
Kim W.

Reserve your entry day and time well in advance because availability of tickets can be tight.  At the reserved day and time, after clearing security, the tour of the Holocaust Museum starts with an elevator ride to the 4th floor.  On the walk down from the top floor, the visitor will see newspaper headlines of the time, view videos, hear interviews, see pictures, and see actual items that were from that dark period of European history.  
The walk down starts with Germany in dire economic circumstances following World War I, setting the stage for Hitler's rise to power and the start of anti-Semitic rhetoric in his speeches.  On the third floor, as his power solidified, the rhetoric escalated into discrimination and persecution.  As you progress down through the museum, the atrocities get more and more severe until Hitler decides on the "Final Solution" for the perceived Jewish problem, which was mass murder by gas and starvation.  The most horrific videos and pictures are hidden from young eyes by walls that are tall enough that children can't see over them.  Jewish people were not the only ones selected for extermination.  Other groups include the gypsies, the physically disabled and homosexuals.  The tour continues through the end of the war and the rescue of the survivors.  Additional displays highlight today's examples of genocide, such as Iraq and Syria and other Islamic countries.  If we, as a society, ignore discrimination and genocide, we have learned nothing from the persecution of the Jews by Germany.

This museum is new , popular & busy. Go early, be there before they open at 10 am.  May not be appropriate for children due the graphic nature of some displays.  Interesting, historical, somber, quiet, reflective mood and  well laid out.  Sobering history lesson everyone should experience.   Free

Everyone told us that we needed to visit this museum. I am glad we went, but wow! The crowds here almost ruin the experience. There is a lot to read and it takes forever to move through the exhibit, considering we had to wait to get in I would have thought the crowds would be more dispersed. It was really hard to read anything or get the full experience. Maybe in the off season this museum would be better to visit. Everyone should see this, just be prepared for the crowds.
I didn't want to go because of all the sadness it would represent but visited anyway, the wall of rememberce was unbelivable sad.
Humanities shame - a must see so we remember although the atrocities seem to continue. It is extremely well done.

On our trip to WAdc, my husband had to tour the Holocaust Museum.  The facility is very nice and clean.  The tragic memory of horrible things that were done to innocent people.  Several times tears fell from our eyes to look at what is so horrendous to see or even believe.  We appreciated at the end to be able to sit down in a beautiful and very open room with candles that you can light if you purchase in memory of someone.  It was all very moving and sad.  The walk past the 1,000's of actual shoes worn by the poor victims.  Unbelievable.  We had special tickets given to us by a Docent, which allowed us to go in at anytime.  Without those tickets people had to wait in line.  There were many, many school kids there also.  It was very crowded.  Every person should see this Memorial and feel lucky that we live in the land of the free.

It's no fault of the museum, but this place has way too many visitors and it's nearly impossible to view it all with a mass group of people.  The museum tries their hardest to only allow a group of people in to view the permanent exhibit at a time, but even so, the crowd was excessive.  

The security officers at the door are rude.  We came in with an opened soda in our bag, there wasn't any notice upon entering that this was not allowed.  The security officer talked to my husband like he was a criminal for having it and said to his coworker, "he doesn't know how to listen" because his instructions to my husband about going outside to throw it away were unclear (my husband asked if he should go back out the door he came in or the exit door).  The attitude given was unprofessional.  

We visited during the height of the summer tourist season, I'm sure there is less of a crowd if you go at a different time of the year.  The artifacts and stories are touching and I wanted it all to change my life, if the crowd was absent, I think it would have.

Very disappointing experience because there were so many wasted opportunities to educate and inform.  There was a very well produced exhibition on Americas evolving response to WWII, it taking the time to remind how that response was not particularly driven by what was happening to European Jews.  

However, unless one could secure a free pass (they were all gone by 11.30) to a dedicated exhibit, one is denied the opportunity to even begin to understand what the Holocaust was.  My son and I have visited similar exhibitions in Paris and, significantly, Berlin.  Both were supreme in the role of educating.  A museum with the illustrious title of The United States Holocaust Memorial Museum should not fail in its obligation to reach the widest of audiences. Sadly and shamefully it does.

We discussed this at the Information Desk and were reminded of many school groups who do secure access to the pass only exhibition. That is to be applauded.  However - I invite the Museum to canvass those very many walk-ins for their respective experiences..... or even count how long it is before those patrons walk out.
Get there early to get tickets at a good time. I am told you can reserve online but wasn't able to. A LOT of information in one place.

very sad of course, but amazing to hear the voices of survivors tell their story was very profound. don't miss that room where you sit and listen to the recorded voices. this is not a museum for young children in my opinion. the museum is set up very well, so it is easy to navigate and not miss anything.
This was good to visit and see and experience however I think it repeats itself too many times to lengthen it
The lines are long so you need to pick up a ticket for a set time. It should be very interested but I didn't know the above

We visited in Saturday mid day.  Its unfortunate that there seems to be no limit to the number of visitors even though timed entry.  The exhibits are moving but print so small you can read unless youre right up front.  Earphone audio would fix this problem or at the very least museum docent to push the crowd along.  Lots of school groups and tours - sadly we encountered a group of disrespectful teens with no chaperone in sight.  History worth seeing but go early!

First of all let me say this place comes off beautifully in decor:) Yet, when it comes to getting people through it, it's a hot mess! They allow way to many in and the walk areas are WAY to small:( Trying to read small signs over groups of people wears you down and after about 30mins. And this was on a Wednesday:( Good luck on the weekends.I give out tickets for allowing people in at certain times, but they don't follow the time frames that might create even more crowd problems:( Loved what LITTLE I saw, good luckkkkkkkkkkkkkkkkkkkkkkkkkkkkkkk

This was going to be one of the highlights of my trip. I got tickets months ago. I have read many books about the Holocaust. They have way too many people going at scheduled times. You cant read anything because you are wall to wall jam packed in. Unfortunately, it was a lot of pictures and written information that Ive already read in books. I was hoping for actual artifacts to see. They need to reduce the number of people for each timing. The guide told us it should take 2 hours, we were done in 30 minutes because I was so frustrated by the amount of people, lack of artifacts, we just basically squeezed through and left. Could he so much better. Daniels Story was way better then the actual exhibit. So disappointed.

This was my second visit to this museum. The first time was relatively soon after it had opened and it was VERY crowded. It's a somber and important place to experience. Many will only want to go once to this important venue, but it will leave a lasting impression.

We visited here and walked through their propaganda exhibit but couldn't figure out where anything else was (or even if there was anything else). The layout of the museum was very confusing and not well marked.
This was my first visit here.  I could have easily stayed three hours.  It was fascinating and emotional at the same time.  Do not miss it.
This is a very incredible exihibit. Very sombering and incredibly detailed. Could be a difficult thing for children under 12 to be a part of. Not to be missed if in DC.
