Highly recommended. Very somber experience. The museum includes a lot of photos and written text to read and learn. I wish it contained more artifacts; can't compare to the 9/11 Museum in NYC (which includes thousands of artifacts).
Very detailed and moving mueseum.  Well worth the time to pay tribute to those who suffered such horrific crimes against humanity.  May God bless all victims and their families.

We were a group of 4 and were looking forward to seeing this museum.  Our reservation was for 2:15pm on July 5th.  The facility is beautiful, but the experience was terrible.  When you first enter the crowd was at least 6 deep trying to see the exhibit.  Why aids there a reservation system if it is going to be that crowded?  I feel as though I wasted my time setting up a reservation to see a museum that I didnt learn anything at.  You finally just get frustrated and skip sections to get moving.  We go to DC a couple times a year, we always do the museums because there is always something we missed.  I will never do this one again,
A deeply moving experience that any person with a sense of history should see. Well done considering the horrific subject matter.
The museum does a good job of promoting the remembrance of the holocaust in a serious and meaningful way without succumbing to the tourism that can mark DC museums.

This Museum you could definitely spend all day in. We spent about an hour-and-a-half and managers just see a good bit of it but definitely did not have time to look carefully at all of the exhibits. All of the exhibits have a lot of signage which can be difficult to read due to the large number of people in the museum. It would take hours probably to read every single sign that they have there is a ton of interesting historical information. You can get by pretty well however by skipping the signs reading those that interest you and looking at the pictures and watching the short videos that they have available. Definitely recommend as a must-see!

Whilst the exhibition contains so much information, there is no room to read anything. 

Way too many people are let in to the point there is no room to more & you cannot get close enough to read anything. Youre crammed in like sardines. 

This left me extremely disappointed as I was really looking forward to this exhibition. 

This exhibition holds so much significance, give it the recognition it deserves and half the amount of people they let in so you can embrace it.
I felt it was not as complete as Holocaust Museum in New York.  Heving to get ticket and wait for our time was not convenient

Unlike all the other amazing museums & memorials in DC, this one is thumbs down. If I could give it a negative rating I would as I came a really long way (from upper Midwest) to visit it and walked over a mile & a half from my hotel (and as Im 60+ yrs old it was quite the effort). To my great disappointment (and I use the last word loosely), other than a lot of walls, security, stairs and gift shops there was absolutely nothing to see that was educational, informational, or inspirational to be found at this location. Bottom line, unless you have an advance pass or tour set up dont bother...

The layout at the beginning was hard to navigate.  Too much info in a small area and since it is at the start everyone is in a clump trying to read it all.  Some very moving parts as you move through the exhibit.  The train car and the shoes were the most moving to m.  We spent at least two hours but would could spend way more.

Brought my 11-year-old son here. He has some knowledge of the Holocaust so it was not new information for him. As far as museums go it is put together in a way that tells a story and you can go through it if you have children with a shorter attention span. Although of course the topic is disturbing, it is not terribly graphic so it is appropriate for children. There is also a special children's section for smaller children.

You learned this stuff in the 7th grade. They showed bulldozers pushing piles of emaciated bodies into mass graves. Why relive it again? It's too powerful and depressing. Not what I want when I'm spending $300 a night for parking and hotel. I'm all for education on the Holocaust but it's not my idea of vacation. Did I tell you about the large crowds within the museum? The whole experience left me with a sick feeling in my stomach.   

Spend your time looking at the other offerings in DC. I want positive thoughts on my vacations. This whole thing just seems out of place.

This was a very sobering visti for us. Hard to imagine what took place and how, but this museum did a great job in telling the story. I have been to the Holocaust Museum in Jerusalem, and this one is every bit its equal, though the two museums each focus on different things. 

Be advised that you need a free ticket to get in to the main exhibit. That is so they can conrol the flow of the crowds through the exhibit. We had to wait about 40 minutes until our admit time.

Please note, I cannot stress enough the importance of this museum.  My review is purely on the museum setup and process; not the message. 

First, in order to get into the main exhibit you have to get there early to get a timed pass.  This part is fine, no big deal, I had no problem doing this; HOWEVER, what I did see as a problem is that each person in line can get up to 20 tickets per person.  So you may wait in line over an hour and not even get a ticket, because there are people like the 3 woman in front of us, that each got 20 tickets a piece.

Secondly, you'd think since they are giving out timed passes, it would mediate over-crowding; it does no such thing.  This museum was an insane, mess.  Over filled with a ridiculous amount of people; including about 2,000 unsupervised children ranging in age from about 8-14!  I assume these were some sort of organized (or disorganized) class trips, but why on earth would they have these kids packed into this museum causing such a ruckus?!  You have 1 chaperon for every 20 or so kids; unacceptable.  You could tell most of these kids had zero interest in being there; I've never seen such disrespectful behavior.  Kids taking selfies next to photos of emaciated bodies, and then laughing about it.  I was appalled.  How can the museum mediate this?  Well, how about requiring a parent or guardian for these minors. I get that it's important to educated our children, but that should be done under the supervision of their parents.  

Thirdly, this museum is set up so poorly and is super confusing to navigate.  Once you clear security, there are no signs and nothing to really advise you where to start. You just enter a large, super crowded atrium area. Good luck.

Again, while I see the importance of education on this subject, there are better ways to achieve it.  Avoid this museum if you can, you will save yourself a lot of time and patience.
One can't help but feel angry about the extreme levels on inhumanity to our fellow human beings. Never to be forgotten.

Everyone needs to understand why we must NEVER forget. 
Take the time to read the names, the stories, the videos.
Cathartic for some.

Provides good history of Hitler, his rise to power, and the atrocities that followed.  Lots of pictures, videos (some may be disturbing for small children), and artifacts.  The layout isn't great given for the large amount of people moving through and trying to read/view all of the material.  Very crowded for a weekday afternoon, so you may be better served going early.

I was immediately struck by the differences between this museum and Yad VaShem in Jerusalem. Although on a significantly smaller scale, the US Holocaust Museum offers a comprehensive and intelligently arranged review of both the prelude to and the appalling actualities of the Nazi genocidal war against Jews, Roma, and other categories of persons they sought to eliminate. It is a museum designed to inform persons initially ignorant of these horrors, as well as hold the interest and attention of those, such as ourselves who upon entry are already well informed about them. It is also thoughtfully designed to offer spaces for rest and reflection, as well as a continuous route that tells a historically and morally coherent story. Altogether, a most impressive institution that no visitor to Washington should miss seeing.

Very sad to see the horrific conditions the jews and other minorities were subjected to during the Holocaust. Very crowded, but worth enduring the crowds to see the exhibits. Make sure you have a tour guide. Otherwise, it is a bit overwhelming.

This is a museum that has a somber feeling throughout the museum which depicts the history of the Nazi era and Hitlers reign from the beginning to the end.  There are videos, audios and pictures showing the camps, the people, survivors and victims and people who helped others hide and escape.  It was very moving and this museum shows the history of the holocaust which should never be forgotten.

I worked two blocks from this museum since its opening but had not visited because I knew how emotional it would be.  I waited until I retired and visited in DC's tourist off season.  Even so, the museum was packed.  There is so much to read and so much most don't know about these events.  Please don't bring small children.  The pace is slow because of the amount of reading and the crowding around displays seemed to make children whiney.

I took my two kids (ages 9 and 12). Exhibits were meaningful for all of us. Very moving experience.  Younger children will really "get it" with Daniel's Story, which is a part of the museum specifically designed for younger children.

This is one of the most compelling museums I've ever visited, but it was so absolutely crowded, it's hard to see things especially in the exhibits at the beginning of the tour. I understand many people want to see it, but it made it hard to enjoy parts of it. However, this museum is so well-done, and the artifacts are incredibly meaningful. You will leave with a heavy heart.

You have to see it yourself; it is very difficult to explain the experience. Incredible what kind of evil forces have been there, and all of it is not history, unfortunately.... The location of the museum is central and easy to get to by Metro. I loved the experience because I had to see it while in D.C. But it is more than that; having Jewish friends...The cafeteria is nice and very "fitting",  with their matzo ball soup; we enjoyed the food!

After about only 10 minutes there, I kept thinking to myself "everyone should visit this memorial at least once". Its so educational, and of course, heartbreaking but in a necessary way because the details are so important. I think you only need a ticket during the summer from what I could tell, but the price was next to nothing. Very important and glad I went.

If you carry an "EDC" (every day carry) knife like I do (a folding pocket knife with a 2.75" blade), don't plan to visit this museum. You will be turned away or invited to dispose of your knife in their amnesty box.

No such policy concerning legally possessed knifes is noted on their website thus visitors cannot make appropriate plans when visiting this museum.

Washington D.C. laws allows people to posses any knife (except switch blades) if the blade is not longer than 3 inches, so my "EDC" knife was well within the legal limit.

I had been told this was one of the best Smithsonian museums, and I have to agree. Its not for everyone though, as it is obviously a heavy topic. The best part, in my opinion, was Daniels Story. Based on his diary entries, you literally follow a path that leads through Daniel and his familys history as they are segregated and then rounded up and sent to concentration camps. The recreations of his bedroom with its cramped conditions, as well as the scenes of the old turnip soup they were forced to eat make the exhibit come alive. Technically the Daniels Story exhibit of the Holocaust Museum is meant more for children but I enjoyed it just as much. There is plenty to see at this museum so budget your time in DC so as to make the most of this place.

Taking a 9 and a 12 year old through this museum is a necessary challenge. Daniel's Story is a nice introduction to the concept of the Holocaust. We walked quickly through some rooms and avoided others altogether. A great place to speak as a family about making the right choice, standing up for what you know is true, and talking about hard topics.
This museum is very moving.  Tough to get through, but many great exhibits.  Recommend this history memorial museum.

This museum really drives home the horrendous acts experienced at the hands of tyrants. While much of the exhibit is hard to see and fathom, I think all who can, should see it. It's important we never forget what can happen when we aren't vigilant.

Me and my dad came here on vacation and thought we could just go in and see all of the exhibits, but you have to have tickets. We tried to reserve them online because starting at 6 am you can reserve them. They were already booked though so we didn't get to see the exhibit.

I was disappointed  as recently  before I was here everything I saw here was on tv. I love history but was not pleased with the employees here as well. One of the only Smithsonian museums I was in that employees need to be re examined for their employment. Seems they weren't there for the tourist. Won't waste my time going back here to see the real tragedy of European history.
The Holocaust Museum is a must visit place. It's here that I met life face to face.  You need guts to see the visuals here.

Went during the summer when everything is crowded. We reserved our tickets for the main exhibit in advance but when we got there we waited in a long line for 30 minutes thinking we were going into a theatre or something. But there was no employees of the museum here so new groups of people from the elevator would just walk past the line and get in front of everybody. The exhibit is in a hallway where people were so tightly packed there was no way you could enjoy the museum much less be able to read the information. After wasting 45 minutes, we just left. Very poorly coordinated. However the other exhibits in the museum that do not require tickets were interesting and not as crowded.

Moving monument - something we all need to remember.  The monument is a little confusing as to where you start and which places to see first.  The flow is difficult to immediately see.  Worth the trouble.

Please go and remember the price of indifference.

I appreciate the honesty about the U.S. position during the atrocities of the Holocaust.  

Make sure to get your advance tickets.

As we entered the museum, there were three women who were Holocaust survivors. It was wonderful to meet them and learn about their stories. Some of the exhibits are pretty gory but that was what really happened. Overall I think they had a nice balance of brutal facts and being respectful. I could have spent the whole day there.

I'm giving this review a three because I felt security and personnel could do more to control unruly children.  There should be an age restriction and a cell phone ban.  The information provided was set out really well.  The pictures and stories are heart wrenching.  Highly recommend but please respect the nature of the museum and your fellow patrons.

We visited on July 5. We had reserved timed tickets ahead but they seemed to be passing out unlimited tickets to get in immediately. I agree with the review below that it was incredibly too crowded. You could not see the displays for all of the people. The museum is clearly not large enough to handle the volume of people. They very clearly need to limit the number of people they let in. We Made our way blindly through the first 2 floors in order to escape the crowd. The next floor was better but I was so frustrated at that point that I vowed to only leave a one star review. The rolling video on the third floor with stories from survivors was very touching as was the Hall of Remembrance which added the extra star. Overall the museum is too small for the crowds they allow. The signage on where to go is not helpful and it didnt seem that there were enough employees to handle the crowd or help guide people either.  I have read many books on the holocaust and they were more moving and informative than the museum was on this day. My advice would be to go first thing in the morning or not at all during the summer.

"The only thing necessary for the triumph of evil is that good men do nothin", a statement that was as true for the Holocaust as it is today. There are 4 floors to this free museum and a self-guided or guided tour starts on the top floor. Upon entering you are given a small booklet about a Jewish man or women Holocaust victim or survivor which you can turn as you travel through the museum. The exhibits are arranged in chronological order starting with the rise of Hitler, the start of WWII, height of the Holocaust, end of WWII and downfall of Hitler. The tour ends in a large circular room where the names of all the concentration camps are engraved on the walls, Candles are placed along the walls lit in memory of the victims. The design by James Ingo Freed is expertly done to evoke an intellectual explanation but more importantly a deep guttural sense of the tragedy. Some exhibits are probably to graphic for some children. The design of some of the very graphic exhibits have waist high walls erected so really little ones can not view them but others do not. One section contains graphic images and video and is clearly marked as such. Video of Holocaust survivors speaking brings the reality of the Holocaust to life. The museum is open daily from 10-5 and depending on the individual can last 2-5 hours. Admission is free and you are given a starting entrance time. Online tickets can be purchase for one dollar and allow you to choose a time. The museum is best approached by metro and the Smithsonian station is closest to the museum. It's a short walk from the Washington Monument. The action and inaction of other countries on modern genocide is a  cause for reflection in today's world.

Museum had very few objects in collections for viewingI know the museum has acquired an un believable amount of artifacts related to the Holocaust  I saw very few and extremely disappointed as that is one of the major reasons I went to the DC area

This is an excellent place to visit while in D.C. The memory of those who died is present in that place and the atmosphere is one of awe and somberness. We enjoyed the displays and actual items they had from that time. It was a realistic look at what those people endured. gut wrenching!

This memorial is not as noticeable as other larger museums. Our family stopped here after visiting the Bureau of Printing and Engraving since they were near each other and we were glad we did. One of the best flows of all museum/ memorials that we visited. The exhibit told the story of the holocaust from start to finish and really brought out some strong feelings and understanding of this historic event. Very educational.

I have been to this exhibit three times and I have yet been able to get tickets for the tour.  i wish they would have more tickets available.  That is the only reason I rate it a Very Good.  Other than that it is a must see.

Not sure just why I needed to be reminded of the atrocities of the Nazi regime, but this was a powerful historical collection presented in a reverent and logical manner. Some film areas are designated as not for the faint.  
  Aside from the fact of the horrible treatment of various groups by the Nazis, what struck me of the rise of Hitler, were the number of similarities to current political rhetoric and the rule of the current administration. Very scary.
   They suggest the exhibit usually takes 2-3 hours, but we spent over 4 hours (we read most of the exhibit texts).
   OK to bring water with you.

There are a few times in history that mankind failed so miserably a the holocaust. What we do not remember, we are doomed to forget. The Holocaust Museum should be required for every human being, to witness what inhumanity can do, can be. This is not a place that hits you across the face with brash and horror. There is plenty of that, horror. But it is done in a way that you can see as little or as much as you can handle. Think it was only about adults, it will disavow you of that. Think, or want to think, that it was all over in an instant, this will who you otherwise. There is no need to scream it at you. A display of shoes or glasses. A hallway of photographs will will show you. Not sure how anyone could survive, listen to those that did. Sit, think, feel. But most important, remember. Never forget or we will live it again and again.

We visited this museum in the afternoon and waited about 30 minutes on line. Once we got inside we asked woman at the info desk where to start. She said that they give out tickets on a first come basis in the a.m. and that they were out of tickets for the day. She said the the ticketed part of the exhibit was 75% of the museum. We were kind of disappointed because the 25% we were able to see was so powerful and informative I could only imagine what we missed.

We had high expectations for the Holocaust Museum and tried to visit on a mid Friday afternoon, but the main entrance area was mobbed with school groups.  Tickets to the permanent collection were sold out for the day, and the only exhibit we had access to, the children's exhibit, was difficult to view because of the crowds.  Unlike the other museum along the National Mall where you can just walk in, this one may require some advanced planning and reservations.

This was an absolute eye opener to those of us who only learn about the holocaust in history and books! It is a very sobering reminder about why we can't take the happenings in the world for granted, and why we need to be constantly on the lookout for 'nut bags' in politics everywhere - all of us have a responsibility to ensure that things like this never happen again.

I was so looking forward to visiting this museum.  We had our timed tickets and once we got in, we were wall to wall people.  We stayed for about ten minutes and left as we could not move or get close enough to any of the exhibits.  A truly disappointing visit!

This museum is definitely a must see if you are in D.C. The history and the artifacts are just incredible and very humbling. 

I recommend getting your tickets ahead of time. From March to August, you wont be able to just show up and get passes for the day. 

I gave it 4 Stars because of the organization at the beginning of the tour. It is self led and I think that was a downside. There was so much congestion that it was difficult to move or get up to some of the displays to read the information. This got better throughout the rest of the tour but the beginning was very frustrating.

This deserves a better rating, but one of the docents was so absorbed with  his "all about me" briefing that some of the audience got lost. Then, we got stuck in a hallway when someone left an unattended package, effecting the eventual evacuation of the building. This puts faces and places into the history books as never before and graphically depicts mans insanity towards mankind.

So, this place takes you back to a horrific time in history, but I believe it is a must see. My friend and I had to visit and only got in during the busy rush because she is active duty military... You have to get there early to get tickets if you want to see the museum in its entirety. Before you enter you pick an "ID" card of someone who lived (or died) during the holocaust... Their story is told in the short bio/ ID card. One of the things that got to me the most (there were many) was seeing the shoe and hair exhibit and the tons of videos and recordings throughout the museum. I would urge taking your teenage children and let them witness exactly what intolerance led to so many years ago.
You have to have tickets to get into the permanent collections, so don't expect to see a lot unless you do.

Took my oldest daughter here to help her with a school project. It was the most impersonal place I have ever been. No one seemed to want to talk to her, just kept moving us along or too busy right now, go ask and then the dreaded point! Waste of money for the lesson. Went to the one in Richmond, cheaper (as in free) and run by real holocaust victims.
had to do a lot of reading and watching videos, had to wait a while for  our tour as well and we arrived 30 minutes before it even opened
How can anyone deny this happened!  Very compelling and moving.  Be prepared to do lots of walking. To do it right will take hours.

It was too crowded because it started raining.
Wanted to visit their current exhibition but I couldnt because they ran out of tickets.

The Daniel story was the best part, very well done.  From our teenagers point of view, the main exhibit was too much historical detail and too long.  Seeing the horror of it was overwhelming, but the history leading up to it could have been shortened.  The staff is very helpful.  With so many options in DC, we ended up just seeing the highlights here which was difficult to do.  Great information, but hard to navigate through.

Our group queued to get into this museum and after a walk around we were left very underwhelmed by the experience. I completely respect the reason for the museum but after seeing other similar museums in Europe this left us wondering if they could have done a bit more.

This was a very moving experience. In about three and a half hours, we only got through half of it and we weren't going particularly slow. If we go to Washington, D.C. again, we'll definitely go back to see the rest.

This is a remarkable review of one of history's worst episodes of a supposed civilized country (Germany) killing millions just due to their faith.  A solemn reminder that even animals wouldn't do the same.

ok we went on july 2 and lots of people in DC. the flow of museum needs to improve. too many people in one section and gets backed up. intense subject matter but tastefully done-if there is such a way to present the Holocaust. very historical as well with Hitler rise to power- that man hated everyone it seems.

Presented with such poise, this glimpse into one of humanities worst moments in history is essential to all visitors of DC. It is a somber & timely reminder of what can occur when power is delivered into the wrong hands. May we never forget.

Tickets for the main exhibit were gone by noon the day we were there. Everything was well done but because there were so many people in the exhibit area, I felt like I couldn't take all the time I wanted and needed to fully appreciated everything.  If you tried to stay in one place for very long, the hoards of people behind you kind of pushed you along.  I would like to return in the winter on a weekday when it isn't as crowded because this museum's lesson is important.

Maybe it's just because I've read so many books about the holocaust that I expected too much.  I wasn't even emotionally moved until the end when I viewed the video of some of the survivors.  They should have had more stuff like that.  It's such a fascinating subject matter, and I feel like they made it into a boring text book.  It could have been presented in a better way.

I don't have words adequate enough to describe how I felt going through this museum.  Mainly it was an overwhelming feeling of sadness and remorse.  I couldn't have imagined how those people must have felt when suddenly they realized they had no more freedom and worse, were facing eminent death.
The pictures will tear your heart out.  The videos might be worse.  Unthinkable how this could have happened, and was the world silent knowing about these death camps.  I don't recommend bringing small children.  No way they can understand the horror.
Don't rush through.  Read as much as you can.  Regardless of your religion, you've got to think - Never Again!
Very sobering. Went in April- too many noisy kids and too cold for me. By the time I got to the end where there were amazing videos from survivors, I was too cold to stay any longer.

We purposely visited this museum without children.  Would recommend for age 10+.  Spent 5 hours and did not see it all. Plan for lots of reading and reflection. Though we were told some folks zoom through in less than an hour!  I cannot image that. Took a lunch break at the cafe next door which had excellent food.  The mac-n-cheese with grilled vegetables was a favorite.  The day was a valuable experience.

We waited in the long queue and then through security to enter this Museum to then learn we could not see the exhibition as all tickets had been given out earlier! We learned absolutely nothing! There needs to be a better system for visitors wanting to learn, to understand, not have us waiting in a queue for no purpose at all.

A repeat visit at the request of my youngest.   Still an informative and very well detailed museum.  Go early to get your free passes to see the live exhibit that you are now allowed to photograph.    Also there are survivors available on a limited time throughout the day that you can interact with that we did not have time in our schedule to take advantage of.   Goes great with the age group who just  finished  studying about WWII in school as this was the case in my household.   They also still have "Daniel's Story"  which is an exhibit to explain through a child's perspective of what he experienced during the time of the Nazi takeover.

The research and dedication to ensure that no individual victim of the Holocaust should be forgotten is impressive. Many people, like myself, think they know about the persecution of the Jews by the Nazis and Hitler, but until you go through all the exhibits, you discover how much you did not know about how a group of human beings could commit such unfathomable atrocities on their own species for such random and insignificant reasons, culminating in The Final Solution - the systematic mass extermination of Jews, but also Jehovah's Witnesses, gays, political rivals, the physically and mentally impaired. This is primarily a visual experience. The historic photographs, documentaries, and videos are astounding. The special exhibit "A Dangerous Lie" was excellent in demonstrating how anti-semitic propaganda written decades ago, continues to persist through today, despite it having been proved to be false many times over. "Daniel's Story", an exhibit for children, was extremely well-done and designed to stir the emotions of children to the plight of the child victims of the Holocaust. I highly recommend this museum to anyone. It will move your emotions and affirm your own humanity (unless you are a sociopath). I could not look at the many visitors who were tearful, lest I lose it myself. I welled up with tears many times. I did not rank it a 5, not because of content, but because of the building design. There are no restrooms on the main/entry floor. The closest ones from the entry are 2 flights down the stairs on either end of the building. For personal health reasons, this is quite important to me.

I was blown away by the Holocaust Memorial Museum.  I brought my daughter who is 8 and there was a kids section where they tell 'Daniel's Story' and honestly I don't think there is a better way to tell the story of the Holocaust to children; my daughter always asks a lot of questions and the way they told the story- it answered a lot of her questions.  The whole museum really just was so unbelievable that I am at a loss for words. My favourite part of the museum was the moment I was able to take my time to reflect in the room with candles.

This museum was a must for me to come and see while in DC. I was a bit disappointed with the actually museum. The halls were filled with boards to read, i know that sounds awful. But after 2 hours of trying to soak up everything and read as much as i could, i had a headache. Nonetheless, it was very moving and im glad that we went. but defintely budget more time than what they tell you.
walk like 20 minutes from my hotel just to hear there's no more passes for today at 2???  you can get in but ther'es only one room that you can visit, no cameras, stollers etc allowed

I came here with a friend a little after 4 one day, and we thought that would be enough time to see what we wanted to, but there were about 4-5 school groups that were crowding in the entrance and main hall area, so it made for a very disorienting situation. We were unsure where to go, because many permanent exhibits were roped off. We walked through Daniels exhibit and it was very moving. We then wandered into the Hall of Remembrance which was very unique. We asked a security guard where all the other exhibits were and she said we could enter through the exit of the main exhibit since it apparently closed early. We got about through two rooms and then a staff person wouldnt let us continue. We asked if we could quickly go through since we couldnt come back for the rest of our trip, but she said better luck next time which really spoiled the trip. We wanted to get information about where we were supposed to enter and the info booth staff said we should have known to start at the top floor and work our way down. With a lot of confusion from all the schools, and lack of clear directional signs, we left very disappointed.

Obviously, the museum exhibits are not the thing we were unhappy with, but the way the staff communicated and expected us to know what to do. I also would say that the museum should only allow a certain small number of school groups so as to lessen the confusion. Or they should only let the school groups go in the morning. Either way, I think it needs to be more clear about how to go through the museum.

I first came here in 2010 when I was 18 years old and absolutely LOVED IT. Don't get me wrong, I still love this museum and encourage every single person to come and see it as I believe everyone should have the experience. But this time around, we must have come at a bad time. I LOVE children and I feel that if they are here at museum, it's wonderful and such a good learning experience. But when we come to a museum where we read, listen, and look to get the true experience, I would rather not have children yelling, talking loud, horse playing all around me when focusing on such a subject as the holocaust. I was truly saddened that there wasn't security or workers to patrol and keep an eye on areas for those wishing to get the true experience. I was sad because everywhere we went, children were allowed to run around, horseplay and talk as loudly as possible, even laugh at some of the exhibits! Many of these were groups from schools whose teachers or chaperones were no where in sight. A security officer was in the remembrance area and it was I along with my husband who had to ask two young boys to NOT blow out the candles which they were doing so, even playing with the fire. I love this museum, but if you want to get the true experience come super early or late in the day to hopefully get a better experience. We came right after lunch and I feel this affected us with the amount of people around. All in all, my favorite museum in DC.

I was a little leery about bringing a 13 and 11 year old, but I'm so glad we did. Big props for the "privacy screens" that sheltered them from the more graphic video footage. The kids asked great questions and were exposed to something I think every child should know about. The memorial museum is beautifully sad and very moving. It was amazing to meet a holocaust survivor and have the opprotunity for the kids to ask him questions. This is a must see experience if you're in DC.
I've been here before, but my GF hadn't, so we went, and again just an incredible display of history.  Its tragic what happened, but very enlightening to learn about the details.

If you are not too familiar with the Holocost, you could spend a lot of time here. There is a lot to read & it is pretty busy so plan on spending a bit of time here. You do need tickets to get in & they are limited. You can purchase them ahead of time online or take a chance while there. If you know a bit about it, you can walk swiftly through certain areas, but don't skip too much. Be sure to look on memorial wall & use computer to look up family names.

I found this a big disappointment.  It was mainly imagery and phrases and vey little substance.  It focused mainly on Jews and seemed to downplay the other victims.  If I had to pay it would have got a one.

I first went to this museum on the very first day it was open to the public. It was five-star fantastic. I'm guessing they have had to take it down a couple of notches was to accommodate the crowds. You used to get a mostly blank passport and would put it into a machine at various points to find out the status of your person. No more ... the passports are already all printed out in advance ... that loses a lot. Also, you used to could walk through reconstructed rooms, etc. No more ... just broad paths with traditional museum displays. The top floor is by far the best, so do not allocate your time evenly to all floors ... it's okay to spend more than half your time on the top floor.

Very nice ....A must see .. But I honestly think they could have constructed a smaller museum. ...it became redundant.  Seeing pictures drawn by children held in concentration camps was especially moving!
The new exhibit "We Were Neighbors" was really interesting and thought provoking.  I did not have time to go through the rest of the exhibits

I went because I am part Jewish.  I went as an adult who has witnessed lots of death.  This place is depressing, horrifying, and gave me nightmares.  It is well thought out.  Every aspect of this museum drives home the point that millions of innocent children, mommies, and daddies died in the most gruesome manner.  I have never been a fan of horror films.  This was just too realistic for me.  I forced myself to go through the whole building but I will never go back.  I believe it is free

I am a native of D.C. This museum has been a favorite of mine since I first went on a high school trip. It is really well organized and sends you on a journey through several floors. This first floor, which has information on Hitler's rise to power, is worth a long visit - read everything!

Packed full of people but still a must see.

Walks you down the timeline of what led to the holocaust right through it and to survivor stories.

A must when in DC. Everyone should visit this museum and reflect on its history! I like the different levels which takes you through the timeline of events from beginning to end. The ID cards given to you at the entrance has a very big impact on the presentation and overall experience.

This museum is an elegant memorial to the tragic time. Its not just a museum to walk through, but you really have a window to the hardship that was endured. There are different types of exhibits to view, some with videos, some with clothing etc, and it grabs your attention and holds on for dear life. I got a sense of love and unity at the end of the museum. I was lucky enough to visit on a day the museum had actual survivors of the Holocaust visit and speak with us one on one. This is an experience I will never forget. I hold this close to my heart.

Get a timed ticket in advance.  I was 1st in line for tickets at 7:30 am. on Monday 6/14.  No tickets given out until 9:45 am.  Was able to go right in, fortunately.  Got too crowded too quickly.  Hard to read the endless walls of documentaries, photograph titles with the sea of discourteous people walking between you and wall/picture.  Suggested age minimum I understand was 12, due to disturbing graphics and audio.  However, even with this suggestion, too many under age 12 kids, who had no clue what it was about.  Couldn't believe how many folks were carrying toddlers that were crying.  I allowed myself about 4-5 hours, and due to the crowds, kids, and bitter cold temperature in the museum left after 2 hours.
Best part, and don't miss the opportunity if available....in the enclosed "courtyard" where they have info booths, souveniers (honestly, do you really want a souvenier?) off to the side was a little table/desk with a small sign on it that had 2 Holocaust Survivor Volunteers willing to chat about their experiences and/or answer questions.  I had the privilage of meeting Estelle Laughlin, a survivor of Majdanek, a death camp.  Although we only chatted for about 10 minutes, it made the entire visit an incredible experience.
I'd like to go back, but have no intention of going when kids are out of school.  Perhaps between Thanksgiving and Christmas would work.
THe museum was very crowded and terribly hot.  So it was not the atmosphere to fully take in what the museum was trying to convey which was a shame because it is an important place.

Truly an impactful and moving experience that alters your very soul and being. The progression from one floor to the next as you walk through the history and timeline puts everything into perspective as to how such a thing could have happened.  The final floor that brings it into today was extremely moving.  The shoes...the shoes...the shoes. Pictures and numbers are one thing but when you see all the shoes that were worn by these poor souls just before they were put to death is what really burns a message into the deepest recesses of your own soul.  How could such cruelty have existed ...but then it still exists to this very day!  NEVER AGAIN, NEVER AGAIN, NEVER AGAIN!

Very disappointed in online ticketing. When I first checked in January, tickets for March were already gone. Then tried the day-of and every time I clicked on a time that two tickets were available, I then got a message that they were all gone. Very disappointed that the system works this way as I had no issues with reserving tickets online for other museums on this trip which release tickets 30 days in advance or have other similar processes. I have visited before and really wanted to share this experience with my niece. I completely understand advance ticketing and restricting numbers as this is not the kind of museum to crowd into, but it seems like the process is not working well.
Did not think the prices reflected the food. We only ate there as it was the nearest place to eat

This museum is a most of the day visit or two days if you tend to take your time and read everything but can't spend most of the day. The videos are good, displays, and artifacts excellent and the material to read is also excellent but enormous. They cover every aspect. If they didn't it would do a disservice to the victims. Any deeper and you have to go for the books, which are plentiful in the gift shop.

We came on a Thursday morning at 10:30 am. The lobby is stuffed full of screaming children and the museum is so over crowded you can not possible hear any of the TV's or see the screens.  This was the number one item on our list to see.  We stayed almost an hour before we just had to leave.  We made it only a tiny way through.  

Terrible terrible waste of time.    Sad.b   Once you get inside, you will not see anything at all.  Just leave and do something else.

We did not know you had to be there at 10 to obtain a ticket (free) to visit the museum.  So we were limited to the various displays in the rest of the building and did not get to see what we went for.  The rest is ok but not what we wanted.

A must see if we are not to forget what we are capable of doing when fanaticism takes over for rational thinking. This exhibit and memorial is a real eye opener. Everyone should see it. The only place better to get a feel for what the "captives" went through would be to visit a concentration camp like Dachau. I was there a yera ago and the Holocaust Museum is a very realistic representation of what went on in Dachau and the many other camps. It is especially informative as to the events that led up to the extermination of so many people. So many people think that it was just the Jews. They forget about the Jypsies, Catholics, Slavs, the Jehovas Wittness and many others.

Unbeknownst to us the heat and or area mechanical issues closed the museum-- however the website still allowed us to buy tickets and gave us a time. 
We got to the front doors and a very small sign simply said closed. 
Mmmm
Security said to just come back the next day-- we fiddled with our schedule and returned after checking the website, receiving a ticket confirmation, listening to the news--- alas it was closed again.  
So disappointed. The next day we received a thank you for visiting email. 
Please improve the communications. We were sad to miss such an important site.

The Holocaust is a sad, horrific reality and this museum reflects that all the way through.  The institution's mission is important, but carefully weigh your ability to handle graphic images and language.  And, please, consider leaving your children at home...until they are old enough to comprehend.  

We first visited the Memorial / Museum on an October Saturday and found unbelievable crowds.  After realizing we would not be able to even see 75% of the material, we decided to return on a weekday.  This made all the difference in our experience, because upon our return, the facility was much less crowded and we could take our time doing the copious reading required.  We'd recommend you avoid Sat/Sun/Holidays.

The story here is well-known by now, but the life stories, the details and minutae are new and valuable in understanding the experiences of the victims.  Seeing the belongings, photos, letters makes you realize that this situation didn't victimize a "mass" of 6 million, it victimized 6 million individuals with hopes, dreams and lives.  I found particularly poignant the two-story exhibition of photographs from a single small village wiped out by the Nazis.

Probably the most disturbing thing to me during my visit was not any of the material on display, but the parent who brought an elementary-aged child who was unable to read the complex material, so the adult read aloud to him/her.

A very moving museum but was angered by the display which stated "The Europeans allowed the plight of the Jews to continue until the actions of U.S Forces". 

How dare the Americans re-write history over such a serious issue.   I was so angry at this that I left the museum as out of respect for the victims I didn't want to appear this way inside this moving exhibition.
completely moving, especially the shoe display. Beware if you do not like the dark or closed spaces.
Museum is extremely well-done and moving.  Not to be missed.  Might be too much for 12 and younger or those particularly tender-hearted.
This was day 2 on a quick trip to DC. Special exhibits are available April to August, check website for that. Free entry, plan on several hours.

Be sure to pack a lunch and expect to spend a long time here. It  is so much to see. It's a eye opener to the things that American had to endure years ago. A lot of history .The movie that they show you tells the story.

We were using the Hop On Hop Off bus around Washington DC and this museum was one of the stops.   So disappointing to find nearly every section closed unless you had a free ticket which apparently you would have had to get early in the day.   There were only a few displays open to view - one only suitable for children.   A complete fizzer.   To add to our frustration the bus never appeared again - there was a Gay Pride Parade starting and the streets had closed!   We had to walk from then on after waiting ages!   Yes, we did see the Parade and it was really good but we had blisters at the end of a long day in extreme heat.   More publicity on the above would have been helpful.

Was somewhat disappointed in this museum - have wanted to visit for many years, so maybe my expectations were too high.  It seemed as you were inundated with political issues and call for money - not what I want to see at a museum.

The volunteers/staff at the museum are extraordinary! The exhibits were well designed and gave the impression that you are participating. I learned a lot. I will never forget walking through and around the train cars.
The museum is historical and necessary to make sure we don't ever allow ourselves to become complacent and allow others to do lour thinking for us.

Museum was set up very nicely.  A lot of history to be learned....but very depressing.  Would not advise young children to go thru it.  You can go thru it at your own pace, reading, etc.  There was not charge to enter but would recommend ordering tickets on line in advance or get in line at least 1 hr before opening.

Went on a Sunday of a holiday weekend and it was PACKED. So hard to watch the videos and everything in the museum since there are so many people. It's definitely a must-see very humbling an experience you don't want to miss.

I couldn't even get inside this museum when a female black security officer kicked me out of there for having a Gatorade bottle in my bag.  First, she yelled at me, and asked me to drink out of my water bottle.  Then, she yelled some more and almost hit me demanding to leave the museum for having a Gatorade bottle.  I offered to throw away the drink but she stupidly said "No".  What a pity   to have so many unprofessional people in a place that so many visitors want to see.  My daughter was so disappointed.  I said, don't worry baby, we can visit other places where no black security officer can mistreat us.

Yes, it's depressing.  But, it's a history lesson that we all need.  The tickets are free, but you'll want to arrive at the museum by 10:00 am when they start handing them out.  (There will be a line during their busy season.)  We waited for about 30 or 40 minutes, got our tickets, headed to lunch, and then returned.  It wasn't that inconvenient.  My only complaint is that the museum was very crowded.  Plan to spend several hours making your way through, especially if you want to catch the short films along the way.  I think we were there for four hours and that was about the right amount of time for us.  Some images are graphic, so keep that in mind if you have young children.

The brochure I saw said to plan for at least 1.5 hr and 2 if you saw the special exhibit. I spend over 2 hours on the 4th floor alone! The museum holds an enormous amount of information and presents it in multiple formats. I found the recordings of survivors recounting their stories very powerful. Each visitor is given a small book about an individual who lived during this time, like a passport, and this helped to give the experience a more personal perspective. It's nearly impossible to get tickets online but you can stand in line and when the museum opens, they give out passes, several at a time. I wasn't there during the busy summer time and there were probably 150 people in front of me. Even then, with at least 2 school groups ahead of me, I probably only stood in line 30 -45 min, and it was truly worth it.

We were able to grab tickets online for the main exhibit at 3:30, all other times weren't available. When we got there, there were many school groups. Standing in line was horrible, there was no system, people continuously cut in line. The groups of students were not supervised and were disrespectful. It was hard to really get into the exhibit when it was so crowded. For that it gets two stars. From what I could observe, the exhibits were filled with information and very powerful. I just wish they had a better system in place limiting the amount of people in the exhibit in order to get the full experience.

The unfortunate experience at this museum is that everyone wants to see the permanent exhibit. We ordered tickets online in advance so we did not have to wait in line, but there were just too many people at the museum. There is a lot of reading for every display which holds up the crowd. There were numerous times that we skipped sections because we simply could not see through the crowd and had been waiting way to long for folks to move.  Most of the exhibit is reading with pictures - nothing truly interactive - so be prepared to simply stand and read. The museum has a very important message but it was lost in the crowds. I would have preferred to pay for entry and have fewer patrons so that I could truly immerse myself in the exhibit. It almost felt that the solemn nature of the exhibit was ruined by the magnitude of people.

This museum appears to have an extremely outstanding content.  However, we could not get near the exhibits and, in fact, could barely move among the wave of people that are admitted.  

The museum is free and there is no attempt at crowd control.  Hordes of people are admitted with no consideration of the Museum's space.

After spending about 20 minutes being scrunched among these crowds in order to get through, perhaps, the first one percent of the exhibits, we left.

When we acquired of staff as to a better time, we were advised that it was always like this.

This is a pity.  The tiny fraction was saw indicated a very meaningful Museum experience.  However, without some meaningful level of crowd control, the content is simply wasted.

I would advise you not to go.

Heard it was hard to get tickets unless you order them early, so I did. There must of been 2-3 school groups ahead of us when we got there and made it hard to see alot of displays.  We've been to the holocaust museum in Israel, and a concentration camp in Germany and found them much more interesting and informative. The staff wasn't very helpful,  may have been because of all the people there but the staff felt rushed to answer any questions.

I am giving the museum 3 stars simply because it was free and because the museum and its information is important. Also, could have just been a crowded day. However, for me, there were a couple of points that could have been better 

1. Signs/direction: Once inside, there is little to no direction as to what you are supposed to do or where you should go (we had to ask). In the permanent exhibit, there were fews signs or arrows stating what to do or which way to go and we felt lost and confused. Had a hard time at the end trying to figure out how to exit the building also.

2. Content: The exhibit consists mainly of pictures with captions to read and videos to watch (few artifacts and many of those are re-creations). Im not sure why there is such a VAST amount of reading material in a place so crowded. It would take many hours to read it all even if you were alone. With the crowd, you are forced to just skip most of it as so many are in a small font and are hung at eye level. Why not make them large and hang them high? Most of it is impossible to see/read over the crowd...

3. Crowds make this impossible to experience, especially the sheer number of children. Seems like this is a common complaint so why not be open at certain times/hours just for school groups? Seems safer for the kids as they seemed to be having trouble keeping up with the children in the large crowd. If they did stop to read, they were hurried forward. What is the point of taking them if they arent there to read and learn?

At the end, we were exhausted and frustrated. Ones emotional response should be for the holocaust and the content of what was just seen and not over the confusion of the crowd/building. This made me sad because I really wanted to learn more about what happened.

I have been in a large number of museums for the Holocaust including Yad Veshem in Jerusalem and was very impressed by this one.
Well laid out and constructed.
More than anything else on a very snowy day it was full.
Just too many people allowed in at the same time ... completely detracted from our visit to the museum.

I visited the US Holocaust Memorial Museum my last trip to DC in August. This is a museum every one needs to visit once to really understand one of the greatest horrors of mankind. The building is new and modern but holds many secrets. It is so very impressive that it begs one to go in as you approach it.  Once inside one pretty much begins 2 to 3 hour journey into the past that takes a look at one of the most horrific parts of history.

It took me about 4 hours to go through the whole museum and closely look, observe and listen to a journey that will have a profound affect on one. I was affected but educated during this visit as I really did not understand just what the scope of the holocaust was! 

The exhibits tell the story and opens one up to a time that is dark and difficult to comprehend and/or understand the suffering inflicted on so many who were just innocent.

As you travel from room to room the story unfolds and the true magnitude is revealed.  Although depressing to me (and most), If one is in DC, this is one of the museums that I would urge everyone to visit to gain true understanding of life and will lead most  to conclude how lucky most of us are. What a collection of exhibits that leads to a film at the end of the journey through this museum, that as you sit down and listen to the survivors, most will be brought to tears. 

I am so glad I visited and learned so much.

This museum is mostly reading.  It was extremely crowded.  Where people were stopping and reading everything nobody was moving.  The idea of this museum is nice but I don't think the layout is good.  Most everything in here can be read in books or online.

Interesting walking tour it was pretty crowded. The tour gave lots of information  that I had never knew before. The tour had a good amount of artifacts that was very unique. I think it would be appropriate for older children.

My husband has MS and cannot stand for hours in queues.  We tried by various means to get tickets where he would not have to wait for a long time.

The staff and voluteers in this facility all need sensitivity training.  All that I spoke with including  on the phone when I called were very rude.

For a place that is trying to teach  understanding and equality for all, this place showed no compassion for someone with a disability.

My husband is also a US veteran but as he left the military many years ago he does not carry any military ID anymore.  So they would not believe him.  If he could have produced military ID he would have got more consideration and gone to the head of the queue.

I emailed the museum with my concerns and received a standard response that they get so many emails they cannot guarantee answering any.

So much for their quote "what you do matters

So we saw a small part of this museum and it was well-done and I'm sure it's a great museum, but..... we didn't see hardly any of it.  And yet, the place wasn't really that crowded when we were there.  I had gone online ahead of time, but not nearly ahead enough, and online tickets were all gone from March through the summer. But the online ticketing was surely just a limited number of tickets and there'd be plenty for in person visitors I thought.  We went one afternoon and they said they'd 'just run out of the passes.'  So we decided to go first thing the next morning and yet still, all the passes were gone.  So how many do they set aside for online vs. in person?  Because we were there and it really was not very crowded at all, the lines were short getting in, etc.  So if people get those online (free) tickets and don't show up... which has to happen all the time because people's plans change once they get in town or because people underestimate all the walking and get too tired, teenagers oversleep, whatever.... then others standing right there don't get access and the museum is underutilized? I just thought that was very frustrating.  So I wasn't left with a very good impression despite the fact that I'm sure it's an excellent collection presented in a educational, respectful manner.

It's a lot to take in, so plan on spending about 4 hours or so. Unfortunately we had to get to a lunch date, and did it in about 2-1/2 hours. There are 4 floors of amazing exhibits, and it takes a lot of time to get through. We went on a Saturday, and it was VERY crowded, which I'm sure was normal. All in all, I would do it again, and plan on spending more time.
This truly touching museum is a must see in your trip to Washington. Yes, you need to pay but so worthwhile. Stunning and sombrero all together
This museum is a very comprehensive display of the holocaust and is an educational experience, even for those who already have a lot of knowledge.  It is extremely informative, emotional draining.

This museum takes us on a sobering tour of what is a holocaust, with special emphasis on the holocaust that was brought upon the jewish people by the Nazi. You have to go early to get a ticket to go to the permanent collection, though, and we didn't know that. Overall, if you have the opportunity it's a great place and everyone should go at least once.
This is amazing and sobering.  Some of the parallels from then in Germany to now in USA are very frightening.

I really looked forward to visiting the Holocaust Memorial Museum, but found it very ineffective at telling Holocaust story.  The museum exhibits, overall, were very disjointed.  The best exhibits are on the lower level and consist of pretty good videos, stories and photographs.  I think that the museum would have been much more effective if it had displayed exhibits in chronological order of events, starting with the early stages of discrimination and exterminations, before the war; how and where these events escalated during the war, thereby explaining why and how this horrible period in history occurred.  That would have had such a bigger impact and helped to educate people that don't know much about the Holocaust.  You can get more understanding about the Holocaust from watching You Tube videos than visiting this museum.

We arrived an hour before closing and were told this on three occasions as we entered. The layout is very difficult to track in terms of the historical time lines. Most annoying with 15 mins to go before closing we were shepherded out like sheep to make sure they closed before time, i am sure to allow the staff to leave which slightly ruined the visit

This museum is a must-see when visiting DC.  It is a well laid-out historical account of events leading up to and after the Holocaust.  Although visitors move at their own pace,  because of this chronologic presentation there tends to be larger crowds at the start of the exhibit where visitors start more slowly so be prepared to be patient, otherwise go during off-hours.
The exhibit is very well put together but I wish there would've been more artifacts versus reading. It was nearly impossible to read all of it with the crowds.

They have limited paper tickets to offer and when gone, you have to get them online. The process is a bit cumbersome as you are standing in the middle of the museum having to navigate odd webpages to do so...well done and informative museum.

A degreed history buff, the enormity of the crimes against humanity represented by the Holocaust has fascinated me since childhood.  Finally getting a chance to tour the Holocaust Museum in D.C., I entered with both reverence and enthusiasm.  First things first:  sign up online and get tickets.  Tickets are released on a day-of basis at 6:00 a.m.  Set your alarm and you can get your tickets for same-day tours. They are cheap ($1.00) and permit you to see the actual exhibits. 

The museum itself was both more and less than I expected, but ultimately, disappointing.  How? The architecture is austere, with inorganic shapes, textures, and materials.  You enter through an iron-sheathed set of doors, perhaps reminiscent of macabre gas chamber.  Thereafter, you encounter impersonal brick and marble textures, used to some effect for the ghetto reproduction in the free tour area.  Perhaps too much realism might have felt campy or, more likely, frightening, but I was left questioning the architects decisions as I toured the facility:   I did not care for the eclectic mix of modern and period-apparent architecture and design.  

Once inside the 3-story, limited-access museum, you encounter a bit of mismanagement.  Tours are collected into time slots and routed into one of three elevators, whereupon you are presented with a series of brief movies and printed-text exhibits.  While absolutely necessary for the story of the Holocaust, it creates a logjam of tourists that cannot abate:  the text exhibits are dense, the aisle is narrow, and there is only one route. Controlling access by 10- to 15-minute increments would greatly reduce the initial jam.  If the intent was to passively make you feel like Jews being herded, perhaps it was effective, although the order of the presented exhibits was out of sync for that purpose.  

The next thing you will notice is the surprising lack of actual exhibits.  In fact, the first floor of the permanent exhibit has very little to offer by way of tangible exhibits.  There is an SA uniform, a data-coding computer and few other exhibits to see. I go to museums to actually encounter the physical, tangible evidence of history. Reproductions and facsimileseven if expertly doneleave me empty.   Would you be as impressed going to the Smithsonian and seeing a mere photocopy of the Declaration of Independence?  Why go to D.C. to see the Holocaust Museum if you could have seen the same thing on the History Channel?  By contrast, by actually standing a foot from the S.A. uniform (the first tangible exhibit you will see), I was able to see the details of the garment:  I had no idea the tunic was a flannel material while the trousers were corduroy, or that the Swastika was rather inexpertly sewn to the sleeve.  These are the things one simply cannot deduce from old newsreel footage or the poster-sized enlargements of old, grainy photographs.  Also:  The fabric of the striped pajamas of the concentration camps was actually a thick, coarse, herringbone with woven stripes.  Sadly, actual exhibits were simply too fewand definitely too farin between to satisfy my historical curiosity.  That said, the rooms of shoes were amazing.  The opportunity to stand mere feet from artifacts of this atrocity was, indeed, moving.  There were also a handful of personal possessions recovered.  Again, my historian perspective is of awe and reverence to encounter these tangible connections to a time so incredible as to still have those who deny its existence. 

Of less interest, from an evidentiary standpoint, were the reproductions. Within the permanent exhibit is the Auschwitz metal gate, a railroad car, gas chamber door, bunk bed, a ghetto wall, and other artifacts.  Of among these, my inner historian was interested (e.g., the Auschwitz gate was surprisingly small, the rail car was even more dehumanizing than I had imagined, the head of the bunk beds was higher than the feetdetails, all, I would have missed otherwise).  Still, one cannot help but be left wanting for more authenticity, even, understanding, as I do, that the actual sites should be never be desecrated.

In the end, the museum tells a story.  If your expectation is to learn details of the history of the Holocaust, you will come away fulfilled and filled with questions of mans inhumanity to manthere are no answers at this place, only more questions.  If your expectation is to come face to face with the actual tangible evidence of the time, things, and people, you will come away wanting.  As macabre as it may seem, the Holocaust Museum left me wanting to visit the actual sites: to see the ground, the trees, the buildings; to imagine the sounds; to wonder what possessed Nazis to perpetrate these vile acts; to actually be where the heinous crimes took place and, perhaps, to better understand the Holocaust.  There will be those who disagree with my criticism of the museum, but its presence perpetuates the memory of the Holocaust such that we never forget.  

May God bless those souls lost and comfort those who suffered loss.
Interesting museum learned many new things about the holocaust, but the main exhibit wasnt clearly labeled and its not clear which way you should go.  Some very graphic videos of the horror shown

I went here with my 2 teenage daughters and when we left, my 14 year old summed it up with 'it's the same as the others.'  I realized she was right - it must be a theme for me, but I have taken them to the Museum of Resistance and Deportation in Lyon, the Museum of Surrender in Reims, and the Anne Frank House in Amsterdam.  If you've been to a Holocaust museum before, I'd recommend using your time elsewhere.
There was a lot of great photos and information provided in the self guided tour. But there was way to many people smashed into the tour at once they need a better way of getting people in.

I brought my family this time, and my mom left. She couldn't finish. Yes, it is hard, but I DO NOT advise leaving early without finishing the entire exhibit. The last part is a "reflection" segment that really makes one think about big things and life's questions. So inspiring. Always reserve your tickets online (it's only $1 per ticket) to avoid the lines, and take a cab or walk- there's no parking. Though cabs in D.C. are always easy to hail. No food/drink/photography of ANY kind. Enjoy.

The US Holocaust Museum in DC is a good place to go, but you have to be prepared for what it is, before you go in.  The brutal reality of what happened hits you as soon as you exit the elevator on the 3rd floor - a huge picture with piles of dead bodies stacked in a ditch.  Shocking, but also a grim reminder of what happened.  It is very large and by the time you get through the third floor and head to the second, you think, 'wow, two more floors of this' - it is very disturbing.  BUT with that said, the museum gives you an excellent account of the atrocities that took place during that awful time.
One thing that II found shocking - there were a lot of children in the museum.  People brought their families, which I understand, but in reality, this is no place for children.

The museum was very crowded. I didn't feel like I gained any knowledge that I haven't already seen on the history channel or from books. I am really disappointed with the museum maybe....I was expecting too much.

Doesnt compare with the real visit to the concentration camps. Covers way too much of the background of what led up to the war. It was like standing up in a crowded line and reading a history book. 
The impact of the concentration camp was lost in the two hours it took to get to it. 
Skip it and read the book. Save your money and go to Germany for the real thing.

I am wishing that museums like this one would require school groups to have more chaperones.  Kids walking through that were disrespectful.  Excellent filmstrips and exhibits, very informative but be prepared to be there awhile.  Crowded and line moves slowly.

it takes so long to read everything, but it is so informative.  You will learn things that you did not know.  They present an intense immersion in the wide spread extent of nazi horror.  You can not finish time in the musuem and not be moved, or worried about current propoganda in our wars.
If your in Berlin A must see. Easy in easy out no lines. try and get a guide to escort you around. 3 to 4 hours.
This was an eye opening experience. Very graphic and detailed. I think everyone should know how this occurred and ended.

Tasteful. Moving. Shocking. Gut wrenching. Humbling. Grounding.

Was in DC two years ago travelling from Australia, unsure whether to take my young teen sons. 
Chose at the time not to. This trip with friends I was able to visit as not travelling with my kids.
In reflection I believe it was the right decision. Partly because of their immaturity, partly because of the amount of reading required and quiet resection. My sons at the time 12 & 13 would have engaged with visuals and interactive segments, too much reading for them to manage.

Well done to the museum curators and all involved. Thank you for telling this story, to give these lost people a face.

We visited July 16th.  Secured time slot specific advance tickets. The number of people in all hall was overwhelming and made it impossible to experience the museum.  Really poor crowd planning, especially having ALL the tools to do it better with the advance tickets. ???

My High School daughter really wanted to see this, remember to get tickets on line ahead of time or you need to get there early and wait in line for the daily distribution.  The exhibit may be too intense for young children.
Probably the busiest museum we have been in in Washington - and the quietest - as visitors of all nationalities and creeds remember the awful history detailed here.

No explanation of really why incidences occurred or what caused such hatred and revenge. No explantion to how a political party which only won with a plurality could have so much power over other parties and the people of Germany? There is much space in the museum that seems to be wasted.

Do not go on a summer weekend. Despite having timed tickets, the permanent exhibit was literally wall to wall people. You couldn't move, much less read the exhibits. I had to leave after about 5 minutes. The museum really needs to limit the number of people who come in it strictly enforce the timed ticket system. I can't believe this isn't considered a fire hazard, nor that anyone who was there could get anything out of the museum. Very disappointing.
Excellent depiction of the inhumanity of mankind - quite emotionally moving. Not for the faint hearted.

Everyone should visit this museum.  As soon as I got off the elevator I broke down crying. The video footage of what the US Soldiers found at the concentration camps is overload of the senses.  The information presented is well put together.  Every time I think of the Holocaust I wonder how could something like this happen. It broke my heart walking through it. When I got to the room full of shoes, the kids shoes, the smell, I broke down and sobbed.  I was emotionally drained when I left.  I hate to say it is excellent because of the horrific topic but I think the set up of the museum is fantastic.  I learned so much even though I have read so much about that time.  If in DC, make this your first stop.

Word of warning... Plan ahead if you want to get in to this museum during the summer months.  We were disappointed when we arrived at the museum because we couldn't get tickets.  Even by noon, the tickets were gone for the day. Plan ahead if you want to get in, I now know that you can reserve tickets online. 

There museum did have a children's exhibit that did not require tickets. There was also another gallery. But the Hall of Remembrance does require the tickets.
Got there and said they were sold out of tickets. Would have been nice to know before I walked there stood in line for 20 min just to be told as I'm about to walk in its sold out

I do my research for my trips and  never saw anything about this museum having advanced tickets required. We showed up, stood inline and were told upon entry by a very curt young man that if we didn't get advance tickets we were not admitted to MOST of the exibits. REALLY??!! What we saw was very interesting. The place also looked to us more like an industrial building rather than a museum. We found it a bit hard to navigate. We were SO looking forward to seeing this museum and very upset to find we "did it wrong!"

This museum is a must see so that we can learn and know and make sure that this kind of history does not repeat itself. Yes, it is very sad and very disturbing, but still a must see. I did not rate it a 5 star because we were advised to get tickets in advance. However, the day we were there NO ONE was turned away. It was so crowded you could not get up to some of the exhibits to even see them. There were kids everywhere and it was not solemn and quiet which it should have been. We were glad to have seen it, but it was a crowded mess. No tickets necessary!

My review of the museum has nothing to do with the people or families that have suffered due to the holocaust. My option is that the museum did not due justice to showing the pain and suffering that these people went thru. The elevator lets large amounts of people in a small room with a small hall. Felt like cattle. There are a lot of picture of people and families. I not the true pictures are hard to see but they give us the true understanding of the peoples suffering. Nothing there for kids under 20.
This is by far one of the most moving museums that I have ever visited.  The one thing that I would recommend is to give yourself plenty of time.  I could easily spend 6+ hours in this museum...
Not sure what this is doing on the Washington Mall. You may easily skip it. There is not much to see or appreciate. Lots more to see and enjoy all around.

After reading a few reviews, it seemed like the smart choice is to pre-purchase your ticket for the permanent exhibit. THIS IS A FREE MUSEUM but when you pre-purchase it is $1 per ticket. They only allow so many at certain times. 

My family and I chose the 2:30pm option thinking there would be less people, (also since there were 50+ tickets left available online for that time) It said to arrive 10 minutes early to go through security. We did, however, there was a line out the door. No priority access for advance purchased tickets. There was also 1...again... 1 security check line which is why there was such a long line. I have gone through several security checks for the area museums as well as airport ones and had NEVER had to pull out my inhaler. Someone else in my party had been pulled off to the side for her GUM PACK! That whole experience was very off putting. 

My mother, my brother, his girlfriend and her daughter (6 yrs old) and myself made our way to the elevators that take you up to the exhibit. When we exited the elevator, I thought someone had turned off a set of lights through the museum. It was so dark; you couldn't even read what they had on display. 

I had taken a seat to watch the 1st video that was offered to watch. It had quickly filled up to a point where it had gotten to be claustrophobic. It seemed like there were about 5 High Schools in attendance there. 
This was poorly built. There were at least 2 different, large, areas that had donated art that took up space. White art pieces on a white wall.what? 

A lot of the artifacts werent real. They were castings made of what they were.
I do have to give props to the fact that they covered the TV's with a wall of concrete to make sure that children do not see some of the videos that may be a bit disturbing. 

This museum would have been a lot more meaningful if it was not a crowded as it was. It had lost all its meaning when you had to fight to see the walls and displays. Nothing here I couldn't see or read by watching documentaries online or by going to a library. 

When we were done with the permanent exhibit, we didn't even want to go through the other areas offered since we did not want to deal with the crowds again.

Go when it opens so there are less people.
It was good experience.I would recommend to visit story of Daniel.There are alot of objects and story about Daniel notebook.
