I visited when I was 18, but that almost 30 years ago and not sure I fully appreciated it at that age,  Very thorough history of everything surrounding the Holocaust.  Very emotional at times.  Very well put together
While  all of us have read about the holocaust or even seen movies on it. The expriancing it at the holocaust museum is a thought provoking unforgettable experience.

We visited this museum for the first time.  There are basically 3 floors and allow yourself plenty of time.  We were there for 2 1/2 hours and rushed through two of the 3 floors.  It is very depressing and saddening.  But that's the nature of the holocaust.  You could spend all day at this museum.  We ended up rushing because we got hungry and wanted to eat.  There isn't anywhere to eat nearby. The exhibits are very well presented and the short films are well worth watching.   I went away asking how in the world can people be so very cruel as to do something like this.  I hope no one ever has to face that kind of horror again.

This museum leaves a lot to be desired.  Many others were good, but this just seemed to be "slapped up" in a hurry.  In my opinion, it is not only a disappointment, but is a disservice to the Jewish community.  And it certainly is not on par with the other museums in the National Mall.
you want to start off with the video on the main floor then go upstairs and work your way down.  Very good museum.
Shocking, visually disturbing, emotional, and more. Yes, man has inflicted genocide thru the ages but this hits you in the forehead.

We really wanted to go here, but we could not seem to plan it right.  The online ticket reservations showed the next available date three months from our visit.  We stood in line for an hour on our last day in DC at 9:30 a.m. on a 26 degree windy day only to find that the earliest tickets they had were for 1:30 that afternoon.  Frozen and disappointed, we left.  

Smart people chose to send one person to wait in line and grab tickets for everyone in their group which seemed to work.  I have heard it's worth it.  Wish we'd been smarter and gotten in.

I don't care for how you are supposed to get tickets. ...that doesn't make sense to me. ..non of the other museums require tickets early,  we wanted to see ALL of the museum,  not just Danny's House (which was wonderful).... I've been to DC three times and I have yet to get thru the museum!

This was the last place we visited on our list of places to see while in DC for a few days.  I knew it would be an emotional experience and it certainly was.  It is the type of museum that might be best viewed on a few occasions because there is so much to see.  It is one of the best memorials I have ever seen.  The shoe exhibit - someone told us that it "smells of ghosts".  What an apt description.  The photos and videos are very moving and well-placed throughout the building.  I would definitely go back again and highly recommend taking the time to visit this museum.

I might be wrong but the intended message of this museum was not delivered to me, i wanted so much after my visit to feel the graveness of the massacre but no, all Europeans had a taste of the massacre during the power, the good thing is that a section of the museum shows the syrian, ruwanda, cambodia massacres    among others

Highly recommended for ages 10 and up.   The museum provides an overview with Daniel's story at the beginning, and then goes chronologically to describe the unfolding of the horrific events of the Holocaust.  Both educational and very moving!  Many a vistor will shed tears when seeing what mankind can stoop to.

We only went through the area for kids "Daniels story".  It is mostly well done and sensitive to children. 

My daughter asked an obvious question "how did he survive?"  I think they need to add something at the end to explain how he ended up surviving.  

The rest of the place is probably well done, but I will say the amount of field trip students they let in is overwhelming.  I believe the complaints about crowd size is accurate.
A complete history on the holocaust. My kids only lasted 2 floors. A little too much reading for them.  Bought tickets for $1 online which is a  must to skip the long line to get a ticket.
It was mostly pictures with a description attached . With the hoards of people they cram in there it is nearly impossible to get close enough to see them

This is my third time to DC but my first time to the Holocaust Museum. Wow. This is very sobering. I think every person who visits DC needs to view this. We must never forget what the Nazi party did to try and eradicate the Jews.

stopped by on a recommendation of someone during out family vacation.

it is a well done remembrance to those that suffered.

This is a VERY nice museum. The parts I thought were the best were the videos, the crematorium model, the shoes exhibit, the voices of the survivors, and the video at the end of the survivors. 
The cons of the museum include too many people. It is impossible to read the MANY signs when there are too many people in the museum. I understand that many people want to go to the museum but it ruins the experience when you cannot even get by people to move on to the next sign or you cannot read the signs because there are too many people in front of it. You literally would have to spend the entire day there to be able to read it all with all the people that are in the way. I honestly think they made the signs too small and should have put them up higher so more people could read them. Also people could be extremely rude, you could be visibly reading a sign and someone would come and stand right in front of you to read it and block multiple other people from reading it. It has so much information and so much facts that it also can be overwhelming. 
I think the museum is stunning and tells one of the saddest stories known to mankind. I only wish they either limit more people or they would enhance the design more. I have been there prior about six years ago and I do not remember it being as crowded and I went the same time frame.

Well documented, well exposed very well taught into making people aware of their mistakes. 
My question is: what does the health department have to say about the "shoes exhibit", and the smell in that place? How healthy it is and how it might impact the visitors? Could be exposed encased? I came back home seek for about a week with an ugly cough and chest pain.
The museum is very well done.  The staff was friendly and knowledgeable.  My 12 year old daughter was very moved by it as was I.
We thought it was just ok    some very moving exhibits    far too many people

The museum trying to show the Holocaust. But nowhere is use proper name of people who did it. The German Nazis,Every where is used only word the Nazis, my child has already asked me what nationality were the Nazis and I had to explain to her that they were German people. This is blurring history

This was a large museum. We had a little difficulty navigating to the sites prior to our visit to the upstairs area. This is a much larger place than you would think. Parts of the museum are painful to see and experience due to the violence that was present during the holocaust. The tickets are free but come early on busy season times to make sure you can get in.

The information and visuals provided are moving, emotional and upsetting all at the same time.  However, we suggest that you plan your trip for a time, as best you can, that may not be as busy as our weekend visit was.  Was on three day holiday weekend and large numbers of middle school aged youth were more interested in their smart phone than anything else.  Was at times difficult to see or get close to exhibits to read due to large numbers of people.  But, the price was right, no charge.

This should be required viewing for all citizens. The Holocaust museum walks its guests through the Holocaust, from Hitler's populist rise to power, to the normalization of dehumanization of a minority, to the tragedy of the internment camps and WWII. It's an incredibly deep experience and conveys the dangers of such governance. It also takes time to address other human rights atrocities such as the Khmer Rouge in Cambodia and the ethnic cleansing in Bosnia, demonstrating that even in this day and age, societies can still embrace dehumanization. This is a must see attraction, even if only once to understand history so we do not repeat it.

Made a special stop for our visiting granddaughter to tour the museum as she studied the holocaust this past year.   I ordered the tickets on line.  My husband and I had visit the museum before so I knew wr had to have tickets.  On arrival we were directed into on of four large elevators that held many people.  Getting out we were faced with a full hallway of exhibits with shoulder to shoulder people in it that didn't move.  We were unable to view any information as the crowds were so great.  I thought the idea of tickets were to limit the amount of people on the tour for all to learn of this tragic time of history and to learn of the suffering of the people.   Very disappointed

This was one of my must-sees in Washington, D.C., but to be honest, it didn't quite meet my expectations, so that was very disappointing.  The museum itself certainly was loaded, in fact, over-loaded with information, pictures, and videos, and hit my emotions numerous times, but it was the employees and other visitors that disappointed me.  First off, we were in the museum for at least a half-hour before we discovered that we had to get a pass for the permanent exhibit.  We had even gone up to the information booth and the woman didn't volunteer this information, but we found out by noticing other people who had tickets.  So, once we got our tickets, we then had to wait about an hour (not so bad, as I have seen in other reviews that some didn't even get tickets).  So, we waited patiently.  While we were waiting, we thought we might go to the cafe, so in our search, we asked some of the employees where it was, whether we'd have to wait to get back in, all normal tourist questions. They must get tired of answering questions, because they just weren't that helpful or nice; in fact, one of them was quite a smart-aleck. They probably thought it was fun to mess with the tourists from time to time.  Well, we ended up skipping the cafe altogether and just waited for our time to get in.  Once in, the crowd of people was unbearable; it was difficult to read and take our time looking at the exhibits.  Some of the visitors were not respectful, either; there was a school group with kids who were on their own, messing around as kids will, but in a place like this, it saddened me.  I also don't understand why people bring their babies and toddlers; they are bored and often disruptive--why subject them to this?  It ends up only making the experience worse for those around them.  This is not the kind of museum for younger children and it's too bad that people cannot use common sense about things like that. We only had a few hours to spend and one really needs more time in order to fully appreciate the plethora of information on display. Nevertheless, it is a very moving experience and must be viewed in a solemn and serious manner. It is hoped that people will never forget the atrocity of the holocaust, but reality shows us that some people just do not really care and that is truly appalling.

The museum is a modern well laid out museum spread over three or four floors and includes a number of exhibits specifically disigned for children, although parents should be careful with small children on some of the final exhibits of showing the liberation of the camos as some of them are quite rightly very graphic.  The museum gives a factual and detailed account of the holocaust from its start in 1933 up to and including the liberation of the concentration camps in 1945 and the aftermath with the migration to Palestine/Isreal.  In addition there are a number of quiet sitting out areas for contemplation and a room of rememberance.  The exhibits show the horror of the events and mans lack of humanity and, as such, is the sort of museum that everyone should visit to stop us forgetting the depths of depraviyty we are capable of if left unchecked.  It also brings us right upto date with a small exhibit of current events in Syria that show startling similarities to some of the vents leading up to the holocaust. Overall, an excellent if sobering museum.  Well worth a visit

We arrived too late in the day to see the main attraction so we did Daniel's house/journey and the Collaboration area.  Honestly, it was too dark for the kids (11 and 13).  It did spark some good conversation but the heaviness of the topic was just too much for them.  If we did DC again, there are plenty of other museums that would be higher on my list to revisit but I am glad that we did it once.

It is never to visit any Holocaust Museum but it is an important history lesson. There is a lot of information, videos and guides to explain all the details that occurred during the time. The photos, the stories are very moving, so take your time and learn. It is worth visiting.
The permanent collection is one of the most moving museum experiences you can have, just be sure to book your time online - daily tickets are limited and you will miss out otherwise.

Trange St up in here. When we went we had to queue to get in, then queue inside to get a ticket, to then wait an hour for our time slot. We then go to the line which is very long and takes us 15 minutes to get to the front to thn be told we're 5 mins early come back later. By this time the line was bigger than before. We'd already spent an hour and 45 mins in this place so we decided no more time to be wasted in there and we left. I will also mention that the guy who was letting people go up in the lift was extremely rude and harsh.
We just showed up here after lunch and was told that the passes for that day were sold out. That was a dissapointment. So I can only tell you to buy passes early and plan on the tour.

This museum is exceptional and gigantic.  In addition to the permanent exhibit, there are several smaller exhibits downstairs on fascinating topics.  But please, leave children under 14 at home.  Parts of the PE are just brutal.  No, your kid is not mature enough.  Daniel's Story is a first-floor exhibit designed for kids.  This place might knock the wind out of you, so don't plan this as the first stop of the day.  There are no restaurants nearby, so plan to eat at the Museum Cafe on the 15th street side or the food cart on the corner of 14th and Independence.  From here you are within one block of the Washington Monument and (down 14th Street) the Bureau of Engraving and Printing for a lighter tour.

Could only get tickets for later in the day and could have used more time. 2 hours not enough time for me. Kids were impatient and went ahead on their own. This is about 3 blocks from a metro station.

I visited the museum expecting more, but I was disappointed.  I visited a real concentration camp some years ago and I was expecting a similar filling that I had on that visit.  Do not get me wrong, the museum is really well assemble and have a lot of information, but I prefer to show this genocide as real as possible to be sure that never happened again.  I know exists more video, pictures and information that can be added showing the real deal.
On other hand the building is very well designed and transport the visitor to the period of time in which the events happened.

We missed the main exhibition and still don´t understand why we didn´t qualify to see the main attraction. We arrived early in the afternoon to the museum and visited the information booth for assistance. The assistant told us that we could visit the exposition of the collaborators of the Nazi regime, She asked us that from what part did we visit and we told her. She said that we had to return to see the main attraction. Since we had only two complete days for our Washington visit, we had to miss the main exhibition. I still don´t understand why we didn´t qualify to see the whole exhibition being a visitor from Costa Rica, a small country that declared the war to Germany for his Nazi regime.

This museum is well worth it and full of facts, stories, and artifacts.  If you want to see the permanent exhibit, make sure to get there early (or pre book online) to get your free tickets.  They are on a first come, first serve basis and are needed in order to see the main portion of the exhibits.  Not light material but it really gives you a true insight of impact of the Holocaust.  It would be rated 5 stars if we didn't have to wait in line early for the tickets, but understand the need for it as it was very busy.
Three boys, 14, 12 and 9. We did the Children's section and was plenty. The boys enjoyed it and learned from it. I do not know about the rest of the museum. There was no line and it is free.

Took me about 3 hours to go through the museum. Be sure to see the two 14 minute films. One is on how Germany became controlled by Hitler and the second is a review of anti-semitism since the crucifixion of Jesus Christ.

The pictures and artifacts made me very sad. But you must go, an experience not to be missed.

Well made if you like and enjoy history. This is not interactive. More like watching videos and few displays.
 Not for recommended for kids.

This museum was very informative about the tragedy that is the holocaust. I learned many things that I never knew before. You need more time than you think to truly expemrience this fantastic exhibit.

I liked it but it wasn't really exciting to see.  I absolutely love History but this just bored me.  There was too many people in the exhibit all at once.  I am short and all the tall people stand in front of the shorter people and so they can't see.
Not for young children. Be prepared to be shocked and saddened. Almost always long lines to get a tour.

The Holocaust museum in D.C. is a must-see museum for D.C. area residents and tourists alike.

One of the most memorable displays is a collection of victims' shoes amassed before and after people were exterminated.

This museum is a tribute to the millions of lives lost and the preservation of their memory should never be forgotten.

Went here many many years ago and enjoyed the visit.  Tried to go back today, Saturday in November without timed entries.   Awful awful.  Too too many people all at once.  Couldn't see anything, and couldn't move due to the crowds.  Went back to the elevator and left.

Fantastic museum with a lot of information about German society leading up to Hitler and the eventual Holocaust. Good artifacts and use of videos and photographs to tell the story. Our only complaint is that it was very, very dark and the signs in the exhibits started with an appropriate font size, but then got smaller and smaller, with many labels and information close to the floor--between the poor lighting and small font size, it was difficult to read and created knots of people who were trying to scrunch down to see it all...

Not for young children (under 12).  It was well documented and educational for those not knowing much about the Holocaust...our grandson (12) had taken a bit of this subject in school this and this museum brought everything into perspective for him.

Must See ... Visit ...
Must remember  and do not forget !!!
Show this dark age of the world and the crazy evil Natzi
A very straightforward museum of the Holocaust that try to give the visitor the feel of this ...
Very well organized
Don't miss the basement exhibits

I was expecting more information on the Holocaust, but the tour was good, so it earn some points back.

They also have information on other countries' holocaust (i.e. Cambodia, Africa, ...) as well.

I think everyone should visit this museum just to experience the reality of the holacoust.  We often hear that 11 million people were killed but the monumental tragedy of that genocide in not emotionally registered until you visit this museum
 .

The building the exhibit is housed is is very beautiful and has almost a foreboding aspect to it. The content is dark and a horrible period in time and the architecture reflects that as well.  The facts at times tend to overwhelm you as you go through this exhibit.  The statistics of the number of deaths  (murders) that happened over this period is truly hard to imagine.  The murdering of people because of the religion they were born under or the race they belonged to is so unimaginable.  The senseless murder of children as well is nothing short of disturbing.  Overall the exhibit has a lot of reading and text and young children are not really suited for this.  Overall i really wanted to go here and was glad I went.  If i did have one criticism it would be that there wasn't as many artifacts as I would have expected.  There was alot fo film, text and pictures but I just expected to be more artifacts - maybe it was all destroyed? I'm not 100% sure what i was expecting but outside of shoes, utensils and a few replica's there really wasn't much from the period.

I was really disappointed with the museum. My grandfather fled the Holocaust in 1937, and I was hoping that the museum would have more resources for people to reflect, grieve, process etc. After I went through part of the exhibit, I went to an area created for that, but it was full of a lot of high school students and just felt kind of awkward. In contrast, I went to the The National Museum of African American History and Culture the day before and I got so much spiritual healing from the waterfall room. In general that museum was an extremely better experience, where to learned more and felt much more stirred up by it than the Holocaust museum. 

I wish there had been a place to actually talk to people who work at the museum about my experience, their reason for being there. The exhibition is totally silent, and it was extremely crowded so while they said it takes 1-1.5 hours to go through, I spent 1.5 hours going through maybe 2/3 of the museum. I didn't like that the museum seemed to be on the defense (something like "undeniable" as it's sign on the outside). It wasn't interactive at all. It was 100% history and nothing calling people forward to imagine what it was like, using metaphors. I would have really liked something celebrating Judaism in it, or talking about how to move forward or anything like that. I knew most of the history from having taken a history of the Holocaust class, what I wanted was something to help commemorate and allow me to reflect on the experience. I think what I actually wanted was the museum of tolerance in LA, which I'd heard a lot about before. I think it's good that there is information being provided, I just wish the experience was different.

The museum is top notch but steer clear of the Museum Café!  First, you have to exit the museum property to get to the café, which means standing in a long line AGAIN to go through security. Second, the food at the café was horrendous: bland soup, and a hair in my mushy sandwich. Third, it was ridiculously expensive even compared to other museum food services. $18.50 for a sandwich, soup, and Pepsi. Fourth, there is very little seating room. It must be packed on weekends, and one bus trip would fill it up.  The other alternative is the USDA cafeteria across the street. I haven't tried it but honestly, it can't be much worse. Recommendation: eat before you go to this fine museum.

I had heard that this museum was very moving.  I was not prepared for how emotional it was for me.  I highly recommend visiting this attraction.  We took our teenager and a 3 yr old.  The three year old didnt  understand(which was good) - but our teenager did and it provoked a lot of conversation.  

I would suggest going to the information desk immediately upon arrival so you can get one of the timed tickets for the main attraction.  We didnt realize we had to do that - so we ended up leaving and coming back later with our timed tickets.

This is definitely something not to be missed.

What i truly admired about the holocaust museum is the message it was trying to send. With exhibits spanning multiple genocides it raised more questions than answers. It commanded its audience to internally reflect and look at modern day events. Are we happy with the world in its current situation, could we do more?

I always believe it is important to learn from our past and this is definitely a place for lots of learning

While all Mall museums are open with masks, this one turned us away. The description on TripAdvisor is wrong, tickets ARE required, and they are gone weeks in advance.  They tell you to log in at 7am to get a timed pass for the day, but either the number of slots is tiny or it's a lie, just got up early for nothing.

This is insulting.  I was one of the many small donors who contributed to building this place.  Can't even show it to my daughter.
This is a very moving museum that clearly defines the horror of the Nazi persecution of Jews, Homosexuals, Freemasons, and others.  Plan ahead to obtain timed entry passes!
While this was one of the blackest periods of human history, I did not really get a sense of that while visiting the museum.

I've been to this museum twice. Once 6 years ago, and again yesterday. I thought I'd be prepared, but it made me cry again this time too.  So horrendous. The museum does a fantastic job with the permanent exhibit. To lead you thru the time period leading up to Holocaust, to the liberation of the camps. It's a place I think everyone should visit, so we can make sure it's not forgotten - so history doesn't repeat itself.  I would not recommend this for younger children, however if you do bring them, the museum has the most sensitive videos behind a wall that you have to be tall enough to see over.

Great museum, very sad and tough to read through.  The museum is mostly photos and a lot of reading (much of the material is covered in advanced textbooks).  There is not as much realia as I would have expected, but I assume most of that has been destroyed.  It's not a museum for young children/families...definitely geared to adults.

I knew this museum would be a difficult visit, but felt it was necessary to experience it.  I went alone, which allowed me to walk thru at my own pace and with my own thoughts.  It was also easy to skip sections if time or interests ran out. It is a somber environment overall but very well done.  Good historical facts esp about the world environment leading up to theses events. Lots of personalization, to put faces on what happened to individuals.  Everyone needs to know this history and never forget how the hate and distorted views of one man influenced so many and affected millions.

I remember coming to the museum years ago and there was a lot more stuff to see. More objects, more videos, just more. This felt very pared down and less affective, with lots of text and not so many artifacts.

Went at opening on a Sunday; line out the door.  Have to go through security, like the airport, so that slows down entry.

There is a coat check but no lockers.  My children, 9 and 12, handled it just fine....some of the more graphic photos are behind 4 foot high walls so the 9 year old couldn't see them.  And we moved quicker through some sections than others.

Small bottleneck at the beginning and it's dark, so kind of  claustrophobic.  If you stop to read every single thing, you'd be there 2-3 hours.  

Although mainly about the Jewish people, the museum told the story of the Roma, handicapped, and other groups prosecuted by the Nazis. Lots of military history as well, with troop movements, why the US did not help more, etc.

I don't think anyone would enjoy this museum but it is well done and very powerful.  The lines are long to get in and the museum is crowded at times.  The exhibits are informative and moving.  Not appropriate for kids under 11 or 12.  There is a nice children's exhibit but it only takes about 15 minutes.  The children's tile exhibit downstairs is also worth the visit.  This museum is so important historically but plan for time after to debrief as It is quite emotional.

Get there before the doors open so you don't have to stand in the long line to get through security.  You can also get upstairs before the big crowds do.  It was very crowded while I was there ( Oct. at 10 am) , you could not read anything in the first couple of rooms because of so many people.  Not a lot of things to see, just a lot of reading.  If you are claustrophobic I would not recommend going.

While I would have spent all day in this museum, easily, as there is SO much to see and read and take in, we simply could not because it was so crowded that it was impossible to take your time and see everything.  I understand the concept of making us feel like the prisoners felt, I'm sure, being packed in and "herded" along, but there were a lot of great exhibitions that my teenage children and I would have loved to spend the time to read and watch and see and simply could not.  It is an absolute must-see if you're in Washington DC, but I wish we had been able to go through it at a more leisurely, non-crowded pace.
While it is very informative but somehow the museum layout and format is not conducive for one to just linger on selectively.  It is kind of like a cattle-herding and squeezy experience.

We spent a nice afternoon at the museum. Explain well the increasing power of Hitler and its consequences, as well as the role of Austria in the Holocaust. Nice artefacts and a touching experience. To see.

One of the top sites in DC, but not easy to score tickets for. Due to not knowing months ahead, I was not able to plan our visit early. You can try for same day online tickets beginning at 6:00 AM, despite trying for earlier in the day, I was able to get two tickets beginning at 3:30. Unfortunately, two hours is NOT enough time to take in the permanent exhibition if you want to do more than a cursory examination. We spent one hour on the first floor alone.
If you take middle school and high school aged students,, I advise they have an understanding of what the Holocaust was before coming here. I witnessed a number of students there that didn't seem to care about being there and probably unaware of what happened in WWII. A few history lessons would better prepare them.

Actually, as an explorer, I went there for taking a look what kind of torture and specific information about the Nazi slaughter. For example, I thought I can see some gas rooms, some other materials that used by Nazi but nothing. Everywhere full of videos and some stories about people whom suffered at that time.
I was so sorry when I listened all the stories that Jews told what had happened to them. But I think it s not worth going there, Also dont go there with children.

With all the continued conflict on the Gaza Strip seeing the true stories of the Holocaust of 6 million Jews and endless suffering of millions more really drives home why Israel needed to be established in 1947.

My second visit (first was in 2006), teaches and makes you face up to the atrocitities the NAZI'S committed.

The Holocaust Museum in DC gave me a lot to think about. The experience of visiting this place is sad, as you would expect. For me, the most moving exhibit is the walkway that visitors can walk upon. Surrounding this walkway is a tunnel on which the names of all the towns in just one country are listed. Each of these towns lost most of their Jewish population in the holocaust. It is amazing the number of cities listed. The list  of towns in that one country seems to go on forever. It causes you to really think about the number of people murdered in a new light. 
     It can be pretty crowded  so prepare to be patient if you want to experience all of it. We visited during the end of the day, around 4pm. It was extremely busy. Morning time would probably be much less crowded.  
     Be sure to read Elie Wiesel's famous quote, which is written on a wall inside the museum. He has written many books on his surviving Auschwitz. He sums up his experience in one sentence. 
     The Holocaust museum in Washington DC offers a moving experience  for those who want to try to understand this time in our history a little better. And, it is free. You will definitely feel something inside your heart when visiting here. It is a strange feeling when you leave this museum and go out into what feels like the real world again.

I have only 3 words - Go See It.  Although the museum's content was a little depressing (not the museum's fault), I liked the way the historical material was presented. It does not just show historical facts, but it encourages viewers to take note of what ignoring wrong-doing can lead to. It certainly is a walk into the past with various personal items on display from victims and victimisers alike.

This museum is phenomenal. We have toured Sachsenhausen and I did not think a museum in America would be as powerful. I was wrong.

Obviously, this is not something to do when you are in the mood for a light or playful experience. The exhibits are gut-wrenching, moving, and heavy. But incredibly well put together and delivered, top-notch. It is certainly a must-see on a trip to DC, just be prepared for a powerful experience.

I agree with the previous comments about the first floor of the permanent exhibit (which is the top floor) being the most crowded and most difficult to navigate. It makes the beginning of the experience more frustrating than anything because there is A LOT to read. It thins out though as you go, so don't let the beginning frustrate you too much!

I found the two story gallery of photos from the one single town that was esentially eliminated by the Nazis at first unimpressive. Take the time to look at the photographs as it is easy to  pass through this tower twice without feeling its impact. The one that got to me was of a young boy, as we were traveling with our own little guy. My wife and I both fought back tears as we saw that photo and were instantly able to identify with that one single loss amid the museum that beautifully remembers the 6 million plus losses.

A true national treasure - again, free. Make sure if you plan to visit during peak season that you get the a timed entry pass. We were in November so we didn't need an entry time.

I'd heard how moving this museum is, but it was so crowded when we went, we didn't get the vibe.  Waking up at 6am to get tickets online maybe raised our expectations unrealistically.  It wasn't presented very interestingly, the kids wanted to leave as soon as we got t here.  I wish I'd gone alone on a day with time to absorb it more.  Not good on limited time.  The World War 1 Museum in KC is done so much more interestingly, I  guess it raised the bar for me.  For adults with more time on a slow day - I'm sure it's great.

It had a lot of cool things to see but we didn't end up seeing half of it because the design of the museum was so terrible. Staff weren't really honoring timed ticket and people with 114t tickets were getting in at 1030. As a result there was a huge line getting into the museum and took us about 20 min just getting in the museum. Once inside the museum it was only a one way direction so everyone was jam packed into the same starting point which is a dimly lit room with narrow hallways. It was so packed with bodies that we couldn't even skip ahead to leave the exhibit. It had a lot of great exhibit but the design of the museum was so terrible it was not an enjoyable experience. The line was significantly shorter as we were leaving so if you do this museum do not go first thing in the morning.

This museum is one of the most well documented, and important attractions in the whole area. It calls on everyone to remember a dark period in history so that we can recognize that these atrocities to mankind did occur. Unfortunately, it must also serve as a reminder that even today ,society too easily can be tempted to avoid the "harsh realities " of injustice to others that we feel do not affect our personal realm , as did many nations so long ago in distancing themselves from the horror of Nazi politics. This museum is a history lesson for all so that we "NEVER FORGET." It is not to be missed!

First of all, I do not think this museum is necessarily a bad attraction to visit. Some people probably will gain alot from going, so let me go over the positive aspects/features of it before I say anything else. First of all, it is free. Therefore, you do not have to worry about wasting your money (except maybe on transportation to and from the museums location). It is in a nice locattion, clean, and easy to find your way around (walking through the Washington D.C. museum of natural history by contrast can feel a bit like walking through a maze). In addition, it brings awareness to genocide, and the attrocities comitted against European jews, gypsies, homosexuals, etc. during the second world war. It also has a small book shop inside where you can buy a good gift/souvenir. That said, I do not feel I learned anything new, and I feel I have been to better, more informative, less crowded, and less politically biased holocaust museums in the past. 

To see much that the museum has to offer, you have to wait in a long line, for a long time, and there is little to entertain yourself while waiting. The people are not overly helpful about explaining what you need to do in order to experience that part of the museum, or explaining what it is. Although perhaps it is just the times I went, it also seems to be quite a bit more crowded than most of the other museums in D.C. 

The last complaint I have is about the section on others cases of genocide. It just so happens that in this particular section all the cases of genocide that the museum tells visitors about were committed  by governments that the U.S. government was not enmeshed with on a friendlly basis, or if it was, the fact was never widely known by U.S. citizens until after they relations changed. For example, the museum tells visitors about the plight of the Bosnians, Hutu/Tutsis, South Sudanese, and Vietnamese (the later who were of course killed by the evil communists, never our military officials) It never mentioned the genocide of Native Americans committed by European Americans in the U.S. and others parts of the Americas (which has continued up into the 21st century in some parts of the Americas, by the way). It does not mention the plight of people from East Timor, or other parts of the world where the U.S. supported the governments involved in killing so many innoccent people. It does mention the attacks on the Kurds. It just conveniently left out the information about how the U.S. gave Saddam the weapons to carry out his vicious acts. 

This review is not an attack on all components of the U.S. government or military officials. (My dad worked for the government and that is why I grew up in D.C. I do not feel he is especially evil or corrupt) I know there are many cases when the U.S. government has helped people from the U.S. and other places. But I feel that if you really want to stop future genocide, we must learn from history, stop white washinng our crimes, and take a hard look at our mistakes rather than inserting subtle propaganda into certain parts of the holocaust museum just because it happens to be located in the nation´s capital or whatever. Also, apart from being biased in favor of historical U.S government policies, I I would like to argue that it is biased in terms of western viewpoint. It does not for example talk about all the attrocities the Japanese committed against various groups from Asian ancestry during world war II, in part because white people who visit the museum cannot empathize with people from color. This kind of attitude is ironically what allowed the killing of Jewish people and others to occur in the first place. I much prefer to not see any information about other cases of genocide apart from minorities in Europe during world war II rather than be spoon fed whatever happens to be the politically correct view of 21st century war/conflict as a whole.

In conclusión, it is a place I might one day take a young child (it is not overly frightening for kids which is another nice feature), my friend from Iran, or a friend from somewhere else in the world where people often do not know much about the persecuation of Jewish people during worrld war II. But I think the average, educated adult from the U.S would find the museum a bit boring, not terrible, but nothing special either.
If you don't have tickets beforehand and don't want to wait in line for the permanence exhibit, there's a cart next to the entrance handing out timed tickets to visit at a later time of the day.

When we arrived at the museum about two hours before closing, we were told that all passes were gone for the day. Apparently, they only give out a certain number of passes each morning, and once they're gone, you can't see the main exhibits. You can see a couple of exhibits, but they're not very good. For a museum that's supposed to be about spreading knowledge, it sure limits access.

I requested and paid for tickets prior to our visit.  This was on the top of list of "to do's" per my daughter.  Very, very, very crowded.  This is one of the museums that I think they never stop handing out tickets.  Very few artifacts.  Mostly, pictures with long descriptions.  Due to crowds, a lot is missed.

What I saw was very thought provoking. Unfortunately I chose a time when there were a lot of school groups. I would have liked it to be a little quieter because of the subject matter. The Hall of Remembrance was very tastefully done.
This museum allows you to take the journey from normal citizen to member of a concentration camp. It really sheds lights on the perils of life during the Holocaust.

Simply one of the best museums of my life. It is unbelievable that during most of the visit everyone is quiet, every corner there is  something that surprises you and make you think about everything that happened. A place to go for sure.

My boyfriend and I went to the Holocaust Museum on a Sunday. The overall design of the Museum, inside and outside, was not impressive. There was a lot of reading, a lot, and they also had tv screens every once in awhile that were redundant of the reading. The layout of the Museum is by chronological order and you wind your way up the floors going through each part of the war. They had some artifacts and one miniture model (unpainted - plain white). I felt that overall it was to technical in it's description of the Holocaust and not very emotional. Bottle necks were being created constantly by people standing and reading what was on the walls. We spent 4 to 5 hours there. By the time we got to the last floor I was skipping some parts because my eyes got tired of reading. I thought to myself I could just get a book out of the library and read all of this without feeling crowded or rushed. I would not recommend this museum on your trip.

The Holocaust Museum is very well done. It took about 3 hours for us to get all the way through it. Get tickets online ahead of time for a time slot. They have a cafe but we did not stop. They also have a gift shop.
this Museum is very moving, and pays honor to all who lost. very good for a afternoon visit.

They have a great kids exhibit that I would really recommend for kids, however past that this museum was very quiet which made it difficult to be able to explain and talk to the kids about what was going on with the rest of the exhibits. Its not like the rest of the museums. It has a completely different tone here. If you want tickets for the ticketed part, come early or they will sell out. We are military so we got to go to the front desk for military tickets. Yes, you don't have to wait in line if you are military. Just go to the visitor desk and they have them for you. However, that part is really not for kids (we have an 11 year old and 14 year old and it was too much for them). We will go back without them or when they are older.
What worth a visit; though obviously the content is harrowing. Everyone should go there to try to make sure such hate & dreadful events never happen again.

A lot of information on the permanent exhibit side (get your passes early; we booked online before we arrived in DC).  It takes a while to get thru, and at times is difficult with the crowd.  The entire history from the end of WWI to today's genoside outbreaks is covered. 
      On the open side, "Daniel's Story" is well done for educating younger children.  I wouldn't recommend taking younger kids into the permanent exhibit.

Wanted to put in a quick tip.  There are two security checkpoints on opposite sides of the building.  The lines are not usually the same length due to tour groups that show up and fill the line on one side.  If your line seems long, send a traveling companion around the building to see if the other line is shorter.  The delay is not admission, it is the metal dedectors, so either door is just as good.

The museum is an attempt to let you absorb a huge tragedy in a short period of time.  Its not overly graphic, which it could have been, so its ok for younger teens.  That being said, the museum is full of long signs of reading.  Along the way, you are given the opportunity to watch a half a dozen or so 10 to 15 minute videos.  Both of these quirks cause the museum to be constantly crowded as hoards of people cram into the video room, and then all come out and start back reading each and every sign.  I'm not talking about small signs that say "Camera from wherever, 1942" I'm talking about 10 paragraph signs that take 2 to 3 minutes to read every 5 or 10 feet!  Which means you spend the entire time (its a four floor museum) shuffling along in a herd.  Don't try and see this museum with a short time window.

I thought the museum was interesting, but I was disappointed with a lot of it. First off, when you get there the guards are rude. Then you have to spend a good amount of time figuring out where to go because there are few signs and the signs that are there are small and easy to miss. The first thing I did was go into the Daniel's story part, it was so crowded and loud that I couldn't see or read anything. Then I went to eat at the museum cafe which is WAY over priced. My family spent $24 on a mini pizza, banana, salad and two bottles of water. Then we went to the permanent museum part and basically I thought there was WAY too much reading, not near enough seating for a ridiculously long exhibit, and it was top crowded to read everything. People were constantly running into eachother, there's no space at all. By the time we got to the end, my legs hurt from walking/standing for three hours, and I was hungry from not eating enough at lunch due to ridiculous prices. If you don't mind standing a lot, reading a lot, and being pushed around for hours then this is for you. And I also thought it was a bit boring because its more of a memorial than a museum. I most likely won't ever come back.

We visited the museum with our 12-year old because she is learning about the holocaust in school and had been considering going there anyway when she was old enough. As many reviewers have already stated, you need to immediately go and get a ticket for timed entry, the wait can be anywhere from 1-? hours (ours was only an hour). While waiting to get into the main area of the museum there are other areas to explore, including a very interesting interactive exhibit called "Daniel's Story". This was completely age-appropriate, it was my daughter's favorite part of the whole museum. The main museum is quite long and goes into a lot of detail about the build-up to the war and the slowly growing segregation of the Jews and Romas by Hitler's regime. There are many tragic and moving images and stories, as well as some wonderful stories about the people who risked their lives to shelter and save the persecuted groups. 

Overall a very moving experience, a time to pause and reflect on humanity and one of our darkest times. In the midst of those images of suffering and loss who could help but ask: If I had been there would I have had the courage to stand up and fight for what is right? Let us all hope we never have to find out.

This museum is very organized, and as expected, is nearly numbing with its overload or horror. Attempts are made to put visitors in the shoes of victims (to a very, very small degree) by giving out passports of actual victims, and beginning with stepping through a cattle car replica. Photos, videos, text, thorough and in depth, are well-organized by theme.

How can I give a rave review to anything displaying man's disgusting penchant for power and prejudice?  It attacks the devastating events directly.  I visited Dachau in September 1972, and the images displayed here [including the ovens] stirred my memories of that horrid place.  Definitely not a feel good museum; more of If you want to feel good, purge the proclivity for prejudice and hatred from your soul and mind.  They say museum can be viewed in 2 hours.  Didn't work for us---need to go back.  Take your time to read all the info and remember the past and eye the horizon of the future

Im not what I was expecting as Ive never visited a museum dealing with the Holocaust, but I did not find the experience reflective or moving. I love history and have visited museums dealing with emotional topics and left feeling contemplative. At this museum I was ready to move on through. Maybe it was because of the hundreds of 8th grade students visiting.

Very moving museum, but the staff was nothing but rude.  Each one we encountered acted like they were doing us a favor and couldnt be bothered. First was security, my daughter had a sip left in her drink bottle, I didnt realize she still had it as I knew no beverages allowed inside. However, they made her go back outside in the rain to either dispose of it, or take the last sip. She then had to wait in the line again and go back through security. 
The man scanning tickets was also rude. When he couldnt figure out how to swipe the e tix he started saying my phone was acting up. I offered to swipe the tickets and he was rude about the process. Later on a guardwas standing in front of the entrance to the bathrooms. My wife asked if it was ok to use them. He could have used words, but instead stepped aside just enough for her to pass and gave her a filthy look.
Upon exiting my son took a complete revolution through the circular doors. A woman standing out front on her break I assume said in a nasty voice  security will snatch you if they see you do that again he museum itself was very touching, but the people working there could sure use training on customer service. It was almost as if they were trying to act as prison guards.
A must for all travellers to Washington.  A sobering experience and  the museum was well organised and informative.

It's very interesting to learn more about the terrible things that happened during WWII, the museum is huge, it's a 4 floor building, you start on the 4th floor and go all the way down. We spent more than an hour on the 4th floor because we stopped at every part of the exhibition, so it was exhausting, I would recommend to skip some of the banners and displays.

I visited the museum with a large group of friends/co-workers in May. The museum is a must stop when visiting DC. Unfortunately, with such a large group it was difficult to see everything the museum had to offer. When visiting plan on spending at least 3-4 hours on site. The displays are educational and emotional, especially the photos. The shoe display will stay with me for a very long time.

The Daniel story was wonderful way to start the tour if you have children with you or if you don't.  It offers lots of information, it you want to take your time allow the whole day to take this musuem in.

Sept was a good month to not have to wait, we got right into the musuem unlike the trip in June where we wouldn't have gotten in except for son and daughter in law having been in the military.

We had the good fortune to leave the musuem just before the Pope's motor cade went by so were able to spot Him waving.

This was a highly emotionally charged, incredible museum. It was a must see, one of the very best on the mall. The way one views the museum is a most unique experience, told in the first person, in part, making one feel the outrageousness of the Holocaust and the powerlessness of those who confront those kinds of events. It was the Jews of WWII, but it could be refugees in the Middle East today, the Armenians of WWI in Turkey, slaves in the Roman salt mines, blacks in the deep South, etc. Of course, the scope of the horror was vastly worse for the Jews, but the human experience is universal. It is a human experience which every intellect, every humane spirit must feel to understand this dark corner of humanity. It completes the soul with an experience we were only too lucky to be able to play-act rather than to experience first hand...

We had no idea how extremely busy the museum would be. We should have thought it through but it was excessively packed. We couldn't really enjoy and try to read all of the exhibits because there were too many people and it was moving too slow. 

I have been to the holocaust museums in Dallas and in Berlin. This one was heads and heels above those. The museum itself was very well done but they need to limit the patrons they allow in at a time.

This museum really makes you stop and think about the horrible things we as selfish beings have done.  This museum is a very powerful place.

A visit here does, however, require some planning.  Definitely get online and get passes to the permanent exhibit.  We just walked up in the afternoon, but they were already gone.  I wish we could have seen the exhibit because their brochures made it look very interesting.

Definitely a recommendation for anyone in D.C.

It's too big, too much, and to much sadness. I guess you have to have the proper mood to be in there and be prepared to monitor many sad things. I didn't see all, I got frustrated and depressed and all I wanted was to be over. It's very informative, and you learn a lot, and you feel a lot, but it's too much going on there. I knew somebody who escaped from a camp (she died long time ago) and I would remember her every time. I hit to much to my feelings. Go, but be prepared to feel this.

I had an unfortunate acquaintance with the front door security. The security guard was taking too long with my hubby. I took a picture of them, because it was too depressing, when one guard forced me to delete the pic off my camera phone. What's with all the security? I thought I was at an airport! It was not worth the fuss. Nothing special about the museum. The museum itself was too depressing, certainly not worth the visit unless you are doing a school assignment. We were on holiday, for goodness' sake! This visit pretty much ruined our day!

Enjoyed the museum except for a few spots which was really crowded and caused a bottle neck. The children's exhibition "Remember the Children: Daniels Story" was especially charming and well-designed. It was very interactive even for adult visitors.
I liked t Daniel's story this is very well done and something children can understand. The exhibits really told the story and sadness

We didn't actually get to see this as we were not aware you needed to get tickets for it.  They are free but they go very quickly.  We were able to go into the other exhibition which was the story of a little boy in the Holocaust but we didn't go in as you were not allowed to take drinks in and as it was such a scorching day we didn't want to be without a drink.  I'm sure it is worth going to see and we shall definitely be going the next time we visit.

I visited the Holocaust Museum this past weekend. It shook me to the core. After picking up our time stamped tickets to the main exhibition, we toured Daniel's exhibit. This is a good way to introduce children to the Holocaust. It begins by touring his family home which was comfortable for the times. As you walk through you experience how his life changed so radically from being relocated, first to a ghetto and then a concentration camp. This exhibit was very crowded. 

We then toured a special exhibit downstairs that showed the sociology of neighbors, friends and even family members turning on each other during this time. This is something I have always wondered about. Just when you start thinking, "Can humanity be anymore cruel?" There are stories of those that didn't turn away and helped their fellow men and women in both large and small ways.

It was then time to tour the main exhibit. I can't stress enough to get your admittance ticket to this as soon as you arrive, as once the tickets have been given out they are gone. We spent two hours touring the main exhibit. We could have spent five. There is so much to take in. I plan to go back and finish my tour.

The Holocaust Memorial in DC is a must-see for anyone touring our Nation's capitol .

JMJ  If you want to see a documented account of only one of the examples of man's inhumanity to man then take some time out for this museum.  It is well done for such a subject and makes you want to find out more about Europe during the terrible World Wars and which countries and groups are being persecuted now.

The United States Holocaust Memorial Museum is among the most popular attractions in Washington, DC.  Because of this, I definitely recommend visiting in the fall when crowd are smaller and tickets arent required, but be sure to arrive around opening. We arrived later than expected because of limited metro service due to track work, so there was a bit of a wait to get through security and enter the museum.  With that being said, be sure to leave any prohibited items at home, but feel free to bring your camera!

This museum is handicap accessible, so no need to worry if youre traveling with a loved one with limited mobility.   The city of Washington, DC is definitely pedestrian friendly, but parking is very limited in the area near the National Mall.  Also, there is a short walk from the metro stations to the museum, so take this information into consideration when deciding how to travel around town with any handicapped members of your party.

At any rate, once youre inside the museum, youll be given a map and a small, paper passport.  The passport tells the story of a holocaust victim and is meant to be companion to the exhibit.  I actually visited the museum twice in the fall (once in September and again in November), and each time, I got a different passport both with very different outcomes to the stories printed inside.  With that being said, you may see artifacts that might make you emotionalthere is, of course, the very well-known shoe exhibit of shoes belonging to holocaust victims, artwork done by children of the holocaust, and descriptions of some of the experiments conducted on Jewish victims.  I saw some tearful guests during my visits, so Id definitely recommend traveling with handkerchiefs or paper tissues if you think youll need them.  Once youve made your way through all of the exhibits, you can visit the Hall of Remembrance where you can sit and reflect on the things you learned during your visit, pray if youre so inclined, or light a candle to remember.  If nothing else, I definitely recommend at least taking a quiet stroll around the hall before you go.  

When making your itinerary, plan to spend between 2-3 hours at this museum.  Its not so large that youll need to spend an entire day like other Smithsonian museums, and there will certainly be time to visit other attractions in Washington, DC if you visit early.  They do have a gift shop where you can buy magnets, mugs, shirts, books, and even mezuzahs for yourself or someone special.  They also have a café located just outside of the museum where you can order some traditional Jewish fare or American fare if you prefer.  (I, personally, tried a knish on my first visitoverall, pretty tasty and a bit like the potato pasty I had in England and on my second, I just opted for a few cookies given that I had plans to eat elsewhere in the city that afternoon.)  Note that the café has its own security checkpoint for your safety and that you cannot consume outside food on the premises.  

So who should visit?  Certainly those who love history, love museums, and love Jewish history. But really, this is a great museum for anyoneits a great place to teach children (who are old enough to understand) about the Holocaust, and its a great place for adults of all ages to be reminded about world history and the actions that led to this atrocity in hopes of not allowing history to repeat itself.
Been multiple times. Always moving and good history reminder. Even those unfamiliar with history will enjoy this museum.

My husband and I were in DC for a couple days and while he was in a conference I decided to head out to see some museums. This one had been on the top of my list for a while so I was glad to get to see it. I did like the fact that they handed you a passport as you walked in and as you went through you learned more about that person, but I do think there could have been a bit information or at least something else at the end that helped draw that together.
The exhibits were extremely interesting and I was amazed at some of the actual items that were there. The shoe room seems to be a favorite from everyone I've heard from. 
My only issue with the museum was there were lots of empty nooks and crannies and corners and stairwells leading to empty rooms. Lots of bare walls and empty spaces. I'm not sure if it's supposed to be this way as a spot for meditation or if it's waiting for exhibits in the future or what it is but at times I just felt like I was wandering the halls. 
The hall of remembrance was gorgeous and I feel they did an excellent job on that.

Having visited other Holocaust museums around the world, I just hadn't made time for this one.  I knew I'd be glad I did---and I am!  It does provide an interesting perspective.  Spent two hours there with the plan to go again. There's a lot to see and absorb.

Of course, the subject is super interesting, and this is why we went.  But there were so many people, we couldn't see the exhibits peacefully.  We had to sneak in between people to be able to see, and we just went through some places as there were way too many people around for us to be able to see anything.  And this is despite having booked the tickets ahead of time!  They just let way too many people in at the same time!  Real shame!

I was really looking forward to this museum given my knowledge and interest of WWII.  Could not enter the main exhibit as all tickets were sold out unless you were military or firefighters etc, not really sure that aligns with everyone should be treated equally ethos of the exhibits.  That said, the WWII and Khamer Rouge exhibits were good and quite moving.  I learned a lot about Cambodia.  What I saw was good but there could have been more but perhaps that is in main exhibit which I could not get into to.  They shouldn't make it exclusive.

This museum captures every possible facet imaginable regarding the Holocaust and its victims.  
Visitors need to purchase tickets at a very small fee.  Each ticket has a window of time that the ticket holder is allowed to enter.
A multitude of interactive displays and multimedia exhibits effectively capture each visitor's attention.  Whether it is being inside a replica of the boxcars used to transport captives, or watching movie testimonials of survivors, you cannot leave without being moved by the whole experience.
However, it was very difficult to see and appreciate all the displays because of the incredible crowds and narrow passageways within the museum.  After checking in, we were sent to an upper level via elevator. Upon exiting the elevator we faced a huge mass of people reading and watching the incredible amount of information displayed in the immediate area.  Because there was so much information on display plus the hallways being very narrow, the crowd was at a standstill throughout thereby making it very difficult to pass through.  At times I wondered if the sheer number of people in a small area were a fire hazard.
Sadly, we had to pass on many exhibits because we couldn't see or get close enough from those who chose to stand pressed up against the glass enclosures protecting the displays.
Perhaps a solution would be to:  1) not book as many people for each window of timed entry and/or 2) redesign the arrangement of the displays to reduce crowding.
All that being said, this is a world-class museum and because of the subject matter, is very much worth seeing if you don't mind crowds.

The exhibits here show a very horrible list of events.  Hopefully the world has learned many lessons from this period of time.  Some of the photos and videos are not really suitable for children.   Many of the adults we saw had tears in their eyes.  It is a very moving experience.
great inf about this topic with real life people to discuss this.  moving and horrid at the same time
Very moving, disturbing and difficult to accept this part of history. Get there early as crowds get big later in the day. The last exhibit is unbelievable. A must see for everyone.

I wouldn't recommend for small children. For older children I would prepare them in advance. There are numerous moving stories on display. The museum walks you through the timeline of what was expierence dim the holocaust. It can be hard to get tickets at peak times to plan ahead. I had to hurry myself along because it became a lot to take in one trip.

I would recommend everyone to go at least once.  Although it was impossible to see and read everything due to the crowds and time constraints, it was still very sobering.  

It is hard to express in words the feelings some of the exhibits bring up. It is a sad commentary on the ruthlessness man can impose on a fellow human being. I appreciate the museum put the more graphic videos in areas that you had to look over a wall to see; I know many people feel this isn't for children, but it is an important history lesson we all can learn from.

This museum is a must see. The museum is very moving and really puts things into perspective. Seeing all of the shoes of the victims was heartbreaking. You can book tickets online for $1 each which is advisable as when we got to the museum they were not offering more tickets that day. The only problem is that they give you a time frame to enter the museum to cut down on crowds but they let way too many people in at one time. We had a 3:00 entry as did half the city! It was so crowded you had to skip some exhibits. There are interactive video clips but you would have to wait too long to get a turn. I would love to see this museum again at a time when it was not so crowded.

I got there with less than an hour to close but as I'm a quick reader I wasn't bothered by how fast I had to get through it. What did bother me was being downstairs and 15 minutes before close they turned off all the monitors and tv's displaying everything which did ruin the experience. I don't care if you're almost closed and busting to go home - I don't live locally to have the luxury to come back. I felt forced out. What I did manage to see (which was ALMOST everything) was as fantastic as I thought it would be for the horrific event that it is based on. There was an area to light candles for different areas that people were exposed to during the war and after doing a lap of the room and almost burning myself on a tiny left over piece of wood as there wasn't anything left - I lit a candle for the people who endured the Death March and Birkenau-Auschwitz. The ID card section was closed so I asked one of the help desk people for a card and he gave me 2, very nice of him. The gift shop has some great items - some overpriced. Good selection of books though, I bought a few.

Visited the museum by reserving my tickets online for a $1 each ticket to make sure I got them ahead of time. There museum is a long museum you could probably spend 8 hours there. There is a lot of reading in this museum, my favorite part was at the end where you could see real person testimonials on a screen.

Walking into the museum, I was confronted by a security guard who said he needed me to step aside, he waved his wand all around and it beeped by an empty cargo pant pocket. When I opened the pocket, he asked me to turn my back on him and I did so. he grabbed the back of my knee which I wasn't expecting and my leg buckled and I nearly fell over. I was shocked at the contact, there was a line of people watching me and I was embarrassed. I wish I said something to management but didn't. There was less security getting on the plane on my way to Washington.
the Holocaust Museum was emotional, gave insight to a time in our own history where mistakes were made.

Wasn't going to visit museum as have done most in Europe but glad i did.

As with most exhibitions very sad to see what happening to the Jewish people.

Not for younger people some things are very graphic and can be confronting but adds to the experience.

Make sure you pop in for a visit.

This is my second visit after many years.  It is still an amazing experience and you realize we may never learn from the past.  Be prepared to walk in with crowds.  The information is plenty, but the set up could be optimized.  There are so many people it is hard to read all of the signs and observe all the pictures.  There are areas where it thins out, so be patient after you first get off the elevator.  Lots to take in and I think we spent about 2-3 hours in the main exhibit hall.  I would really advise to leave the younger kids at home as there are many graphic pictures and ideas that they may find disturbing and leave them with nightmares.

My wife and I brought our teenagers to the Holocaust museum and had the opportunity to discuss the atrocities of WW2. The visit certainly helped. The diwn side is that the museum is not very interactive and so it is difficult fir younger kids to remain focused.

Amazing. So worth a visit there. I kept my emotions checked until i walked into the "shoe" room; that's the room of all the rubber shoes that the prisoners left behind when they were executed...it was the single most touching, emotional experience of that visit. I could still smell the somewhat burnt rubber of those shoes....and i'm remembering it now as i'm typing up this review. 
really sad. Be prepared to cry
Museum worth visiting if it's for free and as long as you are interested in theme. Or good to teach children about holocaust history etc.

This is not your ordinary museum visit. This is an experience to reflect upon and to absorb. We spent three hours there and did not see everything. It is overpowering, somber, and very important to see so something like the Holocaust can never happen again. Please take the time to pause, reflect, and process after visiting.

I've been here four times.  It is a very well executed memorial and documentary of the WWII Nazi Jewish Holocaust.  It appears that all aspects are covered in detail.  It is simultaneously sad, angering, disgusting, revolting, just about every negative emotion you can have.  The most poignant part for me was the shoes.  It really got to me.

It can be a bit crowded in some spaces.  Perhaps that was intentional but I doubt it.  If it was intentional, bad idea.

These displays on the holocaust are so important that the world doesn't forget what happened.  At the risk of sounding unfeeling, I would suggest you don't visit this museum too often in too short of a time as it could desensitize you.
The museum is breathtaking. They do a good job informing their guests. I like their aporach with the children's display as well.
Took our children ages 9 and 11 here.  Moving. Thought provoking. Educational for kids and adults.  Not to be missed.

This museum is a must see for anyone interested in the Holocaust. The exhibits and videos were very informative, and the entire museum was very well laid out. It really makes you think and tries to put you in the position of individuals who experienced the Holocaust. 

I would recommend going earlier in the day, so you have more time to move through the exhibits. I came later in the afternoon, and they were almost out of tickets to the permanent exhibit, and I also had to rush through the end of the exhibit. Overall, very worthwhile.

I went into the children's version of Daniel's Story, which helped me understand about a Jewish person's daily life during the Holocaust. I also visited the Hall of Remembrance, during the week of Remembrance, where they were reading every single person's name who died and lighting candles for them. The bathroom's were pretty clean and the security check was quick.

We visited whilst in holiday over the Christmas period.

It was very interesting and educational however, there is alot of reading to really understand the exhibits and as the museum was busy, it made it difficult to really take it all in.

As the museum is laid out by floors and visitors are directed to view exhibits in a set order, bottle necks occur and some areas of the museum were near on impossible to really view properly.

This is not a museum for young children, in my opinion, but anyone from the age of 12 upwards I'm sure would find it very interesting.

Having said all this, it is definitely worth a visit and it is free, however it may be worth considering your visiting times if you really want to get the most out of it.

Museum architecture reflects the subject.  Hard to fathom the magnitude of the holocaust but this museum does an excellent job of showing it.  The final walk through a narrow hallway with huge glass encasements on both sides....filled with thousands of pairs of confiscated shoes from the concentration camps is something I will never forget.  Excellent photos, artifacts, a train car used to transport Jews...all add to the experience.

If you are a student of WWII then there's not necessarily a ton of new information, but the holocaust experience is still well presented.  For folks who are not students of WWII history, the museum is an eye-opener.  That said, my party of mostly non-WWII students all said that by the end of the walking tour of the multiple floors that they were becoming somewhat numb to everything.  Very crowded, even with the limited tickets.

Felt an upsurge of emotion flowing out from me that was difficult sometimes. I spent almost three hours in the museum accompanied with really friendly and knowledgeable guides. Its a must visit for all travelers having interest in exploring histories. I myself got to know a lot about WW2 which I probably had never known if hadnt visited Holocaust Memorial. But the most disturbing element was crowd only. The management must do something to control it.

Compared with Holocaust museums in Berlin, Germany and others in Europe, this one was much bigger but lacking in as many authentic artifacts. The ID card booklet of a female or male Holocaust victim that you pick up before entering the scary looking elevator was very moving. In the elevator, the video of an allied soldier entering a camp and then when you get off, the life size picture of a mass grave with decaying bodies was the most impactful for me and I almost started crying just a few steps into the permanent exhibit.  

After that, it is like walking past pages and pages of a history book with blurry photographs and long paragraphs of history text (written in English only) to read the political and historical events as Hitler came into power.  You definitely should go to the museum during non-peak times, since it would be very hard to read on the words and see the pictures if it were crowded with too many people.

It was a good way to review a lot of history you may have forgotten, but my brain was in information overload so when we finally got to the areas showing more pictures of the victims, I was feeling a bit numb. Many items were replicas, such as the three tier bunk bed.  I liked seeing the "Arbeit macht frei" (Work makes free or work will make you free) sign that you pass through to give you the feeling of entering a camp, a train car, and gas chamber door.  There was a small, long scale model of what it looked like for people passing through the area where they had to remove their clothes and leave their belongings, the gas chamber showers, and crematorium.  The big picture of thousands of people's hair that the Nazis had shaved off and collected to make things such as mattress stuffing and hundreds of actual, moldering shoes you walk past were the most moving for me.  Another thing that stood out was a prized red bowl that a woman used for three things - to eat her soup, to use as a toilet at night, and for bathing to give you an idea of the conditions the people endured.

The blurry pictures of starved, skeleton-like victims or beaten or experimented upon people would be inappropriate for young children or sensitive people.  

The museum is about two blocks from the Smithsonian metro exit and has a café outside.  Admission is free.  You will need to wait outside to enter then pass through a metal detector and your bag through an X-ray machine.
Wish I had a week to spend in this museum Spent 3 1/2 hours and it wasn't long enough. Very moving museum..

One of the few stops that you are in the dark about entrance, tickets, tours, etc.
I am sure the exhibit is an amazing experience, but after waiting on line, going through security and entering the museum, then I was told that there were no more tickets available and we could not tour the museum. What? Maybe that should come before you pat me down, search my bags, and waste my time?

This had some haunting items from the war (tons of shoes that survived the concentration camps), but IMO the overall experience was just average.   Dark, narrow hallways with poor lighting made it a crowded experience.   Poor lighting and too many posterboards with unreadable type left a lot to be desired.   Also, everyone is funneled to the top floor initially which gets the whole thing off on the overly crowded, hard to see anything experience.

Bottom line, probably worth doing but keep expectations in check.   Could be and should be a lot better, presentation-wise.

I wanted to love this but I felt like I could have just read a book on the subject and gotten more out of it since I wouldn't be subjected to the waiting.  We went in with about 7 or 8 people on our timed ticket.  But when we got on the third floor and rounded the corner it was pretty packed.  It was a lot of pictures and reading, reading reading.  By the time we got about half way, we basically skimmed the displays.  The atrocities committed seemed to have been watered down.  We watched some of the videos that were semi-hidden, but couldn't get to them all due to the crowds.  It was worth the visit, but I had pretty high expectations and they were definitely not met.
