This museum takes you into the appropriate atmosphere for looking at a difficult past (and present) and make you reflect. Too many museum let you float by greatness ... This one involves you and makes you accountable. Wow.
I was disappointed in the Holocaust Museum.  It was a lot of reading and photos but overall lacked the impact that I had expected.

This was a somber, thought provoking tour that brought home the atrocities committed against Jews, Catholics and others during WWII.  It leaves you with the desire to hope that we will prevent anything like this happening again.  As the tour ended what struck me was the quietness of the people
We started out with this museum. Wish we had waited. It's a great museum but very sobering. Seeing what humans can do to other humans. Scary.
I remember going back in the 5th grade for a field trip, but going back again as an adult, the day passes were sold out at 3 PM so we could only walk through the free parts. It was okay.

Get up early to get tickets and come back later. Sometimes you get lucky and get in without a ticket but either way be flexible to get in. I went to Dachau in Germany and it brought back this chilling feeling of being heart sick. I did witness other people that were not affected at all. So much pain in this museum. Sad face~

We made the mistake of signing up for the 3:30 admission. This is an excellent, important, and content rich museum that takes a lot of time to work your way through -- like the National Civil Rights Museum in Memphis.  Unfortunately, nothing on the museum's website let's you know that it will take a lot of time. We arrived at 3:30, and were only halfway through when the museum closed at 5:30. We left feeling very frustrated, and very angry, given the importance of the subject. If you really want to get the most from this (difficult but critical experience), go early.

Obviously this is not a museum that will leave you feeling upbeat and positive, but in addition to the heavy subject matter, the overall experience of the museum yesterday was very draining. Despite the timed entry on tickets, there were far too many people admitted meaning that it was often difficult to read or view the exhibits unobstructed (particularly as many are at body height or in text so small you really need to get up close), and impossible to take your time and reflect on them in peace.

Content wise, there was not a lot of information here that was new to our group of 4 (in our late 20s/early 30s). The museum might be best for teenagers or those who have never studied World War II and don't know much about the Holocaust. Having said this, the museum did have some exhibits which presented some of the well-known information in a unique and very powerful way.

Compared to other museums, the exhibits tended to be heavy on text and light on physical artifacts. Some of the European museums dedicated to the Holocaust have achieved a better balance between these.
Way too crowded, couldnt really appreciate any of it.  Museum not designed for crowds that it receives.  Fascinating exhibits but couldnt see most of them.  Very disappointed.

My husband and I went through this museum on our own and in hindsight, it probably would have been better to take a tour.  I caught snippets of some of the tour guides which definitely added to the experience.

I was really looking forward to visiting the Holocaust Museum during our visit. While it was rich in history, I was disappointed with the conditions of the museum and exhibits. There was dust everywhere, and the bathrooms were filthy and smelled of urine. It felt like a disservice to those who suffered during this time to have their stories being told in this way.

Get there early, as tickets for the main presentation are gone by 10:00 a.m. Tickets were gone by the time my family and I arrived, so we just did a self guided tour of the other exhibits.  Very nice, but would have liked to see the main presentation.  Limited only by our short stay in D.C.

My husband and 13 yr old daughter went to this museum during our recent DC trip. My daughter had been learning about this and was excited to go. I followed the advice on the website that said it wasn't appropriate for kids under 10 so I took younger kids somewhere else while my husband took my oldest here. My husband agreed it wouldn't have been wise to bring my 7 and 10 yr olds.

My husband is a history teacher so he typically loves this stuff but said he was slightly disappointed at some of the preaching. There were a lot of references to situations going on in different parts of the world today that are somewhat similar to what happened with the holocaust. They were preaching not to let it happen again and stuff like that. Both my husband and daughter felt the displays were good and they were happy they went. My husband was just a little irritated with the preachiness of it. In talking to others this doesn't seem to be the norm. 
I guess I would recommend but it wasn't at the top of our list of favorites.

I almost don't have the words to describe how I felt after my visit.  It is a truly remarkable and thought provoking Museum.  First be aware that tickets are timed, you can get them on line but we were there in a less busy time of the year so we just walked up and got ours, we had to wait because our entry time was for about 1 1/2 hours after we got there but decided to look at the temporary exhibit on Propaganda, we spent an hour in the temporary exhibit it was so educational and it had some many things to look at read and think about.  This exhibit had a cell phone interaction that you texted when you got to certain parts of the exhibit and it asked you questions about what you were seeing, and you texted your answer back a great way to draw  teens into the exhibit, I am not a teen and I did it and enjoyed it.  

The permanent collection is so amazing and the museum is very well set up.  I like that you start at the top of the museum and work your way down.  There is a lot to read, see and absorb.  We had planned on spending 2 or 3 hours but ended up being there 4 1/2 hours and we could have spent more time there.  When I realized we had spent 3 hours on the top two floors we picked up the pace a bit.  I think this is one of the don't miss places in DC.  I would go back in a heart beat.

Was very solemn but it was very crowded on a Friday AM.  They really should have some type of limiting per section to control the flow.  Many interactions, but disappointed with some of the attendees almost pushing someone out of the way.  Plan to spend several hours to really experience it all = starts at the top and walks you through the process of the holocaust.

It was interesting to learn specific information about the holocaust I didnt know before, just didnt like the lack of organization once inside. I felt very claustrophobic and felt like i had to rush through the exhibit.

The memorial is free to enter. Guided tours are offered within and aren't too expensive. The memorial is very extensive but there were times when there were far too many people crowded around the same bits which made it difficult to see properly.

A must see.  Well done but much too crowded which impacts the ability to experience the exhibits.  My understanding is that this is a private museum, so they are perfectly within their rights to present information as they see fit.  However, as an American whose family members fought in WWI, and as a Vet myself, I felt that multiple displays were politically slanted, repeatedly stating that the US Forces didn't do enough to fight and end the war, didn't take action soon enough, should of done more select bombing ...you get the drift.  These exhibits were blaming the Americans who fought and died, of not doing it "correctly".   I just thought it was in very bad taste.
I didn't expect it to have as much as it did.  Very informative as to what took place during that time.  Had diagrams of actual places and events.

As hard as this is to walk thru it is so much a part of history that it is a must see. Probably not suited for younger children. Plan on 2-3 hours to walk thru and read everything. There is no charade for admission.

There's no other way to describe it.  This memorial was probably the saddest place I have ever been in my life, but it was also solemn and beautiful.  I cried a few times, once when walking through the barracks where prisoners slept, once when seeing the door to a gas chamber, and probably a few more times just because it's devastating to see what hatred can do to people.

I would have given it 5 stars, but it was so incredibly crowded, I unfortunately couldn't see everything I wanted to (which of course is not at the fault of the establishment!  I would recommend going earlier in the day during the week possibly).

staff not that friendly but worth the trip, as soon as you get there go and get a ticket so you know what time you are allowed to get into the main rooms, we went to the Propaganda display first , it was very well done, but didnt know to get a ticket so ended up having to wait two hours to go through the rest of it......

I was able to walk up and get into the museum in 45 minutes on a Wednesday afternoon in July.  So much is so valuable.  I was disappointed in the adults who brought their children who lay down on benches or the floor throughout the museum,  don't bring small children.  It teaches disrespect.

This was my second time here, both with 8th grade class trips. My group had a reservation and we arrived in plenty of time. Our guide checked the group in and we waited for about 10-15 minutes past our appointed time. When we finally let in our group was split and put onto an elevator that they really filled FULL. When we got off on the top floor we were unable to move too far away from the elevator. There were far too many people (not even from our group) trying to navigate through a somewhat narrow display area.  It was impossible for anyone to see much. (By the way, there are disturbing photos blown up and visible to everyone so please consider this when judging whether or not to take children. Many of the graphic pictures are hidden by a barrier but one of the 1st pictures is not). We were very disappointed that we couldn't see and it felt very warm in the museum. My small group of 3 students finally got through this area but we encountered other bottle necks throughout the museum. This caused us to have to breeze through many other parts of the museum because we had a set time we had to depart.

The museum is well done and sobering. However, they need to spread out group arrivals more so that everyone can experience what they are trying to preserve.  Upon entering you can pick up a passport of an actual person living at the time of the war. It has a picture and brief history of the person.  The back page tells you what happened to them.
The museum is centrally located near main attractions.

Tickets can be secured in advance, but if those are all gone, go on to the website starting at 6 am on the day you want to go and they release same-day tickets. We went on at 6:30 in the morning and were able to choose from 2 tour times.  Even though tickets are required and rationed, there are a TON of people in there. I don't know how many tickets are given out for each hour but it is obvioulsy a LOT.  Once inside, I felt like all we did was move from board to board reading. It was like walking through a giant text book...but along with the facts they went ahead and added a lot of opinion which was not appreciated.  There were very few exhibits that were anything but boards to read, and many of the few items that were in there were reproductions.  This museum is a huge miss...don't bother going.

My son and I visited this very necessary, memorable, and intense museum on our last day in Washington, D.C.  Neither one of us will ever, nor do we want to, forget some of the intense sights and sounds we experienced here.  This museum should be forever maintained as an active proof and memory of what mankind is capable of producing if unrestrained.  We must be forever vigilant to the atrocities which can be perpetrated on all of us.  The many many lives suffered and lost must be remembered, both out of respect and future preventions.

Not taking away from the significance of this very important museum, we visited in late April when it was supposed to be an off day.  Walking over from lovely hotel we expected to leisurely walk through, study and take in the important messages that this museum attempts to share.

Unfortunately, the crowds and especially the teenage students made it virtually impossible to enjoy the experience.  The rooms were so crowded that standing in the back was useless. Hoards and I say hoards of people were milling about making the experience so unpleasant that we left after 1/2 hour. 

If you go, call ahead and find out from the museum their least busy day.  Additionally find out at what time there are less people.  That might help.

Excellent content - interesting focus of American involvement that I had not seen before (British) 
Not sure about ending with modern day activities as need to end it in a more positive angle (see Yad Cashem in Israel)

Luckily we had purchased timed entry tickets online weeks before going.  When we arrived it was very crowded and inside the main exhibit the crowds were even worse.  Despite the crowds it was still very interesting and I learned a lot.  I would highly recommend going but I would also suggest getting your tickets in advance.

Was told to definitely go through this museum. Man was I glad we did. Unbelievable at the history that was obtained and placed for everyone to see. The stories from the beginning to the end. Couldve spent all day just inside this Museum and learned so much. Pictures, videos, letters and much more. Definitely will try and go back here again. So much we missed because high school children going through. They had all of the phones taken so we couldnt listen to any of the videos.
Did get a lot of pics.

This is a tribute to all the human beings that suffered.  I would recommend citizens of any country go and learn how to see this coming and how to stop it.  Not to take anything away from the documentation of such tragedy, but why would a timed tour be beneficial to anyone wanting to visit the museum.  It was overcrowded and restrictive and left me questioning why the organizers of the museum viewed this as the best plan.  Also, we had to stand outside the museum in line and listen to a docent harshly telling everyone to stay in a single file line for 30 mins apparently because the museum organizers also thought it would be a good plan to have the overly small security check within 10 feet of the entrance to bottle neck the entire entry procedure.

This museum must be amazing, I guess. My partner and I didn't get to see it because you have to get a ticket online, this is only available on the day from 6am and you have to got to the museum before 9:30am otherwise "it gets imposible to enjoy", mentioned by one of the women from the information desk at the museum, where we got told that tickets had run out for the day and it was 1pm!
It makes it really hard for a tourist.
way too crowded and not well organized.  A lot to read.  Several displays are appropriately blocked so young children cannot see (but then also anyone in a wheelchair also cannot see)
This is one of the places which you have to visit within your tour on Washington memorials that shows you part of the American history.

This museum was very interesting and informative but the crowds were large and they let too many people up the elevator at a time so that when you got to the top floor it was so crowded that it had to be a fire hazard because we were packed in like sardines.it was a very moving experience and you couldn't help but want to cry for all the people who suffered thru it.

A true depiction of "man's inhumanity to man". I was traveling with a teenager and we left in a somber mood. The Daniel's Story Exhibit, may an impact on youth as well as adults. Some things are not appropriate for young children, but the museum has arranged many of these displays blocked for children. Be sure to go online and get advance tickets (the museum is free but advance timed tickets make sure you gain admittance)

The Holocaust story is a terrible one, and one that I think we should never forget. This museum does a good job with pictures, stories, and video testimonials of survivors. Unfortunately, there were far too many people allowed in at once that I never once really got to take my time and take it in. I felt rushed the entire time, could never see anything, and just sort of annoyed. Too bad.

I have really mixed feelings about this place. It is a masterpiece of architecture and deals with an evocative and emotional subject in a detailed and masterful way. For students of history it is a must and for the education of children it is essential.
What is a shame is that they let people inside! During our Friday in the holidays visit it was too crowded, too noisy and far too full of screaming kids to get to read or see any of the exhibits up close. The general clamour and screaming of children kind of destroys the atmosphere. Ok you don't want to visit a museum to walk around in a somber mood and feel like slitting your wrists afterwards, but the volume of visitors causes too much disrespect to the subject matter and dare I say it makes you somewhat conditioned to the horrific scenes you are seeing so that you pass them by with a 'oh not another concentration camp' kind of feeling.
Go when it's quiet and allow plenty of time.

I promised myself I would never visit another Holocaust museum because they are so upsetting to me. I still am deeply touched and so saddened by what people can do to other people. And, could it happen again? Actual survivors are there. It is hard to get through this museum without crying.

One can't underestimate the importance of this museum. I was surprised to find that in addition to the main topic, there is a lot of coverage of the Pol Pot era in Cambodia, which I was interested in learning about. Covers savagery of dictators/hate groups throughout the world and reminds us all that we need to speak up and do something to stop hatred and discrimination. We had some trouble navigating through the museum without a map-- be sure to pick one up at the front desk. There is an exhibit for children which we did not see, but probably good for younger visitors who otherwise would either be overcome with sadness and confusion or miss the point altogether as most exhibits require a lot of reading.

The museum is beautiful and humbling to walk through.  However the primary exhibit was too crowded to really relax and enjoy.  You exit the elevator on the second floor and immediately get into a long line like cattle. You're supposed to start at one end and work your way to the other in a long slow moving line. I couldn't give the museum a low review as they lay out the timeline of events in a very moving and thoughtful way.  Visit, you won't be disappointed, but be prepared to wait.
Bring your kids here. There is a lot to read with a lot of pictures. This museum is not for someone looking to expand their knowledge on the holocaust or to see items from that era.

This was not one of my favorite venues and I'm not exactly sure why.  I am a history buff, so have read and seen a great deal on the holocaust.  It wasn't a downer -- it was done so well that one did not get depressed.  I could have listened to the holocaust survivors stories all day  had we had time.  Had I known that was the last thing on the tour, I probably would have gone directly to that.
An incredible and very moving museum.  Must see to never forget the horrors of the Holocaust.  Done with an American perspective

It is well put together, has some actual artifacts from Germany. But nothing that we haven`t seen or studied. I recommend if you are studying it in school and want to see more reality from the books. The message, not to forget about it is very good as well.

As a son to Holocaust survivors parents I appreciate this museum for educating the public relative to the horrors of the Holocaust.  However, it  pales in comparison to the Israeli museum Yad Vashem.
The staff has been helpful when we searched for names of relatives in their archives and did their best to assist as we weren't able to secure the tickets.
I have been there before, about 20 years ago, and will visit again when I find myself in DC.
We were really looking forward to this museum but were a bit disappointed. It is good but mostly reading and looking at pictures. The one in Dallas is better.

This museum probably embodies the most all-encompassing presentation of the Holocaust. It is quiet, as it should be. It is serious, as it should be. What one sees and learn will stick with one for the rest of the day -- it really, truly, makes one think. Not to be squeezed in between other museum visits. Schedule a day for this (get tickets ahead of time), and then leave the rest of the day open...to ponder.

Wonderful Museum, Every one should visit here in DC and educate your children,No one should ever forget,Lots of reading, exhibits, original train carriage that carried people to the Concentration Camps,Like all Holocaust Museums in the World you always come away feeling very emotional,but glad you went and reminded yourself, The building is very dark in places, nice cafe area.Worth a visit.

This museum is sad, but done very well. However, we walked around the first floor for about 20 min thinking that was all there was. We were ready to leave when we realized people were going up in elevators.  We thought what we saw was the museum.  There should be more signage or workers providing instructions to head to the elevators.
Easy to navigate. Excellent exhibits, and people were naturally on their best behavior. Don't let the outside of the museum fool you. It takes a few hours to go through it all
I can't give this a better review, because when we got there, it was HOURS before we could get in to see.  But, next time we visit DC we will try again.

Spent several hours wandering around the exhibits. Amazing how quickly your friends can be made to turn on you by the government. Everyone looking out for their best interest rather than the best for all.  Just as stunning were examples of individuals who risked their own lives to help some Jewish stranger.
Be sure to order your tickets online at 6:30am the day of - they sell out fast (tix are free, but $1 each service charge).

This is a neat museum but maybe not for kids.  It's a lot of reading and walking.  Their is a smaller kid friendly version in the Lobby that is really good. It's called "Daniels Story".  If I were to do it again I may just go for that.
What can you dislike about this place except for the lives loss during this period. If you are into understanding and appreciating history, you must visit here.

I think that the museum is great. It is a very important part of history and every one should see it. But it not a pleasant part of history.
I didn't find the layout of the museum to be conducive to learning because you could only see bits and pieces. They allow too many people in and they bunch up and no one can see any thing. Their is a great deal of reading required and it is very difficult to see with every one standing in front of it. It is almost too much and frustrating to try and read it all. 
We went when you did not need to get a ticket but could stand in line and go in but the linewas still long.
The staff running the museum is very misinformed and very rude. 
We went through part of the museum and my son needed a break to get a drink. So we stopped and tried to return down stairs.  It was very confusing to get back and a guide took us to the elevator and down stairs. 
We asked at the main desk Where to get a drink and the man said go down stairs and out side to the building next door. I asked can we comeback in that way since we have leave and he said yes they will let you back in.  That was a total lie. When we returned from the cafe next door the attendant at the door said no you can't come back into the museum. You have to go back to the front of the museum and stand in line again and wait to get in. He was the rudest person. There was just the three of us and so we had to stand in line for another half hour to go back to the museum.  Then the same man from the back side of the museum showed up and started yelling at everyone to make sure we empty our pockets. He was just nasty. I don't think I would ever return here again.

Visiting the Holocaust Museum was a must-do on this trip to the capital, and it mostly lived up to our expectations.  Be sure to book your tickets online ahead of time.  We got ours at 6:30 that morning and there were only 2 time slots left for the day.
Before going through the museum, we took the guided tour of the special exhibition: "They had Choices", and we found it, and our guide, very thought provoking.  Then we headed to the elevators and rode up to start our tour of the museum.  Our first 1/2 hour in the museum we were in crowds so thick that we could hardly move and certainly couldn't read any of the exhibits.  Also, the air was thick and stifling - not sure if this was on purpose or not.
Even though the museum is limiting the number of people entering, there are still far too many for the space provided.  As we moved further through the museum, the huge crowds definitely abated a bit.  
The museum beautifully and thoughtfully presents a painful time in history.  It is well worth the visit.

I've been to several Holocaust memorials including concentration camps, and Oswiecim. This memorial really doesn't match the ones throughout Europe. It's focus is solely on the plight of the Jewish community - not much about the other 5 million killed during the Holocaust. Combined with a lot of posters to "never let it happen again" - but there have been many atrocities since WWII and some that are going on now, but there is no mention of this, making the sentiment seem somewhat disingenuous. Obviously it provides a lot of insight, and the cinema area they have is very interesting - but if you've been to the ones in Europe I wouldn't worry with it - there are much better things specific to DC that you can visit.

Did not initially plan to visit this place as I generally avoided sad places but a friend strongly recommended that we visit it so we went.
A very stark lesson on man's inhumanity to man.  Especially seeing how the prisoners were unknowingly gassed when they arrived at the prison camps under the guise of taking a shower to the hideous experiments carried out on prison inmates - it was extremely sobering.
But I must say that it was all very tastefully done - visually graphic in some places and less so in others but the point was very clearly put across of the atrocities done to the Jews.
Please go and remember that we must never again allow this to happen!
The memorial is not terrible, on the contrary, it's very good. But, as one can imagine, it's too sad. I didin't see everything because I was really getting depressed in there.

My previous trip 15 years ago was very moving. However, it has changed and now has fewer displays and more reading displays that are too difficult to see due to crowded, narrow rooms. (and we went during the off season.) It's an important part of history that could be better represented.

The crowds of people were everywhere in this museum but we were able to see many exhibits as long as we kept moving.  We were not part of a tour and were not always able to read the information in each exhibit area.  With this said what we experienced was mournfully sad and brought the holocaust to us in a real, factual way.  Many photo exhibits of families, children and clothing was very hard to look at.  The boxcars, fencing, piles of shoes, and dioramas of  the gas chambers were profoundly disturbing as well.  We were not uplifted by seeing the museum but it was very important for us to see what was part of our world history.  The museum also tied together "loose ends" of things I'd read but didn't realize regarding the many, many concentration camps in so many European countries.
I would absolutely not bring young children to this museum.  As we saw people crying after viewing certain graphic photos it could be upsetting for little ones to understand why.

Visited here late afternoon on 8 April. Its very crowded with lots of schoolkids and associated noise, which prevents a proper appreciation of the place and its significance.

To get into the main exhibition you need a timed ticket, so watch out for the signs at the entrance saying whether these are still available or not, they often are not. Being a singleton, a kindly smile and my English accent got me a one-off ticket from the 'Government/Military' allocation (yes I did once work for government).

The museum content is OK, I particularly liked the temporary exhibition on at the time, though there are few actual exhibits, its mainly a storyboard and photos narrative. The most moving exhibit is the shoes display. 

The worst thing about the museum is the rude and arrogant security. I don't appreciate being bawled at by some over-zealous individual carrying a gun and wearing a uniform with a big badge and being told to 'back off' and 'I said lift your arms', when I had done both - being a 'senior' I may be a bit slow-witted, but that's no excuse for rudeness. And don't proffer the excuse that this guy was 'only doing his job' - there are ways of doing the job without sargeant-major/nazi style behaviour, if you want an example, go and look at some of the larger UK airports. The management of this place needs to take a close look at this, it has been referred to by other reviewers.

A must see to truly understand the history of the WW II. It was moving and respectfully done for the people to experience one of the greatest tragedies of modern time. It is our responsibility to bear witness
We walked around this for about 15 minutes before it closed.  Seemed to be just like other museums.  It's very thought provoking that this is still going on today in this world.
Not easy to see, but important and it will move you. If we can't act on it, to never again, at the least, Never Forget.

Contact the museum months in advance for admission tickets.  Be sure to get your little passport when you get into the museum. I still have all of mine and treasure them. Once the elevator door closes and the audio begins if you are not a stone cold person it will change a part of you forever. Be patient as there are many rude tourists so don't be afraid to step back on their foot. It is a museum that you need to take your tim, don't pass by a thing. There is a photo in there of a German holding a dog and the dog is looking right at you. It has stuck in my mind. The artifacts, prisoner uniform, the bowls, the pictures drawn by the little children. The whole experience is a mind changer. Much for me since I just in the last few years learned I have a jewish line and people in the camps. Please make the plans and admission reservations to go. A trip to Washington is not complete without goig there. Please
Excellent job. I spent approximately 2 hours here and absolutely could have stayed longer. Very heart wrenching.

Moving, but not real. It's a museum, so this is about something, not a site where anything happened. When visiting holocaust sites in Europe the experience is powerful, whereas this left us a little empty, as if going through the motions. Perhaps we'd feel differently on a return visit.
Its powerful. Everyone should experience this at least once.

The displays were very informative, the only complaint I have is that it was very crowded.  They packed us in and it was very hard to see the displays, we ended up skipping several just to avoid the crowd.
After having seen the Anne Frank house in Amsterdam, I was disappointed by the museum. Also, numerous documentaries on National Geographic could give you a much better idea of the holocaust.

I visited the Holocaust Memorial as part of a high school trip. We had two hours to spend at the memorial. The challenge is that it is not clear  to guests where in the journey they are. As a result the group spent too much time at the beginning of the experience and essentially had to walk past at least one-third of the exhibit. Perhaps guests could be offered information about what to visit and for how long, based on the amount of time they have to spend at the memorial.

That said, the memorial is exceptional. Starting the journey by setting the context leading up to WWII was incredibly important. The Holocaust didn't "just happen." There were a set of events that occurred setting the environment for the Holocaust.

There is so much to take in and contemplate. Even a few months later I reflect on some of the information presented in the memorial.

This is an incredible museum.  An absolute must-see.  Plan on being there at least 4-5 hrs, longer if you can take it ... and be prepared to be thoroughly drained afterward.  You will need a couple hours' break before doing anything else.

Admission is free, but the permanent exhibit is constrained to ticket-holders due to overwhelming demand.  Strongly recommend getting advance tickets online (I think $1/person).  It is possible to get same-day tickets at the front desk, but unlikely -- and then you may have to wait a long time before your allotted slot.

The permanent exhibit thoroughly spans 3 floors and covers the Holocaust in great detail, starting with the pre-WW II rise of the National Socialist party in Germany.  It describes all of the groups that Hitler attempted to exterminate.  Plan on it taking about 4 hrs due to both exhibit size and number of fellow visitors.  You can take much longer if you choose to watch all of the side videos, read every sign in depth, etc.

On the 2nd floor, after exiting the permanent exhibit, you will pass through a few rooms describing past atrocities in Cambodia, Bosnia, and Darfur.  This currently includes a special exhibit on the mass-murderous Pol Pot regime in mid-1970s Cambodia.  Plan on these taking another 30-45 minutes.  These areas do not require tickets, but they're on the way out of the permanent exhibit anyway ...

The special exhibit (Cambodia) is described in more detail in the basement.  Plan on this taking 10-15 minutes.  You can do this part when first entering the museum, before starting the permanent exhibit.

The museum is very convenient to the Metro, 1 block from the Smithsonian stop.  Sadly the museum needs to have very tight security, so you will need to pass thru a metal detector and cannot bring any food or liquids inside.  A security officer was killed in a past attack on the museum, so it's understandable.

The museum offers SO much to take in. It's free and there's PLENTY to occupy your time with. My husband and I were in there for about 4 hours. The permanent exhibit is just...wow...something to be remembered.

First off, I am reviewing the overall visit of the museum as a whole, not specifically the content in it.

We went in with our 8 month old daughter.  Be prepared to go through security and throw out drinks other than water, which I had no problem with

I went in with grand expectations, but was disappointed right away when I got lost trying to find the main exhibitions.  The whole layout is kind of a twisty, convoluted type of set up.  It needs to be more explanatory how to start the tour.

The self guided tour starts out ok, but I think really lacks.  Not in content, but presentation.  Please read previous reviews that were rated average.  They tell the whole story.

The whole display is mostly reading, which is fine if you can see everything.  We went on a cold December day.  I can't imagine trying to read everything during peak season.  

It was obviously a very somber setting, with the crowd generally hushed to take it all in.  That being said, it is not the best place for young children.  If they get distracted, their noise will cut through the silence.

I think they could've done more to really show you the horrors of the holocaust because they do have a train car, gas chamber doors, and bunkhouse, but wither your expectation, because its just that.

I think a lot of things could be done here, but I want to dwell to much.  Just read a little bit past the rave reviews.

We also had a hard time finding the exit.

Again, let me stress, there is A LOT if informative content here, and will show you the holocaust and its atrocities, but.....

Simply stunning. I have been there a couple of times before, but this was the first time with our 9-year-old. It was tough for him, of course, but absolutely worth going. Despite what some governors think, our kids need to know about the inhumanity and cruelty of history -- not just what makes them feel good.
This museum is a must visit.  It is the most complete museum of the holocaust that has ever been my privilege and duty to visit, with amazing films to support the static exhibitions

This museum was an important stop for me visiting DC. My senior thesis in college focused on the rise and fall of Nazi Germany. There was a lot of information to intake - perhaps a little too much. Many of the artifacts and plaques on the wall I feel could have been left out. It's like they were trying to cram and entire history book into two floors. It was so much information yet they never mentioned certain aspects of the why Holocaust happened - just the how. It could be that I know more information than what they are explaining to people but it seemed that some very important facts were left out of the museum. It is information overload for someone who wouldn't know much about the era. Now, this is one of the few museums with most helpful staff. It has a coat check and the service agents in maroon are super helpful. The gentlemen who puts you on the elevator explained that this is a learning experience and to not take your time would defeat the purpose of visiting. I think everyone (over a certain age) should take the time to visit this museum.

I went to the Holocaust museum on a Thursday afternoon in March. Arriving around 2:30pm, there was no line (though the inside was a bit crowded with school groups...my advice? let them pass by or move ahead. If you are not part of these school groups, then listening to the teacher's dialogue and/or watching students take selfies in front if exhibits is going to seriously distract you). 

The museum is well set up. There's a ton of info. If you are the type that likes to look at and read everything, then count on 3 hours. I was there 2.5 hours and did have to rush a bit at the end. It's extremely educational...and really walks you through how the holocaust happened, from the initial seeds that were planted to the horrors that we are all familiar with. Most of the info is in pictures, videos, and printed signs. There's a lot of reading involved here.

I didn't have the emotional reaction that others did...even though I expected to. The thing is, I've read a lot of material on the topic, I've visited Dachau...the information, while horrible, was not really new to me (except maybe for the US's initial lack of response). I've also visited the war remnants museum in Ho Chi Minh city, the 911 memorial in NYC, read up on other genocides. And while the situations for each are very different, I'm well aware of the atrocities that humans are capable of committing against each other. 

That said, the exhibit with the thousands of shoes was chilling to say the least.

Overall, it's a good museum and important, especially for younger people that may not know the history of the Holocaust so well. I personally wish there had been a bit more in the way of exhibits and/or interaction vs printed signage, just because I find that more impactful. But overall, a must-see in DC and something that will stay with you for a long time.

I titled this "A Depressing Place". It is not meant to be derogatory, it is just the way it was for me. I've been through this place twice. (The second time because we had out of town relatives with us who insisted on going through) As soon as you start through the museum you'll notice how quiet it gets. Very few people carry on conversations. There is a lot to see and a lot to read. It is hard to contemplate just how cruel and cold the Nazi's (or any humans) could be but it is all documented.
 
I guess everyone should go through this place at least once so that humanity doesn't forget just how bad things can get. Just look at Isis today and the way they kill and you'll realize that it "CAN" happen again. So far, just not on the scale of the Nazi's
. 
I guarantee I will not go through again.
They heard you through this exhibit like cattle, with such a profound exhibit and experience --- must be a better way to spread people out.

Interesting however the route and layout of the museum seemed somewhat haphazard, also a separate entrance for school parties would be good so single visitors don't have to fight through huge groups of babbling kids :( not fun
Get reservations in advance- we did not, so we only we able to see a portion of the museum.  But what we did see was well done. The remembrance area was a great opportunity for reflection.
Really provides the history behind the holocaust - very interactive, emotional journey. EVERYONE should visit

It was definitely memorable and a must-see. I ended up being rushed and had to go through it in a little over an hour which was not enough time because it was closing. It has four floors and should take about 2.5 hours to get through and really have enough time to appreciate it all. I learned a lot and appreciated what i saw. It made me remember that i should stand up for others and be kind. It almost reached my expectations, but i felt it lacked something

Being a History student and having visited Auschwitz and Birkenau along with a nunber of other Holocaust museums such as the Berlin one perhaps I was expecting too much but I though the museum was nothing special and fairly basic at times. Although the videos were interesting. 

The museum opens at 10am but to get tickets you need to be in the queue much earlier. We were there for 8.30 (as we were up early to get Washington Monument and Bureau of Engraving and Printing tickets). You probably need to be there before 9am to have a decent chance of getting tickets.

A really nice place to go if you have children and older children. They have the main exhibit which younger children may find boring but they have an exhibit for younger children to help them understand what happened during the Holocaust. Tickets are free but you have to wait outside in the morning in a line because they only give out so many tickets per day so be prepared to stand outside in the morning!
Found this very moving, unable to complete the visit but we need places like this to make sure we never forget.  Entrance is free.

One of Washington's finest. A must see by everyone as covers the topic so well and seeks to remind us that  the world needs to ensure this never happens again. Saddest part is soon their will be no survivors left to tell the horrors of this era.

The degree of security in this museum was borderline ridiculous, furthermore, there was a lack of seating. There was a space for about 20-25 people, and you were not allowed to sit on the steps. This museum completely disregarded the needs of people to rest whilst taking in the surroundings.

A great place to go to reflect and learn more in depth about the holocaust. The nurse in itself takes about 2 hours to go round if you read most the information and watch the videos. Some of it can be quite hard hitting so maybe not the best place to take small children.

Highly recommended.  So much of what happened in Europe during the early 1930s is eerily similar to what is happening today.  More people should visit and take note.  So much to take in that the museum could have been even bigger.
This free museum is amazing. You will really get an understanding of what the Holocaust was and the horrors committed.  If you want to study everything then plan on two to three hours.

Everyone should see this memorial. It is a tragic part of our history and needs to be remembered. The building is, I believe, 4 stories and each level tells its own particular part of this history. Well worth the time. Allow a few hours to adequately take in all of its content.

Such an interesting and sobering museum. We arrived about 40 minutes before the museum opened and had to wait in line for tickets (you can actually order tickets online in advance) and waited about 1 hour to get in.When you enter you receive a card about a survivor or victim of the holocaust, telling you about their life story. A lot of the images are graphic especially for children. There is an area called Daniel's Story for children and the employees will suggest it if you have children with you but it's totally up to you. I didn't go into Daniel's Story since we didn't have any children with us. It is a self guided tour. I was very impressed with all the things the museum covered. I would love to go back and spend more time. It is not nearly as crowded as most museums (probably since you get tickets and only a certain amount of people are allowed in at a time).
Said it would take 90 minutes took 3 hours to really stop and reflect. Get tickets online  if possible very inexpensive.

Excellent display of artifacts and great history lesson for students and adults.
Large crowd but moves smoothly. Obtain your free tickets early, always busy.

We arrived about 1pm and were unable to visit the permanent exhibits.  They begin giving tickets out at 10 am and the line begins well before that.  Once the tickets are given out you are only able to see the special exhibits - while informational was geared more to children - David's Room I believe was the exhibit

I really wanted to go to this museum and spend a great amount of time here. I was really looking forward to it but was sadly disappointed. This place is one big bottle neck, literally. And because of this we couldn't read anything on the walls. The bottle necks throughout the museum were so bad that one couldn't move, at all. And just think if there was a fire or emergency and you couldn't go anywhere. You'd end up in your own version of Auschwitz. I know that's rude but it's true. All of this was so very frustrating. So much so, that we wanted to get out. I believe that the exhibit layout needs to be changed immediately. As for the exhibit content, I will leave that to the other reviewers because I only got to see maybe 10% of it in the 2 hours I was there. Sadly disappointed.

was very interesting and a very large museum. however very crowded and need to have tickets to see whole museum. Tickets are free but need to be there early like 9:45 am to make sure you can get them.

We spent about 3 hours touring the museum and memorial.  I would recommend giving yourself that amount of time to be able to fully take in all of the museum.  There is a nice balance of video and photographs as well as printed material to read.  I have read much on the subject over the years, so nothing really surprised me, but it really helps you connect with the individuals who lived through the experience and feel the tremendous loss of those who didn't make it.

Thought this was going to be what it was once touted to become, a museum that would compare the Holocaust with other genocides that have occurred before and since the Second World War. While this is a well put together museum, and evocative, it is very deficient when it comes to telling the story of the genocide of native Americans, Ukrainians during the Holodomor, the Armenian massacre, Rwanda, what's happening against Ukraine in 2022 and so on. Rather disappointed.

I could not wait to see the Holocaust museum during our trip. Maybe I expected too much but although informative and powerful, I was left disappointed in the displays. 
Sure there was a bunk and a rail car and the wall of photos was moving but I at least expected to see a striped camp uniform and that yellow star that every jew had to wear on every article of clothing they had and there was nothing like that.  The shoe exhibit was removed as well. I just expected to see some more personal items from the victims. They are the ones who suffered. I had seen practically all the written material and pictures on documentaries. I did appreciate the identification card we were given at the start. That did give a bit of a personal touch to things.

Had to pay $ 20 to become a member to get tickets for the main exhibit. They give free tix out in the morning but we didnt know that. Exhibit was extensive and you could spend several hpurs going through it.
and its prime location, my girlfriend and I were able to walk to many sites and memorials with-in a short amount of time on foot, and get back into our comfy Hotel at the perfect time.

good to visit,very sad in terms of how man can do to another human being,you could spend a good half day here easy great set up.history is very detailed and there is a theatre you could go into and see a historical show of human madness.
very well worth it.a must see but you must be prepared for sadness.

The Holocaust Museum is a very well designed museum. You start with an elevator ride to the top floor and then work your way down. It takes you through the rise of the Nazi Party, each floor showing the escalation of the Holocaust. 
This is a very emotional experience, be prepared for it. I wouldn't advise going through the whole museum with young children, but they do have exhibits designed for younger children on the main floor. Even older students need to be prepped for what they will see.
There is a great bookstore, and sometimes they also have survivors present.

Thought the purpose of tickets was to limit the #of visitors in the exhibit at a time. Didn't work. It was so crowded that the Fire Marshall would have had apoplexy. I certainly did. Signage was too small to see in the crowd. They should designate an "In" and an "Out" for the theaters to help with traffic flow, have more people on the floor to help with traffic flow, and limit entry.

You will head in to this museum with somber excitement and leave feeling depressed and angry.  Obviously you know what you're getting yourself in to but be prepared to patiently wait through lines and read/look as tragedy unfolds as the museum leads you through the history of the holocaust and the intolerance of Hitler.
This is a very organized and well explained memorial museum. I like the way the museum is organized and set. Lots of pictures and explanation. I really enjoy every time I am in town.
You'll need at least 3 or 4 hours to really see this place. If you know  much about the Holocaust, there will still be some things here that will open your eyes.

I held high expectations for this museum upon entry. We arrived at 10 am (opening time) to ensure a ticket. Once we got our tickets we went into the elevator and started the main portion of the museum. 3 and a half hours later we finished and this was just walking through, not even reading all of the exhibits. The museum itself was well put together, but if you have a pretty in depth understanding of the holocaust then this may not be for you. Definitely not a place to take kids 9 and under, it's pretty much a lot of reading and graphic videos. The memorial however was very nice except for the guard that yelled at me for getting too close to the candles (...whatever...). Overall I am giving the museum an average because it has good content, but my personal experience was a poor.

This museum tells a story and is very well done. I had no idea what to expect but was impressed by the presentation. I got caught up in the whole story and spent much longer than planned but glad I did. And free entry as well. Well done to those involved.

We were looking forward to this museum, as it was highly recommended, but when we arrived, found you had to have pre purchased tickets for the permanent display. Which were unavailable on site. This was not clearly outlined. The other exhibits were all free to the public. 
Therefore very disappointing.
This museum is amazing. 4 floors of full size diaramas, collages, videos, and more. There were so many impactful stories. I highly recommend.

Very glad we visited this rich historic place that truly expressed the history of a group of people who were persecuted for being of their faith and this should never have happened and hopefully will never again.

We were in town to see the cherry blossoms nearby. Being the son of a holocaust survivor, nothing in this museum was new to me. Yet the architecture and the presentation are beautifully (hard to use that word in connection with the holocaust) done. Seeing my birthplace in a Central European country on etched glass listing thousands of Jewish shtetls, settlements that no longer exist, made me very sad.

A must see, especially for those who either deny or minimize the holocaust's existence. For the rest of us, a reminder of what we are all capable of.

On the practical side, entry is on a timed basis. If you know in advance when you p,an to attend, you can reserve on line. Otherwise, you can get free tickets when you get there for sometime later that day (assuming they are not sold out). The Museum opens at 10. We joined the long line on a busy Saturday at 9:30, got our tickets by 10 and were in the Museum by 10:20. If you our military, a vet or a first responder and have your ID, you rightfully get to jump the ticket line.

Not to be missed

Planned my trip to DC especially to see the holocaust museum.  Walked in and the place was loaded with kids running around, didn't really see any signs pointing to any particular events, just saw Daniels story.  Kind of disappointed.

This museum was very well done. There is much to read, many photographs and videos. Some of the images and truths regarding what happened during the Holocaust may be too graphic for young children. 

Overall the museum was well thought out and educational. Our teen and preteen got a lot out of the museum too.

I was an au pair in the Sates for 6 months, once i went with the kids and the father to D.C, I have a day off and the father told me to went to these museum...I don't know if still is working like that, but you have to go on advance and get an hour to visit it. I went in the morning and get 4 pm time to visit it, no problem, mean while I visited the others monuments around. 

Once I arrive, it was very impresive, the details of the passport it gets you thruogh. The train in the middle and all these picture witch show you the real thing, as it was...definitey not for children

what I don't understand is...why it's a museum like that in the States? well I gest that is for the jewish inhertance. 

DON'T MISS IT!!!

Agree with another review to reserve tickets to the Holocaust Museum the system is not good at all. We set our alarm for 6:30 am 3 of us different cell phones 7 am there were slots already mark as taken, on one of them made it past the selection of the tickets and time then started thinking??? and sorry unvailable we tried for the past 10 minutes and nothing. Its frustating why have them and when you check they are taking slots with 17,14 right at the starting time. Is it a glitch? Then we walk by it ask a security officer if for first responder they were still giving tickets said yes we parked pay for 3 hours it was raining for nothing; get there and they told us no. 03-16-2022

I can't provide an in depth review as we only had maybe 45 minutes here.  But it was just enough to determine I would give myself at least 4 hrs here to do it justice.  This is not a place you rush through.

I thought I had heard all the stories especially about Anne Frank but there are more and everyone on earth needs to hear them.  So much new info that I didnt know of both Anne Frank and the atrocities of the Jews.  Even though is was a terribly sad way to spend an afternoon, it is a must so that it will never be repeated.

My stepfather is a Holocaust survivor and I never truly understood the magnitude of what he went through as he could never fully explain what it was like, it wasn't until I went to the Holocaust Museum that I could really get some sort of an idea of what he and millions of others had to endure.  

No matter how much you read, see on TV or in movies, or talk about it, it just won't prepare you for what you'll see at the Holocaust Museum, and the museum is an experience that every adult and teenager should go through to properly understand and appreciate the atrocities that millions had to suffer for and millions others had to go to war to fight to eliminate.

My three students and I were there on a particularly hot day and it was quite warm inside the museum.  One of the students commented it was really hot and it was really crowded.  I asked him to close his eyes and picture the people he had just read about and seen on the exhibits going through a dark, hot and crowded place not realizing they were going to their deaths.  He quickly got the idea and did not complain once after that, and later he said he did not realize how good he had it and had no right to whine.

The permanent exhibit is sad, dark and crowded, but completely worth the experience of going through to properly understand the atrocities that happened, and we must never allow ourselves to forget.  To forget is to take things for granted, and that's when terrible things can happen.  If you know it's going to be a busy time in Washington, I highly recommend pre-ordering tickets online - it's worth the processing fee ($2 I think) to avoid the long lineups that likely will be there.  I went during a non-peak time in March and there was a lineup stretched down the block.

My wife was wanting to visit the Holocaust Museum so we decided to drop by on a Sunday in October.  Not being busy season, we were not required to have a preordered ticket.  Upon entering, it was kind of confusing on where to go.  I stopped by the info desk and asked for a map.  They said all we needed to do was go to the elevators and they take you to the top and you work your way down.  Simply enoughor so I thought.  Just before entering the elevators, there are several stacks of passport booklets. We each randomly chose one.  As you tour the museum, you read about a victim of the holocaust.  You learn a bit about their story and if they survived.  Stepping out of the elevator, it was dimly lit and very quiet.  There was also a huge line of people that moved at a snails pace.   Come to find out, the whole place is nothing more than a bunch of reading about pictures and videos to watch.  The halls are quite narrow and the people ahead of us must read at a 1st grade level.  They read so slowly and it was like they were analyzing every word of it for a test later.  I like to think I am a bit of a WWII history buff so I already knew quite a bit of the Nazi history and atrocities against man that they committed.  It really seemed like most of the visitors had no idea of most of it.  We made our way through the museum in about an hour.  While the subject matter was quite horrible, I am not sure why everyone finds it to be so moving.  I could understand if you lived through it or were Jewish, but for the average American I cant see this being at the top of your places to visit since it did not happen in the US.  Just my opinion as there are so many other options in Washington DC to visit.
expected a bit more, museum interiors were a bit  labyrinthine. special exhibition was hard to locate. The main exhibition was ok

Yes you get a sense of history and the atrocities of what happened to the Jews but as an attraction, you don't get much to see and not really enough artefacts to intrigue you like you get in Berlin
Disappointing I that a memorial should tell
more of a story that this place has to offer.
However it us free to visit.

This was a must stop on our recent visit to Washington DC.  I was anticipating a great museum but found it to be more of reading documents.  There were so many people and so much to read that it made the visit unenjoyable.  I would recommend visiting the museum during the offseason.  We actually left before completing the entire museum.  A positive note would be the section were "Daniel's House" is located.
Get there really early in the morning if you wants tickets for the main exhibits. The children's section gave a very straight forward visual timetable of events.

We would have gotten more out of the museum if they offered an audio tour.  There was just too much to read in the time we allotted for our visit.  The museum displays can get very crowded especially at the entrance which made it impossible for us to stop and read because the crowd was wanting us to move along.  Still very moving and sad

Just one piece of tip, if you are aware and read lots of books regarding the holocaust, and already have extensive knowledge, then the museum won't be as exciting.  There are some videos they show which I have not seen nor know about, which was heart breaking, and a few items were displayed from all the victims...lots of lots of reading.  So give yourself a couple of hours in there.  Was it worth it? Def was!

The Holocaust Museum provides a well rounded view of the tragedy of the Holocaust in a variety of mediums, i.e audio, visual, movies, etc.  They try and make the information accessible for different age levels.  It is a sobering visit so for my family it worked best at end of day.

I went to the museum after lunch on a day when all of the exhibition tickets had already been allocated for that day. You can still go in but I found there is not very much at all to see without an exhibitions ticket. What little there was to see I couldn't look at properly because the museum was too busy. Tip: book in advance or go early in the morning.

Even though the Holocaust Museum is free, if you go in the summer, make sure to request your tickets early as they tend to run out. They will give you a time slot for your entrance. 

It's a chilling, haunting experience but one you definitely shouldn't miss. They often have survivors there who talk about their experiences or come to sign their books. Overall, another great not-to-be-missed experience in DC.

We did not know that you needed reservations/tickets, so we got to the Holocaust Museum to find out we couldn't get in the main attractions (since we weren't military or any of the other exceptions).  We did get into the section on Daniel's Diary -very well done & suitable for my 13 year old grandson, and the Hall of Remembrance.  It was well done, but I really wish we had been able to see the main sections.  I hope this review causes others to do the research & find out how to get the tickets before you go to DC.
  Picture is of a poster outside the museum.

Seeing this museum was a very emotional experience for both my husband and myself.  We actually felt quite depressed upon exiting the museum but I don't think that is to be unexpected considering the subject matter.  The displays are heartbreaking - especially the display of "the shoes".  This museum should be seen if for not other reason than to honor those who perished during this awful time.
Was in there a couple of hours and it is a lot of reading but very informative. My husband liked it, but my sister in law did not. She is more a visual person.

The exhibits are very nice and really leave you thinking about the devastation of the holocaust. But it is WAY too crowded even on a week day. It was frustrating trying to read anything in the exhibits because it was not well lit, and every time I started to read, another person stepped in front of me because it was so crowded. Because of this, it took so long to get through the main exhibit that we had no mental energy left to see the other features and exhibits. We will go back sometime to see the rest of it, hopefully when it's less busy.
EXTREMELY DISAPPOINTED in the front gate security however. This museum is NOT Smithsonian run. All of the Smithsonian museums have security that is friendly and quick, but still safe. The security at the Holocaust Museum was exceptionall rude. There was a long line to begin with, which is always bad and frustrating. My wife wears an insulin pump, which by medical standards is considered part of her body much like a heart device. She asked if would be problem and was told "No Problem." After going through the metal detector, they forced her off to the side and required a full pat down and body search because of her medical device that is no bigger than a pager. This was highly disappointing consider she was told only 30 seconds earlier that it would be no problem. To make matters worse, they had no female security officers on duty, which meant she was searched and touched by a male officer or could not enter the museum. Absolutely ridiculous.

It is hard to walk through this museum without becoming emotional. I wish that the smithsonian would break down the number of people visiting into groups so that everyone is able to read the material since it is a self guided tour. They try to pack as many people onto the lifts and a lot is missed because of this. You can spend all day here and not get through the entire exhibit.

Even though they limit the number of people inside, it is so crowded that you cannot see some of the displays.
Really like Daniel's Story as it helps all ages see what it was like through eyes of a child.

Having gone through actual concentration camps in Europe all the facsimile pieces here made it seem somewhat 2nd rate to me. That being said the educational value to newer generations is obvious and not all can see the real thing in Europe . That experience was so much more real and draining emotionally than the Museum.
This was a very emotional museum but fantastic in the way it educates you throughout, a few of my European friends may find it difficult but it's history.
Whether or not you are familiar with any or all of the Holocaust, this museum is a must visit while in DC.  It is outstanding, poignant and moving.

Sure, it is a good museum but not the best one on holocaust.
Maybe a huge plus is the presence of some WW2 survivors with who you can talk and ask questions.
I was really let down with this exhibit. I thought there would be a lot more information on the Holocaust.
Perfect for a needed reflection.  Get tickets early during summer months or go to their website at 6am day of to get tickets. They are usually sold out by 9am online.

While this museum is more of a review of the historical background and catastrophic results of Hitler's "final solution", it is important and horrifying at the same time. It is not as personally or emotionally moving as the Yad Vashem museum in Jerusalem, but nevertheless should be seen by all.

This museum was the most striking and emotional museum that we went to on our latest visit to Washington, D.C. Head to the information desk, get your ticket (it's free, they're just used to manage crowds in the exhibit section), and head to the elevators. There, you'll receive a small booklet detailing the story of one Holocaust victim.

You'll get into the elevators, and head up to the fourth floor. From there, you make your way through the exhibits until you end up back at the ground floor. Read through your booklet as you progress through the timeline to follow the story of the person that you chose. Prepare yourself for a masterfully created look into the horrors and vileness of the Holocaust. The museum consists of text, movies, and actual items from Nazi Germany and it's controlled territories. You'll learn a lot, and you'll feel for the victims. Everyone should come here at least once.
The most detailed and complete information I have seen on the Holocaust.  Should be viewed by all, although I think it may be upsetting to very young children.

We planned ahead for this trip and printed our passes out before leaving home. Our time-slot was 3:45 pm which we chose because we meticulously planned our days to fit in everything we wanted to see/do.  On the ticket it states that many first time visitors will spend 2 to 3 hours in the museum and we were planning to be among them! As complete history buffs we took our time reading every word and watching every video clip until we heard over the speakers that the museum would be closing in 35 minutes! We had only been in there for about an hour at this point, and had not yet reached 1/2 way! We then tried to rush and skip certain sections just to get through it. When we were about 3/4 of the way all monitors went off and a voice came across the speaker telling us the museum was now closed, please make your way to the exit. The employees were extremely rude herding us out saying things like "please make your way to the exit we have been closed for 2 minutes now!". In my opinion, the guide that took us up in the elevator should have told us how much time we had before closing...and they we would have to leave immediately.  I am a little bitter, because this was one my must see's and I really wanted to see it all.

The museum does cover the history of Nazi Germany and events of World War II pertaining to Nazi Germany and other groups they felt were inferior including the Jews, gypsies, handicapped, etc. The museum is educational. However, I recommend the Illinois Holocaust Museum and Education Center in Skokie, IL for good coverage of the Holocaust as well.

This museum was very crowded when I went. It did a good job explaining the lead up to Nazi Germany. Where I thought it missed the point was it made it seem as if Jews were mainly the people exterminated when all actuality there was a huge amount of the handicapped, gays, political prisoners, Eastern Europeans and others. European Memorials such as the Anne Frank house and Dachau do a much better job at teaching that if you don't stand up to you fellow man there may eventually be no one to stand up for you.

We expected that if we started our day here, it would be a dark cloud over the day. We were pleasantly surprised that, while there was a ton horrible images and negative things, there were not gruesome pictures of the dead and tortured prisoners. It is set up very well, with the the rise of hitler on the top floor and the subsequent history on your way back down to the main floor, ending with the freedom of the enslavement camps. It is a lot of heavy sad history, but is interesting. There are did bits of that time period that I did not know. There is a continuous film playing towards the end with people reflecting on the time, some are escapees, some helped jews with freedom/ protection, some reflect on how they wish they could help. All are equally moving and sad. 
If you wish to get into the exhibit you need tickets. I am not sure if you can reserve them on line, but they do hand them out outside the building until they are gone. If you are a veteran, ask at the counter for tickets. They were out of tickets the day we went, however when discussing the disappointment of not being able to see the exhibit as a veteran, they stated they have a few tickets they hold for veterans. You must show your ID though.

We were told by everyone that this was the place to go and the thing to do when you're in Washington DC.

Did these friends realize when they were hyping this place that we would have a 30 minute wait outside and a two hour wait in the lobby for the main exhibit once we got inside?

Did they realize that it would be so crowded once we were finally in that we wouldn't be able to read any displays or participate in anything without being bumped, pushed, jostled and physically carried along by the crowd? That it is so claustrophobically overcrowded that my friend Louise actually became nauseous?

I am 5'2...There was never a chance of me seeing a thing.

This may be a great museum but we left before seeing everything because it's tough to concentrate, or feel safe, with strangers breathing on your neck.

Get your tickets in advance online, and no matter what, be ready for crowds!  Especially nerving are the school groups which behave so poorly and disrespectfully.  It's a good museum, but it's run poorly and too many people are let in.  Way too many.  You'll leave needing to go back, but not wanting to.

An absolutely fascinating and simultaneously horrific experience.  There are more displays and exhibits than could possibly all be read or viewed in a single experience.  The information is all there in all of its raw- yet non exaggerated for effect- form.  It is a must see for all history buffs and adults.  There are portions that have not been edited or censored.  This is much more graphic than anything on the Discovery or History TV.  Might be too much for younger kids

The museum has an excellent layout with a well thought through exhibit of all the aspects of this terrible part of history. At the end one cannot but wonder how on earth could this happen and how do we still allow these atrocities to happen. A good link up with current history and affairs with emphasis on events in African countries.

If you have little time on your schedule, I recommend buying (you can do it online) a timepass.
All in all very interesting.

After yad vashem and nyc  holocaust museums  this was disappointing    too llarge like a gallery showing the.awful history in large areas  lacked the feeling of belonging    cannot compare the experience with the two other special holocaust centres  sorry
Could easily spend 3 hours here.  Such a wide variety of information related to a terrible period of our recent history.  Very well presented.

Having visited this amazing museum previously with my 10 year old grandson...I better knew how to prepare my two 10 year old grands this time. Both children had read I Survived...a appropriate childrens novel about the devastation of the Holocaust.  They both paid a lot of attention to the excellent displays...stopping to read the extensive material about the rise of Hitler and the demanding power that he came to possess over the German citizens. Both a devastating and moving  experience for us all.

What we saw left us speechless. Our only complaint was that to see the main exhibit you need to buy a ticket. We did not find out until we got to the museum that you had to standing line in the morning or buy a ticket online. Our hotel did not have any VIP tickets. However we spent three hours going through the free part of the museum and it left us speechless. be sure to get a ticket beforehand for the main exhibit.

This was a very sobering experience. I'd always wanted to visit this museum, and I think it is a place everyone should go once. It is a horrible part of history, and shows how cruel and Inhumane people can be. It is important that we remember what happened. I learned a lot that I didn't learn from history textbooks and seeing the artifacts was eye-opening. I love the fact that this, and almost all museums in D.C., are free! We spent a full 5 hours here, but took our time. You could probably spend less time if you didn't read everything... We really enjoyed the videos and audio recordings of survivors and their stories. Go, but be prepared for an emotional visit.

First time visiting this well known museum. At least for me, it was very difficult to appreciate any of the exhibits with the mass of people there. I certainly want to tour the museum but clearly need to visit on a weekday during the winter when I would assume it is much less crowded.
so we queued for an hour before it opened - still over 100 people in front of us.  However, do not be put off as this is a must for any visit to the city.  It was both moving and interesting.

My family of 4 (2 adults, 2 kids-14, 12) thought this museum was outstanding.  Please allow 2-4 hours to go through the museum.  We spent 3 hours going at a casual pace.  The museum was crowded due to the summer hours, but it is a must see.  Powerful and informative!
There is a lot of reading on this tour.  Thought there would be much more artifacts from this terrible time, but it's mostly reading.

Even if you think you know a lot about the Holocaust, you will be glad that you made the effort to tour this museum.  Some of it is not easy to view or read about, but it will make you think and remind you why we must be vigilant to never let something like the Holocaust happen again.

We came to  the  Holocaust  museam a few  years ago but I couldn't finish   the whole thing  - to  much  to take in so I wanted to come  back to it - we  went  back  and  we finsihed the whole  permanent  exhibit and  it  didn't get to me like it  did the  first time.... In fact - I don't think  they had  as  much  as they  did  before -  they even had a  whole  wall  blank!   Very surprised!!!

Everyone needs to visit this museum, to remember what happened, and to realize the true human cost. We went with my son's 8th grade class tour, so we only had 2hrs here...not enough time! Plan for at least 3-4 hours. Everyone gets a card with a real person's story on it at the entrance. You start on the top floor, with the rise of Nazi Germany, and work your way down, ending with video testimonies of Holocaust survivors. I will come back.
Really interesting looking at the artifacts and the history. What was haunting was the shoes exhibit. So sad, yet informative.

I had a few hours on my hand and decided to visit this Museum.  The exhibits are along familiar lines of Holocaust-themed museums.  Lot of reading and pictures and artifacts and walking.  There was a presentation on how the U.S. government, the media, and the public processed the news from Europe and Germany during the period.  I found that fascinating -- what struck me was the strong prevailing sense of not wanting to get involved in the rest of the World - isolationism.  Of course, after getting involved following Pearl Harbor, the U.S, is now fully engaged with the World!  Earlier, I had visited the Holocaust museum and memorial in Jerusalem -- which are excellent and highly educational -- and thus I was not overwhelmed by this Washington version.  This is a free museum (though there are big jars throughout where you can insert dollar bills and coins as donations.  Surely worth visiting if you are curious about European Jewry, learn how Nazism consolidated itself, and to admire the many acts of heroism.  There is a gift shop offering many specialized items.  Just off the Mall and thus very easy to get to by Metro - Smithsonian station.

For those of you who went to the museum 20 years back, its different. 20yrs back it had various features that took you back almost as if you were present in the holocaust and you could connect a bit with the pain and suffering. Now its just history which you can get on any internet search.

Now its seems to be more "family-friendy" or "kid-friendly" but something else is lost

The more I read and watch about WW2, the more I want to see what each museum around the world can offer on it. This museum is very striking, from the moment you get out of the elevator at the beginning. It is simply chilling. There is only one museum I rate higher than this one, and that is the Oskar Schindler museum in Krakow, Poland.
