But this place holds some saddening but very broad insight into a time period in history that will never be forgotten. As Washington DC is a city of museums, you really cannot visit without going here.

They give a limited number of tickets a few months in advance.  Then they say every morning at 7AM they release some for same day.  Good luck with that.  If you really want to go here, you may have to pay a tour company for the opportunity.  I do not believe the everyday Joe has a chance at getting a ticket.  Go to their website, you will see.  Just pay the tour operators, your only chance really.

My only complaint is the workers and the museum were trying to leave early so they blocked off the stair so we couldn't visit the upper floors at 5:12 saying that they were closing.  I told the guy that your website and signs say 5:30 but he wouldn't let us up stairs saying we could come back tomorrow.  The only problem is we are leaving in the morning.  It was very irritating that the guy with glasses and curly hair wouldn't let us up the stairs because he wanted to leave 10 mins early.  Disappointing a fellow american.

This museum has had a LOT of money poured into it. I understand the sentiment behind it and the necessity of telling the story of the holocaust. Somehow it just felt really overdone and really overspent..It was crowded when we visited and the exhibits and displays were just what you expect to see in a place this specific- it was all a bit weird to me- perhaps sensory overload, but the emotions and the stories are pretty intense so it could be just too much all at once..

We have travelled to Washington many times but somehow not previously managed to get to this museum. As we were born in the forties it was not revolutionary but very poignant nevertheless. It was noticeable that whilst there were many youngsters on some sort of trips they were very quiet and seemed to understand the solemnity of the exhibits. We intended to move on that day, but the displays and explanations of this terrible time kept us riveted until closing time.

This museum was done in a true to life of all that occurred with Germany, Hitler. It was was interesting, educational and sad.  We enjoyed being there to see all that they had on the history of this devastating part of history. But it was important to see. To recognize and never forget it because we don't want this to ever happen again. We all thought it was a great record of history.

The experience in this museum should be one that moves you to tears, but they have decided on a cold, industrial, modern, concrete-look and have a decided lack of personal stories about what happened and why.  Lots of videos of marching Nazis are shown at the start with excerpts of Hitler's speeches, but unless you are German, you have no idea what is being said or why those words were so compelling they would whip the masses into a frenzy.  This is a bludgening with images without appropriate discussion or context.  I should have been moved, but simply seeing a pile or shoes or other items on the floor, all dusty, simply does not give the visual or visceral impact of other ways to display those items or to help the museum goes swear on their heart that "We will never let this happen again".  I was hoping for something so much more and so much more moving.  This was an assault on the senses like a zombie movie, after a while you become inured to it all.  I would advocate for much more personal stories and showing more about the lives of the Jews before this holocaust started and more about the triggers of their persecution.  Without that, it is hard to know how you can stop it before something like this gets started.

Live about 2 hours away but this was my first time visiting here. A lot of reading and walking. It still is upsetting to know that people think and act the way they did to all those human beings. It is even sadder that ppl still believe nothing happened back then... I pray that these actions never are forgotten...

We took our 9 & 15 year old daughters - the 15 year old begged to go - and I was a little disappointed - probably because I had to stay with the 9 yr old the entire time. It was very crowded & warm & could easily take 3 hrs to see so be prepared to stay awhile if you insist on covering it all. We had to rush it a little because of the younger one - so you may not want to take your kids - but the 15 yr old appreciated it. Though some exhibits were very interesting I had the feeling that I had seen it all before on TV documentaries - so if I were pressed for time I would skip this again.

It was very hot and there were too many people to really be able to see and read anything.  Long line ups to get through the security.  Very few actual artefacts, mostly videos and posters which you had to read, but couldn't get close enough to read due to the number of people they let in the permanent exhibit at a time.  Nothing I couldn't read or see online anyway. I'm glad I didn't pay anything for the museum.  Such an important part of history that should never be forgotten should be provided with a much better educational experience.

This was a very moving experience. Obviously being about the holocaust I would caution young children as it's very intense and there's a lot of reading/silent time/movies. I enjoyed the experience as did my husband and would definitely recommend to others!

There are many reasons to visit this place not the least of which is to get a more realistic exposure to man's inhumanity to man. Congratulations to the museum for its recognition of the total holocaust and not simply those atrocities suffered by the Jewish community
It was heart breaking visit to a musseum. Very rich of information and the details in the exhibitions was outstanding. I finished my visit sitting in a bench meditating in all what I observed.
Highly recommend this museum. Certainly not a pleasant subject, the content is a good reminder of what the human race is capable of.

to say this memorial is interesting is a understatement . it is so moving it is not for the faint of heart the do not pull any punches the piles of shoes the bales of hair the pictures omg . to see what people did to other people is amazingly scary . if you go you see why they say never again !

I brought my children here. It was a powerful experience for my 13 year old who will study the Holocaust in school this year, a little too much for my 10 year old, but with some guidance a good introduction.

This thoughtful and moving group of exhibits takes visitors from the early 1930s through the end of WW II and deals with the Nazi campaigns against Jews, the disabled, homosexuals, political dissidents and races considered " inferior."  The museum also presents exhibits on the genocides in Cambodia, Rwanda and Darfur. The most graphic exhibits are considerately recessed behind walls high enough to prevent small children from exposure without parental help, but this is a museum to visit after some thought and preparation. Important, valuable, but not an easy stop among DC's many offerings.

The 90 minutes suggested are not enough to tour this museum.  We were there for over two hours but that was only because I was so overwhelmed that I had to rush through the latter sessions. This might be better suited over a few visits as it's very difficult to see some of the photos and hear the videos. 

The only reason I did not give it 5 stars is because even though we went on a Sunday, there were a few middle school or young high school groups going through that were rushing through. They should definitely be made aware of this part of history, but maybe these larger school groups should have a guided tour.
This is a not to be missed museum, well done in every aspect. Be prepared to be touched emotionally.

We expected this to be moving and it was. We missed the main exhibition (as you need to get there in the morning to get tickets) but the Collaboration and Complicity exhibition was thought-provoking and well worth a visit.

While the Museum is a great place and very educational and also a sad place to be, the workers were extremely rude.  If asked a question they were hateful.  Make sure you don't buy a coke or water from the truck vendor outside because you will have to get out of line and throw it away.  And Pictures is a no go, however, I would understand that if I had heard it or read it but we were herded in like cattle and I could not hear the lady until she tapped me on my shoulder and told me to delete the picture I took.  She could have been polite and that would have been fine, but she was smart and rude.  I just had a bad experience with the staff their.

We've just went on our visit to see family over the Thanksgiving Holiday and it was not that crowded. An absolutely MUST see. Extremely informative and moving. It takes you through the historical events over the years that eventually led up to the Allies eventually saving what people in the camps that were remaining.  You can roam as you please too.  Very well done.

It would be helpful if there were audio devices people could carry with them, so you don't have to wait to read about each specific exhibit.

I'm not sure why there are soo many great reviews but in my opinion don't wast your time going here! It's not very spectacular and when there are a lot of school groups there it's packed. Realistically what you do is walk down walls on a few levels and watch and read stories and movies, boaring.

While the museum itself if well designed and accurately handles an extremely serious topic, the rude tour guides ruined the experience. There were many small groups being led by tour guides that were volunteers at the museum. These groups would simply stop directly in front of exhibits and not move for more than 5 minutes. When we were trying to read exhibits, the tour guides would walk right in front us until I started angling my body so they couldn't push in front. There must be a better way to handle this situation especially in that first crowded hallway on the fourth floor, but it seemed that the volunteers didn't care that they were being rude.
Be prepared for a powerful and shaking experience. The first section explaining the reasons why Hitler raised so quick, which were the foundations of holocaust is very informative.

Let's start this by saying my family got to the Holocaust Museum too late to obtain tickets to the special exhibit there. We did the exhibit " Daniel's Story" about a young boy taken to a Concentration camp with his family- my 2 girls found that interesting. There's a propaganda exhibit that's long.I think if we were able to get tickets to the special exhibit ( I believe there's  an exhibit that had actual shoes in it ), I may have liked this Museum more. I actually found it quite dull, and I was really looking foward to going to this museum.

While some may be discouraged at the thought of this museum, it is instructive for everyone to be reminded how inhumane those in absolute power can be.  When we visited for about 4 hours which was not really enough time, it was very encouraging to see groups of schoolchildren also visiting as the events relating to the Holocaust cannot be allowed to be forgotten.  Although the subject matter of the museum is very disturbing and in places very distressing, the museum is a place of dignity and respect for those who suffered.
Excellent history lesson. Be prepared to experience the mood of the era and the different rooms: photos and shoes.

Listen. I wanted to see this museum SO badly. I liked the museum it was very informative. They have a lady there that is a 99 year old survivor on the holocaust that comes every Sunday. She left early because she wasn't feeling well on our trip though. The rail car room made everyone choke up. I would go for the experience, but when compared to the 9/11 memorial it's not so great.

Wonderful explanation of the horrors of the concentration camps.  Not for the weak of stomach or those lacking in some brain capacity.  This building was well thought out, and the sequence of the displays was perfect.   I would suggest one reads up on the concentration camps and the treatment of the Jews by the Nazis and the Germans before going.  Don't bring kids that will make noises and ruin the decorum that it deserves.  I will return some day after gaining some more knowledge of the Jews before and during the war, and hopefully appreciate what the folks are trying to get over to you a bit more.  I highly recommend this site and hope you take time to visit while in DC.  Get tickets at the corner at 0915 or write ahead to be sure you don't miss it.

Book in advance to avoid disappointment. Tried to book on the day many times and website either crashes or once you secure your tickets, your tickets are gone by the time you compete check out. Have heard great things about this museum but unable to get tickets due to their bad website. Tried to contact the museum using the email mentioned below (in other reviews) but the message bounced back. Really badly organised.

We were too late to get tickets for the main exhibit. Not well publicized that tickets are needed. Without seeing that exhibit I think we wasted our time. I was pleased to see they have an exhibit that discusses current genocides and brings attention to them in an effective way. The memorial/flame exhibit was moving.

Didnt realize we needed tickets even though it is free. Stood in line for maybe an hour then found out we needed tickets. Could only get in to watch movie and one had started about a half hour earlier and next one started in an hour. We spent some time on genealogy computer and then left. Wish we could have gotten in.  No availability on our weekend visit to DC.

We were fortunate to be in the audience to hear from a survivor about and his family and their struggles. Exhibits were informative. Some video exhibits near the end of the walk through the museum were graphic - but definitely needed to be to depict the horror of what happened. Tickets can be obtained online or at the museum.

My Mom and I made a trip to DC from Maine, and this was one of our main reasons for coming. We visited on a Friday and it was PACKED. Tons of people, and then SEVERAL large school groups. We got our ticket for the Permanent Exhibit and then explored the rest of the museum before our spot was up. Honestly, there wasn't nearly enough to fill the hour and a half we had to spend... we walked through Daniel's Story in about 10 minutes and then were wondering, what else? There were a few spots to watch short movies, but not much else. The Permanent Exhibit was fantastic, but SO PACKED... for the first 10-15 minutes it was elbow-to-elbow, totally right up against each other, but it eventually thinned out, thankfully. I think we both felt like we missed a ton because of this... so many people in front of you (and behind you), you feel like you can't take the time to really explore and read everything (and there's LOTS to read). But the room of shoes is something that will never be forgotten.
Permanent exhibition: self guided tour requires 1-3 hours; parking very challenging! Forget the cafe  expensive and tasteless

We have visited a camp in Germany a few years ago so this was not really that great for us.  The pictures here are very edited down compared to what we saw at the camp.  There is a lot of reading.  This really focuses on the politics of what happened more then the camps themselves.

Its hard to leave this museum without being appalled and revolted by cruelty and evil of the Nazi regime.  For younger less mature kids there is an age appropriate exhibit about a boy named Daniel.   I might suggest skipping the other exhibits for children younger than 10-11.  There is some graphic stuff there.
A real tear-jerker experience.  Very sad but necessary reminder of the horrors of that time in our collective history.  Go early and then go somewhere happy, like Air & Space.

My daughter and I really enjoyed talking with, and listening to, the woman who was an actual holocaust survivor.  I was not as excited about the memorial itself as I felt that if you watched tv about WW!! you have already seen most of the pictures displayed.
this is a sad memorial from the WWII Nazi Holocoust, good for the family with kids 14 years old and up, no for the entire family, no good for minors

This museum does a good job of opening your eyes to the horrors of the holocaust, it does rely heavily on printed display and video footage so does not make it feel as real as a trip to Anne Frank's House or Auschwitz would.

Wonderfully done. Youll be asking how could this happen? after leaving.

Its crowded at the beginning but as you move along the crowds thin.

The first floor of this is so overcrowded it's a little overwhelming, especially as the lower floors then become very spaced out.  There is so much to read, lots in quite small print, and so many people (even with time controlled tickets) it is very slow moving at first.  But as you go down the floors it spreads out.  It's a shame that the exhibition on American responses (in little telephone booths) can only be accessed by a very small number of people, as this was of interest to me as a European (the rest was history I learned at school).  But the lower floors were better, and the reflective 'chapel' (not sure what else to call it) is a nice space to sit and contemplate the whole experience.
Get tickets ahead of time. Very well done told through stories of the people. Did spend a little too much time focusing and how Hitler came into power. very busy go in the fall not summer.

Accompanied by different family members, this Holocaust Museum has been visited on loosely an annual basis ever since it was relocated from Maryland several years ago.  Over that time, it seems to have lost some of its luster and sense of reverence for the grave indignity inflicted on mankind followed by the inspirational redemption of rescue at great cost by those with no particuar conviction or connection other than being Americans devoted to liberty and justice.  As Eisenhower said at the time of the "holocaust death camp liberations", if we're not vigilant, there will come a time when civilization forgets that the holocaust ever happened.  Viewed against that political backdrop, this is an exhibit to be seen from time to time with a consciouness awareness of "What's the intended message? And, "How well is it being conveyed?  Those whose "sense of judgment" is outweighed by their "sense of compassion" are likely to reap the most value from visiting this exhibit.  Those who have experienced "loss" are likely to reap more than "entertainment seekers".  

My family can spend days in discussion on "what's the message", only to return and have an entirely different assessment.  We seldom get as  far as reaching consensus on "How the exhibitors could have done a better job?"
This museum is a must see in DC. The museum is very informative - giving many details as to what led up to Hitler's takeover. Such sad stories of what the Jews endured.  The museum is free.
It's a long, slow, adagio from beginning to ebnd, but sobering an, ultimately, uplifting.

If youre looking for a light-hearted turn through a museum, this isnt it, although it WILL make you think.  The train car exhibit gave me the chills; this is a great learning opportunity for all Americans.

lots of detail..liked the cards given to us to help us personalize our experiences
We have been to Dachau but still learned a bit more from an American's perspective.

I really liked this museum, as much as you can like something about a horrible event. I thought it was really interesting and the permanent exhibit had a lot of information. However that is part of the problem as well, there is just so much information and so much to read that at times I found myself skipping over some information so I could see whats next.

I like the progression through the floors, but it is a very long tour and something you should do on the first or early in the second day you are there. Tired legs and minds do not go well with this museum. I would also not recommend this for small children. Teenagers are fine, but the children will get bored easily or will see things that you might not want them to see quite yet.

You have to visit it whenever you are in DC. I just found it to be very dark inside, which I understand it is in keeping with what it represents. Unfortunately the display of shoes was under renovation when we visited, but other then that I highly recommend it.

We didn't make it in time to get one of the allotted number of tickets for the day for the permanent exhibit. Wha the found while wandering around the rest of the museum was disappointing. We kept wondering if we missed something and couldn't read the map right.

This was a very informative museum. There is so much to take in it is almost overwhelming. Even though "Daniel's Story" exhibit is geared toward children. I felt that it was just not appropriate for children under 10 and was quite shocked at the amount of people who had small children in the museum. The subject matter is so serious and is hard for any one under the age of ten to comprehend. Please use caution!
The museum shows you the history, and actions of nazi persecution of the jews. My wife & i found it very informative & moving. Everyone should experience this, so it may never be forgotton.

An extensive museum which you could easily spend half the day there. I spent about 2 hours. First you take the elevator up to the 5th (or 6th?) floor and then walk down. Things are arranged chronologically. A lot of the pictures and stories I've heard before but many I haven't. Just the volume of it is impressive. The most interesting part was the model of the gas chambers, since I hadn't seen it before. It was pretty crowded but no problem.

Had an easier time getting tickets to Lalapalooza. Ridiculous. And we were trying mid-week. Learned we would be in town just under a month in advance, so we were unable to get advance tickets. The problem is they release the same-day tickets at 7 am, but if you get through, you can snatch as many as 25 tickets for $1 each. They should be putting a reasonable limit on the number of tickets to a single buyer. Apparently, they believe 25 is reasonable (though logic indicates that generally if you're traveling with 24 other people, you're likely making your plans at least a month in advance and would be able to take advantage of their advance ticket option.) So if you can't reserve your tickets at least a month ahead of time, it's probably not worth the 7 am stress every day of your trip to try to secure tickets.

This was not in my original plan, however, I am glad that I visited it. A lot of information about holocaust. The children section is very impressive, that tells the story of a boy who passed thru tough times during holocaust. Too much to see that a short visit (of less than 2-3 hours cant do justice to this place.

Being contrary is never easy, however, this museum is wrong headed. Beginning with the purposeful haunting of the site. The layout is confusing, including the difficulty in obtaining tickets for the main exhibit. The flow of information given during the tour is segmented. I was never sure when to feel anger towards the nazis and sadness for those persecuted. I much prefer the History and Discovery channel documentaries on the period. That being said, my 17 year old found Jewish history to be fascinating.

Apparently it's free, but you have to wait in line starting at 8am, for a 9am opening, to receive a pass to tour the exhibit.  Nobody told us that! So we walked in and were only able to go through a small "special" section, that was quiet boring, full of posters, and old films.  
Plan ahead :)

I visited this memorial several years ago with two of my children.  It is a difficult and intense subject but worth the visit.  My son was too young as there is a lot of reading throughout.  Still overwhelming the cruelty that humanity can exhibit.  Definitely not for young children; I would suggest at least middle school age.  The shoes had the most impact.

I went to the United States Holocaust Memorial Museum on 9th of July 2023, and I liked it its a sad yet educative experience. I highly recommend to go with family or friends and it really portrays what happened back then.

A very sad refective place which is very informative.
The childrens wall of rememberance is very thought provoking.

With several recommendations from friends, my family made a special point of making sure we took our 11 yr old and 13 yr old kids to the US Holocaust Memorial Museum while visiting Washington D.C. for a week of monuments and museums. While the impact and importance of the Holocaust is clear and without dispute, I came away disappointed with this particular museum's presentation of the materials, as well as suggestions that this museum is appropriate for families and kids.

As an educator, I prepared my middle school-aged kids (both of whom are excellent students) with the historical context of the museum, since at their ages, the history of WWII and the Holocaust has typically not been extensively examined yet. Immediately upon entering the main exhibit, visitors are confronted with large photos of Holocaust victim bodies in trenches, and my daughter was quickly horrified. Once again, I think it's important for people to see and understand the reality of the Holocaust, but it needs to be done in the proper context, with informed adults and students at least in high school who have studied the subject. In addition to the scenes of death, the museum also shows photos of people about to be executed. If this museum were a motion picture, it would be rated PG-13 at least, and likely R for depictions of horrific violence and content. Although I did not see many young kids in the exhibits, as we were leaving we passed by an elementary school class just leaving.

Strictly in terms of museum design, I was disappointed with my experience. Unlike any other Smithsonian or museum on the National Mall, this museum has a specific path you need to take, beginning on the 4th floor and winding your way down. This creates tremendous traffic congestion if people or groups linger at specific points which interest them. And I was there in the off-season. Upon entering the museum, we could not figure out where to go, so we headed to the only identified area, the Info desk in the lobby. The person in front of me in line asked the attendant how to find the exhibits, which saved me from asking the same question. Once you begin on the 4th floor, you quickly realize this museum lacks interactivity; nearly all exhibits consist of text and/or still images on the wall, like a giant newspaper article. A few exhibits contain videos or small theaters, and occasionally an artifact is presented amidst all the text. To say the exhibits feel lifeless and tedious is an understatement. To claim the museum experience is intentionally boring and static to emphasize the dreary plight of Holocaust victims is a cop out, as well. The overall museum design is clearly meant to evoke gas chambers, warehouses, and monochromatic industrial sites, with dim, nearly dark lighting through most of the exhibits. With the content of the museum being naturally grim, the entire museum experience does not need to be so bland while still maintaining a sobering, respectful tone. While generally attempting to keep the exhibits on a chronological path, this logical design principle is violated numerous times, confusing those who are not already history buffs and fluent with the events. Remember that most adults can not point to Germany on a map, let alone give you major dates from WWII. Towards the end of the prescribed exhibit path, the exhibit quality deteriorates further as the chronology moves towards the end of the war. The creation of Israel is briefly shown and could've been an excellent opportunity to end the story on a positive note and a silver lining. In terms of museum and exhibit design, this is certainly the poorest example of all the popular museums in D.C. Given the historical gravity of the content, I was hoping to be blown away with designs of equal importance.

The museum does have some extremely interesting content, and a few things are done well and poignant, such as the wall of victim shoes, a single walk-in rail car (although this is a huge missed opportunity to expand, in my opinion), the mini-bios of people who helped victims escape the Nazis, and the candle memorial at the end. Clearly much research has gone into all of the museum's content, however there are many Holocaust museums around the world, even in the US, from which they were able to draw. To the museum's credit, several other more contemporary genocides are depicted at the end of the exhibit path, however their exhibits seem like after-thoughts, with little more than a few glossy photos and some text on the wall.

The museum is free, but if you go, leave the kids at home, brush up on your WWII timetable, and be prepared to spend several hours reading walls. I hope over the years this museum takes advantage of it's world-class location to bring in some world-class museum exhibits for this important and tragic piece of world history.

Never again can these horrors happen are my thoughts as I see the exhibits but unfortunately even now tragedies like this are still icy around the globe albeit on a smaller scale. Sad, moving, and reality.

As the name suggests, visiting the Holocaust Memorial Museum is not your normal "day at the museum."  It's quite sobering and sad, but very interesting at the same time.  The exhibits contain actual items that were collected from the concentration camps, including uniforms that were worn by those imprisoned there. The museum is huge and it takes all day to go through it, so if you really want to experience it, you might not want to plan any other sightseeing events for that day.
The museum itself was nice just very depressing.  It was nicely set up and easy to navigate.  Wouldn't recommend for younger children.

Very disappointed in the online ticketing system.  Do they assign too many tickets to tour operators?  
I had the same experience as many others, finding out that tickets were unavailable 3 months in advance, so trying every day with their "same-day system" at 7:00am, which never worked.  They should not even hold out hope of those, it was such a waste of time. 
My son lives near DC, said he and his wife went to the door (back in the fall of 2021) and asked if they still had any and got some for a couple hours later.  Unfortunately, that didn't work for me as my only chance to be there was during my spring break (west coast teacher) and there were just too many people competing.  
I will try again next year, hoping they have improved this.

This should be near or at the top of any D.C. visitor's list. This is a profound educational and emotional experience. But be prepared to take the time to read a lot of information throughout the museum. Allow at least 2-3 hours.
Have you ever wondered:
- Why the Nazi's came to power?
- Why the German people blindly followed Hitler?
- Why the US and other nations were slow to get involved?
You learn about:
- the life of Jews in Europe before, during and after the Nazi's.
- the extermination of 6 million Jews and other "non-desirables".
- the similarities of yesterday's and today's global immigration crisis.
This is an ideal opportunity to teach older children about anti-antisemitism and racial bigotry.

"FOR THE DEAD AND THE LIVING WE MUST BEAR WITNESS"
Elie Wiesel

Experience was spoiled upon entering the premises. As a tourist, i would normally switch off my thinking cap. Hence, i would normally remove all handphones, belts and almost everything that could be detected by the system for all museums' entry points, airport etc. Was indirectly questioned by a security staff if i've listened to entry instructions like a kid. Apparently, i have remove stuff from my body more than i'm supposed to, and this is an issue to them.This was further gravitated by jokes and laughter shared amongst the security officers. They could have just repeat the entry instructions, rather then annoying me, as a visitor. Having been through so many security checkpoints such as JFK airport, various new york and washington museums, such treatment and redicule had never happened before.

Apart from the service level rendered by the security staff, the history and gruesome images in the museum is top notch, no holds barred. I would recommend one to book before hand to avoid the queues and cater at least 5 hours as there is so much to see and comprehend.

This is one of those museums that you need tickets for.  We went in April and they do offer a limited number of tickets available online the morning you plan to attend (if you arrive during high travel periods you need to get tickets online prior to travel).  Unfortunately, I logged on 2 minutes after tickets were available and they were sold out.  So we showed up at the museum an hour or so before it opened and there was already a line developing for tickets.  Advice, get there early!  

This museum could take you all day to get through.  Some of the videos and images may be disturbing for children, but they do have barriers around anything that might be disturbing for children so parents can decide whether or not they want their children to see it.
Learned so much history here.  Everyone should visit this museum at least once in your life time.  It was a touching and profound experience.

I was disappointed in this museum, I guess I just had my expectations set too high. I thought there would be more to it than there was. The tragedy that occurred is horrific and should be honored so that it is never repeated. The Daniel story was the best part to me.

A history in incredible detail of a time some try to deny while others try to forget. The memorial clearly sends the message that neither must ever be. The holocaust has changed how we govern ourselves in many ways: one simple example is the use of Informed Consent in medical and drug testing procedures. In some ways the detail is to such depth that the message becomes repetitious and runs the risk of consequent loss of impact. But the message and the stories of suffering and the depths of depravity to which we humans can descend and then try to dent knowledge of clearly comes through. Think three hours.

My wife and I took a weekend vacation to the National Mall in Washington, DC and visited the Holocaust Memorial Museum for the first time.The Holocaust was a period of time in the 1930's and 1940's when Adolf Hitler's Nazi Germany killed approximately 6 million Jews. The museum is very informative with many films where you can see and hear stories from some of the people involved and their families.
A somber but incredibly moving experience, delivered with objectivity, sensitivity and purpose. A must see and gives one a renewed perspective of life, war and tragedy.

Of course it is a compelling museum but I thought it was very very crowded as you wind through it in a line of people.  It makes it difficult to stop and spend time looking at different things. Very dark to keep a solemn environment. Had exhibits not just on the Holocaust as learning for other similar (but smaller scale) events to be aware of. I think the new Holocaust Museum in Skokie IL is better. Hate to say it's not great and sound insensitive to the subjet matter, but as far as a museum experience goes it was just ok.

My heart breaks for these men, women, and children. This tour was so informative, and gut wrenching. So many things I did not learn in school. My only peeve was so many people in one spot just standing there. I wish there was someone to move people along especially in confined spaces. If your claustrophobic like me, you will probably walk past some things to get by people standing around.
Don't Miss this emotional informative tour and make sure you tell them if you are a veteran at the ticket counter.

I don't have too much to say for my title line because this place was interesting, but it was sad, so it was hard to say. I gave this review a 5 because the information that they have there is excellent!!! The way they set things up in there made you feel like you were actually living in the times of the Holocaust, it was very emotional, so this is not a place for little children or ones who get scared easily. There's a section all on Azhwits I don't know how to spell it...anyway it was one of the worst concentration camps around, so when you get to that section of the museum be prepared to cry it is sad and a bit scary.

This memorial provides an important and informative treatment of the unspeakable horror of the Holocaust. That said, it did not have the emotional impact I had anticipated  perhaps it was the crowd. The interspersed video displays tended to clump up visitors making access to the displays which dolled in the narrow hallways difficult to access.

I had been wanting to visit this museum.  There was a lot of reading.  At the beginning one of the museum workers was giving a brief synopsis.  It was great but it didn't continue through the museum.  There were so many people and each exhibit had a lot of things to read.
Bad open hours; drab, uninformative to general public; did not get on 2nd floor because closed early; lower floor nothing.  Needs  a lot of work.  Lots of thought to present properly.

I got the impression as I was walking around that there were people visiting who hadnt visited a Holocaust Museum before, which will always be the norm. But it makes you realise, from some reactions, that their knowledge of the Holocaust is minimal. The exhibitions were excellent and varied. It was shocking to see real film of what happened.

This building is a spectacular use of space and an intersting yet moving exhibit.  This is not a tour that you want to end the day on as I would say that afterwards you will be moved to reflect on the holocaust of WWII Europe.  

Each guest receives a card and a name...  At the end of the tour you can find what happen to that person.  The railcar display, the picture hall and the shoes were all reminders of a grim past.  I would recomend this to anyone in D.C.

I do not like that  this seems to be the only museum where you have to vie for tickets to see the best part of the display. I did fortunately get the LAST ticket of the day and it was worth it. This was a fantastic and easy set up to see the beginning middle and end of the holocaust period. I admit I saw some things that will forever stick with me. I think if you are taking families your children should at least be 10 and older. Its history, its true and horrific and fascinating and I think everyone should see this..

This is a place that whether or not you are Jewish you MUST visit when in DC.  It is very moving and brings you close to the horrors of the Nazi regime.  It is a way to show your children why things like that should not ever be allowed again.  A wonderful experience and an eye opener.

Let me say I like the museum and its displays. Everything from the way you view it starting at the top working down to the variety of exhibits you get to experience, it is a very well done museum. Our problem was we scheduled to visit on a Sunday in the Spring when every middle school student was also there. I have a middle school and they are a special age.  Anyways it is the great visit people discuss, just make sure your visit on a day when it is not over run with middle schoolers.
This is a must when traveling to DC. Take your time; We were there 2 1/2 hours and still weren't able to really soak it all in and see IT ALL

The exhibits were few and far between but were well done.  The museum speaks to all genocide and not just that of WWII.  There is, and has been, so much genocide in the world's recent history there could have been so much more to this museum. I entered the building bracing myself not to cry. And, although the exhibits informed and pulled at my heart, I did not leave the sobbing mess I had anticipated. And it doesn't take much to make me cry.

Very solemn and thought-provoking testament to the atrocities of the Holocaust.  On a Saturday with perfect weather, visitors were out in droves, so it was quite busy. Recommend arriving early, if possible. But don't miss it, it's definitely worth the (free!) visit

This museum was very complete and well done although if you have seen any of the movies depicting the Holocaust, the thousands of devastating pictures won't be anything new to you. The museum was very crowded and difficult to maneuver and see things.
A predominantly depressing slice of our history.  Difficult to take in all the pain and abuses that were a daily reality.  Great exhibits that challenge your morals to the depths.

The explanation and historical facts are clear, so I can feel the situation when it was happened years ago... It is a good museum to visit, for we always aware about a concept that have a rasial motives could be changes the world into nightmare....

Very moving
Very well put together 
Certainly I would recommend for anyone visiting Washington to take the time to visit. Went with our teenage daughter who also found it thought provoking and incredibly moving 
No answers here just questions
This in not entertainment it is history.  Very classy well done museum.

Nobody could visit this museum and not be moved. Even though it is my husband who has an interest in this type of history I did enjoy certain sections of the museum. I did find the level dealing with the concentration camps quite harrowing but I feel it is an experience that people should endeavour to have.

A very sad experience, of course, but something that you should see. I was especially moved by the display of victims shoes. We had time only to view the permanent exhibit and visit the Hall of Remembrance and light a candle, but I'm glad we made time in our day to go. We got our passes weeks in advance since we were visiting in the summer. We went in at 12 and there wasn't a line to get in, but the exhibit was packed. When we left the museum there was quite a line, so I would recommend going earlier in the day if you are able.

Stark, honest, humbling recounting of an era that should be remembered exactly like this. Busy during school hours with kids, who most just don't get what they are experiencing. Certain areas hit you hard, like the model of the camps, the train car, the hair and the shoes. Close to the Smithsonian Metro.

We expected much more, if you have studied any historical books or watched any documentries about the Holocaust you will be very dissapointed with the content. 
It is a striking building and important to visit, but we just kept feeling like we were missing important areas, its so spread out with very few display areas.

Granted we may have just missed so much of the content due to the very short length of our visit, but this museum did not do much to draw people in to learn of the truth about the Holocaust. To be far, I think that I'd have to go back and see more. I guess I felt that the entrance should have drawn the visitor in more to learn more of the horrors of what happen.

For a mature audience. Perfect for my middle schooler, as they are studying this topic in school now. She said she was nauseated when we left though. Very sad and disturbing, but everyone agreed it was a must-see.

I bought a membership in order to get the priority passes to see the entire museum since all the passes online were sold out.  With the priority passes you don't need a time stamp or date of visit, you show up & give the information desk your passes & they let you in.  So worth it.  Be prepared for a ton of school children and the museum is definitely crowded but you still can see everything.  The shoes, all those shoes, that really got to me.  Plus, the accounts, the survivors telling their stories. How did they survive? How can we ever forget?

I was a bit disappointed in this museum.  A walking tour isn't enough.  Luckily there was one guide and he was interesting.  If not for him, this memorial would have been ho-hum.  Admission is free, but there is a long line to gain entry.  Hope you don't mind waiting at least 45 minutes.

This museum is well laid out and very thought provoking being given a passport of someone who was part of what happened gives a different perspective to the visit.  The museum gives an unbiased view of what happened.  Some of the films through the museum are quite harrowing. If you have time you should add a visit here.

I wanted to love this museum because my sister did. We arrived at 3:30 or 4 and were told we can't see the permanent part of the museum because tickets are already given out. We watched from the lobby and upstairs and only saw 3 small groups walk in. If it's empty inside they should allow others in. Something has to CHANGE!
This museum is so well done. The displays flow into each other to create a never to be forgotten memory. The films were outstanding. We will not forget!

We spent 3 hours here - had to rush through the last part because we were exhausted.  They could have "shrunk" some of the exhibits because it felt like we were beaten over the head a bit, but it was very informative and very moving.  My father was at Dachau the day that it was liberated, so I had seen pictures but it was great to learn more about before and after that.  A very chilling reminder of man's inhumanity to man.  Definitely worth going to.

I was excited about going to the holocaust museum, we went there 45 minutes before closing. They close at5:30 pm. 

Let me say first , it is a moving and touchy place, and they display the story behind many items, which makes you feel sad and emotional for what happened  to that person or family,  

but I was expecting to see more exhibitions but I was surprised  by the number of artifacts and photos were displayed.

There was kind of construction there, I don't know if that has something to do with the number of exhibitions. Forgot to mention, it is free admission.

Very good but not my scene. Memories would not be ones I would like to have let sink into my head. That is only me though, it was very worthwhile and interesting and sad to think of all the times past

The museum does a fine job explaining the WWII Holocaust but it totally neglects other horrific holocaust that have taken place in recent memory. Specifically, left out was the Communist atrocities of Soviet Union Ukraine, China, Cambodia as well as the Ottoman Turkish regime that murdered 1.5 million Armenian, Greeks and Kurds.  Perhaps I missed that section since it was very crowded but I think this a huge oversight.  Also, the building has a poor design flow to it and a lot of wasted space.

Get tickets to the permanent exhibit ahead of time online! Once you're allowed into the exhibit be prepared to stand in a wall to wall line to get through the first part of the exhibit. It felt like a fire trap, I'm not sure how they are managing the number of people they let in at one time. It was claustrophobic feeling. If you can get past that, this is a really moving museum.

I've been to other Holocaust museums and this one is just average. They have some unique displays and I do like the way they tell the story in chronological order.  Hard to get into during busy tourist times.  Best to check with your hotel concierge to see if they can help with tickets.  Although there are better, I think this is still a must see when in DC.

We have visited many holocaust museums around the world and in our opinion the U.S. Holocaust Museum is the best.  It should definitely be near the top of your list of museums to visit when in Washington DC.

I had one day for sightseeing in Washington after a 20 hour flight from Australia. I decided I wanted to spend it at this museum. After getting there, I found that there was no availability to enter that day - you need to go online and pre book to see the museum. They sometimes have same day tickets available, but for hours after you turn up.
The free exhibitions you could see were good but not worth the trip there over and above the other amazing sights in Washington. I was really disappointed not to be able to see the exhibition. How do you know you are supposed to go online days before to get a ticket until you already turn up and have wasted your time? 
I tried to explain I had only one opportunity but no good. 
I feel that if the job of a museum is to educate, they could be a little more flexible with people that won't have the chance to return. They do give out on the spot tickets to certain groups, such a military ect, which is really nice, perhaps they could offer them to people who can prove with a plane ticket that they can't come back. Such a shame to get there and miss it.
This museum is very well done.  It is a solemn, heartbreaking memorial to the Jewish people who lost their lives.  A visit here will stay in your memory forever.

Sadly i can't leave the museum 5 stars due to the LACK of crowd control, I was so excited to hear that there was a "ticket" option to this museum for 75% of it! Because of the other museums being a jungle with no tickets! I WAS WRONG! they give out WAY TO MANY tickets per time slot! The crowd was STUPID! so hard to stop and read anything! Someone walking in front of you, pushing, shoving and just being rude! ANOTHER pet peeve... Teenagers SHOULDNT BE ALLOWED! they are more worried about taking a snap
Chat of the entire place and putting their phones in front of you while you're actually trying to READ what's in front of you! Ugh! 18 or older PERIOD. No I MEAN NO exceptions... From the little bit we did get to see it was great. I will never come back during peak season, or on a weekend day ever ever ever ever again! And I STRONGLY recommend you don't either!
You need to be interested here as it is quite sombre. However it needs to be on your list as people should remember. The reflection area is beautiful
I was really excited to come here and berry glad we went, but there was really nothing there that I had not seen or read about before.  I still think it is a must see when in DC.

I hate Nazis...but I also hate that the free world did so little to help the Jewish refugees... 

I've been fascinated with World War 2 history since seeing Schindler's List in junior high, and I double majored in Economics and US History, but there are still so many things one can learn at this amazing museum.

I was happy to find that entrance to this museum was free as I believe everyone should have free access to learn about the atrocities committed during the Holocaust to ensure they never happen again. It was a moving and humbling experience. I would suggest getting there early or at an off time...the crowds did make parts of the museum tough to experience if you suffer from mild claustrophobia like me. It would help if there was a moving walkway through certain areas because it does tend to bottleneck. That is why I gave it 4 stars instead of 5. It does cut down on your ability to learn and experience the museum when people are blocking each exhibit. But overall a remarkable and powerful museum.

Not suited for young children.  The atmosphere of the museum is very solemn and somber to the point that it will make you sad.  Collection is very extensive and whole museum is very well made.  They start of the exhibit by asking you take an ID which contains the name and information of someone who died and perished during the holocaust.  They make you take this journey throughout the museum with this person in mind. It has a 14 minute film about the rise of Hitler.  At the end there is a place where you can pray and light a candle in that person's memory.
I was disappointed. Too much reading and not enough seeing items. However I did enjoy the room with the public polls. It shows how close we are to the history today.

We thought we plan well enough ahead in order to see all of the exhibits, but we were sadly incorrect. BUT, we still were able to see a good amount of the Museum. Even though there's a lot of reading, it was from many different sources (personal letters, newspapers, governmental decrees...) Its very hard to read through all of the different personal accounts and realize that so many atrocities happened to so many people just like me. 
Although, its very difficult place to spend time, they've done a fantastic job helping you to understand and relive a little of the time.

This is on the National Mall also. I can't say I won't recommend because you have to go through it once to know the history, but man, is it depressing. Actually, it's grisly but it gets the point across and that's why I didn't give it a 5. 

I won't go into the specifics because you have to experience it for yourself. I had a chance to go back a second time. I got tickets for my friends and at the last minute I said, "no thanks and gave my ticket away."

We had heard good things about the Holocaust Memorial but left a little disappointed. The tickets or the Permanent Displays were sold out, which we are assuming is the key to this memorial. If you dont have tickets to the Permanent Displays, dont bother. Side note: The Anne Frank documentary in the lower level theatre was excellent.

Oviously this is a somber place.  Not a place for children who can't be serious.  The exhibits are amazing.  At the beginning, you pick a card detailing a person who experienced the holocaust. At the end you will find out their fate.  As you go through, noone speaks very loudly, there is an air of reverence. Different exhibits will affect you most.  For me it was walking through the train car and the piles of shoes taken off the feet of people as the entered the camp. At the end, there is a remembrance room where you can light a candle and generally collect yourself before re-entering the present world. I needed a good 10-15 mins here.  Its not that peopke are sobbing and crying all the way through, its not like that. It is incredibly moving and people are respectful and absorbed in the experience. 

This was probably the highlight of my trip to Washington. I would recommend it highly. It will be an experience you will never forget.

We were very disappointed. We had reserved our time entry tickets so we were under the impression that those tickets are offered for crowd control but the place is way to crowded! Shoulder to shoulder people hard to look at displays and read anything. We just shuffled through the best we could but missed most things. Very hot in there too. Most displays are pictures and reading not a lot of artifacts. Im sure its a well done museum but a very bad experience.
The Building ans architecture are amazing. However once you have visited the Yad Vashem Holocaust Museum in Jerusalem,there is no comparison .
Sobering and somber. Surprised there were almost no actual exhibits to view. Confusing layout. Overall disappointing as there are so many stories to tell.
It addresses the Holocaust socially, financially and politically but also the current issues of genocide around the world.  There is an exhibit that speaks to explaining the Holocaust to children.
From the moment the elevator opens the exhibit grips you and takes you on a very emotional and powerful journey. By far the best museum we visited.
Lots of history. Impressive.  Arrive early and avoid the long lines. Near the National Mall. You will.enjoy it if you like history.
I suggest that everyone that visits DC should never miss this tourist spot.  Very enlightening and makes you reflect about life in general.
Not much else I can say. Aside from Yad Vashem it's the most respectful and informative museum on The Holocaust.

It was good but being that we didn't get there until after 4:30 pm and it was a Saturday the permanent pass didn't run after 4:30 pm. Next time we have a chance to go back we need to come here earlier in the day.

We arrived upon opening and were able to stand in line to secure tickets for the permanently collection.

There were FAR too many people on the permanent collection at our ticket time.  We could barely read the multitude of captions on the myriad of photographs due to all the congestion from people standing in front of us. We were disappointed that there were very few artifacts other than photos, especially on the top floor where the tour begins.  It was a huge let down to get to the shoe room and find the shoes were not there due to refurbishing.

I would NOT recommend this museum for non-reading children.

The BEST PART OF THE ENTIRE MUSEUM was a lecture from a Holocaust survivor.  His speech was meaningful, poignant and incredibly relevant.  Tears were shed by many audience members.

I would not recommend by his museum to others except to attend a survivor lecture (which are streamed on-line).

The matzo ball soup at the cafe was delicious (and expectedly pricey).

I went with an 8th grade class trip to the museum on a Sunday. It was PACKED. Like, shoulder to shoulder, barely able to move, could hardly see or read the exhibits type of packed, which is why I downgraded a star. What we were able to see was unreal and very emotional (namely the shoes, train car, barracks, children's drawings). We all would love to go back on a calmer day to see all of the exhibits.

P.S. The museum does NOT take Discover, so have another form of payment ready!
An incredibly detailed account filled with information, so much info that one cannot absorb the detail provided. A must visit in Washington DC

After eventually working out how ticketing was done, we killed time waiting for our slot to enter the main exhaustion. A thought provoking and educational display over a number of floors. We did find our senses dulled after the first floor, not being able to concentrate and read everything on each and every display. I did learn a few things and would say that it is well laid out describing backgrounds and time lines rather than just the atrocities inflicted. Worth a visit.

I have an intense interest in the subject and have utmost respect for the awful suffering of the Holocaust victims and their families.  There was no way that the exhibits could really be appreciated because of the large amount of people let in at the same time.  If the intent was to make people feel like cattle it was a success, but there was little chance to take in and read about the exhibits.

Visited here to pass the time whilst waiting for our BEP tour next door.  Learned nothing about the subject I didn't already know.  If you want a proper holocaust experience go to Auschwitz in Poland, went there in 2008 and cried all the way through it.  I suppose this museum may serve a taster to those who know nothing about the Holocaust, but as i said i found it a bit boring but that's obviously just my opinion, other reviewers obviously differ!

Got right in with military ID (without reservations). Thank you. Difficult subject made more difficult by huge crowds and people trying to read posters on both sides of the hallways. Not very well designed if you ask me. (Much different than Newseum). How can anyone deny existence of the holocaust. 

Such atrocities and depravity so alarming... but not unprecedented- rape of Nanking, killing fields in Cambodia, Rwanda, Soviet massacres under Stalin, etc. War is hell but how can anyone justify such inhumane behavior is beyond me.

We reserved timed-tickets prior to our trip...I recommend this. An emotional exhibit with a ton of pictures and information.  It was really busy and sometimes couldn't read complete exhibits.  The children's side of the museum was so well done.

We went to the mint next door to find it packed and unless we had a reservation it, it was out of time. So we went next door by mistake to this museum - BIG MISTAKE. This is not a place for either kids to go to or be it near national mall with lots of kids. I don't know what the Parks System was thinking.

Well, first we were not sure whether this museum is for us. But after entering, we were taken on a tour to experience the real life of one of the holocaust victims. This, you inditify yourself with that person and feel her/his pain in your heart which makes the whole museum a never-to-be-forgotten experience. We took a while afterward to arrive back in the present. (Some displays are not for children but they are behind higher walls so that kids cannot see. This may also be a warning to adults not to watch everything behind a wall as it could tear your heart. - Crazy wha man is able to do towards his own!)

The museum limits entrance to control crowds, but I admit that my visit on a Saturday afternoon, the crowds were nearly claustrophobic. There is a lot of reading in the galleries, a lot of videos, and the crowds come down to a near halt as people try to absorb the information. If I wasn't a professional historian who already knew much of the information being relayed, the crowds would have made it impossible to enjoy my visit. They nearly did. And the crowds were oddly concentrated: right after you exit the elevators, they start through much of the upper floor, but they space out a bit on the second floor, before jamming up again, then space out a bit on the third, before jamming up. And I mean complete halt, no one moving, crowds. If you have claustrophobia at all, it is going to be overwhelming at times. PS, do not allow double wide baby strollers in your museum.

The exhibits themselves are moving and I saw more than a few people with tears in their eyes. The history of man's inhumanity to man is on full display here. I will admit the special free children's exhibit on "Daniel's Life" was quiet well done for children. Adults could learn something from it as well. 

**A small traveler's hint: there are two entrances to the museum. When I arrived the first, main entrance, had a 30-45 minute security line. I went around to the second, rear entrance and the line was 10 minutes through security. If one line is too long, look for the other entrance on the opposite side of the building.

This somber and moving experience stand as a true reminder of the dangers of government tyranny over it's people, and a painful lesson that we cannot be bystanders as others are discriminated against, persecuted, and executed. Moving and well done.

The layout of this facility is terrible.  There is barely any seating, and guests enter into the main exhibit in enormous waves every hour or so.  This makes the already difficult to navigate museum VERY crowded, hot, and genuinely unpleasant.  Also, and it may just be me, but I felt like the entire museum was very redundant.  Plus, most of what I read, I already knew.  The majority of school districts in the United States educate students on the holocaust these days.  There isn't much else to learn with the exception of some minuscule details.

The museum is free and effectively explains the rise of Hitler and the Nazis and the progression from harassment of Jews to the elimination of them and other people that the regime considered undesirable, including Poles, gypsies, mentally and physically impaired, gays, political opponents.  
Especially powerful are the cards that you get going into the museum which briefly describe the lives of real people who lived through the period covered by the museum.
I found the smells particularly impactful.  The wood of the train car, the shoes seized from those headed to concentration camps, the candle memorial room.
The museum helps you understand how the Nazis came to power and how such oppression could come to pass again if the citizenry does not stand for the civil rights of all Americans, not just the ones we most identify with.

We visited on Memorial Day weekend. Had tried to get tickets to the permanent exhibit online ahead of time but they were unavailable. So we arrived at 10am, waited in a long line outside and went in to try to get tickets. It was 11am by the time we got in and we were given tickets for 1pm. Unfortunately we couldn't stay that long so had to give up on the permanent exhibition. We did get to see some other non permanent exhibits though ( including a very interesting film on Hitler's rise to power in Germany)  so we at least saw something. I agree with the previous poster that the ticketing system is a bit confusing. There were literally hundreds of people sitting around in the open spaces waiting for their tours. So my advice is to go there with the intention of spending most of your day there. Don't have anything else on your agenda. The videos that were on the little screens around the exhibits were for the most part very moving and emotional. This is a museum that everyone should see and I only wish that it somehow could be put online for all the world to see.

We got our tickets in advance and it was 3:00 on a Wednesday afternoon and it was so crowded it was hard to even get to read much of anything in the memorial museum. I was a little disappointed. My 8 year old son really enjoyed Daniels story even though it made him sad to learn about it.

Got to museum at 3:00, was open until 5:15 HOWEVER tickets to the MAIN attraction were already given out early in the morning and the remaining exhibit was uneventful.  Should be noted somewhere that you need to reserve these "free" tickets early otherwise don't waste you time going there.  Disappointed

This should be a place for everyone to visit if you are visiting DC.  It is very sobering and hopefully remembered by everyone.  We were able to actually visit with a couple of the survivors that were there that day to take questions.

After a LONG (several miles) to reach he museum from our hotel, we were greeted with a "we are out of passes for the day". We were quite disappointed because the passes are required to view the main parts of the museum.

We were allowed to enter and there were still a couple of 20 min movies to watch, and a few exhibits that did not require passes. Would like to return with full access one day.

One huge negative: no access to water except public water fountains. No vending, etc.

The U.S. Holocaust museum in Washington D.C. is well worth the visit. It is an emotional and highly educational experience. Not only does it tell of the rise of Nazism in Germany and the horrific atrocities perpetrated on Jews, Gypsies, Homosexuals, and non- Jews, it also brings up the holocausts perpetrated in the Sudan, Cambodia and other countries. This museum is teaches us so much and should be a definite on anyone's visit to D.C.

This memorial has just so much information, and has many videos, photos, and readings that really educate you on the importance of understanding it. It has videos from back when it was actually happened, and has real artifacts.

Huge disappointment!  This was a horribly weak presentation of one of the most horrific cases of genocide in man's history.  The museum was not easy to navigate, on a quiet day there were still difficulties with bottlenecks and figuring out where to go, and the parts that were accessible without a ticket were quite frankly a joke and failed to evoke any of the emotions that should be overwhelming in such a memorial.  Oradour-sur-Glane in France left our family introspective and deeply moved.  The US Holocaust Museum left us feeling frustrated and deeply disappointed.  So much more could have been done with the space to educate the huge number of people that tour this museum each year.

Your ticket to the main exhibits are timed, but the frist gallery was jammed with people. To many to take in the first gallery at all. Many of the interactive exhibits were not working. The galleries that were open(untimed) were much better.

The museum is very informative and well designed. It was an humbling learning experience. There were so many things that I personally didn't know and was really overwhelmed. This place gets crowded so I would plan accordingly. We spent approximately 2 hours here.

This is a moving exhibit about the Holocaust.  You pick up a card at the beginning and learn at the end whether you lived or died. There are some exhibits that are intentionally staged to prevent kids from viewing...realism everywhere

I gave average rating because of the limited number of tickets to the permanent exhibit available each day.  Tickets are gone months in advance, so make sure you look far enough ahead before you plan your travel.  Personally, I think if the museum wants us to appreciate the full experience, there should be more tickets available.  I couldn't see many people inside the exhibit and the only tickets left for that day were for military.

This place is serious business and not for the light hearted holiday maker. If you've settled into the notion of a fair, brutal, emotional pummeling, then go.  Searching for an analogy, I'd say lthough I appreciate the experience and hope others will take it in on their own terms, I cannot recommend it, much as I cannot recommend quitting smoking, for the experience. The value of doing it cannot be understated, but the misery cannot be overstated.

I learned so much of the history I was not aware of.
This is not for the feint of heart.  So heartbreaking, and
sadly, so true.  You actually walk through a train car that
bought so much tragedy to families.

Very moving and powerful.  The museum is amazing.  The exhibits are thoughtful, informative, and thought-provoking.  The only negatives are that it takes a very long time to get in, so you end up waiting in line outdoors, which can be bad, depending on the weather. The other negative is that the building is sooooooo crowded that you get pushed along with the crowds and cannot really see the museum at your own pace. That being said, I was moved beyond words.
During my visit, however, I was disappointed by the behavior of many visitors... people who were texting, fooling around, and snapping inappropriate photos on their cell phones.  Of course this is not the fault of the museum and there is nothing the museum can do about this (except for maybe make a no electronics policy), but it did take away from the overall experience.  In spite of some poor behavior of the public, I was very moved by this museum and I think it is one of the most important, must-see museums in the world.

By far the best museum we visited in Washington. We have all learnt about the Nazis during WW2 at school, but the level of content on display is quite overwhelming. 
Its gets quite busy there, but what a wonderful and confronting depiction of all the events, that led to this devastating disregard of human life and the role of that Hitler played. 
There are also audio recordings and some short interviews of the survivors of the concentration camps. Just speechless!!!

After fighting entry into the memorial (same security as the airports), I was amazed how small and understaffed it was.  Miminal artifacts and far too many exhibits that cost extra.  The only two reviews that were worth the effort were the Memorial Rotunda and the walk-through of a mock-up of a house.

We were disappointed.  There was only one good exibit and that was the one about the boy and his diary.  If you wanted to see more good exibits you had to pay.  So this museum is not free as they claim it is.
talk about sadness...I dont know how  a jewish person could go through without breaking down....at the same...time....everyone should see it.....changes your perspective ....

Worth a visit, but best to book tickets to see the whole museum.  We didn't know that you needed separate tickets for that and on that day were sold out, which was very disappointing. The Hall of Remembrance was worth seeing.

This is a great museum.  It is well laid out and has so much information in it that is all so interesting.  We took our teenage daughters who also appreciated the visit.  It was very poignant and led to some lengthy discussion when we left. Highly recommended.

Worth it to stand in line for tickets.  The museum is very informative, sometimes overwhelmingly so.  Visited with 2 10 year olds.  They were very moved by the experience.  Actually, moved to tears by the end.  Must do on your visit.

I agree with other reviewers who say that this is a place you should visit. The material is important and it doesn't take long to recognize some parallels between then and now (for example, what if the US and other countries did agree to take in a significant number of Jewish refugees?). The video containing accounts from survivors was harrowing and the most emotionally affecting for me, as was the section devoted to people who actually helped save even a handful of families or children. It was so inspiring, despite the other more somber areas of the museum.

I have to admit, though, that I expected more. Maybe there is too much hype. But there were also SO MANY PEOPLE trying to look at photos and read captions and stories that I had to abandon some of the sections altogether and I think it prevented me from really immersing myself. I hate to say it, because this would make the wait even longer, but it might be better to allow fewer in at a time so it's not so frustrating. Also, note for parents: Maybe consider whether or not you should bring the kids if there is a chance that they are going to play games on their phones rather than pay attention to the museum. I saw this in every room, in every section. Sometimes I'd even get run into repeatedly by a teen staring at his phone. 

I noticed security being pretty rude to people coming in as well, and that certainly doesn't make one feel very welcome.

All in all, it's worth seeing and I did learn quite a few things. But prepare yourself for crowds, and consider going as off-hours or as off-season as you can.

We were expecting more to this museum.  It was nice and was educational but it was not what we thought it was going to be.  We have visited actually holocaust camps in Europe so we were expecting more than what was there.  But for people who never have visited a holocaust camp in Europe, this place would be very good with a lot of information as well!

I toured this facility with a group of middle school children. The staff was extremly rude to the children. Many threats of not being admitted and various instances of drill seargent type of tactics made me feel I was in a prison camp. I get it the holocaust was horrible and should never have happened. Childern wishing to ride on a buss all night should be treated more like friends than potential enemies. Especially when they were not misbehaving.
Spent 3 hours there & could have spent another hour. Extremely well done - not too gruesome, but explains the Holocaust well. Worth it to stand in line early for tickets.

If you only see one museum in DC, this has to be the one. It seems incredible that millions were slaughtered while a nation and the world watched. It is very well illustrated and follows a timeline from the rise of the Nazis in Germany to the end of WW2. A moving and emotional experience.

I was expecting a better organized museum. There did not appear to be any flow or go here first, or do this instructions. I love the fact there is such importance and respect placed on the tragic events, I just wish it was easier to navigate and actually see items on display throughout the museum. If you did not know how the Holocaust began or why, it seems the only thing you get from this museum is Jewish people were killed by Nazi Germans.

The museum was interesting, however I was disappointed by the amount of stuff to read rather than see. I would say 75% of my time was spent reading history rather than seeing real artifacts from the time period. It was a great history lesson, however.
A must visit museum. It's very sobering. Not great for small kids, some very graphic pictures. A good reminder for all to see, and the museum is presented in a very realistic way.
Only go if you like to cry. Its depressing and you will love it or hate it.  I do not like the glorification of the Mighty Natzie's, 'Final Solution." let these people rest please.

I liked the museum but my son thought it was lacking. I'm a masters level historian and my son is 20 years old. He's very artifact oriented and, while there are artifacts, this museum relies heavily on stories, words and pictures. It's more of a documentary than a museum.

The museum itself is wonderful, but the experience was not good due to the crowds allowed in. The first room of the tour was packed full of people waiting just to be able to see/read parts of the exhibit, and it was HOT. Some didnt wait in line and just went around others but then would push in when they wanted to read something, slowing things down for everyone else who actually did wait. People were 6-8 deep in front of every single glass, and I think we moved 20 feet in half an hour. We read everything and still spent most of our time just standing and waiting to move. 

After the first few rooms, it got a bit better but not much. They just allow in way too many people at once, and its a constant flood. You cant go slower or speed up to get out of the crowds like you often can with other timed entrance tours. We were really looking forward to this, but were disappointed by the experience. I definitely would not try to visit again on a day when all of the tickets are taken because theyre just allowing in too many at a time. Especially with the number of people who were coughing and obviously sick in the crowd, it was not a positive experience. Too bad because the museum would be great otherwise.

I have always been a big history buff. I wanted to see this and learned a bit more about the war and Hitler. Once we went to the big exhibit via the elevator  and walked our way down it got more depressing. I have always been sympathetic to the Jewish nation and upheld Judaism. This exhibit seems to make you feel guilty for not being Jewish and suffering. NOTHING is ever mentioned of how Jews have overcome and shown success in the post war world.

Well run museum..staff was attentive and very knowledgeable. We went in late February so we only had a short wait. We have been to Auschwitz and Nuremberg and had seen a lot of similar information. What was different about this museum was the listening room where they continually play the voices of survivors telling their stories. They also showed how people reacted in the United States..who tried to help and who turned a deaf ear. They also highlighted different camps in Europe..ones you may have heard little about.
All need to go and experience this museum.

I arrived only 2 hours before closing due to long lines earlier in the day.  There is SO much to see and read and you need more than 2 hours.  Very impactful, but I found some of the captions a bit preachy, which was off-putting.  The museum staff was rude and flippant and basically told us we'd be thrown out at 5:30 on the dot.  I get it, museums close, but there are more tactful ways to urge people to be timely.  The woman who ushered us into the elevator basically scolded us for coming in so late.  Also, the staff should remind people not to stand directly up against the glass of the exhibits blocking everyone else's view - I got so frustrated at people who were completely oblivious to the other people who were trying to read the exhibits and watch the videos.  The museum is structured in such a way that you can easily see everything without being on top of it.
