Even so, a large percentage of the patrons acted as though they were the only people in the museum (of course, I think this happens a lot in this day and age anywhere - people seem to completely lack awareness of their surroundings, which is why they stop right out side of revolving doors and escalators--it isn't specific to this museum).
The Museum is a good place to have an idea of what the holocaust was, as they use creative ways to get through to adults and kids!
People just stand around....lines do not move.  Can't imagine this place during the busy time of the year.  Confusing layout.

First I must say, I have read many books on the holocaust and other parts of World War II.  I was really looking forward to this museum, only to be disappointed. I've seen other museums do a far better job.  No disrespect to the designer & creator, but it's a huge building, but they have not utilized the space well in telling the story of the holocaust.  If you were an individual who didn't know a lot about the holocaust, you would walk out knowing the basics, but they have much more space for more detailed exhibits.  The remembrance hall was very nice and well done.  Daniel's story was well done.  I didn't expect to see exhibits on other country's genecides. Others standing around us were confused and trying to figure out where it fit into the holocaust.  When I walked into the museum I expected to see a lot more info. on the holocaust, but it just wasn't there. With as many people who visit this museum, I think they did a disservice to really getting the message across well, so it never happens again.  Why not a wall of photos (many exist) of those who died in the holocaust.  Granted they don't have them all, but photos tell a story and many of those stories have been documented.  Personal stories are part of the prevention.  Where are they???????

I've now been to three Holocaust Museums and this was one of the best.  A bit confusing to me when you read you need a ticket for a visit time.  Not sure I still understand that because when we arrived we went right in to the top floor (??).  Anyway, the museum is really good and well done.  I've visited the one here in Southfield Michigan (large Jewish community) that is equally good and the one at the Imperial War Museum outside London was a bit better.  Always heart tugging to speak to a camp survivor.  Somber topic, but the museum does a great job.

We didn't know that you need to arrive early to get a ticket to go through the whole museum!  We enjoyed the maybe 1/3 of the museum we got to go explore.  Very moving.  But we would have loved to go through the whole thing.

Immediately upon arrival at the museum, I was told by armed men in uniform to get quietly in a straight line and pass through "SECURITY."  I joined my fellow free and brave American countrymen and complied.  This was the most revealing part of the experience, if any part of it was intended to make us wonder "How could this happen?"

I was most looking forward to visiting the USHMM of all the museums in our 2.5 week trip across the East Coast of the USA. The museum itself was really and truly eye-opening and had a lot of moving content. It was really insightful to read all about it, and view the artefacts. However, on the day we visited we arrived at opening time - we went into the museum which was so full you could barely move, let alone read any of the information and content or view any of the images etc. We went in to one of the theatre showings to view the 14-min clip hoping it wouldve calmed down by the time we came out, unfortunately that wasnt the case. There were several group guided tours on the day we visited (I believe police force) and it became so busy and actually I wish we had visited on a different day. One of the women conducting the group tour physically moved me out of the way as I was stood in front of the glass, reading. She then continued talking to her group and said Right thats better, now we can see. Completely inappropriate, we were all there visiting the museum and no one person or group is more important than another - to be completely honest, I was really taken aback and this ruined our trip.

I would love to visit another time but unfortunately as we live in England and this was whilst we were on vacation, I doubt we will be visiting again for a while. Such a shame.

Every one said this is a 'MUST SEE' museum, after moved to DC for two years, I tried to get in a couple of times, every time when I see a queue in front of the entrance, I turned around and left.  Figuring that there will always be another time.  During the summer, you need to book your visit on their website, but in the winter you do not have it.  I decided New Year's day is the day for me, because people will be too drunk to get up to go to the museum.  Whether my speculation is correct or not, I have no idea. However, there was no queue after parking the car.  So I got in.  There is a no photo policy of any exhibit but one can take photos outside the main door and in certain public area.

I found that what is nice about the museum was that it is like reading a book, and everything was organized in a time line fashion.  However, there are not that much exhibits but a lots of photos and some video footage.  That is what I like about it.  I have been to the Dachau concentration camp in Germany, so I did not actually felt I was overwhelmed by it.  Probably because having actually been to the gas chamber and seen other lab with human experiments, things I saw here had less impact on me. However, this is a great place to learn about the history and pay respect to all those who lost their life for the senseless violence.  No museum or exhibits can ever tell the sufferings.  I am sad and touched by the survivor's courage.  It is definitely worth going though.

We had no idea (mostly my fault for not doing more research before deciding to go) that a limited number of passes to the main exhibit are available and sold at the crack of dawn each day. Needless to say, I did not have one. Without a pass you can see the children's exhibit and some of the special exhibits on other genocides for free. But without the pass you really miss the entirety of what this museum is about.

It was a solemn experience. Every person should visit this museum; however, prepare to have serious conversations if you bring children, mine are between 11-14, and they have read about the holocaust in schools.

This museum was astonishing. It is very well done. You walk through this museum and you feel like you are hearing these stories for the first time (and honestly there is so much information--you are bound to learn more than you ever knew before). I walked through this museum and felt so much sadness and confusion. I could not grasp the hatred or superiority that would cause one individual to create such a massive overflow of destruction on so many groups of innocent people. You cannot leave this museum without a renewed appreciation for the freedoms that we currently have in this country and the inspiration to LOVE other people. This museum makes you feel and I believe that makes it a MUST-SEE in D.C.
If you have a problem with tight spaces, avoid.  I think the layout and display at Dachau was better put forth in an attempt to get your head around the enormity of what occurred.

When coming to this museum, you need to be emotionally prepared to take in all of the history. I visited at a time where there was a field trip with lots of middle school students who were not on their best behavior so that did not make my experience the best but I would definitely recommend coming here any other time.

As a Jew, I'm taking the liberty to say that this museum is the most boring museum in DC. The Holocaust is being presented in an extremely shallow way. In one of the exhibits there's a button that says "press here to see where is Germany"... 

For kids and people who never heard about the Holocaust in...
Be prepared to spend a lot of time in here. It is definitely at least a half day's worth of sights. Very interesting to see the progression and how it all happened

Perhaps because we have been to Yan Vashem in Jerusalem, this museum was an enormous disappointment in comparison. The exhibits flow from one to another without a timeline or historical context. the signage is in a small font and the inadequate lighting contributes to an overall inability to read them. The crowds, obviously, did not help. And although I appreciate young parents not wanting to shut themselves out of museums, pushing an infant in a stroller through these crowds only exacerbated a poor situation. We saw a film that explored the rise of Hitler which was informative, but by and large got very little out of the visit. There were four of us - all in our 70's and avid museum attendees, and we were all in agreement. Far from the best.

My last visit here was 13 years ago and I remember the impact it had on me then. This time I took my 14 year old daughter who studied the holocaust in school over the past year and thought it would provide added information and reflection. Without a doubt there was added info - lots and lots and lots of text. As other accounts report, the visit begins following an elevator ride to the top floor when you step off to a crowded room of people competing for space to read the numerous didactics. Why cant certain Museum visitors make room for others to look/read? Bypassing the first few halls helped us through the frustration as the crowd thins out - what was missed, the rise of Nazi power in Germany can be easily Googled. Further along are displays and exhibits more compelling than the dizzying array of text.  Bottlenecks crop up and crowding issues force more bypassing. While a lot is taken in, unfortunately much is also missed. Otherwise, this is a compelling, comprehensive focus on a horrible period in human history.

An amazing walk back through time. The stories all heart breaking of the pain and loss. This museum tells the story of the rise of Nazi power prior to WW2 and their evil solution. Voice recordings and photographs bring the history alive making it all real.You become connected to this human tragedy with hopes we never forget or ever repeat.

This museum is one of the best in DC. The combination of forums is key to exhibiting this tragic historic event. It's obviously not a "feel-good" museum. I've been a couple of times but prefer to go to other museums for repeat visits.  Once here is enough.  Make sure to go first thing when they open to get tickets. Then you come back later in the day on the specified time.
Ok but crowded and sort of depressing subject matter. Worth a visit however
This one got to me, I did not know that people could do so many things like this , these people needed to be killed they did not deserve to live . this is the place for the  A bomb.  not the japs.
Not well place to visit. A place to torture people. get surprised when see shoes and hall. and hope never visit this kind of museum again

This museum was an excellent and informative piece of history.  The chronological way the information is presented is a realistic view of what was happening during these times.  It is a sad rememberence of how bad things got so quickly.  It is incomprehensible to think people could treat other human beings this way.

While I feel the message of this museum is very important, the organization is atrocious. You can tell they try to limit the number of people who come through the museum by issuing timed tickets, but the afternoon we were there was rough. There were hundreds of people, including several field trips, walking through the exhibits. Aside from feeling rather claustrophobic, it was difficult to read or take in many of the artifacts and displays with so many people in one place. By the end I was ready to get out and away from the mobs of students. We will probably visit again in the future, but highly unlikely.
This museum requires booking tickets in advanced due to crowds. Made the mistake of going and not being able to get in. You can book timed tickets online. Great museum and definitely worth the time!

We are not youngsters, so most of what we saw here has been seen in bits and pieces before.  Walking through this very crowded display, however, puts everything into perspective in a very powerful and significant way.
The events depicted here can NEVER be forgotten of denied, and the museum does a spectacular job of keeping the record straight.
Seeing all of the displays, however, was very difficult for Charlie, simply because she was confined to her power-seat, and the throng of people simply would not yield.

This was an interesting museum, however it wasnt on my primary list of attractions.  I happened to walk by it on my way from the war memorials to the Smithsonian and figured I would take a quick peek.  It was worth the time, however it was so cold inside the building that it was unbearable after an hour as well as dealing the amount of tourists inside.  There is much information regarding Hilters rise to power and the atrocious committed by the Nazis.
Was a bit disappointed. Thought there would be a bit more. But I enjoyed it. I don't think I would ever go back. There were two women there that were survivors

There is not much to see in this museum. My wife is wheelchair bound and we did it in less than 2 hours. Syria info on third floor. A childrens exhibit on first floor. Basement had info on the US and how US and allies did not believe what they were hearing about the camps. Would recommend a trip to Germany to see a real holocaust museum.

First off, not a place for children under 12. In some cases, parents are instructed that children are not permitted. For that, I believe it is Dr. Mengeles lab. They have that lowered in a glass case to observe for adults. For the entire walk through, videos included, you should reserve 5-6 hours. Too much information, not to be missed.

This museum was very educational as well as emotional! Each film was very detailed with facts of what happened during the Holocaust, went with a group of students and they were very respectful in the museum.

I visited this museum shortly after it first opened some 20 years ago and came away just shattered by the cruelty and emotional turmoil on display in exhibit case after exhibit case. I went back last weekend and did not have anywhere near as satisfying a visit. Put simply, the museum can't cope with the number of visitors. You have no time to stand in front of a case for even 30 seconds without being budged and jostled by the throng, and therefore you can't just stand there and think. Crowds of schoolchildren flock through the halls. For some reason the museum allows guides to take individual guests through the museum and these guides are loud voiced and make even more noise. This museum has a crucially important lesson for mankind - "This must never happen again" - and is unfortunately sabotaging how it gets that message across. The museum needs to bring in a system of timed tickets.

We visited on a Friday in July - we did not have advance tickets, but entered the lobby around 12:30 and were able to get same day tickets for 1:30.  We went to the cafe until our ticket time - note that the cafe is outside the lobby of the Museum and  you have to go through separate security for the cafe and the museum.  There are exhibits that you can see without the ticket and three floors that can only be visited with a timed ticket.  The ticketed exhibit is a designed experience, meaning that everyone follows the same "route" through the museum.   You take an elevator to the top floor and wind down through three floors.  The day we went, the museum was very crowded and it was difficult to see adequately or read the information plaques (which were often mounted very low, increasing the difficulty - they were easily blocked by guests).  Great care has been taken to explain not just what the Holocaust was, but how it happened, and the gradual erosion of Jewish freedoms in Europe.  There is also an emphasis on personal narratives, with many listening stations where you can hear accounts from survivors.  Again, on a crowded day, this was hard to negotiate.  You really need to allow at least 2 hours, and perhaps more to travel through the three floors.  The museum focuses on the horrors, but also on the less well known stories of resistance by both Jews and others.
we did not plan this well and did not realize there would be such a queue and wait. we did enjoy the visit to downstairs area. it gives a good overview what happened in nazi germany from 1933

I have visited twice - both times there were groups of young teens who were loud and disrespectful (overall) of the content of this museum.....sitting around on the floor, with cell phones, chewing gum talking loudly - yes they are "kids" but chaperones were MIA.  Recommend you visit when it is not spring break.  It is awe-inspiring, educational and will break your heart - something we all need to see.  Only other complaint is you have to exit the museum to visit the "café" and return through security (again).

I'm writing this review so that others interested in this museum won't experience the same disappointment that I did. The museum offers many films, displays and artifacts, but I was very interested in seeing the shoe display. All the comments from reviewers and even on the USHMM mention the shoes. How moving they are. Well it would be nice if they mention that the shoes aren't currently there  - the display is empty!!! I understand that they must restore displays from time to time, but let the public know. Very disappointing to stand in line for tickets, wade through the crowds, only to find an empty display case.
We took two teenage grandchildren on this tour. It is a piece of education that everyone should have so we never forget what horrors humans can commit.

We had heard many things about the experience and wanted to attend.  The walk thru involved a lot of time and not completely interactive.  Probably not a great idea for a 5 year old.  We liked the Smithsonian museums much better and would put those ahead of seeing this if you are limited on time.
This is one place you MUST SEE when in Washington D.C.!!  Be sure to sit and listen to the interviews at the end!!
this is a historic account that brings to life the ideology and propaganda of ADOLFF HITLER.artifacts and time sensitive videos are included.

We were travelling with children (ages 8-15).  The children's area of this museum was enough for this age group.  The message was strong and emotional but delivered in a way that was appropriate and we highly recommend seeing the exhibit.

While there are incredibly moving memorials in other countries -like Europe and Israel- where you can walk through actual concentration camps, etc., the US Holocaust Memorial Museum is the best I've seen in the US and a fitting tribute to remember those who were killed so we do not allow it to happen again.  It is somber and emotionally draining, but worth the time.  Leave a couple hours so you don't feel rushed through the exhibits.  You need tickets (free) so show up early to pick them up and have plans to do something else in the immediate area or a book in case you have a wait.  If you know/are someone in the military, you/he/she can pick up tickets for immediate entrance into the museum with no wait; you will get on the next elevator.

Great for a teen who is learning about world history.  The whole experience was moving a tiny but disturbing you might want not to bring a little child who is squemish.  Free for the most part but I did not do the tour.

TheHolocaust Museum was one of the " must sees" on our recent trip.  It was all that we had been told it was. Quite disturbing and heart wrenching and at the same time the stories of people who risked everything to help and protect European Jews and the resolve of the Jewish Community was uplifting.  We arrived late in the day, about an hour before closing and were disappointed that one of the permanent exhibits that we had been told about (where visitors are given a passport with the information of a concentration camp prisoner) closed an hour earlier.  So, arrive at least 2 hours prior to close to see and experience everything.

This was a really great museum but we have to give it 4 stars because there were just too many people and that it made it impossible to read all the information displayed at the permanent exhibit.  Other than that it really is a 5 star experience that everyone must go and learn about.  Its so moving and important to remember the past so that we dont repeat it in the future.
Very moving.  Extensive exhibit.  Leave plenty of time.

I saw this museum as a teenager, and my disappointment was great enough that I have not been back. I dont know what I was expecting. I was expecting to be more stirred, more deeply moved, to learn something new, perhaps, but most of the museum was rather like walking through a line of documentaries (a lot of television screens showing documentaries are scattered throughout). I dont feel like I learned or experienced anything I could not have learned or experienced by watching PBS or the History channel. I think a memorial would have been of more effect than a museum, and, in fact, the one portion of the museum I found most moving was the Hall of Remembrance with the eternal flame and the scripture carved on the wall. This memorial, standing alone, might have been more powerful than having an entire museum. This memorial I could rate at 5, but the museum itself is a 2 at best.

We then headed to the Holocaust Museum (not a place Tom liked to visit).   The most meaningful spot there for Tom was the Daniels story, which was very moving.  Tom had been to the Holocaust museum in the upper level of the Military Museum in London and was NOT interested to repeat another glimpse at what horrors occurred during WW II to an innocent group of people.

The exhibits provided much information about the political situation at the time which made us realize that everyone has a part to play if we are to prevent similar situations from occuring in the future. The exhibits helped to give us a glimpse of the scale of the holocaust as well as the brutality that can be inflicted - something that is very difficult for most of us to understand.  Some of the pictures are very graphic.  The video interviews of the survivors were very emotional. It can get crowded so going early and standing in line for the doors to open is not a bad idea. Phototaking of the exhibits is not permitted. Be prepared to spend half a day there even if you selectively read most of the exhibits. Definitely worth the visit.

I've been to many museums and memorials in Washington. I don't believe the Holocaust Museum and Memorial isn't the best I've seen. but you should definitely visit it when your in Washington, DC. Tickets to the full museum aren't easy to get in advance, but if you get to the museum an hour or so before it opens you can usually get tickets for that day. It takes about two hours to go through the whole museum. It's not quite what I expected to see, but like I said, it's a place that made me think a lot about things going on today. I'd go again.

This museum is not suited for children, but is a great place to visit for adults. You have to go there prepared to read a lot and to be ready to absorb a great deal of information. This is also a great place to pay respects to people who suffered during this horrific time.
Information section is completely wrong. You need a ticket just to get in the building and there's very few for walk ups. Check the Museums actual page first.
Families with small kids under 12 stay downstairs and see Daniel s story and talk with a survivor...too emotional upstairs...very educational
This is a very sad remembrance of the  Holocaust. I pray that no one ever has to suffer through that again but I fear it still happens but on a smaller level.

The museum is just okay. What? You say? Yeah, to get a chance to go on the very cool tour; you mut get their early...be ready to go in or if you are lucky to get a ticket for a later time in the day. Very limited tickets for the cool tour.

The museum has several sections. The Permanent Exhibit is the main part which is heavy emotionally and has most of the artifacts. I thought it could've had more to look at and less to read.  It was also tight walking thru.
This is absolute MUST see! From the moment you  begin the tour you will be in stunned silence. The museum is a beautifully done memorial to a tragic time in history. Bring tissues and be prepared.
I enjoyed Daniel's story and teaching the children about some of the terms and cultural influences that have have influenced our modern day culture.  Very moving and informational.
There wasn't much there and mostly everything was something you had to pay for. We were very disappointed.

I'm glad I went but I did have nightmares for weeks afterward.  The information is well presented.  When you walk in you are given an identity card with a photo and bio of a holocaust victim.  Her picture haunts me.  I did find some of the photos a little too graphic for my taste.  I particularly remember a large photo of a young woman who had been operated on on her breast by the "experimenters".  She had a smile on her face and I think it was that she was hoping if she smiled they would be nice to her and maybe not kill her but I'm sure they probably did.  Her smile was just too much to take.

I found this museum WAY to overwhelming. There is 4 floors of hundreds of people shoving and pushing all trying to read one wall. If you had all day, you could actually follow along and read, but I lasted 15 minutes and had to get out. Way to much for me, not recommended

I wanted to love this place.  I'm a world war II buff and can't get enough of the stories and history of the war and things surrounding it.  I was hoping for a deep experience, but I just felt like it was all video displays and plaques on the wall.  There were a few features that were heart wrenching, but overall I wasn't impressed with the setup.

Staff and security were unfriendly.  The layout was terrible.  Like a maze through narrow dark halls.  Did not smell the best because of this.
Skip this place, spend the 3 hours it takes to walk through this place on other venues.

The lines extended out the door into the 98° day to get through security. Then you wait in line to get a ticket that provides entry during specified hours. When your time is reached, you stand in a line to take an elevator up to the exhibits. And once you reach the exhibits, its wall to wall people, over 60 from my quick estimate, in just the first large room. It was so crowded we couldnt see the materials on the wall without waiting for everyone else to slowly shuffle by. Being handicapped and unable to stand for the hour plus it would have taken to get through, we turned around and left. Absolutely no way would someone in a wheelchair be able to navigate. It was far too crowded. The museum is organized such that theres only a single flow through the exhibits. Not a bad thing in itself when showing a timeline. But it creates bottlenecks as the crowd can only move as fast as the slowest person. 

The museum has a fantastic exhibit on America and the Holocaust on the lower level. It was uncrowded and the exhibits clear, concise and the messaging powerful. Unfortunately, where I would have liked to have stopped and spent time reading or watching the newsreels, there were no seats, which made it very uncomfortable. 

My recommendation to you: skip the holiday periods and summer as what would have been a wonderful learning opportunity was soured by the inaccessibility of the exhibits. 

My recommendation to the museum: limit the entrance even more to eliminate the crowded conditions that keep people from seeing the exhibits youve so carefully chosen to display.

I really struggle with rating this museum average based on the nature of the collection and the impact the Holocaust had to society. We pre-booked tickets for a 12:15 on a Saturday which gives us a one hour window to arrive. Upon arrival at 12:16 we were cattled into an elevator with little instruction from staff about a video that most could not see. Upon exiting the elevator, we were faced with total congestion prohibiting us from seeing may of the displays and reading what was there. Most of our time was spent shoulder to shoulder throughout the floors of this museum which minimized the impact the items should have on an individual. We spent nearly 4 hours on location and I was truly amazed at the history and story they told. The displays were well put together and I wish I'd had more time to spend there with far less people.  It's a must see, but a frustrating must see based on their desire to pack people in.

I had an afternoon free while in DC on business. I wanted to visit this museum. I waited in the long line outside, went through security only to be told I had to dispose of my bottled soda that was in my purse OUTSIDE. I had to go all the way back outside to get dispose. I then had to get back in line to go through security AGAIN. Once inside, I waited in line to get the free ticket to see the permanent exhibit. It was 1:30 in the afternoon and they were out of the ticket allotment for the day. I would rather pay to get in than to go through all of that just to be denied. Why were there not signs outside about disposing of bottled drinks? There was a security guard outside but he said nothing at all to any of us standing in line. Why would there not be a sign letting people know that they were out of tickets and you could only get in if you had a reserved ticket? I was disappointed because I really wanted to visit this museum. This was a HUGE FAIL.
Very in depth. Wish it had more visuals. You have to read a lot to learn. May be hard for younger people.
It is a harsh, heart breaking experience. Be prepared and expect to feel what you have felt when reading and learning about the holocaust.

Unfortunately I din't have enough time to look through the whole museum but from what I saw it is a very interatcive but honest respesentation of the Holocaust. It draws important aspects of the events in order to appeal to more age groups and is very effective in doing so.

Special trip into DC to visit the Holocaust Memorial Museum - reviewed web page several times for hours etc and never did they reveal that once you are there there is very little to see without tickets (free) to the permanent exhibit - timed entrance to that exhibit meant we could only get the 4 PM entry - which does not allow enough time to view the exhibit before closing.  Also this might be the most user unfriendly museum I have ever visited  - no maps available - poorly marked other info - like bathrooms!   The permanent exhibit is very good and well done but the rest is just awful and misleading and disappointing.

The museum tells such an important story.  There are parts that are quite graphic, and some may be uncomfortable with it but for the most part is is incredibly well done.  It is also good for all ages.  It is perfect for teenagers, especially those studying WWII.  Make sure to see "Daniel's Story".

We went to the holocast museum and were disappointed to find out you need to get tickets to see the real display.We walked around and saw some generic things,but if you want to see the real deal you need tickets.Sadly they were sold out the day we were there.

Definately go through the Daniels story exhibit. Especially if you have kids!  It runs through the holocaust though a childs eyes and it really helped my kids understand what they must have felt like. All the hype about the permanent exhibition was a bit overated. Overall the exhibit is very sobering. The first floor of the exhibit is primarily about how the nazi's occupied germany. There is alot of reading so be prepared! It was extremely crowded; even with the timed entry. As you decend floors it begins to show more about the actual holocaust but I think we expected to see more of the Jews struggle than the facts about what happened.

I was very disappointed in this museum.  Very difficult to get tickets and while we were there, there were so many that were turned away because there were no tickets left.  The intro video into the museum is awful and not truthful or factual on real history.  I recommend reading Anne Frank or another book instead of going to this museum.  Too many other great places to see in DC.

This is one of the best memorial museums in the world, hands down! Tips when booking your visit, spend the $1 to reserve advance tickets (per person) especially during Summer tourist peak months otherwise you'll have to face long lines starting at 9am to get some admittance tickets. When you plan to visit, try to avoid bringing any liquids, open containers of drinks (typically not allowed but permissible case-by-case basis..more than likely if you've got kids, baby, you'll be ok) Also know everything & everyone passes a metal detector apparatus just as one does when traveling at airports so safety is priority #1 at the museum. The staff is very friendly, helpful & accommodating. Upon arrival, I'd recommend going to the front desk to grab a few museum maps to orient yourself to know where to go, but basically everyone typically starts off by catching the elevators to the left of the entrance to begin their journey through the museum. Before they enter the elevators each person is given a passport-like card with the name and photo of a victim of the Holocaust. Through this method it adds more personal experience as you learn of that victim's story and it really brings the Holocaust home. As you walk through the museum (at a brisk pace) you're entrenched deeper and deeper into the history before the Holocaust, causes & effects and you find yourself immersed in what becomes such a horror of what man's depravity is capable of. There are long corridors here and there throughout exhibits (almost as if to give one a pause or breather to gain one's composure) Plan to spend at least 2-3 hours once starting your museum experience so you're not rushing through it all. You want to give ample time to see all the exhibits and short film exposes the museum offers to bring your journey. All in all this entire museum's purpose to you the Visitor is to expose the truth, facts and to personalize the entire history of the unspeakable horrors so we do not forget and let this happen ever again. It not only memorializes those fallen victims but keeps them alive in each and every one of us that leave that museum and follow our own journey there after.

I was a little disappointed by the museum, because most of the exhibits were just reading about events or stories.  It was hard to stand in one spot and read about something without getting jostled by people rushing past.  Also, a school group was there when I went and they were rude and obnoxious.  A few exhibits were really moving, like the shoes and the railcar, but most of it was lots of reading that was impossible to do because of the crowds.  And I went during offseason.

The Museum is outstanding in detailing the horrors of the Holocaust. It is solemn yet powerful and thought provoking that as humans we should never allow such atrocities to ever occur again - especially with the reality of what is happening globally in various parts of the world. The tour and layout is over several floors commencing from the top .. pacing is at one's own pace; but to fully appreciate and contemplate the Then, The Now and The Possible Future ... I suggest a slow pace. Yes it is sad and yes you do walk out feeling a tad 'off sorts' ... but its an experience I would gladly feel again. 
My wish would be for those countries at present experiencing conflict; to have their leaders see the past/visit the exhibition - hopefully such a reality check might lead to a world in which no conflict exists.

Honestly, I was a bit disappointed, as I have always wanted to take a tour of this place. The admission was only $1 per ticket for advance tickets. I am familiar with the Holocaust, so this experience felt more like a repetitive history lesson. I suppose I was expecting more shock value. The museum is entirely too crowded especially toward the beginning, so that was a downer as well. If you need a refresher on the history behind the ordeal, this is the place to go.

My family (me, my husband, and children ages 10 and 12) couldn't have been more disappointed in our experience at this museum. Despite timed reservations, the museum was incredibly overcrowded.  It was difficult to navigate the people in the crowds and to simultaneously immerse yourself in the experience. Much of the museum requires reading a great deal of information which was made nearly impossible by the number of people pushing their way through and 'jockeying' for position along the way.  You would literally need to spend 8+ hours in this museum. Given all of the other incredible attractions in D.C., it would not be time well spent. All of that said, the recorded testimonials of survivors are incredibly powerful.

Visited the main exhibition hall with my two teenage boys for about 2 hours. I wanted them to see some of the  atrocities that the Nazi did a few decades back .  Even though we did not read and reviewed every single item on the museum , I think a few images were enough for the concepts to sink in. We visited the National Archives and the Martin Luther King memorial to top it up

The online ticket system for timed entry is a mess. I logged on at 7am and all the tickets for same day entry were sold out immediately. I think there must be professional bots snatching them all up. I recommend USHM do something about this, very disappointing. Additionally, I would also like to point out that no other museum still has Covid restrictions. USHM is the only one still doing this kind of stuff.

Always being improved, this museum is especially important for young people to see. They will find it not simply an education experience, but one that will leave a lasting impression on their emotions.

This is one great experience that anyone would love. If you love history and want to know more about the Holocaust this is the place to go. There is something around every corner, you won't regret the experience and feeling that you feel throughout the event
Beautiful experience with my daughters to show them hat we all are equal, and we all have to respect each other..very nice to place to visit

I thought the museum was very well organized and planned.  You are able to pace yourself on the tour and spend as much or as  little time as you  want on each display.  It is not a fun experience but an experience that is worth your time and is essential to learn about for continued education.

This was a very emotional and interesting museum.  We all learned a great deal while we were there.  Next to the Library of Congress this is probably the best museum in DC.  The only negative about it is how crowded it gets.  Not just because of the number of people but because of how it is laid out.  You are all forced to walk down the same exhibit halls.  Most of the exhibits require a lot of reading which holds everyone behind you up.  It took a couple hours to get through it.  Even with those negatives it was still worth a trip.
Although it was a bit unsettling, it is something you have to know about and see. Live videos from that time as well as interviews with survivers and how they feel today.

I've visited Auschwitz and found it incredibly moving. I've visited the Sydney Holocaust Museum and that was also very good - very informative and thought-provoking. For some reason I just couldn't connect with this museum. I found the layout confusing which didn't help but overall just didn't find the exhibits as impactful as those I've seen elsewhere. To be fair I didn't see the exhibit that needed tickets so maybe I missed the key one. I think if you brought children, Daniel's story was probably something they would get a lot from as the settings for his life before and during the war have been set up and it's quite interactive.
The best display we almost missed in the basement.  Having a survivor to speak with is awesome. Pretty soon they weill be gone.  What a blessing to have them now.  Needs some more visual effects.

I felt that this was too gentle a display of THE attempted genocide of the Jewish people in times that remain now most clearly in a generation that is passing on. Children are catered for but less so for senior school and young adults for whom this space should cut to their hearts

This place was daunting.  How do you tell the horror story of Nazi cruelty during World War II, and not have people run screaming from the venue?

I think they did a relatively good job.  There were a lot of places to walk through, as they portrayed the programmatic oppression of people who were of any Jewish descent, marginalizing them until they were forced to get on trains to concentration camps.

There are numerous displays of the camps, the trains, the horrors inflicted on these people by the Nazis.

I guess what bothered me was the historic slant - why didn't the US accept more refugees?  They spent a lot of time showing how people had written and asked for help, only to not get it.  It's definitely told from the side of the refugee, and sadly touches only lightly on the US urge to stay out of the wars after the losses experienced in the Great Depression and World War I.  Those events caused deep scars for the American people - to ignore those scars and say "You should have done more" really accomplishes nothing. 

In any event, if you have the desire to see just how sick and cruel and insane the Nazis were, this is the place you can do that.

We arrived had to go threw security guards which were more aggressive than anything encountered else where in DC. I then found we could not visit the main section because we had not pre booked a slot on the internet. This was a shame as this place is a credit, designed well lending it's self to the story it was telling. The temporary exhibits we were able to see were important, moving and well presented I would loved to have been able to access all areas. A little more flexibility for oversea visitors who may not have internet access and calm the security guard and I would happily advise anyone in DC to visit.

The put extra tickets on their website at 6 am the morning of.  I happened to be awake and was able to get a 230 time.  We got there about 2pm and the line was long long long.  Even when we did get inside long long long.  We enjoyed the museum despite all the people.

Tickets can only be purchased the day of the visit from 9AM or online starting promptly at 6AM, with the museum opening at 10.  Earliest time we could get we could get online was 11.  The museum expanded since I came 6 years ago. 
Be advised, if you're driving and park on the street you have to use a phone app for parking and the max time is 2 hr, so we had to renew since we went here and the Bureau of Engraving, which is right next door.

Like other reveiwers, we arrived to find an enormous line outside of the museum.  Thankfully our hotel had told us to reserve tickets ahead of time, this saved us the trouble of standing in one of the two lines.  
When we entered there were tons of people just milling around.  Eventually we made it to the next line to enter into the elevator.  
Getting off the elevator there were tons of people standing in lines to look at everything.  We had 6 people in our group and it was rather difficult to stay remotely together as there were just SO many people everywhere. (we went around 11 on a Thursday in March)  
I was also surprised at how much the museum focused on the politics and political figures of the holocaust and less on the tragedy of the holocaust.  Maybe it was because it was so crowded, but I just didn't see much at the museum that impressed me or taught me as much as watching "Shindler's List" did.  We have a holocaust museum in my home town of St. Louis, MO, and frankly, I was more moved by the museum here than the one in DC.  
The only emotional part of the visit was the memorial room, but again, maybe that was because the memorial room (where you could light candles) was one of the more empty rooms and, therefore, there was space and time to read things and look around.  The rest of the visit was just so crowded it was impossible to spend much time to really take in the exhibits.
I was really disappointed.  Particularly because after having visited the holocaust museum in MO, my 13 y.o. son was very interested to see the museum in DC and was actually the person in our group pushing for this visit.  Too bad.

The website said you had to have  a ticket ahead of time because its such a busy time, so we did that and bought your tickets on line.  Only to find out that they were just letting everyone in and handing out tickets right then.  It was so crowed you couldn't read most things and it lost the impact of the whole place. I was very disappointed.

Of the displays I saw...I did find them interesting.  However, it was really too crowded to enjoy.  I know its a popular place and that they do limit the number of people entering by time slot...but in my opinion that limit is still too many based on the size of the exhibit.  The first section on the fourth floor is very narrow and there was so much to read, it moved very slowly.  The hallway was completely full with people and we couldnt read most of it because of where we were.  We ended up skipping a big part of that section just to get out of the crowd and get some air moving around us.  The exhibits are well done...dont get me wrong...if you are a patient person who is not bothered by crowds in small spaces you may very well get a lot out of you visit.  We had 3:15 tickets...maybe first thing in the morning is better?

When we got to the museum we didnt find the main exhibit very easily so were a bit disappointed initially. Having gone upstairs and come back down without seeing anything other than three museum shops, we stumbled across a man who directed us into a lift to the main exhibit - it really should be better signposted. 

Once on the fourth floor we walked round three floors of heart wrenching, lump-in-the-throat videos, photos and maps depicting the rise of the nazis, the plight of the Jewish and Roma people (as well as others including the disabled) and the camp liberation. Some of the videos are shielded with warnings about viewer discretion - incredibly difficult to watch but you really have to look to understand the utter horror of what happened. 

Its something everyone should see.

As the title suggests, this museum is very disorganised.  When you walk in there are no signs to direct you where to go
Most of the display is downstairs and it wasn't until afterwards that I found it and the museum closed at 5 and I was unable to finish my tour
What I saw down the stairs afterwards was good

A powerful place of history and what evil has to offer.  Everyone says not to take your children, but NEVER AGAIN mean something to the Jews and you must preserve history and to tell the truth, even to your children.

Even though several people told me I needed to visit, I was not sure I wanted to go. I walked past it twice, but the next day I went in as I was walking back to the hotel.

Wow. This museum is amazing, yet in a very depressing kind of way. I learned much more than I thought I already knew.

The short introduction on the ground floor sets the stage for the whole experience. You really must plan a visit. Do not miss this museum.

I would say that it is all sad. I was even more horrified of those horrific acts against these innocent people. They had to pay for something they didnt do. I believe the museum is pretty good but its kind of confusing to get around, who knows if I missed something. Overall its worth checking out a part of histories saddest moments.

We were not aware that in order to see all of the exhibits you need to apply for a pass on line prior to your visit or get in line at 10:AM the day of your visit to get a pass on first come first served basis. We were too late to get a pass.

This is my second time visiting the Holocaust Memorial Museum in DC and it left a marked impression each time.  The museum begins in the post-world war I era and proceeds chronologically through the Nazi party rise to power and the subsequent genocide that followed, finally ending with the liberation of the concentration camps by American, Russian and British troops.  Allow about 4 hours to take it in and watch the several short films throughout the self-guided tour.

I have lived in Germany for 3 years and visited Dachau and other World War II memorial sites but while emotional and moving, none have compared in impact to the US Holocaust Museum.  A must see.

Although the subject matter of this memorial is very sad, it's definitely worth a visit.  Like all the museums in DC, it was very crowded, which distracted from the experience and your ability to take it all in.  But the holocaust was such an epic tragedy, that as a human being you need to pay respect to the victims and be educated about how it happened in order to prevent something like this from being repeated.  We thought the memorial was well done.

With a time specific reservation,  this was the worst line and least cautious staff. We had seen both the one in New York and LA so we skipped this one. We understand the need and the purpose.  Too bad getting inside is a night mare.
but don't expect to come away feeling uplifted.  The story of Man's inhumanity to Man was brilliantly presented but not for the faint of heart.  Everyone should see this museum at least once.

A very thought provoking place telling stories of things that should never be able to happen again. When we visited we met a holocaust survivor who we were lucky enough to chat to as she was doing a book signing, what a remarkable lady.
I highly recommend this museum.  The shoes brought me to tears.   I'll remember this museum for many years to come.   Everyone should see and understand what happened during WW II.
This museum brought on sad feelings about had bad somebody can treat people. It was very repetitive. It was very crowded and was not about to spend the time needed to really read everything.
I like the subject but there's nothing there to be worth a visit. I dont recommend it... Its free but I dont think its a good choice... Many others very close to this one are better

Luckily because of the cold weather and no needed for passes on December 30th we were able to get in within 10 minutes of standing in a queue. The information desk was helpful and we were on the elevator to see the permanent exhibit within 5 minutes of entry. But the crowds there were thick and I could not walk and read the captions and descriptions adjacent to the photos without being jostled. My 10 year old daughter tried and gave up within 10 mins. We wound down the floors quickly. Somehow the pile of shoes did not bring about a great emotional response.

My daughter found Daniel's story to be more absorbing and useful and so did I. Unlike another poster who said this place was not for children under twelve I think that the emotional maturity level is more important that chronological age. May daughter handled it very well.

I would be more moved by visiting the actual places like Auschwitz which I plan to do in the future

We came here at 1pm and had to wait in a line outside, to get hurded into an elevator and dumped off into another crowd on the top floor.  Place was jam packed with people, temperature was way to warm and it was very tight quarters.  People moved at a snail's pace and it was hard to enjoy.  We spent 4 hours here and we're ready to be done.  A lot of reading and pictures to look at which bottles everything up.  Glad we came, but wouldn't come back.

We had just been to Berlin, so it was a good history lesson to tie in this museum with the tour we had in Berlin. When you walk in you are given a card detailing the life of a person that was in a concentration camp. You follow that card along as you move through the museum. It adds a personal connection to the exhibits. It details the atrocities and at times it was difficult to watch some of the videos. It was rather hot in the museum (and it was cold outside) so we appreciated the coat check.

The Holocaust Memorial is an important museum, yet the way that it is laid out makes it difficult for those going through it who have time limits. There are four levels, but it is easy to spend so much time on the initial level that you have to scurry through the subsequent levels if you are on a schedule. I would suggest thinking through tour alternatives based on time frames as a way to help individuals or groups get a sense of each important topic.

The museum is important. I especially appreciated the initial level that provided context on the political and societal background that helped foster intolerance. It helps me understand that the Holocaust just didn't happen overnight. It was a series of specific actions and events that occurred.

I would suggest that you plan for at least two hours to go through the museum. I hope that the museum would suggest a "highlights" tour for those who have less time.
I thought it was depressing.  I guess there's no way to make the holocaust uplifting, but the museum just felt dark and gloomy to me.  I'm glad we didn't bring our kids.

We did not expect this museum to be this good. Very detailed story of the Holocaust. We did the tour on the permanent exhibit and also did the temporary ones as well. It is a must see.
BVnLJ

Went into the Memorial to see how the Holocaust was presented. Would recommend this as a great learning experience. Book for you tickets ahead of time for easier access, although the service desk is super helpful in helping you obtain tickets.

This is one of the best museums I have ever been to. you will need to get tickets in advance, as it is a very popular place. try to go early in the day before it gets too crowded. It will be a sobering experience, but you will not regret it.

GREAT museum--just not me to read SO much.  It is TOO overwhelming--too realistic and shame on me for not being able to spend the time and appreciate it.  The shoes got me, and I was done.  
To enjoy more, be prepared to stay Hours, know that this is not sugar coated, and try to avoid kids/young groups.

Sadly there were no tickets left when got there but we looked around the rest of the exhibit. It was interesting as a Brit  to see the different way the USA viewed the end of the holocaust..not quite the same way we see it here in Europe! But interesting all the same.

The museum is a must see as it educates us about the atrocities that occurred under the Nazi regime.  The exhibits are very educational and flow very well using many different types of media.  It is great reminder that this sort of racism and bigotry is still happening and each of us must do our part to stop it!

A very interesting museum which helps give an understanding of Hitler's rise to power. If you have been to Auschwitz or other camps it really give a sanitized feeling of what those camps were.  We would have liked to spend more time there, but after 3 hours we were hungry and no place to even get a quick snack there.

great and informative museum.  They have a children's exhibit called Daniel's Story which my daughter enjoyed and it kind of broke it down in a way that a child could understand what happened to the Jew during the Holocaust.  Then in the basement the Children's Wall was nice for my daughter to see.  There were many other exhibits that were really interesting and I think everyone should see this important museum.

The most moving museum that I have ever been to.  I would suggest it for kids 12 & older. A must see in Washington D.C. 
Nice cafe to enjoy once you are done.

I went to this museum in the summer of 2000. 

It was obvious from my first steps inside that a great deal of effort had been put into not only recording the histories of Holocaust victims/survivors and those associated with the tragedies of WW II and pre-war Germany, but that a great deal of thought had been put into using the setting/props to evoke empathy from visitors (by giving one a passport, identity, and history...by herding one into a cattle-car with their family...by having documents and pictures side-by-side with camp artifacts and films of experiments performed on Jewish children).

Of course, the experience is a traumatic one, especially for one like me whose family suffered in Dachau, but what really marred my visit was being herded into a room by security officers with guns, because a white supremacist group had called in a bomb threat.

I plan to go again, next week, if I can handle it.

My top suggestion: the museum is often crowded and exhibits flow in a linear fashion, so if some members of the group get ahead or behind, it is easy to become separated...so have a plan of where to meet with your companions!!

Also, I'm sure security is pretty tight, so be mindfull of what you bring with you, and don't come fresh off a plane with a backpack full of stuff.

A must-see museum in the national capital. It details the rise to power of the Nazis and the Holocaust unleashed on all Jewish communities and other minorities (Gypsies, political dissidents, gays, handicapped people, nationalities viewed as "inferior") that fell within the grasp of this ultimate evil.Sobering and essential viewing.

Spent over 4 hours inside the museum and it wasn't enough to cover everything. The facts were presented in various forms to make it interesting and engaging to its audience. I've learned a lot about history in my visit.

This place is amazing very nice architecture design 
Exposition are very good 
A nice reminder what racism can do to humanity 
Something to remember and never forget

First, I would visit without children if you are interested in doing a lot of reading. Our kids got bored quickly. There are a lot of stories to read. I was expecting more artifacts. You get a card at the beginning and learn the story of what they went through. It starts pre war life and ends with post war life. This was not my favorite museum. If you are short on time, I would do another.

Every exhibit within this museum tells the tragic story from a different lens or perspective, and each of them cannot fail to touch your heart and make you wonder how this degree of inhuman behavior could have gone on...  Be prepared to be depressed when you leave, so plan your visit when you know you are able to tolerate the pain you will feel.

I was very disappointed to finally get to the museum, only to find out we needed tickets for the main part of the building. Without tickets, there was minimal things to do and was disappointed that my kids could not learn such an amazing lesson about the Holocaust.

Our family really enjoyed the Holocaust Memorial Museum. The kids are ages 15 and 13, and had studied the Holocaust in school.  There was so much information, so many video's and so many exhibits here, we were there for about 3 hours.  We could have stayed longer, but had another tour that we had to leave for.

We had to line up to get into the museum only to find that we were then given tickets to enter the memorial which meant we would have to wait another 2 hours before we could go in. There is a couple of other things to see, but not enough to wait for 2 hours. Very disappointing.

The title "Holocaust Museum" is a misnomer.  This is less of a museum and more of a learning experience.  There are very few artifacts and many of those on display are "recreations" of the actual items.  The museum is cramped and the halls are narrow and it is impossible to stand and read your way through the exhibits due to the overcrowding. There are monitors that play videos of information, but you can't stand and watch them because others are moving through.  I expected more, and while I was glad we went, I left with the hollow feeling that the experience could have been much better.

Security is tight. Tour was so crowded we couldn't take it in. It was challenging to reserve a ticket. We paid online and still had to take late afternoon show with huge school groups.  May be when we went but didn't feel welcomed by staff- felt like I was being carted around and bullied.

The Holocaust Memorial is full of information. It is a disturbing subject matter to say the least. However, it IS SO IMPORTANT the people who were directly involved in the Holocaust deserve to be remembered. I recommend it to everyone!
Do not know what star to give. I had to leave because very intense and upsetting. It was crowded. I plan on coming back to see.

This memorial is very well done. The exhibits convey a really bold and emotional message. I made the mistake of taking a girlfriend there as a date and we both left very upset. Don't make that mistake but be sure if you are in town, you visit the museum.

If I had looked online before I visited the museum I could have been there in time for a survivor that was speaking in the auditorium downstairs.  

This museum is not to be missed.  If you can, make this your first stop of the day to get your tickets for the permanent exhibit.  

There were several people with wheelchairs in the museum.  There were elevators throughout, so it should be fairly accessible.
The top floor is the 30's before the Holocaust and how it cam about. If you want to know how it could happen again I think the answers are here.
As a history buff, I've always been interested about the holocaust. Visiting the museum made me feel emotional looking back to what the survivors had to go through.
The permanent exhibit is just amazing.  The collection of stories and pieces of history are so moving.  I absolutely believe this is a must see museum if you visit DC.

Did NOT enjoy our visit.  For what should be a somber, respectful, informative and moving experience - our visit felt like a middle school building between class change!  There was a group of adolescents that came through the musuem while we were there - apparently unchaperoned and extremely loud and disruptive.  There was no musuem staff members within the musuem to monitor disrespectful/inappropriate behavior.  Once we exited, I went to information desk to voice my complaint - I was given a form at the gentleman who "looked to be in charge" pretty much blew me off!  The kids came out of exhibit loud and nothing was said to them!  Highly disappointed in the lack of staffing throughout musuem to monitor children running amuck and disappointed in response to complaint when kids came out into lobby !

I'll try to keep this post as polite as possible. Our experience was absolutely terrible. We arrived at the Museum at 10:30 am, we stood in line for about 45 minutes just to get to the front to find out that they were sold out of passes for the day. So if you go, make sure you ore purchase passes. The staff was super rude and not helpful!
Thought it was interesting for part of the exhibit, but then I just began to get bored with it. I would recommend it to people who are very interested the Holocaust.

Lots of written testimony to read amongst many pictures. A little short on contemporary film and actual relics. The shoes were so moving but more needed.
However, this was memorial weekend on a very hot afternoon so the crowds of children didn't make for the sombre experience it should have been.

Our recent visit, July 2015, was bittersweet. As a volunteer firefighter my husband was able to get our family immediate access to the museum. That was great as it was absolutely packed in the facility. Perhaps it was the throngs of hot tourists that dampened our visit but it was hard to read parts of exhibits because too crowded. Although it helped me also put into perspective the intolerable conditions the victims went through--like our comfort even matters! All I'm saying is I'd like to go back when it is less crowded, if and whenever that is. What we did see was incredibly moving....history of Hitlers rise was educational for me. The amount of killing doesn't leave you after you leave the museum.

This museum was very nice and very moving as a reminder of the horrible past. There are several exhibits within the museum that you can access without a ticket a few that you need a ticket to get into. We only saw the ones without tickets since we got there later in the afternoon and the tickets were sold out when we arrived. The exhibits were very informative and gave a good insight into the life and times of the holocaust however they were not very well organized and made the event very overwhelming. There is so much to see and read that not having any direction about how to flow through the exhibits made it somewhat frustrating to go through them.
My daughter who is 15 studied the holocaust and was moved the most by this museum.  The movies are worth every minute and excellent way for kids to really understand.

I hear the one in California is better.  Lots of repetition in the exhibits.  Kept reading the same thing over and over.  It could have been better presented chronologically as well.  It seemed to jump all over the time lines, thus needing to repeat the information over and over.

A must see for all.  We must never forget the atrocities that took the lives of so many.  This museum educates and with simplicity, imprints the message of "Never Forget" on all who see it.  Beautiful done and will permanently stir your soul.

I had never been before so I wanted to visit. Most of the museum was reading and walking through rooms. Lots of kids on school trips, hardly any of them interested in this significant history. Learned a few things I never knew about the Holocaust. Very sad and disturbing.

I went in the off season. So, one would expect that the crowds are smaller than, say, the peak season. If this is so, I HIGHLY suggest never going in the peak season. The museum was crowded, and I felt, poorly planned out. There are exhibits on both side of the hallways/corridors you go down. These corridors are too narrow for the amount of people that pass through them. Thus, you just find yourself pushing and shoving and you do not get to read everything as you feel as if you are just being hurried through. The theaters are too small for the crowds (maybe seat 30 people).

I felt very detached from everything. The museum was not as interactive as I thought it would have been. Oh, and it is not very friendly to those that are handicapped or injured. Elevators are hard to find and the museum is sprawled out over 4 floors. 

The best holocaust/genocide museum I have been to is the Museum of Tolerance in Los Angeles. Go there if you really want to feel, interact, and learn from this tragic event in history. 

The bonus for the DC museum is that admission is free - at least in the off-season - reservations may be required during the peak season.

No coat check/bag check during the off-season.

Take a free walk through the United States Holocaust Memorial Museum and then dare to say the Holocaust never happened. This must be an obligatory stop for every human on the planet. Take a box of tissues with you because if you aren't moved, you aren't human. Enuf said.

The whole museum is very moving and a must see if for no other reason than to understand just how low humans can sink and how susceptible we can be to propaganda.

We were there on a Wednesday off peak hours and it was still very difficult to maneuver through the museum. There is so much to read and so many visuals to absorb that you have to stand in line for each exhibit. Once you have reached an exhibit you are the one holding things up because you can't just skim anything you are always engaged.

Plan on spending at least 2 hours probably a bit more.
This was a must do...we need to remember what happened to as to not repeat it again. This museum really helps when trying to grasp what happened no so long ago.
When I was here, seeing all the things in display, I was overwhelmed with emotion. It is hard to explain it, so just go see for yourself. Very educational, sad, yet very informational.

We recently had a 2 p.m. ticket on a Friday in July. Tip: if you get there early there are things you can see before your ticket into the main exhibit (which is on 3 levels). For example, prior to your timed ticket you can go through Daniel's Story this gives everyone an overview through a child's perspective. Also there were two survivors talking to as many people that could huddle around their table. When we started our timed tour (on top floor) it was very crowded. At times we were just shuffling along; there was a lot to read. I think that they let too many people in at a time. The next level was a little bit more open. By the time we got to the final level, my 11-yr old had enough. A person could easily spend 4+ hours there.

This was a very moving visit with my children. I would highly recommend this museum. It is quite large and will take some time to experience it all. I would say 3 hours. They close at 5:20...will kick you out.
Visiting the holocaust museum was  very interesting and educational. Not sure if children can appreciate this as much as adults.

From the moment you step out of the elevator on the fourth floor you will feel magnetized to the displays, videos and articles written. The museum is for the most part set up in a timeline so that one can comprehend how everything happened and why. 
My biggest complaint is that most of the readings are placed at eye level, and with a packed museum it is virtually impossible to read anything without someone being in the way. If those where moved up higher then it would make it possible for more people to read at one time. 
The one image that sticks out in my mind is the replicas of the gas chambers. An image that I will probably never forget.

Wife and I went and while we overall impressed, there were a few issues.

First, Nazis occupied Channel Islands of Britain.  Brits there were collaborators and the Nazis there deported people just like they did in Europe.

Canada at Auschwitz was nentioned a lot by the prisoners there because it symbolized wealth, not just a collection point.

That being said, Canada was also guilty in the participation of the holocaust because it too had restrictive immigration policies and it too refused to let the SS St. Louis land.  In addition, Prime Minister Mackenzie King was a bigot and antisemite.  The Protestants and Catholics hated the Jews and other minority groups within Canada back in the day.  Example:  Christie Pitt's riots of 1933. 

Canada was not mentioned as a postwar destination.  Scores of people moved there after the second world war.  This was not mentioned.

Finally:  Not sure if it was a planned thing but the air conditioning was on full blast.  Very serious for someone who has a heart issue.

I found some information interesting and the "what would you do different" proposal a great question for everybody. 
But, overall, it is reiterative and depressing. I think so much more has been learned from this sad episode of humanity.

My husband & I visited the Holocaust Memorial on a recent trip to Washington, D.C.  
Let me begin, by saying I am not a museum kind of person, so this was an unusual stop for me.  The place is huge -   you walk in, and don't really know where to go, or what to do.  Eventually, we figured it out -   you get in line, and you get a ticket to go on the "permanent exhibits" tour (tickets are free).  Your ticket will have a time frame on it that you can begin your tour -   this is because there are so many people going through that they need to "control" the flow.  As it was, I found it very crowded, and often difficult to read the text at the Exhibits due to having so many people standing/walking in front of me.  The tour is self-guided, with photos and video -  some videos have audio, some do not.  There were portions of the museum we did not get to see, due to the number of people there that day -   just didn't have the time to wait for the next showing of the movies, etc.  As is the case with most attractions in Washington, D.C., be sure to go as early in the day as possible, otherwise you deal with throngs of people.

Where else can you visit the museum and then get a comforting bowl of matzo ball soup after reliving the holocaust experience?  i go to this museum to remember that history is constantly repeating itself.  I go to the holocaust museum to remember how horrible the holocaust was, and to remind myself that these atrocities can be repeated if we aren't vigilant about exercising our freedoms in this world.

This Museum is a needed experience. We must never forget the past and what is possible when evil is allowed. A tribute to those who died for their beliefs . Not a pleasant experience but very worthwhile. Very well done and very educational....should be seen be all and taken to heart.

We went to this Museum with a family of 7. We have 5 boys ages 10 through 16. The younger boys did not understand as much about the Holocaust but I do feel that the museum was laid out in such a way that it did help to educate them. I really liked the way it was organized  chronologically and felt that this was probably one of the best museums I had ever been to. Such a tragedy for so many people but this was a beautiful tribute to many families who lost loved ones. Would have planned more time because it really takes about 2 hours. Wish we could have heard the holocaust survivor who talked too.

Visited this museum in July with my teenage son and husband.  Had booked tickets for the exhibition a couple of days previous.  Arrived and got through security quite quickly went up in the lift to the exhibition and joined the end of a very long and slow moving queue.  The exhibition was overcrowded and it was difficult to see or read any of it on this level.  Whilst it was very interesting and moving what we were able to see the sheer number of people in the museum detracts from the experience.  Fail to see the point in pre booking tickets if there is no limit to the number of people admitted to the museum in a day!

Good presentation of the material, starting with the early history of the abuses of Jews and then working up to the Final Solution for Jews, Gypsies, Gays and mentally challenged. Moving pictures of ordinary people caught up in the madness. Most moving for us was the exhibit of a room full of shoes, then off by the victims just before entering the gas chambers.  However, hallways can be narrow and restricted in some areas.

The Holocaust Museum is free, but has staggered entry times.  If you don't want to wait in line to get a ticket to enter, you can reserve a time online at the price of $1 per ticket.  If possible, I would recommend going during the morning.  We went in the afternoon and could have spent more time in the museum when associates came through saying it would close in a half hour.

I like the feel of the museum. It's industrial and cold, and fits a museum displaying such brutality.  You start at the top floor and walk your way down.  There are two movies and a LOT of reading information on the fourth floor.  I think we were on that floor for over an hour.  On lower levels, I liked how they shielded graphic movie clips from children. We were disappointed though, that when we went, we couldn't take any photos other than the lobby and the remembrance hall.
This museum was very difficult to walk through but very necessary. This is not a feel good place.  This is a horrible reminder of what these people endured.

The other day I took a trip with my child's school and had a small group of children with me along with my child. After, a long bus ride we had to wait 30-45 minutes just to get up to the entrance. When, we finally got to the door they had a TON of security to get through. We were told to get rid of all food and drinks and had to get all the bags checked. Then, we had to wait 45 more minutes to get on the elevator and it was very hot in the area we were told to stand and they shoved us all together until we could hardly breath. After, that LOVELY experience, we had to listen to a lecture about how we needed to stand even closer together! When were finally allowed down to the elevators we had to stand through the entire speech about the meaning of the museum, which would have been so much better if we had gotten to sit down on the floors, but NO. After, standing for way over an hour we were finally allowed onto the elevators and the whole thing had made some students faint it was so hot. After all the waiting I was expecting a good display, but that wasn't the case, the museum was very crowed and hard to move through some people even shoved some of my group members so that they could read what the pictures said. There was also alot of reading and my group got very bored very fast.Also, I love that you can't bring in water but they sell it in there gift shops.When we finally got to the end of the Museum  we needed to eat and of course as we were ordering our food it started to pour. We had to run back to the museum and stand under the covered area right out front in the cold rain. After we had eaten we couldn't go back inside because we had drinks. Then, students got yelled at for being rowdy. But they are the ones who offered the trip to a bunch of teens. Also, all the personal were grumpy, pushing, loud, and very mean to the students. Overal, this horrible trip and I will not be comeing back.

Was told this was one of those "must see" museums.  While I was aware of lot of the history of the holocaust the detail of the brutal atrocities was sickening.  The last time I came out of Museum feeling like  I did was the 9/11 memorial museum in NYC.  The sign going in says Never Forget.  Will never forget and pray this never happens again

Didnt enjoyed that much since i ve been to those in Berlin with much more history! Its small and just "ok" of the history of the holocaust! Considering the 11 free museums in the National mall i wouldnt recommend this one.
Great museum!   Glad I had purchased my tickets ahead of time!  Very moving and educational tour.  My 12 year old and 15 year old enjoyed it as well.  Very well presented in different mediums.
There are many museums in the city, this one you should not miss. Many of the exhibits and photographs are extremely stark and thought provoking. Make sure you read every word.
Everyone must go and take a serious visit to the museum, spacially kids. Go with enough time to see everyting.

If we were able to actually view most of the exhibits this would have been an enjoyable visit. But the amount of people allowed to go through the main exhibit at one time is horrendous. So many people on top of each other, cant even move around much less enjoy the exhibits.

The museum should go with smaller groups. 

What I did see was very moving and informative. Would have loved to go through this will a lot few people at one time.
