I heard so many good things about this museum and it seemed like the majority of the place was closed off that day...I guess I shouldn't complain about a free museum, but it would have been nice to know before waiting in a long line that there wasn't much to see.
It's all about gloom and doom.  If that is the goal, it has been achieved.  If we are to learned from the past, then perhaps an adjoining museum should be built to show what we have learned.

This museum caters to those who like the shock and brutality of seeing others suffer.  I really wanted to go to the museum, and was embarrassed by my suggestion after the tour was complete.  It was a gratuitious, sombre, look at a dark moment in history.  You will not learn anything new about the Holocaust.
You'll be moved by the testimonies.  This place appeals to all of your senses, moving you to feel what people in those days had gone through.

This place was very sad, although I do think everyone should be made aware of the what really went on and the suffering people was made to go through, it is a very moving place you are given a ID card which I still have to remember my visit, also I love to walk away from some having learnt something, I did that day, How many brave people there had been and how without their help many more young people could have lost their lives. It is put together really well and gives you a journey to follow even children can experience and be grateful it did not happen to them but made aware that it did happen to some young children adults and older people, and it was not there fault just the cards life dealt them. My boys were very pleased to be able to understand and were also very moved by there visit here.

First off, the lay out of this museum if really poor. We found out after looking at pictures on TA that we missed several exhibits. It was so crowded and hard to find your way around. So maybe if we'd seen the whole museum I'd have a better impression. What really ruined the experience was the private tour guides that were bringing student groups thru.  They would barge in front of you. I was tying to listen to a video of the Cambodia exhibit and couldn't hear any of it from the tour guide and tour group talking. And they were talking about nothing related to the tour. So annoying.

I recommend visiting here , but we were disappointed a little with the layout. It is confusing trying to determine which way you should go.  The museum doesn't flow very well.  But, I recommend spending time looking at the artifacts and listening to the stories that are told. Such a tragedy what these people went through.

I was very disappointed. First of all they should prepare you for the amount of standing.
There were few places to stop and rest. The information is complete but there is too much reading. There was a older couple next to me that was complaining that they could not see the exhibits nor was there a place to rest as you moved from floor to floor.
I also found things not to be well marked.

Do not go after lunch (1:00 - 1:45). They crowded so many people in there which made it uncomfortable and impossible to enjoy the Museum. I am a short person and I could not read the signs. It had to be a fire safety issue too. I was so looking forward to this experience and I was so disappointed. It is a somber place and the emotions can only be felt in the quiet and the ability to move about your own pace.

Didn't see the museum, walked a half an hour in the heat to get there and then found out they give out passes in the mornings that you have to have to go in. Passes were all gone a long time ago. Funny, because the museum seems pretty quiet, wouldn't see why they wouldn't want more people to go in the afternoons. Seem to have some quota.

Despite your knowledge of history and the events of World War 2, nothing can describe the detail and accuracy that this museum provides on the events leading up to, right through the Holocaust to the end of the war and liberation.  The mood is somber and dark but it captures your interest from the moment you walk in the door.  In  high season you must book a time to enter this museum but we had no difficulty accessing it.  Plan to spend a good chunk of the day there.  Somehow looking at all the displays and reading all the info takes way more time than you expect.  It's easy to get wrapped up in the historical and chronological events, so before you know it the museum is telling you they are closing their doors.   I will definitely return to this museum.

This is a must attend. It is very informative, but not very well laid out/managed. The amount of information is overwhelming given the somewhat small spaces at times for certain exhibits and the pressure to move on for fear of "holding up" the line. Granted, this is a summer month, and it is expected to be crowded, I agree with another reviewer that it would be helpful to have a video at the beginning on what, when, where, and how.

From March through to end August you need timed tickets to get into the musuem. If you arrive around 9.30am you get a good queue position. They start handing out the tickets around 9.45 and then at around 10 you can get into the musuem. Head straight to the elevators near the information desk where they will check your ticket. When you get to the top floor you can see the exhibits without the crowds. There are couple of videos that you can watch ... at this time of the day you can generally get a seat to sit on. As mentioned by others it is very cool in the musuem .. worth taking a jacket. (especially so without the crowds). Easy to get to musuem .. only a 10 minute walk from the Smithsonian Metro station.

This is one museum that you should NOT miss!  From the moment we entered we were completely absorbed in the exhibits.  We took our time and spent several hours here.  My 11 year old daughter was focused for the entire time-this was very enlightening for her.  The museum is designed so that you can choose to stay clear of exhibits that just might be too graphic for your child.  Highly recommend this one.

We really, really wanted to have a great experience at this museum.  We purchased our entry time tickets in advance, expecting it to allow us to actually be able to move about the museum and see the exhibits.  We spent two hours in the museum, but saw very little, besides the backs of the people we were surrounded by.  It was a crowd-shuffle from the minute we got on the elevator, through every floor, down every hallway, until we exited.  Our kids saw far too many strangers' backsides and not more than a few brief glimpses of exhibits.  We understand that exhibits need maintenance, but were supremely disappointed to find a huge empty space, where the famous "victims' shoes" exhibit would be.  (Possibly the summer tourist season wasn't the only time this had to take place?)

I have visited many holocaust memorials around the world and was looking forward to adding this one as well but when we arrived at 1 o'clock we were told we needed passes, which were free but there were none left despite it being only lunchtime, no idea why they restrict this so much but left feeling very disappointed, we some some side elements but wonder why people are being denied to see this history first hand, having travelled from Scotland it should have been better

As a former teacher, I thought it would be an interesting experience.  When going, note you will have to walk several floors and read a lot, however, it is worth it!  The museum is well planned and it brings reality to the stories we have all heard.  It was very saddening!  I would highly recommend going.  If you are a very emotional individual, you may want to pass.

In short: how to do a nice museum without artifacts. The exhibition rely mostly on pictures, videos and reproduction. Few artifacts althought some casts are very interesting. Queue outside was too long, only one security check instead of 3 open. Not all the people of the staff are kind when you ask to know more.

Rude staff. Told had to stand in the rain when I got there early for my appointed time, could not even be close to wall of museum.  Told had to stand apart from my daughter when looking at exhibits, even after telling the guard that I was with my daughter, other couples or families were not even approached.  If they did not want people there they should have been shut.  Stay away from here

My wife and I planned a stop on our trip to New Jersey to see the Holocaust Memorial Museum.  We arrived in Washington, D.C. about 2, but had to find some place to park.  With traffic, we found one in about 30 minutes.  Once parked, we made it to the museum by 2:30 and received tickets for the 3:00 tour.  We only saw a few exhibits before we started our tour.  The tour is lengthy and we tend to read most of the informative details in the exhibits.  We were just entering the last portion of the exhibit when we were informed that it was time to close the museum.  Wow, two hours when by fast.  The main exhibit is informative, well designed, and mentally/emotionally exhausting.  We appreciated the depth and breadth of the details regarding the Holocaust.  We were continually amazed by what we were learning.  When we were escorted out, we needed some time to process the material and took a leisurely walk around the tidal basin.  While I cannot say we enjoyed our visit, we can say it was enlightening and informative.  Just what we had hoped for in this Museum.

Thorough, relatively compact and not maudlin. The Eastern European Memorials are better but that's to be expected. No photos except with permission. Heavy security which I would expect. Good information available to take away and read later. Relatively inexpensive and worth a visit

Love the way this museum is designed - the interior architecture transports you to the time period. The display of information, videos, images are moving. Very informative and wish I could have spent more time going over the information.

Check in at the information desk and get a ticket for next admission time - a 1 hour entry window. Initial rush in entering the exhibit can be crowded. Next time I would wait 30 minutes or so before starting.

Not impressed with this museum. I expected SO MUCH MORE. You must make a reservation online for a specific time slot. Everyone is gathered in front of the elevator with their designated time slot then let out. There's about 100-200 all in one spot and the line moves extremely slow. Very hard to hear whats being said on the speaker and very hard to see what's in the showcases. Wish it wasn't so crowded. Would not recommend for anyone with children or strollers, etc. Definitely do not recommend for anyone with claustrophobia.

I think this is an important museum for people of all ages to visit and understand the past. But, the masses of school groups makes it very difficult for regular visitors to give the exhibits the time and reflection they deserve. 

My parents and I went on weekday in May and were overwhelmed by dozens of groups of 50+ pre-teens and teens from all over the country, with seemingly few chaperones. The kids seemed to only be concerned with poking each other, showing off, and yelling throughout the visit, things like "Ewww burned bodies, I'm going to throw you in the oven!" and lots of other disgusting phrases. There were some groups playing tag with each other in the train car that carried the Jews to the camps. I spoke to a museum staffer who said it is like this every day during high season!

By the end, we were just trying to get out quickly because it was impossible to stop and reflect on any of the information while being surrounded by incessant obnoxiousness.

Thankfully, the museum attempts some degree of crowd control by staggering waves of visitors throughout the day (must book a time in advance online). Perhaps the museum could separate school groups in a certain part of the morning or afternoon, and allow regular visitors at other times. Or, please stay open until 7pm during the summer like many other DC museums, so people can go during off-peak hours. For example, we visited the National Air & Space Museum from 5pm to 7pm, and practically had the whole place to ourselves.

This museum is beautifully done; it is thoughtful and so meaningful.  Unlike most museums, it is very linear and there were several bottlenecks.  We thought we could do it in under an hour but there is so much to read and experience, 2 hours flew by.  There is a lot of reading and our girls 12 and 16 were overwhelmed.  I wouldn't take a child under 12.

This museum is so very moving.  This was our second visit here and we were impressed again by the exhibits and variety of media used and also by the respect shown by the other visitors/tourists.  It is a must see.

The content of the museum is both sad and interesting. Very well put together. If you can find the exhibits. The museum is like a maze, everyone was wandering around the corridors searching for the exhibits. We gave up and left.
The first time I went it was so much more feeling into it, it was as if I were reliving the time, I hate that I hyped it up to everyone...sorry.  Try the one in NY

We pre bought tickets, which is a must.  When we arrived, the tours were already sold out for the day.  We were able to get right in within 5 minutes.  The exhibit was great.  There was lots to read and look at.  They had several 14 minute videos to watch as well as actual testimonies from survivors.  The only drawback was that it was too crowded.  We went on Saturday at 12:45.  They should have allowed less people or did different timing.  The very first section was so over crowded you couldn't really see any of the visual boards or even move.  We ended up skipping sections just to get away from the congestion.  All in all it was a great visit and a must to see.

The integration of history, artifacts, photo, and film make this museum's exhibits both informational and emotional. Exhibits include rooms of suitcases, eye glasses, shoes, and other possessions that once belonged to real victims of this historical travesty, as well as an actual train car used to transport these people from the cities to the camps in outlying areas. This museum was one of the most memorable experiences of my Washington DC visit, and one that I will revisit on future travels to the area.

We're on vacation from Chicago with our teenage sons and this was our first museum.  It does a great job of telling a horrific story.  Plan on at least four hours and don't rush the experience.  A must do in DC.

Website made ticket availability very confusing. got there at 2pm and tickets were gone for the day. You could only see the temporary exhibits, only 20% of the museum.  Upon questioning the info desk person about where to go, there was a fire alarm and were immediately evacuated from the building and left.  This is the one place I didn't want to leave D.C. without seeing. Very upsetting situation.  Poor advertising on their part.

After visiting a few museum in Washington, I must say I was disappointed by this one a lot. I've seen a lot of movies on the subject, read a lot, researched and there wasn't anything new there.

The interior decor is really interesting but I felt it wasn't use to it's fullest. I liked the section talking about genocide all around the world; we forget it's still happening everyday. 

But other then that... There is already so much information going around on the holocaust, I feel if you make a museum, you have to show something that hasn't been shown before, have us, visitors, live it, not show us what we've already seen.

It was free to get in but there where a few tables with people soliciting for money.

Of course it is overwhelming and powerful to learn about this from survivors first hand and see the vast collection of horrifying artifacts.  I appreciate the thought that went into organizing the floors by period, but I would have preferred to have more flexibility on the tour.
Very informative. Wonderful museum for a horrible atrocity. Very sobering. Wonderful learning opportunity.

While the museum is good from a historical perspective it feels too sanitized.  Having traveled to Auschwitz and Dachau previously I was disappointed walking away from this museum that visitors will not get a true sense of the horror.

There are few experiences like the Holocaust museum inside the United States. I sincerely hope you take the time to go through the long exhibit. I was almost numb when I reached the end. Words cannot describe what I saw. You need to see it for yourself.

The holocaust memorial serves a very important purpose-to insure the world never forgets what happened there. It is a big story to tell and it tells it well. The problem is that it tries to do too much.

It took three hours to get through the museum which involves too much reading in places crammed with other people trying to read the same thing you are. It is a good idea to have timed tickets but there are huge bottlenecks which make it impossible to see or read everything. Plus three hours of walking up and reading displays really becomes mind numbing. There needs to be more interactive displays. The occasional movie clips were excellent such as those  dealing with the rise of the Nazi's.

There is much good here and it is certainly worthwhile. However even the teenage students were flagging long before the end of the presentation and smaller children simply couldn't appreciate what they see. As an adult it pushed my limits of endurance

My teenage boys really were looking forward to his museum and we all left a little disappointed.  Despite having an assigned time, they must give out way too many tickets per hour. You start up on the 4th floor and the elevator ride up was so crowded, we were all  claustrophobic, but then we got off the elevator and it was jam packed with people that you couldnt move, let alone see or read anything. It is important to get the foundation and history but there is too many people. It thins out as you go along, but it was almost too late. From what I did get to see, it was very moving and really made you wonder why more was not done during this time.

I was really looking forward to visiting here. For a holocaust museum it is so loud inside of people not being respectful, it's difficult to navigate and there wasn't much to see. If you want to be more in depth and get a better experience you must get passes in advanced. To me it was disappointing because the museum at home is unbelievable that to us it seemed not up to the caliber we were expecting from the nation's capital.

Very powerful exhibits from start to finish.  We procured VIP tickets through the offices of one of our Senators and were among the first to enter at 10:00.  We spent 2 1/2 hours and probably could have spent another hour or two.  The only thing I didn't like was the number of school groups (even though we were there in July); the kids in these groups were most definitely not quiet and respectful.

This museum must be the most striking and emotional museum that we went to on our first visit to Washington. We visit it during the off season, therefor we do not have to make reservations and not crowded at all. 
After getting your free ticket you'll get into the elevators, and head up to the fourth floor. From there, you make your way through the exhibits until you end up at the ground floor again. The facility is designed as a self guided tour. The museum really gives a complete picture in chronological order of the horror that occurred during the Holocaust. It is packed with information.

This museum has done an excellent job of telling the stories of the people that lived and died during such a horrific time.  It is sad to see, but you learn a new respect for everything and everyone around you.
This museum is well done. The Holocaust is real and it takes one back in time showing the different concentration camps and what the people suffered through. Very moving experience.
Do not miss this.  It will tear your heart out, which is a good thing.  Make sure you see the children's section on the first floor, the propaganda exhibit, and the scale model.

One of the best Museums I have been to.... ever. 

On the history side of the Museum it is awesome, very informative, to the point and without an agenda (No, we the US saved the world from the Nazis without any help) The lay out was cool, the staff was very kind and there is a free coat check....Awesome. 

On the personal and emotional side this is a tough museum, so much pain hate and death. It really made you think about yourself and what would you do if you were in alive and had to make these decisions. 

Would you risk your life to help others?
 If you lived in Germany would you be a Nazis and believe what your government is telling you?
 Would you fight or would you give up?   

Tough to answer honestly and I hope that I never have to live these answers. 

If in Washington DC you must go.... MUST
Even my then 14 year old son was so blown away that we could not get him out of the place. You can't prepare for ow powerful this is. This is a must see in D.C?

So many people say that this experience is not for the faint hearted. And believe me, its not. But I was so terribly frightened of attending, that I almost didn't go. As a Catholic with family who lived in the UK and Italy, working in internment camps during World War 2, I felt I had an obligation to see it. And I am so very pleased that I did. While it was terrifically hard to keep my act together and not get overly wrought by some of the scenes and information you take in, while on the tour, I found it was one of the most intriguing and well, terrifically educational experience on the Holocaust I have ever seen, read, etc. You are provided with a passport and identity of someone in Europe during the war, who is captured by Nazi's and taken away. You can be a young girl from Poland, or a male shopkeeper from France. Until the end, you don't know your fate. And that is terribly powerful. While the horrible attrocities that one sees during the tour are awful and tear inducing at every turn, you feel this powerful connection with fellow visitors so you know you're not alone. I found the info, and news events leading up to the war and what Hitler did to be very intriguing. I did not know what the atmosphere was like before war and how so many clues led up to this happening with so many countries turning a blind eye to so many being persecuted and killed for their religion, skin color, handicapp, etc. Hoping this will never happen again and working hard to understand it is really important in this day and age. An amazing place.

The Smithsonian did a great job bringing alive such a terrible part of world history. I agree with the other reviews that express frustration with the crowds- you need to read everything to get in the feel. I certainly would not bring any child under the age of 12, as to take in this museum requires social/emotional/ academic maturity.

Visiting this museum is a must.  It tells you how Hitler came into power, the evolution of his madness, and how the atrocities occurred.  We were surprised with the number of actual artifacts on display, including an actual train car.  Arrive early to avoid  crowds.
This exhibit is extremely well done and a graphic reminder of how humans can both treat each other and also look the other way and fail to help each other.  Well worth a couple hours.

Of all of the places I visited during my stay in Washington, D.C., there were three that touched me deeply...Arlington National Cemetery, Vietnam Memorial, and this one...the U.S. Holocaust Memorial Museum. This museum is a moving tribute to so many who lost so much. It's hard to imagine that people can do such atrocious things to other people but this museum does a very good job of telling that story.

Were very excited to show our kids the Holocaust Museum to enhance their education.  However, we were very disappointed that the Museum has an entire movie that mainly promotes hatred against Christians.  In a museum that shows what hatred can do to people, why have an entire movie within it  blaming Christians for the Holocaust?  Really took away from the whole experience!
The museum itself is up to expectation but the exterior is pretty special. It is located at the side of the Tidal Basin though. From Smithsonian Metro Station, it is around 5 minutes walk.

Many Exhibits to view not a place to visit with a short amount of time expect to spend at least a few hours here. lots of pictures audio and visual, but the thing that made it most real were the shoes.
Very well done display about this sad pat of our history. Highly recommended but not for families with yound children.

I do not like to go through a security screening worse than the airport when entering a public museum. We lingered on the sidewalk when we exited the property waiting for a tour buss and were told that we has to move down the sidewalk by an overzealous security guard. My wife and I and friends are in our 60's and do not pose a threat. If they are so afraid SHUT DOWN.

a very intense place to be and spend several hours. They did a great job in giving facts and telling peoples stories without loosing its tracks. A place to visit. They want you to open your eyes and understand. And they did this very, very well!

My niece and friend were making their first visit to Washington, DC.  #1 on my niece's  list was the Holocaust Museum.  Our first day we went over to the Museum and were told that there were no tickets left for the day, but we could go in to see a few of the exhibits that are not part of the "permanent" exhibit.  They also told us to go "on-line" for tickets for the next day.  We did go "on-line" and there were no tickets available for over two weeks.  They did not tell us that there would be tickets available early in the morning. When we arrived, there were again no tickets. We got there first thing the next day to see the part of the museum available, which was not much.  We were told there was a presentation by a survivor we could listen to, but after waiting 30 minutes in line, we were turned away as they were out of room.  They should have started seating people earlier or counted the number in line so that they did not waste time.  I went there 10 years ago, and it was fabulous.  There were no "permanent" exhibits.  The people working were condescending.  The one highlight was the volunteer survivor, Jacques Fein.  He spoke to my niece for several minutes about his life.  She loved it.  I will not be back.  They definitely need some signage as the people working do not give the correct information.

I brought my family here with high expectations. The museum itself is very beautifully constructed and I really like the self reflection room.  But overall there is a lot of reading to do if you want to get anything out of the experience, and a lot of people trying to read at one time.  There are very little artifacts.  I felt it lacking in experience, especially for young teens. We bought the prepaid tickets online.

This is an experience that all people should have.  It can be very disturbing, it is designed for us to experience a history that all should learn.  I deify even the most callous not to be moved.  It is a testament to both the greatness and the potential for horror that lies within the human race.  If I had children that I felt were mature enough for this I would bring them to Washington for this museum alone.   It could not help but make them better human beings.  The shoes alone speak volumes.  Do not rush to see it but take some time to experience it.  I am not Jewish but this place and My trip to Anne Franks room in Amsterdam opened my heart to the suffering of all people and helped define my humanity.  Your children deserve this.

One cannot describe the Holocaust as anything but horrible.  This museum gave the topic the somber and moving descriptions and exhibits required to adequately address the subject.  The exhibits were quite extensive.
Hoping to make a day of it. Alas, no ticks available.  Think I will not try again and skip it! A bit of a waste of time.
Don't miss out on this museum. It is sad but very touching. By the end, no one was even speaking. It is important to visit this museum to see what really happened.

We enjoyed the guided tour of the exhibit downstairs, but when it came our ticket time the boy working put way too many people into the elevator. Once the elevator doors opened we could hardly stop off because of the crowd. The top floor was so overcrowded that we were unable to read the exhibit. The crowd thinned out as we went down the levels. Thank goodness! This was the one museum that I didn't want to miss, but was disappointed because of the crowds. I feel that I missed so much of what there was to see and read.

We could not reserve tickets online as they were all released when we were coming and this was 4 weeks before our trip. We showed up 30 minutes before they were handing out same day tickets for the full exhibit. We were faced with several large groups of students in front of us that were rude and uncontrolled. After waiting 45 minutes we were told that all tickets for the full exhibit were gone but we could see the bottom two floors. So disappointed that school groups are allowed to stand in the same day ticket line to eat up all the tickets instead of booking in advance. Without seeing the full exhibit we really missed the full impact of the Holocaust
Museum. They need to figure out a different system and not allow groups larger than 15 to take all the tickets.

WOW.   you think you know the history and then you visit this museum.   And it will change you.    This museum was a living memorial to all that died so horrifically.  And to those who actually lived.   But as you go through, I challenge anyone to do so without shedding a tear.   Because it will move you in a way that you cannot speak.  

We went through and you could hear a pin drop.   When we left, we couldn't speak because honestly, I didn't trust myself not to cry.  It's an important museum to see if you are going to be in Washington.

We were disappointed with this. We waited years to come here. We waited until our daughter was old enough to understand. Don't get me wrong, it's very emotional and moving but we were expecting more artifacts and less reading. Cold, impersonal tv cubbies to learn about this era. We really expected so much more.

Undeniably a heartbreaking story about the plight of the Jews in Germany, of that there is no doubt, but, personally, it is too protracted. We watched the film of 33 minutes before going upstairs to wander the four floors telling the story of what went on that took us around 2-3 hours, but in all honesty the film had already told us what we needed to know. Then in all honesty, because of the crowds and the repetition, we skipped a floor and half. Yes it was bad and should never, never have happened but, again I repeat this is my personal opinion, it is too much.

We were a little disappointed that we didn't get tickets to see the set display. Although it is all free, they give out tickets for you to see the set display on a first in first served basis. We got there about 11am and the next time avail for the set display was 2.30pm. Too long to wait. We did however look at he ground floor and took in a couple of films that were showing. We had lunch at the cafe there which was a bit expensive but I guess the museum is free so it didn't matter. You have to pass,through a security check on entering the museum and the cafe.

This is a very somber memorial  Several of the more gruesome pictures/videos are in enclosed boxes that are at a height to restrict viewing by smaller people.  It was shocking to see how methodically the exterminations and persecutions were carried out with scientific precision.  The kind of place you think about long after you have left the building.

We visited on a Sunday, and it was very busy. I'm sorry, but you should not be bringing a group of middle school kids, and even younger, who do not understand the intensity of this event. They just walk around and are in everyones way who actually want to look at the exhibits. This is one reason I did not really get to take it all in. It is an impressive museum, and was designed very thoughtfully. A must see when you are in town.

Crowded small museum. I felt that i couldnt properly immerse myself in the experience because i was always in someones way. Its a little dated but they had a great exhibit on Syria in the end that most people tended to skip.
Give yourself at least two hours to go through the main exhibit, which takes up three floors and is a harrowing but wonderful trip through the horror of the holocaust. Bring tissues.

This review is about the dysfunction related to museum logistics. Three displays, including the hall of shoes, were closed for renovations. It was so crowded that we were forced to herd together with everyone while they read about the displays despite the "assigned time passes" distributed to visitors to view the permanent exhibit. It appears that too many passes were given for a certain time frame because we visited around 1pm and the museum was so crowded we literally could not walk and began to feel claustrophobic. Nothing was explained as to how to best navigate the museum, you ascend to the fourth floor via elevator and are left to your own devices. Staff are no where to be found to answer questions. We did find a nice Hispanic woman cleaning an area, but she did not speak English and was unable to respond to our inquiries. I felt the experience was flawed due to the above issues and the overall impact of the museum was less than expected.

As a guide to the history of the rize of Nazism the museum does a great job. At this point my admiration lessens materially, however.

The museum correctly demonizes Hitler and Goebbels, peopl who no one in their right mind admires, and are long dead. The museum does not make a serious effort to document the practically universal anti-Judaism prevalant in Europe before and since the Holocaust. Even more egregiously it does not demonstrate with any degree of emphasis or eloquence the high death rates of Poiish Jewry, where Jews had been over 10% of the population, and the indifference or active assistance of Poles in pogroms during the 1800's and the Final Solution. 

Modern concerns similarly are not mentioned. The discussion of the Mufti of Jerusalem points out his Nazi support but does not reference the fact that Yasir Arafat was his close relative. The museum absolutely fails to address Israel's growing delegitimization. In short, the museum is great at bashing people whose memory is ignominious. It carefully skirts politically delicate issues such as the past and continuing hatred of Jews, of which the Holocaust was admittedly the worst example.

Overall, the quality of the exhibits was disappointing. Much of it read like an 8th grade Social Studies text would.
Better than I expected at least. They tried their best to make something out of it, and they did quite well.

So many ways to describe this play. It was many things, I'd say. Some parts indescribably horrid to look back on and to put it lightly, nothing was censored. I would strongly recommend anyone even slightly interested in learning the true history of the atrocities committed by the nazis, you need only come here. It's so sad, but really puts into perspective how horrid WW2 was.

I was a little disappointed in this. I expected something different. The main display was built around the experiences of a young boy who survived the camps. But it seemed lacking. The reflection room to all the camps gave me a strange feeling cant really describe how I felt. calm but even though Im not Jewish felt an overwhelming sense of loss

The US Holocaust Memorial Museum is an amazing lesson in history. The museum is very crowded, so expect to spend more time than you think it will take to get through.  There is also a lot of material to read so you'll want time to work your way through the exhibits.  It's very well organized and appropriate for people of all ages.  They were able to collect many artifacts and pictures.  It's a moving experience and definitely worth the time spent.
I've read alot about the Holocaust but this really made it real. I didn't take any pictures because this is a place you need to see and experience yourself.
This is a place that everyone should visit. It's not a "fun" museum, but it has a ton of historical value. It puts human faces on a momentous time in history.

A must see in Washington DC. A gift of perspective with each visit.

Unfortunately the crowds were massive and unruly. The main room has been a zoo of children each time I've visited. Security stopped at the door, poor crowd control inside the museum.

The Holocaust Museum presents an objective, yet emotionally moving view of one of the low points of human history.

The museum itself is located in a building with a severe industrial architectural design emphasizing steel and concrete that calls to mind the grim concentration camps it seeks to evoke.

The permanent collection is contained on three floors and you need to get a (free) ticket in advance to get in at a particular time.  Despite the limited access, the permanent collection was still very crowded.  One floor focuses on the rise of the Nazis, another on the Holocaust itself and a third on the conclusion and aftermath.  The exhibits consist of sobering artifacts (a railcar used to transport victims, shoes of the victims), short documentaries on a variety of subjects and many, many photographs.  There is also a special exhibition on the lower level that changes periodically, and a large interactive exhibit for children.  

The museum strikes a balance between showing and describing the many horrors of the Holocaust while refraining from wallowing in the gore and gruesome acts.  There are definitely the horrible photos and videos of dead bodies, emaciated victims, executions and medical experiments that you would expect to see, but there is also a room full of pre-war photos from a Jewish village that was obliterated that has the same impact as a photo of a pile of bodies, while being less graphic and more personal,  The dual approach is highly effective.  Similarly, the museum is not out to cast blame or take positions on controversies.  The presentation is highly factual, with reasonable conflicting positions presented on multiple issues such as whether the US should have boycotted the 1936 Olympics or whether the Allies should have attempted to bomb the concentration camps.  The museum is also comprehensive, considering both the villains and the heroes of the Holocaust, the Jewish victims and the many non-Jewish victims (Russian POWs, gypsies, homosexuals, Jehovah's witnesses) as well.  In short, the museum does not have an agenda other than to memorialize and inform visitors about the Holocaust.

The museum advises that most visitors take 1-3 hours to tour the permanent collection.  We spent 4 hours there, only saw about half of the videos and by the end, were rushing to see all of the exhibits.  We did not even have time to see the special exhibition.  I really think 1/2 to a full day is necessary to really see everything there is to see.

We reserved timed tickets well in advance of our trip.  We entered the museum with hundreds of other attendees.  Unlike other museums this was does not do a good job spreading patrons out.   The museum is one big bottle-neck.  It was impossible to see or read any of the information.  There is little here that cannot be found in books or documentaries on the subject.  This was a huge letdown for someone who is well read on the subject.  Skip the permanent collection.  We had a more meaningful time in the Chilren's area which does not require a ticket and was less crowded.

I think the museum itself is beautiful. I think it's easy to navigate the Permanent Exhibit (which you will need a timed ticket for). However, it is hard to "feel" anything when you are surrounded by hordes of people, some of whom have no respect for the subject matter. There were actually small children (under 10 years of age) in the Permanent Exhibit area. Aside from the fact that they do not understand nor appreciate what they are seeing, they are not interested and so they disrupt and annoy other people's experience. 

Don't even get me started on the two women breezing through discussing dinner plans.

Most other visitors seemed courteous and truly interested, but with such huge crowds, it is just not fun. I'm hoping to return when it's not tourist season.

On the plus side, it was easy to get timed tickets for our group of 14. I went in to get tickets at 11:30 a.m. and could have gotten them for 12:45 p.m., but you can pick your time. While you wait to get into the Permanent Exhibit (the only thing you need the tickets for), you can look at the rest of the museum.

The security is almost as bad as the airport, so be sure to get all your coins, phones, cameras, etc. out of your pants so you don't have to be "wanded" like I did (for a very small belt buckle). However, the security guard was pleasant and patient.

Great museum from an information and historical flow perspective, but it gets incredibly busy, and youre forced to follow a very prescribed and inflexible route, sheep-like, from start to finish, which does not work for the volume of people going through it.

Its almost as if it was designed expecting half the footfall, because we often got stuck by queues of people reading all the (great) information panels, but blocking the narrow walkways.

Simply not enough room for everyone.

Shame really, as the content is some of the best Ive seen in any museum.
This is an important educational site that not only covers the Holocaust with multi media, but also includes more current genocide exhibitions including Cambodia, Syria and others.

We went out of season and were surprised at the unruly mob we experienced. Although supposedly only fifteen people are admitted at a time, there were so many people that we could seldom get near enough to the exhibits to see or hear anything. The few times we could get close to anything we were jostled and even shoved out of the way. It became a sad experience in every way.

An absolute must see...It's extremely tastefully done considering how horrific the Holocaust actually was.   Allow plenty of time. ..there's a lot to digest and a quick visit will not afford you the full experience.   The video presentation of survivor interviews at the very end is a must.   Do not skip, take a seat and listen

I am so glad I managed to fit this museum into our long weekend stay in DC.  It is something I knew I wanted to see. It is a very sad experience as one would expect given this horrible part of human history.  The exhibits are many times graphic but the experience will move you in a way I can't put into words.  Don't forget to go to the beautiful memorial room honoring the victims.

Upon entering the museum, you have to go through metal detectors. This is what can hold the line up outside. When we walked up, the line was very long and we thought it was because people were in the ticket line. Once inside, the tickets are free, but they only give out 2200 tickets a day. There is a little piece of the museum on lower level that does not require a ticket. Once you receive your ticket, you have access to the actual museum that starts on the 4th floor. 

The museum is very informative with lots of pictures and videos as you walk through. Much of it can be graphic as there are videos of the torture these people endured during that time. In one area, there are hundreds of shoes taken from those that lost their lives. Seeing all these shoes really hit home. There were all sizes of shoes as no one was spared. Very sad.

I'm glad we visited this museum, but visiting it one time was enough for me.

Probably a strange thing to say but this museum had the same effect on me as the 9/11 memorial in New York. Silence. Sadness. I came away thinking what animals the Nazis were and this can never happen again.
Please visit to support the memorial and the people it happened to.
This was quite an Experince so much history. Very informative make sure you allow enough time for visit. This is other side of what we saw overseas in Dakau and other camps.

The architecture and placement of exhibits are clearly well thought out. Pieces of history are very powerful, especially the room of shoes and photo exhibit. Layout and historical explanation was great. Pay respects in Remembrance room and do not forget to pay respects to the security officer who lost his life protecting visitors!

Although there was a lot of excitement about this museum I was a little disappointed.  I like the handout you get with the child's name and story on it but the rest of the museum was not very interesting.  The shoe room was good and the remembrance room was awesome.  Very short stay for my group.

My teenagers (13, 17) and I visited the museum and it was a heartbreaking but beautiful experience for all of us. The quietest, most solemn museum I have ever been in - I read other reviews where there were really loud kids/people but luckily this was not the case when we were there. Everyone was quiet and respectful.

There is a LOT of reading to do (you really can't take in the museum without doing the reading) as well as graphic pictures and video. I would not take children younger than probably 10 or so for both of these reasons...or kids that tend to be loud, hyper etc. 

Nice gift shop, and a little cafe on site.

We arrived just after lunch and were told there were no available tickets for the rest of the day. My daughter was really disappointed. It was quite a long trek from the other museums and there was no indication of the problem outside the building, only told after we had gone through the rigorous security checks.

The first thing that I read about the Holocaust museum was that you get to "become" a person 
that was actually in the Holocaust. I thought that you learned about you person and then didn't find out until the end, if you lived or died...but you read your whole little card and find out right then. The suspense was gone and this was disappointing. I think everyone knows the story of the Holocaust, so it's not shocking that you know most people lost their lives, but it's still hard to see the images and stories. About 2/3 of the way though, I was done. I was too sad to continue. The way it was set up was amazing and done well...but like I said, it was just "rough"
stuff to handle, so prepare yourself. One positive thing is that I left feeling the need to do something nice for someone. I had noticed the security guard at the door need a tie pin. I bought him a pin at the museum and gave it to him. My last thing, was that you should actually find out at the end of your "person" lived or died. It might have made me stay though the entire museum.

We visited during half-term, which was probably a mistake; there were numerous school groups visiting, and while the majority of pupils were well behaved, a number were quite obviously uninterested, noisy and verging on disrespectful.  This is a moving and eye opening exhibition, that steps through the events leading up to, during and after the Holocaust.  It is generally well laid out, but some areas are busy and become natural choke points making it hard to stand and read some of the exhibits. That said, this is an impressive facility and somewhere that society in general should certainly visit, for reasons too obvious to state.  We spent 2 hours inside, but allow about 3 hours to read (and watch) everything.
Very spooky. but that;s what they were trying to do. It doesn't take long to see it. Make it a side stop on the way to the capitol or the Netherlands Carillion.

This was very interesting. There was a lot to see. At some places, there were buttons to press to hear more information on what you were seeing. It was very quiet, so you can involve yourself in all that was there to see. It was sad to see and think that man can do such things to other men!

I received many recommendations to visit this museum enough to inspire me to visit on a Saturday.  One mistake I made straight away was not to get tickets for the upstairs exhibit.  When I realized my mistake I had to wait another 90 mins to see it instead of getting the tickets right away and seeing other parts of the museum after.  We ended up spending twice the amount of time here as we would have liked and wasted nearly half a day here.  However, the eyewitness accounts of their holocaust experiences were moving and I would definitely a visit to this museum.  Be wary of the crowds however.  The density of the masses here took away the quality of the experience for me to some degree.

Make sure to get tickets, especially in the summer.  I forgot and had to get take the time out of a morning to wait in line for same day tickets.  For a small fee you can do it online.

This is a wonderful museum.  We went with two teenagers and an 11 year old.  Our 11 year old son was very upset and confused by why something like the Holocaust would even happen and it opened up a good dialog for us to talk about it and that the purpose of the museum is so that we never forget and make sure it never happens again.

My visit to this museum will be one I'll never, ever forget. I'm not normally an emotional person but after a couple of hours walking through and looking at the exhibits I sat in the reflection room and my emotions got the better of me and I wept. How mankind can do what the Nazi's did to their fellow mankind defies understanding and belief. The museum is very tastefully done and they ensure some of the more upsetting video clips are behind slightly higher walls that smaller children would be unable to see. If there is one place in Washington DC that I would recommend anyone to go to, it would be the Holocaust Memorial Museum. But take tissues with you!

This museum is crowded. Go early to get a ticket. The minute you go in go to the ticket desk to get a cue to see the permanent exhibit. Even with the ticket it is crowded up there. The displays and films are done so well. It is an emotional museum but a good reminder of what we need to make sure never happens again.
This is too much for younger viewers.  I would say anyone under 18 should not visit.  It is too hard even for adults
what you will see here is just great, see the reality and ways germans used to kill, be prepared to cry
This is definitely something everyone should see at least once. We've been twice and it's sobering but important.

I think everyone should expect this museum is not going to be fun. But the exhibits were well done and educational. Not a detail was missed. It was kind of crowded and I think it would work better with guided tours instead of having to read so much but its free so I cant really complain much. Just felt rushed to read so the next group could come through.

So far this is my favorite spot in DC. Should probably warn you certain parts are very graphic and very sad. Really puts things in perspective for you. Do not leave DC without checking this place out. To see what those people went through is unbelievably tragic.
This museum is a must. The only thing is that i thought they would have had more exhibits and artifacts. Everything is well told and the staff is really nice.

The building is nice to walk around in, however, if you want to see the permanent exhibits, you must have a ticket to get into that section.  We tried for two days to get tickets only to be told they were out for the day by a gentleman sitting at a table inside.  Personally, I think it would be better to have people come and go through the exhibits without the ticket requirement even though they are free.  There is a little section to go through regarding the Holocaust through the eyes of a child, I believe it was Daniel.  That was interesting.
Some of it was cool but it was mostly just pictures. I fell that I have learned more from movies about the holocaust. I wouldn't go back

Took my kids there, but we were a bit disappointed. Espositions are very simple nothing special. Staff very unfriendly.
Can't bring in your backpack with water bottle.

It's not what I thought that it was going to be. There wasn't much there if you're not prepared to wait in line and book to see one of the permanent attractions. The only plus was seeing Daniel's Diary (the boy who survived the Holocaust and lived to tell the tale).
Make sure you plan something happy after your visit, it is very emotional for most. Does get a little crowed at the start.

We visited the museum the week between Christmas and New Years.  NO passes were required in the winter months but they allowed too many people in the buiding at once wihich caused an awful bottleneck on the top floor of the exhibit... Very claustrophobic... A lady beside me told me felt faint and was afraid she was not going to make it to the exit...When you were able to finally view the exhibits..which did not happen till we descended to the third floor. They were very moving, sombering and well executed. This museum is definitely NOT for children.

I had an unfortunate experience here as, there were quite a few exhibitions closed on the day. The overriding theme here is 'we must not forget' and I feel we have, as we are still perpetuating global atrocities. I'm glad I went but not sure I'd go back
Self guided tour to show uprising of Horrors of 20th century.  I learned many new facts. First hand accounts were moving.

We were looking forward to the Holocaust Museum our entire trip but were disappointed by the massive amounts of people. The employees were regulating the number of people but it just wasn't enough. Had to constantly stand and wait for people to move in order to see or read the displays. Basically gave up after an hour and a half and just walked through the rest to just be done with it. By far a disservice to the memorial and it's intended purpose.
This is a necessary, but very emotional and difficult museum to view.  We need experience this, but you will leave feeling heavy-hearted.  I do not recommend this for small children.

Maybe it was just not my cup of tea, but I found it to be much more of a memorial than an museum.  And I am sure that if I had stronger ties to the Holocaust, it would have been much more interesting, but unfortunatly to me, it was rather boring.  Don't get me wrong, it was definietly a history lesson that should be taught, but it didn't hold my interest.  We were in and out in an hour.

Waited in a line to get in. Once inside, three elevators took all the people to the 4th floor. It was so crowded and the lighting was very poor that you really couldn't see or read much.I felt like I was in a sardine can. (wasn't sure if that was part of the experience). If you are claustrophobic, think twice about this museum. We didn't make it past the film area on the 4th floor. We couldn't go forward around all the people. Too many people stop to look at their cell phones. Are you there to see the exhibits or your cell phone? Finally was able to get through the crowd, going backwards, and found an elevator that only takes you back to the first floor. Found some back stairs/ramp and walked up to the 2nd floor. Way too crowded.

So much to read and learn. My daughter, who's a young adult spent an afternoon at the museum.  We were fortunate to listen to two survivors before entering the exhibit.

This is definitely a must see.

I am homeschooling our children this year to travel the US.  My husband and I took our kids (13 & 16) to the Holocaust Memorial Museum this summer and I am so happy we did.  An amazing sobering experience for each one of us.  We have all read about the atrocities suffered, but nothing prepares you for what you experience going through the museum.  The displays and stories, the pictures and shoes, we each cried and towards the end it became to much for my 13 year old son.  We had to leave.  We had plans for later in the evening, but after our experience at the museum we decided to head back to the hotel to discuss what we'd seen.  Amazing museum and a must see, I would be cautious in taking younger children, this was overwhelming to say the least.
there are much better holocost museums. the one in Israel is ten times better. aside form the shoes it isn't worth more than an hour

We didn't go thru whole museum because I had small child with me so wasn't sure about seeing some exhibits. We did see Daniel's Story and is definitely a must see. To see what a small child had to endure was complete sadness.

The Holocaust museum is one of the most impressive museums that I have visited in the United States. At the beginning of the tour you are given the identity of a person who lived during that era and you unfold their story as you walk through the different levels of the exhibit. The museum is visually stunning and the artifacts, videos, photography, and even the architecture of the museum pull at your emotions. I am a high school teacher who has covered the holocaust in class and is generally knowledgable about the topic but there is so much detail in the museum that a child with no background in the holocaust and an expert can both enjoy the experience. If you are in DC you must go visit the Holocaust Museum!

The actual museum was great, they have some of the best coverage I've seen and I really liked the kids area where they explained a young boys story. I have been to Yad Vashim in Israel and found that most of the artifacts were either on loan or copies of what they have there.

Please frame this in the reference that I visited Auschwitz and Berkenau before visiting the museum, so it was not as moving of an experience as actually visiting places in person. The most impressive thing that I came away with were the numbers of Jewish people from all over Europe that were affected, and the reluctance of other countries, including the US and Great Britain to take in Jewish refugees. I think they did a good job of explaining what led to the rise of Hitler and the Nazi party. I thought the events that actually happened in the camps could have been better portrayed with models -- there was only a single wooden bunk bed and some eating utensils, no cells or small rooms housing prisoners to give some impression of how stifling the conditions were. Regarding the museum itself, the writings accompanying the many pictures were in too small font with dim lighting, resulting in crowd backups. The theaters with videos were too small, and people entered and exited through the same doors, again, resulting in crowd control issues. Things got so backed up at the beginning of the tour that you literally couldn't move. They need to adjust the spacing of exhibits at the beginning to space people out more.

This is an amazing museum to visit.  You need to prepare yourself.  It is not an easy thing to  begun to understand the depravity of the Holocaust.  When I visited, you could visit a rotating exhibition in the basement and visit the permanent collection that represents the museum proper.  The regular tour takes you by elevator the top floor and you wind down through all entirety of the exhibit without easy egress from the linear display.  This will take you 2 to 3 hours to complete and it is not a museum where you can easily progress in a non-linear path.  This why I rated it as 3 stars rather than 5 stars since it is a controlled experience.  I would not recommend this for children under 12 - too long and too complex and sorrowful a topic.  I live in DC and have been twice, I don't plan to go back unless a guest says that it is on their must see list.
Hoping to make a day of it. Alas, no ticks available.  Think I will not try again and skip it! A bit of a waste of time.

Don't get me wrong, this museum is very well done but unfortunately I didn't learn anything about the Holocaust that I didn't already know. I was unaware there is a limited numbers of visitors each day as I just visited when I had the chance and there wasn't even a queue!

This place is must go for people interested in WW II and how the stories of Holocaust. It will simply blow your mind by knowing the hardships people had to go through in those times. It was bit crowded though!!

It is very thorough, tasteful and respectful while being presented in a hauntingly realistic way. There are images and films which are terribly disturbing, but they are slightly hidden from plain view for those who don't wish to see them.

What can be said about such a tragic time in history? Just gets you right in the heart reading everything and seeing the exhibits. Plan on soaking up lots of facts and you can spend as much time as you want.

We found this museum to be laid out in a confusing fashion. We went around the area where we were encouraged to consider if neighbours who allowed the mistreatment were as guilty as the Nazis. There was a lot of good displays and exhibits telling the story slowly of the persecution of the Jews. However, it abruptly ended with them entering the concentration camps.  We looked at our maps and wandered around but couldn't find anywhere which told the story of life in the concentration camps or the subsequent liberation. 
We walked through the area aimed at children which told a Jewish boy's story and also a large memorial room where people were reading out victim's names. 
We left without feeling the whole story had been told. 
Maybe we missed out an area completely? 
We feel we learnt more from visiting Natzweiller Struthof concentration camp in the Alsace.
A lot of information in the museum but it was very difficult to see everything. They allowed way too many people to tour the exhibit at one time. Very very crowded.

We got thru the security line with one security guard barking orders and being plain mean. I fully support the screening of our bags but he was over zealous over a bottle of water. Overly aggressive.To the point another guard told me on the aside to see him after I went outside to throw my bottle away. Not a good introduction to a museum.

I am posting this  'the emperor has no clothes' commentary primarily so that people visiting will be aware of what to do, but also in hopes that the museum curators will remedy the situation (I have sent them a direct communication)
  I have visited the museum twice now, and the exhibit itself is quite good  and informative, in the unlikely event that you can actually find it.
  The first time I visited, a few years ago, I entered with my college age daughter approximately an hour before closing. We looked around the somewhat cavernous entry hall. We visited the letters and archives sections on various floors. It all seemed rather low content. Then, about 15 minutes till closing as we were getting ready to leave on the ground floor, we saw some few people wandering off down a darkened hallway towards some elevators. I asked the man there what was over there, and he informed me that it was the exhibit (!!) and that we could possible run through it before the place closed, which we proceeded to do (got some good exercise, it looked like it may have been informative). 
  I went back again to the museum last weekend with my other daughter, this time knowing what to look for but also wanting to see if my prior unfortunate impression was accurate. Well, I am afraid it really is true. We walked in, passed thru security, and again saw a large cavernous building with many people sitting about. There was no line or flow towards the exhibit. The information desk was sparsely in use. The exhibit entrance really is visually about as invisible as it can be (a darkened hallway with some guy standing in front of some closed elevator doors where you might expect to find some bathrooms - sort of like the Wizard of Oz 'pay no attention to the man behind the curtain'). Just people milling and lounging about in the lit area of the building.
  I have seriously never encountered as poorly a designed museum.

At first, we hadn't given much thought about visiting this museum as we had our plans set on visiting other Smithsonian museums at the mall. At the end of our second 'museums day' we decided to drop by for a short visit... which turned into a four hour stay.
We learned so much from this museum, and as other reviewers have put it: the whole experience is such a great reminder of what we humans are capable of doing--the good, the bad and the evil, it also reminds us of the human's strength in trying times. One exhibit to another, all were presented in a respectful manner.
I would suggest you prepare yourself mentally for a trip to this museum... some of the exhibits left me felt mentally exhausted, no kidding. Overall, this is a must see museum when you're in DC.

This museum was at the top of our list of museums to see, but we were unable to get ticket for our 7 days in Washington, DC.  No one and no museum had told us, before getting to DC that you need to get admission tickets for this museum BEFORE your trip to DC.  Even the DC tour guide did NOT mention the need for tickets.  I am in a wheelchair so we were unable to get to the museum and get in line, before 10am, to wait and see if there were any tickets available for that day.  People started lining up for that-day available tickets before 8am.  Those hoping to see this museum either contact them months in advance for tickets or be ready for a long wait in line and still be possibly unable to get  admission that day.  This was our biggest disappointment of our trip to DC..

Expected much more. Great showcase of the testimony, however they were some historical inaccuracy when it came to certain dates. Fail to expose the roles of many countries besides Germany. Other victims were not showcased enough. Most importantly fails to clearly explain why such a tragedy happened and why it could still take place.

maybe it's just me but I really didn't learn anything new about the Holocaust and I learned most of what I know from the movie Scheindlers list. I did spend a lot of time listening to the survivors stories of their individual experiences.
Well done.  This is something everyone should see.  It was a little crowded and there was a lot of reading.  I don't mind the reading, but be sure you allow enough time to get the full experience.

My daughter and I really enjoyed our time here. It is very well done and there are some 1/2 walls for people to not see more graphic things if they don't want to. The videos are very informative. It took us an hour to go through the 4th floor. We spent 2 hours total here but you could spend more time. Great location by the monuments so you can walk there when you are done.

This museum was the entire reason why I went to Washington, and I walked away bitterly disappointed. I know the museum directors were trying to teach through understanding, but it was lost by the kids playing in the lobby, the lack of explanation of the elevator (it was a cattlecar, but only those who studied it knew), and one floor spent entirely on politics. My favorite part, and the most important in my opinion, were the rooms of pictures. That was the connection I needed, but the feeling was almost lost when I went back into the lobby. Why don't they move some of the staff for bag checks to the lobby? This is supposed to be a place of prayer, worship, and rememberance, not a playground for children.
Unbelieveable exhibits.  Can be graphic for small children.  Take time to read the stories, gives lots of information about nazi propaganda and history leading to WWII

There were too many people in each grouping which made viewing the exhibits difficult.  The placards beside the items and pictures were small and so you had to be right on top of them to read them so you had to fight your way through the crowd.
Wonderful museum and worth a visit.  Lots to see on several levels of this place so plan ahead if you wish to take in the many exhibits. Well done.

You know I am on the fence about this museum.  On one hand it was humbling, everyone needs to know about the history and the 1st hand accounts.  On the other hand, the issue to me was the execution.  There were just too many people to take in everything this museum had to offer.   There were areas in the museum where it bottle necked and you were 3 deep trying to see a display.  The print and pictures were too small, so unless you were at the front you couldn't see.  People would try form a line but the next group would just walk up and push their way through.  The big high school groups were just too much, a lot of laughing and joking around.  Maybe have a blocked out time that's just for school age groups.  I still would recommend, maybe try and be the first ones in or the last ones out.    I would love to go back and take my time and really be able to take in everything, but unless they have smaller or after hour options I don't think I will return.

Very informative center from not so distant history of all those who died in the holocaust.  Some exhibits are disturbing and wouldn't recommend bringing your children as the include films of experiments on them.  Very large center.  In depth look of man's inhumanity to man.

We visited with a large group (15) of mixed ages. We bought tickets ahead of time and arrived at our designated time. The exhibit areas were so, so crowded the entire time. We kept getting separated because of the push of the crowd and it was at times impossible to make it up to the glass of the exhibits, much less read all the information before being pushed out... several people in our group had visited before and said it was so much more impactful on their previous visits since they were able to actually immerse themselves in the story being told because  the crowds were so much smaller... it got to the point that we just began walking straight through rooms just to get out since we couldnt see anything anyway...  I was very disappointed, as my family is from Germany and lived through WWII there, and I have always been fascinated with this part of history. I hope on another visit I will be able to enjoy it more!

WOW !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!  If you miss this  you might as well  stay home. It's very well organized and the presentation couldn't be better. I have a new respect for the troubles the Jewish people's of WWII. God bless. Will take others there.

This the second time we have tried to visit. Still not sure how to get in the place. It may say free, but you still have to have a ticket........in advance. Preferably, a week or two in advance. Good luck! We gave up.

I would have given this a lower rating if I didn't feel so strongly that this is a very important part of history for everyone to learn.  I know some things about the holocaust, yet still learned something new at the museum.  Here's what you need to know before you go:
1.  Even though they have timed entry to limit the crowds, they still allow too many people in at once.  It was the most crowded of the museums we went to, including Air and Space.  Part of the problem is the way the museum is set up.  There are sections that bottle neck, with a lot of reading or videos, combined with an occasional narrow hallway.
2. This museum had the strongest security (with probably the exception of the capitol and White House).  What's worse is I don't know why.  They took my aerosol insect spray and made me drink from the bottled water I brought.  OK, not a deal breaker, but the artifacts were few, and were all protected.  Most of the museum was reading and videos.
3. If you pre-purchased tickets, you can go right to the staff person at the elevator.  The museum doesn't give good directions.

We wanted to go to the Holocaust Museum on previous visits but felt that the kids were too young.  We finally went with our 14 and 10 year old.  They have each read several books on the Holocaust and going to the museum was a way to deepen everyones understanding of how this could happen.
Logistically there were a few areas where there were "bottle-necks".  If you don't mind missing a few displays you can move past the bottle-necks.
Be sure to see the movie when you first get out of the elevator.
Also for the children be sure to see "Daniel's Story"

Free passes before or when you arrive. The museum is very well done. It walks you through the horrific events of the Holocaust. Very quiet as you walk through because everyone is just speechless looking at the pictures and reading the facts.
I found it very sad and moving. They only thing I didn't like us a lot of it us in German and not converted to English. So not sure what some of it was about.

We got there right before they opened and were about to get tickets for 10:15a. We walked through the exhibit about the little boy which was ok. Very repetitive. Then went to the permanent exhibit. It was way too crowded. Very hard to read and see things and reflect on this part of history. We ended up skipping part of the exhibit because we were just fed up with all the crowds. The museum is full of information and replicas or casts of interesting items.
They need to organize this. There has to be a cap on how many people are allowed in at a time. There are so many people in there you cant read or look at a thing.

As others have said this is NOT a warm, fuzzy, fun museum! It is based on history. It is not for young children or children who do not like reading. I found myself getting tired of reading so much on the display boards and trying to get a spot to see the exhibit. I wish there had been an option for headphones that so many museums do today.  I was by myself  and was able to navigate the crowds and it was crowded. The flow is not fast because there is so much reading.
Check out the website before you go to find out about the online tickets and how the day of passes are handled!  I got lucky and there was a single ticket that was for 15 minutes after I arrived!
Plan to spend at least 3 hours. I was there for nearly 2 and was tired from all the reading!
Don't take me wrong you will find several holocaust memorial museums as you travel around the world. This was one is not particularly the best one. It has several levels and can be confusing.
Very powerful exhibit, but moves very slow. This is to be understood because there is so much to take in. Go early to beat the crowd but worth the experience

Visited the Holocaust museum with my 12 yr old son recently. Got the tix online for 1.00 each since I've heard it can be hard to get tix the day of. The museum was very crowded. Exhibits can be very difficult emotionally to take in at times. I would recommend to try and go at least once. I am glad we visited since I do not think I could go again. I wouldn't recommend for children under 11 yrs old.

Very crowded but well done exhibits. Everyone starts at same place, and was almost unbearably crowded at beginning. But crowds thinned out as you go thru museum. Very informative. Pick slow times to visit.

You will go through security so the lines may be long to enter the museum.  The friendly and helpful security guards worked diligently to help everyone enter quickly.  If you are unable to secure a ticket for the permanent exhibit there is still much to see.  We especially appreciated Daniel's exhibit written from the perspective of a young boy.  The book store had a book signing from a Holocaust survivor and the help desk staff worked with me to plan my next visit.  She suggested arriving early during the summer months in order to make the most of the day.  Winter months are not as busy.

A museum that tells the truth about a very sad time in history....very real with many accounts of victims and survivors.  Important for everyone to see.

Grammy Sharon

To say that I saw a see of backsides would be an understatement when trying to enjoy this museum. They schedule too many people each viewing period that it is hard to see much. People all want to see it and have absolutely NO problem stepping in front of those in wheel chairs. If they limited it even further, the amount of people they let in, I believe more disabled people would be able to enjoy this amazing piece of history.
we didn't know but in the morning we have to looking for tickets to see special exhibitions. and at the middle of the day no more ticket so we wasn't able to see thoses expositions.

+It is very well designed tour that builds up the true story what happened 
+The Memorial is very touching with the hundreds of candles 
o There are mostly pictures and videos and still very sanitized. In Europe there are several historical sights with huge amount of original objects.
-Somehow I managed to do in reverse direction and there are few people also coming with me (maybe I also followed someone). It did not ruin it, but the intended direction makes more sense definitely.

We were really looking forward to this museum, however the staff were all so very rude from the moment we entered the museum.  It really put a damper on our experience and made it hard to get into the exhibits.  It seemed that the staff were all very unhappy with working at this museum, and it was very evident that none of them wanted to be there.  I'd like to visit this museum again in the future, and maybe I'll be able to thoroughly view all of the exhibits without being harassed by rude staff.

A very crowed, somber, gut wrenching memorial that everyone should be required to visit.  The message our family of teens took away from the visit...we cannot ignore what we know is happening around us, we must do something.
Tickets were booked for a month when we checked prior to arriving to DC.  Same day tickets are available each day online beginning at 6am through the website.  It took us a couple of days to grab a few.  The tickets for the entire day are gone in less than 5 minutes!

While planning our family vacation to visit DC. The holocaust museum was the first visit we wanted to make. Months before we knew we needed to reserve tickets. Their website shows you need advance tickets. Starting in January their website had already had all their tickets reserved through May. Therefore, I needed to reserve tickets day of. Every day at 6:50am, I logged onto the website to no avail. One time I got as far as the verification I wasnt a robot and once that was completed the site told me the tickets I had chosen were no longer available. However 4/5 of the days the site at 7:01 stated No tickets were longer available.
I understand why it works this way, but either the system isnt working correctly or theres a system that is capable of claiming all of the available tickets at once. BEYOND DISAPPOINTED IN THIS COMPUTER SYSTEM. And extremely bummed my family and I were not able to get into the museum.

I had expected more displays to honor those who had died or held captive.  It just wasn't what I thought it would be.  It is very disappointing.  I was hoping on more history and visual displays than what was there.
As a European I thought this was a very poor quality museum which tried to sensationalise this horrific genocide.  The whole story was too Americanised.
You really need to get tickets before you go or it is not worth the trip.  We did not see anything worthwhile. I don't know why you can't walk through without a ticket.  It is a great building.
