I was skeptical at first but found the museum very tastefully done in it's presentation of the lives of those who were persecuted for no good reason other than for their political and religious beliefs.
I was very impressed with the dignity and respect shown for those, and especially for those who lost family, in this most horrific times in modern human history.
I commend all of those who gathered the multitude of resources to ensure that we never forget this time period in hopes of never repeating it.
this is my third visit to the holocaust museum. i think it a powerful, startling, sober, reminder of our history. i recommend everyone visit. allow time afterward for reflection.

This is a place every american should be required to visit before getting out of high school. The amount of history and information packed into this building is amazing. The stories are heartbreaking and you can't leave without feeling more. If you haven't been get on the bus now. In this crazy world we live in the experience is priceless.

12 is about the minimum age for this exhibit as it is a lot of information and exhibits to absorb.  The layout and flow is fantastic and provides a great understanding of stories and events that occurred.  It is obviously a somber museum but done very well. Takes about 3 hours to get through.
This museum has been perfectly curated to present a moving explanation of one of the darkest episodes in humankind. An emotionally rewarding experience.

We went into the memorial today and expected someone at the front desk to provide assistance, there were 8 staff around the desk talking to each other and the only one staff member looked at us and said nothing. We tried to figure out where to start but gave up and left. We understand it is a place of remembrance but with no assistance it is difficult to navigate your way around.

This was a very moving experience that could have been improved if the displays were setup to allow more people to view each area.  Often, it was very crowded and difficult to move.  This could be a reflection on the popularity of this exhibition.
Very moving.  I'm really not sure what to say.  It is a factual account of what humans can do to each other for reasons  that are beyond comprehension.  Pain and suffering of a horrific nature.

The Holocaust Museum is one place every person ought to visit at least once in a lifetime.  Thought provoking and startling is the reminder of man's inhumanity and the precious balance that exists to protect civilizations from repeating the awful behavior that is chronicled.  Somber, quiet and contemplative; it served to remind me that each of us is responsible to maintain a careful watch to avoid repeating such atrocities. Second visit.
Everyone must see this museum at least once. We enjoyed visiting a few of the Smithsonian's but the holocaust museum was by far our favorite.

We were on an organized tour of D.C. and we simply didnt have enough time to absorb all the information and displays available for viewing. My advice to you is to budget at least 3 hours and then find a little time afterwards to decompress.
 With teens this is a great opportunity for discussions during and after the visit. Most kids like to know how these events relate to their lives so talking about things afterwards is really valuable to them.
 You will walk out of the museum affected  and I think that is what the museum wants. The hall of photos itself is moving. 
Be sure to take a booklet before entering the elevator as it will give you the story of a person who was Part of the holocaust and that will help personalize the experience when you read their life story.

I gave this a 3 because when we showed up we just wanted to start walking around but apparently you need a map to figure out where to start. Once we got started we were going backwards with the flow. We ended up leaving because there were no signs pointing to the start and we really dont want to wait in the line for a map at the guest services. However, one thing I did like but we skipped was they had to holocaust survivors there you could talk to.

A very complete look at the Holocaust--pre and post. Took our 12 year old as this is something we all need to see. Was nervous about taking him but they do a nice job of shielding some of the more graphic exhibits.

I toured one section of the museum that was a story being told with the voice of a young boy. It was so realistic that I had to go out for air. After composing myself, I realized I just couldn't go back. It too real

Quite a somber experience. Very respectful. I studied this topic a lot at school and still learnt things I never knew. 

Took us around 1-1.5 hours. Probably 2 if you read every word and watch every film start to finish.

Would definitely recommend.

We arrived about 45 minutes before our ticketed time of 4 P.M.  First up was a long security line of almost 15 minutes just to get into the building.  Next, we checked out the Daniel's Story exhibition.  This is geared toward children and if you are visiting with young ones, definitely check it out.  Since we didn't have any with us, it was a little too simplistic.  Following that, we took the elevator up to the permanent collection.  It was wall to wall people as soon as we exited the elevator.  It was impossible to view the exhibits unless you could see over the three people in front of you.  I'm short so that was hard to do.  There was no place to move.  You just had to follow along with the crowd since you were surrounded on all sides by other people.  Eventually things would flow into a larger area, but the museum has several funnels that would force you into the massive herds again.  I was expecting to get an emotional response from the exhibits, but I was only feeling claustrophobic and cranky from the giant crowd of other visitors.  This was not how I wanted to remember the Holocaust Museum, so we found an exit and left after seeing less than a quarter of it.  Hopefully I will get a chance to go back again at a time when it is less crowded.

I waited till my son was 14 to visit. That was appropriate. You must get the pass to visit all the floors. If not come back when you can. It is very congested in the summer especially when you first get off the elevator. Go when you can take your time and absorb the information. It is incredible that this happened and seeing it on a timeline just makes you wonder how. We must learn from our past or we are destined to repeat it.

This is a great museum and tells the very moving history of the horrors of the Holocaust. We missed out on originally pre booking tickets online, but were able to obtain them 6am the morning of our visit when they released the same day tickets.  Only complaint is how crowded it was, it was very difficult to work through the exhibits and be able to read and absorb the information due to the sheer number of people admitted. Would love to see the exhibits expanded and somehow be able to better accommodate everyone that wants to visit .

This museum details the horrors of the murder of millions of people.  It is 4 floors of pictures, stories, maps, and videos.  It helps one understand the magnitude of what happened.  Our 11 year old grandsons had their eyes opened at the children's holocaust museum.  You must get tickets ahead of time and the place is still crowded.  Tickets are available on the net beginning at 6 AM each day if you did not get tickets ahead of time, but it is a limited number.  Plan your trip if you are going to DC and gets tickets ahead of time.

This museum is obviously about a grim event in history, but the building itself was oppressive. It was far to large for the amount of artifacts on display and there seemed to be a great deal of unnecessary walking on the tour. The information being conveyed here can never be cheerful, but I do feel the the museum in New York handles the material in a more productive way with before and after stories of the triumphs of the Jewish people and the building there is more intimate. However, this one is free. Don't bring small children, it is graphic.
This is very educational and needs adequate amount of time to go through.  Saw some of the younger children in complete awe, as they had no idea that this had happened.

This really is a great centre to visit, their are lots of different areas in this museum, all have different approaches, but deliver the same message. What the Jewish people had to endure, is horrific and what Hitler and his followers did should never have happened.
Very moving, very sad, this visit is etched into my mind, forever.

This was my favourite museum. I can't believe it is free for visitors. It is very emotional but was an important experience for my 11 year old. Suggest you prepare your kids for the level of sadness. They have a kids tour that shows life of the holocaust through the eyes of a child.  Go early. They line-up at 8 a.m.  We arrived at 9:45 a.m. but got to go through the major exhibit for 10:45 and had enough to see and do in the other sections until it was our turn.

I would recommend visiting after seeing the major attractions of DC. Museum almost has too much to absorb at one time. Allow at least three hours. Was really crowded the day we visited. Some of the information seems repetitive. I like studying American history. Some of the information seems politically biased. Such as "The U.S. decided not to bomb Auschwitz". The way it is written it implies that the U.S. should have bombed Auschwitz. Bombing a P.O.W. camp would have been unprecedented for the U.S.  Bombing a P.O.W. camp could have resulted in the enemy killing everyone in the camps. It is really easy to second guess decisions years after the fact. Always remember historical events in the context in which they were occurring. The shoe exhibit and the medical experiment exhibit make quite an impression. This museum does serve a purpose in reminding people to not allow such events to happen again. This museum is not for young children.

Dont bring drinks unless you want hassled for it. Security is rude and the made a 20 minute debacle out of 2 bottles of water and and a soda. If a water is opened you  have to take a drink of it in front of the idiots at the security desk before you can enter. An opened dark liquid of any kind you have to take it outside and trash it. Museum was very nice though.

Ive been told by many to make sure we go to the Holocaust museum. I cant even begin to tell you the emotions that hit me the minute I got off the elevator to start the museum. The first picture I saw, just broke my heart into pieces. It is an unbelievable museum. It is heart wrenching, sad, and honestly made me cry, but this is history and as horrible as it was, I think the museum did an amazing job. I cant say it enough, this is an absolute must visit!

We had two visits here. One in the afternoon and did the exhibitions that you don't need tickets for and the next day we went first thing to get tickets with no wait for entry. All the exhibitions were well laid out and informative, very thought provoking through out. The entry to all exhibitions are free, need to get a ticket for the main one
The photos,films and stories make the exhibit very moving experience. This is a piece of history that truly must never be forgotten or ignored.

I went into this museum expecting to see more artifacts than it contains.  Most of the museum consisted of videos and things to read.  If you have access to Netflix you can more or less watch all of the videos shown here on there.   There was plenty to read but it was not laid out well.  Guests were bottle necked at the beginning because each display offered 2-4 paragraphs of information that would take each individual at least a minute if not two to read.  An audio tour or more spaced out reading materials might work much better.  Still, an informative place to visit once.

The museum is beautifully designed. It is arranged in a timeline fashion, so you head up to the top floor and move floor by floor in chronological order. This was not explained well and we had to ask several employees before getting useful directions. So basically go to the elevators in the alcove near the information desk.

As you wait for the elevator up you pick a male or female ID card with the story of a survivor on it. You can think about them as you travel through the museum. What was happening to them as you read about the horrific events unfolding?

The displays are thorough and thought provoking. There are pictures, objects,audio, and video.

The hallways can get bottle necked especially around the videos. We visited in the fall, but on a Saturday afternoon. I'd recommend an off season weekday visit to optimize your experience. During the peak tourist season I'd go first thing in the morning and hope to see everything. You do need a timed ticket in the summer.

Even though I disapprove some of the choices made by the museum (for example the use of shocking pictures), I think they did a good job with the narrative and the informations. They also provide some context about anti-Semitism in Europe before the war, which is sometimes not very clear in many Holocaust museums.

Stopped by on our first day (Monday) and all time tickets were gone. Was told to call the next day, first thing at 6:30 am to reserve for tickets and we should get in. Took the kids through Daniel's Story but they wanted more.
Called at 6:30 on the dot and message said all tickets were gone. ??? Made two more calls 5 min. apart and got the same thing again. Very disappointed.

I was so excited to visit this museum and was very disappointed. Too many pictures and clips all ones you see on the internet.  Not enough displays and "recreations".  This museum needs to update and visit the 9/11 memorial for a feel of how to really do this type of a museum right.  I would not visit it again.
It was rather confusing on where to begin and Im not sure we seen everything that was there. It was interesting.

This is the most powerful museum in DC. From the moment you enter, until the last film, it is captivating. Upon entering you are asked to pick up a small booklet that tells the story of a victim. Some survivors, others perished. I will keep my booklet forever. The layout is perfect, taking you from the rise of Nazism and Hitler, right to the end of the war and liberation. Some exhibits are too powerful for young ones, and are blocked by short walls. There are films, artifacts, and pictures. Among the most powerful are the room of shoes, a three story room full of photographs, a transport rail car, and childrens drawings. Make sure to leave a half hour to watch the film at the end. It is a series of interviews with survivors, allowing you to hear some of their story. The courage of these survivors is beyond belief. It will allow you to leave with a sense that the human race can survive anything, and that there is hope for us all.

Great museum although I have suggested to the museum that it would be wonderful if they displayed other atrocities from history and the world.  But if you want to learn about the Jewish Holocaust (and that is what this museum is for) then you can get your fill here.  Personally, I know a lot about that subject but I would be interested to learn more about some of the other victims such as Gypsies, Russian POWs, the disabled and religious groups that were persecuted. 
They try and give you a somber feeling in the museum but I wonder if it is too much?  I mean that I would like to learn more and have more information rather than have an atmosphere generated for me.
Still this is something everyone should see and reflect on how we are all humans.  Period.
The tour is at your own pace. It was crowded on the day we went through the museum, but we learned so much.

I thought this museum was the best one I saw while in DC and I saw them all. As far as tickets go if you are a federal employee or military you do not need to get one of the timed tickets. All you need to do is show your military ID or federal ID. However, if you are like me and did not bring your federal ID with you that's ok. You can use other things to prove you are a federal employee. I was able to use my federal insurance card. If you are in the military I would assume  you could use something similar to show you were/or are in the military, but I don't know that for sure as no one I was with was I  the military.

The museum starts buy having you choose a ID book for either a male or a female. Inside you will find the identity of someone who was affected by the Holocaust. The book tells you where they were born the year and tells you what they were doing staring from the 30's and the rise of the Nazi's to of the war if they survived. My book contained the identity of a man born in Poland in the 20' he was sent to Triblanka camp and escaped in 1945 and was discovered hiding in the woods by Russian soldiers and survived the war.

The museum has a lot of great photos and movies that outline the rise of the Nazi's and the history of Anti-Semitism in Europe leading up to the rise of Hitler. The fist part of the museum after you get off the elevator is really congested it is a small hallway and it can be hard to see all of the pictures and read all of the captions. However, this does not last long as the museum expands and more space becomes available.

The museum is very powerful and can be overwhelming at times. Some of the content is very graphic if you are a parent with younger kids I would strongly consider whether you want you kids seeing pictures of people who were burned, in death pit and people who were or are about to be shot. The content will probably require an explanation for children when/if you bring them.

This museum in my opinion was the best one I saw during my travels. You learn a lot  about the people of the Holocaust and the other groups that they targeted such as Gypsies, Freemasons, and Homosexuals. You also learn about some of the heroes of the Holocaust such as resistance fighters common people who did not agree with what was going on. They also have a few captions related to Nazi Officers who did not believe in what they were doing and help Jews escape.

At the end of the museum experience you can lit a candle for people who were involved in the Holocaust. The candles are set up by Camp and Massacre.

I would recommend this museum to anyone who is interested in this period or wants to see the similarities to the retheric and propaganda that was going around in the 30's and 40's and the stuff going around today. There are a few places that are difficult handle but they add to the power of the museum.

Massive building with very little in.  Really disappointed.
There are mainly videos running and a few Exhibits...feels like they could have done much more for such an important subject
A fantastic place to visit. Get there really early otherwise all the entry tickets will be sold out. If you can`t tolerate air conditioning take a cardigan to this place!!

This museum has been on my bucket list for as long as I can remember. If you're in D.C., this is a must. It's free too.

Get there early, when it opens at 10am. I had to stand in line around the building but I did get a ticket. They only allow a certain number of people per day inside. On your ticket will have the times, an hour's time where you have to enter the permanent exhibit which takes 2-3 hours on 4 floors although I think if I could have spent the whole day if I could have handled it more. It's powerful & overwhelming. Extremely well done! Moved me to tears many times, so bring Kleenexes. Some of the things made me sick. How evil that Hilter & others were, is beyond me! 

There are bathrooms on each floor for your convenience. 

There are also 7 other exhibits. I only made it to one. 

Whoever designed this museum & the curator(s), hats off to them. Excellent! 

They'll tell you before you go in but this is not appropriate for children under 12. I dare say it wasn't appropriate for me being 37. A 9 year old that came up the elevator with me & her family & others, cried & cried that the family had to leave. This is moving & heartwrenching.

We took the whole family to visit. We were all left in tears. When the kids watch the war on TV, it doesnt show the reality, such an eye opener for us all. Trying to explain to a 9 year old how people can act was confronting but well worth the ongoing family discussion.

Our youngest family member was 9, and there wasn't anything I felt was too "mature" for him to understand or witness.  The special section called "Daniel's Story" is good for kids, and not graphic at all.  The rest of the museum seemed to be repeating the same things over and over about people being rounded up and moved to ghettos, then to concentratino camps.  There is a small part about what American soldiers encountered when they first arrived at a concnetration camp, not knowing what it was.  There was a large display of shoes collected from the camps that was rather poignant. The whole thing was rather dimly lit though, so much that it was too dark at times to see very well.  I guess I expected more about the cruelty inflicted, rather than the call for activism to prevent it happening again. (It's already happening in different ways in the world.  Look at world headlines and you will see it.)
Gutwrenching experience. However, it opens your eyes to how fortunate you are....and the how easy it is to fall to bad leadership
This is a must see. While this is a sobering visit, we must remember so history does not repeat itself.

Though somber, this is a educational exhibit. The new memorial  exhibit was large and airy.  Beautiful scriptures inscribed on the walls of  the memoriam.  I was with 2 teens who wanted to see this museum.  The ID card at the beginning of tour sets the tone for a engaging experience.  There are tour guides available but felt that it was not necessary for us.  There are also lots of additional video clips available to watch throughout the museum for an added informative experience.  We spent 2 hours and a brief trip to the gift shop (which was small in comparison to the size of the museum).  We could have easily spent another 2 hours but one of the teens attention began to wane.

We visited here having recently visited Auschwitz so we already knew a fair bit about the Holocaust. The museum was very busy when we visited, and you must go through a security 'beeper' before entering. When they saw a bottle in my bag they asked me what it was, I said 'water' and they told me to drink some in front of them. It made me feel like a criminal which I didn't like at all. I wasn't asked to do this anywhere else in Washington Dc and we went into a lot of museums.

As the museum was busy, it was difficult to read anything properly when walking around. There was a lot of information.

One of the good points is some of the models they have, like the train carriage.

I probably wouldn't recommend visiting if you've already been to visit something else associated with the Holocaust.

Although the museum is free it is recommended to book in advance tickets to see the Holocaust exhibits or you can turn up on a first come first served basis (www.wshmm.org)
Daniel's Story is suitable for kids and such a sad story.
Inside are interactive stations, photos, forcing visitors to confront the horror of the Holocaust.
Be aware its graphic and emotionally disturbing but a must see made up of four floors.

First thing you must know if you want to go here, get to the museum early! They distribute timed tickets and it's first come first serve. I wish I did my research about that ahead of time. If you don't get tickets, you'll only be able to see about 10% of the museum. The permanent exhibit is the rest and you'll need tickets for that. 

Second thing you need to know, start with the permanent museum then if you have time see everything else. My friends and I didn't do this, which was a bad idea. When we finally got to the permanent exhibit, we didn't have a lot of time to go through everything.

Third thing you need to know, bring tissue! Or just be prepared for an emotional roller coaster.  I can't even begin to describe everything I saw and read, truly heartbreaking.

Next time I come to visit DC, I would love to come back here and soak up more information about the Holocaust.

The visit can get emotional.  I suggest to begin your visit early in the day can get packed and you will expend almost all day if want to see everything inside. For us was one of the best musseums on the world.  Very complete collection.  Kind of sad feeling.  The tour at the elevators is a must do.  Please I advise to be respectful with others visitors that can get emotional because all the history envolved. Is a lifetime experience if you appreciate it. I wanna visit again. Each of the rooms surprise you more and more and some can be shocking. Worth the visit. I strongly suggest to visit.

I cant remember if free and it is depressing but people who cant get to Dachau or Auschwitz should then go see this place that gives you a timeline of the holocaust.  it could have been bigger and more knowledge but would have cost more. small Kids are shielded from  any gory details by a  ft ? high wall.

We were four in family with two teenage kids. We managed to get the tickets and squeezed into the long queues by abt 1.30 pm. We came out three hours later, spellbound. History illustrated was shocking. New experience for our kids. The visit made them realise that we live in a better world and better time. Unforgettable and not to be missed tour in WDC.

The Holocaust Memorial Museum is a nice tribute to the victims of the Holocaust and World War II. If one does not know a lot about the subject beforehand then it is very informative. I studied the Holocaust (and also the Museum itself through it's website etc.) quite a bit so a lot of the information and  general set up I knew about before hand. That said, I found the photographs and artifacts on display very moving. Good museum but if you're a history buff you might not learn anything new.

The Daniel's house exhibit is the best part.  The rest of the museum is somewhat tedious. The one part that acknowledges those who helped was very small and incomplete...no mention of Dietrich Bonhoeffer or Corrie Ten Boom and her family.  The architecture is striking and adds to the overall experience.

The museum was very crowded during our visit. I would recommend going very early when it opens or not during Spring Break. We had a difficult time seeing a lot of things due to the number of visitors.

Although at times a bit overwhelming at the loss of live, still a place to visit  It gives you a better understanding of the rise of Hitler and the hardships faced by those in the concentration camps.  Gives you a glimpse as to how life for those affected changed so quickly.  Definitely worth the trip.
Give yourself plenty of time to go through this museum, there is so much to see and listen to, all of which is very moving.  It is an amazing place.

Having heard tour guides claim how wonderful this museum was and with a deep affinity for the subject matter, we looked forward to visiting it during our very brief stopover in Washington.  On entering, we were confronted by a large, almost cavernous, very crowded building notable for an absence of signs with directions on where to go, what to see and any prices that may apply. Enquiries at an information desk indicated that the earliest viewing of a 'permanent exhibition' was at 3.45pm, which meant a wait of more than 2 hours. Had we read in any of the tourist literature that this exhibition was timed, we would have organised our visit accordingly. Unfortunately, with little spare time, we had to give it a miss and take in other museums. A quick climb up a flight of stairs to see what else was available (in the absence of clear signage) revealed a room with computers for checking possible relationships with Holocaust survivors. A display downstairs of a child's perspective of the displacement program was very basic and clearly aimed at young children. We'll take the guides' word for it being a wonderful museum but would have loved to see it for ourselves.

The museum security was AWFUL~ Don't plan on bringing any beverages of any kind *even kids sippy cups. They were rude, and just down right unprofessional. One lady even commented "Geez, people these days are just stupid" about me. I had a medicine infused in my water and I asked her if I could drink it. She first told me yes, then when I took a sip to down it...she said..."Lady, I told you, you can NOT drink that in here!" My daughter (5) and Son (8) were freaked out by her. It didn't start well with the tour.
The children's section is VERY well done..and I give large kuddos to the creative minds behind that. 
Having been to Terezin while in Prague I enjoyed this museum, and it is a great addition if you can't get to a camp during your life time to see some of that history. 
Just bypass the nasty security! :-)

I was expecting a lot more out of this museum. The displays require a lot of reading. There are some visuals but both my wife and I did expect to see better. You honestly get a better feel for the atrocities by watching a movie, and there are many out there on the topic. 
The train car is shocking, the size and number people they packed into those cars is unbelievable. It is a very somber and sometimes eye opening trip through the rise and horrible reign the Nazis had.

We took our children ages 11 and 13.  It was so informative.  Very moving.  We spent 4 hours there and will go back because we didn't really get to see everything.  Great knowledgeable staff.  A must for every American.
While dark and sad this is after all what the holocaust was. More spiritual and a call to human rights and justice; not necessarily a good place for little kids unless educated first
